references.title: Contrastive Personalized Exercise Recommendation With Reinforcement Learning

extracted.Students: 3879 ASSIST09 learners, 2119 EdNet learners, 17886 ASSIST12 learners, 19113 ASSIST13 learners, 821 BePKT learners, 120 learners for User Study
extracted.Initialization Method: Base recommenders’ weights randomly/init default; add two heads: RL head and contrastive head; a pre-trained DKT supplies the reward signal (dynamic reward)
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: After each interaction, the state is updated and the RL head optimizes cumulative reward (long-term needs). The contrastive head updates representations using augmented positives/negatives; DKT-based reward tracks changes in knowledge mastery (dynamic reward).
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Three data augmentations (mask, permute, replace) used for contrastive pairs; ablation varies augmentation ratios (0.1–0.9). Assist09 & EdNet_small used in these tests; excluded from user study due to insufficient content info.
extracted.Comments: Architecture: model-agnostic RL+CL wrapper; DKT-guided reward aligns recs with knowledge change. Results: improves ranking (HR@10) and learning gain vs. base sequential recommenders; supports classroom/OJ use and teacher oversight.
extracted.Limitations:
Data needs: relies on rich exercise metadata; some public datasets lack content detail (not used for expert judgment). 

Compute: RL+CL training adds cost. 

Scope: focuses on sequential recommenders; not a KG method; no explicit classical KT baseline comparison (BKT/DKT etc. are not benchmarked as recommenders).

User study evaluated essencially by 2 programmers only - small n.

annotations.text: Personalized recommendation of exewrcises often comes short in practice due to a couple of relevant factors:

The authors introduce RCL4ER, which makes recommendations by mixing self-supervised learning and reinformecement learning in the model’s output layer. Authors also explore a trained KT model while testing 3 distinct data augmentation techniques. Testing on 4 distinct public datasets shows promisisng results.

annotations.text: MOOC success requires great amounts of SR skills. Authors look to understand self-regulated learning behaviors in n online chemistry course and a high percentage of first-generation students.
Authors use video data to create 2 distinct indicators of SRL: video completion and time management and then perform k-means clustering.
Authors find 4 groups: Early planning, Planning, Procrastination and Low Engagement and see that planning behavior is essencial towards success in the course.

Mapping clustering to first generation status, the authors found that First-Gen students with planning behaviors perform just as well as non first-gen. However, Firt-generation low engagement students were the lowest performing students.

references.title: Enhancing educational evaluation through predictive student assessment modeling

extracted.Students: 253 second and third year undergraduate students
extracted.Courses: 5 Classes in Web Design, Web Technologies and Java Programming
extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, Student Demographics, Academic Background
extracted.Features: [Consistency, Following, Review Score, Attendance Score, Midterm Exam Score, Compulsory, Average Score in Other Subjects, Entrance Exam Score, Age, Gender, HighSchool GPA, HW Submission Rate, Subject]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Data was standardized and nans were imputted using kNN. 

Categorical data was One-Hot-Encoded
extracted.Models: kNN, Random Forest, Logistic Regression, SVM, Naive Bayes, Stacking
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.826 - Stacking, Precision: 0.805 - SVM, Recall: 0.825, F1-Score: 0.792}
extracted.Comments: Authors do a standard classification problem on data from an Online Platform. The goal is to predict end of course performance.
extracted.Limitations:
Models overfit.

Prediction of average grades would benefit from Midterms but authors intend to use it earlier.

Additional data sources could be explored.

There is no integration of other AI tools.

annotations.text: The work uses an interactive LMS to collect data about students and use it to forecast success. Data sources involves interaction patterns, time on exercises and, finally, progress across learning activities. Authors use multiple classifiers, with a Stacking model being the better one at predicting grade rages.

The rise of Covid increased the demand for digital ways to deliver educational content, which has contributed towards making the grading of students online somewhat of a nightmare, especially if the professors are not well versed in online evaluation.
In previous studies, authors have attempted to use different measurements to collect data “on the fly” and use it for predictive modelling, but most studies have been rather unsuccessful.

The aim of this paper seems to be to use “any means” to extrsct student data and use it for predictive modelling of student performance. The model is meant to explore different instructional conditions. RQ1 is aimed to verify whether it is possible to predict student performance from the start of the course. RQ2 explores which factors are the most likely to determine an outcome.

It is difficult to create an early warning system: The methods we use are often unreliable to detect students who may be struggling. The authors consistently contest methods used to assess what are effective predictors of student success:
#### Machine Learning-based works
ML has been used for multiple purposes in Learning Analytics. The authors start this sub-section with a review of which feature extraction strategies are the most commonly employed.
Authors highlight 3 main types of data:

Fernandes used GBoost on demographics and in-term grades and found that static attributes (the kind that is non-actionable) exhibit the highest predictive potential - gender, demographics. Jiang found quizzes and peer-assessments to be some of the most effective determiners of course-performance. Bernacki was able to only use LMS data to predict performance.
Something interesting:

Unlike Bernacki, these authors make a clear argument that including grades is paramount to predict performance.  For example, the authors fully accept a work that uses the midterm exam grades to predict the end-of-course grades (even though, more than likely, these grades are a part of the computation of the final grade). Well, at least they end the section acknowledging that the cited works only self-propagate already existing biases.

They also mention the use of a Multiple Features Fusion Attention Mechanism Enhanced Deep Knowledge Tracing (which I always understood as the task of predicting the answer to next question based on previous questions - not specifically something to measure student performance explicitly).

In general, this literature review makes a very broad and general broad-strokes look into “what is student performance and compiles multiple ways of going about it. There does not seem to a specific rule on what is the task at hand, or the data. It’s very confusing and awkward to read.
I’ll be honest… I’m at the halfway point of this paper and I still do not understand what these authors are doing. Hopefully I can finally understand it:

Data: Authors use multiple features extracted from five different classes made available in VnCodelab. Students are allowed to interact with the platform during class. Actions by student and professor are captured in real time.

Data refers to 253 undergraduate students of Computer Science with similar demographics and backgrounds.  Authors collected demographic data and 2 main variables: total time dedicated in the system & time spent doing reviews outside regular class hours.  The system also includes other performance-related daqta - Grades, GPAs, etc…
Data Cleaning and Normalization
The authors collected data about multiple facets of the interaction of the students with the environment. One such cases refers to variable Time Interval.
Time Interval: The authors intend to compute the difference in time between the students being on the same slide as their professor. This allowed to compute a variable called the “Mean Focus Rate”.
The final models considered 13 key variables:

Consistency, Following, Review Score, Attendance Score, Midterm Exam Score, Compulsory, Average Score in Other Subjects, Entrance Exam Score, Age, Gender, HighSchool GPA, HW Submission Rate and Subject.

Model Selection - Algorithms considered were RF, LR, SVM, NB and KNN. Stacking classifier uses all of them into a SVM meta-estimator. The task at hand is multi-class classification, where the goal is to predict a Grade Range.

Authors start by checking the performance of the models using only features that are directly used to compute the grades. Then, they exclusively use demographics, then followed by interaction with the course and (finally?) performance in events prior to current course/subject, whatever they are considering… I’m so confused…
Model evaluation:

The models were evaluated using classification metrics and other things that definitely are not metrics such as the “confusion matrix”. Also, how are these ROC scores being computed if we’re using multiclass classification? Are the scores seen the ovr averages? So many questions…
0.792 F1 score on test set.
Por algum motivo, estes autores decidem revelar os resultados da sua avaliação para “todos os conjuntos de dados ou seja, o dataset todo”. Ao menos poderia fazer cross-validation, não ??

Reconhecem a existência de overiftting no seu trabalho, vá lá…. mas não atribuem os seus resultados a, sei lá, um pequeno sample size (Dataset de teste tem 50 observações). Also, aparentemente Stacking deles não overfitta porque fazem 3 folds de CV. Resumindo…

Comparam 5 base models vs um stacking dos 5 modelos. O melhor modelo aparenta ser o stacking por ser aquele que tem melhores resultados e, simultaneamente, não overfitta.  O melhor score utiliza 13 variáveis??? Confesso que nunca percebi muito bem qual é a experiência…
Impact of Features on Model Performance

The authors start with the analysis of a correlation matrix between features. The explanation of color coding is incorrect. Attendance, Midterm Exam and averaqge grade on other subjects display the highest correlation with the Grade Range for the course.

The authors also do a second level of analysis where they look to create different models by iteratively removing one of the features and then looking into the decline in predictive performance. By looking at both techniques, the authors find that Gender and Compulsory have close to no impact on Grade Range.

The authors argue that they are proposing an EWS, which they definitely aren’t as their model uses, among other things, the Midterm Exam score. There is also no mention as to when they are making the prediction (i.e. they mention that they use data from 5 classes, is this the first 5 classes of a semester??). Their overall ML discussion of voerfitting is also innacurate.

Prior academic performance is a predictor of student success, same with attendance, in course performance, etc… Gender does not seem to be a significant contributor. In fact, the authors answer RQ by explicitly mentioning that demographics seems to play no role on a sample where they themselves claim to be almost homogeneous).
Interestingly, the authors deployed this model to 2 lecturers who have praised its utility. Ultimiately, both lecturers seem excited by the potential of these models.
Os autores identifcam as seguintes ao seu trabalho::

references.title: Implementing AutoML in Educational Data Mining for Prediction Tasks

extracted.Courses: 3 compulsory university courses: Physical Chemistry I, Physics III, Analytical Chemistry Laboratory
extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, Student characteristics, Learner grades
extracted.Features: [Gender; forum views; page views; resource views; folder views; URL views; assignment views; assignment grades; course total views; course total activity]
extracted.Moment of Prediction: Monthly early prediction during semester; 6 cumulative snapshots from Feb to Jul (except Physical Chemistry I starts in March)
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Custom Moodle plugin exported cumulative monthly reports. Students with no access to an activity received 0. No missing values. Final grade was transformed into nominal pass/fail. Auto-WEKA search space was restricted to tree- and rule-based classifiers for interpretability.
extracted.Models: Naïve Bayes, Random Forest, Bagging, PART, SMO, IBk-5NN, Auto-WEKA over Decision Stump, J48, LMT, REPTree, Random Tree, OneR, JRip, PART, M5Rules, M5P
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model:
Best AutoML results:
{Accuracy: 0.9302 - Auto-WEKA LMT for Analytical Chemistry Lab (June/July)}
extracted.Comments: Main contribution is showing that AutoML improves early pass/fail prediction over default ML settings while keeping models interpretable by limiting the search to rule/tree-based learners. LMT was the most frequent Auto-WEKA winner.
extracted.Limitations: Single institution and only 3 courses. Results depend on cumulative course-specific Moodle reports and assignment grades. AutoML training is slower and sensitive to time limits. No demographic variables beyond gender.

extracted.Limitations: Only 2 courses modeled for dropout. Class imbalance constrained the analysis and excluded one course. Dropout operationalization as empty final grade may miss nuanced disengagement patterns.

annotations.text: EDM has surfaced as an alternative to use DM techniques on large swaths of educational data. Due to the multimodality and complexity of educational data that is available today, creating effective models may require skills in DS and/or domain knowledge that is not accessible to everyone. Thus, it is imperative to find novel ways to extract meaningful features.

Models used only consider tree-based or rules-based options for interpretability purposes. The results show that the AutoML based approach ended up surpassing other techniques in the task of prediction, which represents a positive aspect for non-expert users. No indication of early prediction.

references.title: Early prediction of learners at risk in self-paced education: A neural network approach

annotations.text: Problem is student retention in self-paced educational programs. Authors use LSTM to predict student success and achieve remarkable results on OULAD - both when using the entire dataset (84.6% accuracy) and even when only using the first 5 weeks (71% accuracy). Authors also use Shapely to identify what are the most important predictors of student retention.

There is smaller and smaller funding, so universities need inventive solutions to address the challenges they commonly face. Among such problems is student retention - failing and, in the most extreme cases, not concluding the cycle of studes at all. Early prediction is something that is attempted by institutions for multiple reasons: both pedagogical and administrative.

Most work in prediction of performance is done on MOOCs and blended courses, but vcery few is done on self-paced courses. Moreover, the techniques employed are general traditional machine learning approaches without great expansions into deep learning - with explainability being pointed as one ofd the main factors behind this.
Thus, the aim of the paper is two-fold:
The literature review section is divided into 2 main subcomponents:

Self-paced learning allows students to be more flexible in their study. However, this often comes at the expense of decreased success, especially among those students who lack the proper self-regulated learning abilities. There is some work on self-paced learning published but not a lot in terms of self-paced.
Usually there are two ways in which prediction is performed:
Typical inputs:
Modeling approaches:

references.title: Participation-based student final performance prediction model through interpretable Genetic Programming: Integrating learning analytics, educational data mining and theory

extracted.Features: [Object, Subject, Tools, Community, Rules, Division of Labor]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
On a first level, the logs were categorized when it comes to 4 participation dimensions.

The preprocessed dataset was then classified according to one of the 6 features used.

Target variable was obtained from clustering analysis. Clusters used as input human-evaluated assessments of each student’s achievement.
extracted.Models:
GP-IRCM
NNge
Random Tree
Logistic Regression
Perceptron
Naive Bayes
extracted.Comments:
Authors present a theory-driven approach that tries to optimize performance and prediction in a CSCL setting.

The work starts with the categorization of learning activities on Geogebra according to participation dimensions. These dimensions help a secondary categorization of the features into the Activity Theory constructs.

These are then used to predict the performance labels that result from clustering. 

Different algorithms are compared and the difference in performance between GP and other candidate algorithms is done using a t-test. Metrics are evaluated as an aggregate (all 5 labels) and specifically for the at-risk label.

GP ICRM outperforms all other options when it comes to overall predictions. Naive Bayes, however, outperforms the remaining models.

Authors follow up with an analysis of the understandability of the models:

Random Tree and GP ICRM are the best models on this mark.

Results show that student participation can be used to predict student performance.
extracted.Limitations:
Level of students is not mentioned → different levels of education usually exhibit different behaviors. 

No early prediction.

Alleged ease of use requires extensive pre-classification of learning activities into the activity theory constructs.

Small sample size  and baseline models selected for comparison are all rather “simple”.

Performance labels are inferred by a clustering algorithm.

No assessment of quality of work generated into the features.

Difficult to reproduce if unfamiliar with the VMT environment.

annotations.text: Common issues with predictive models of student performance stem from lack of practicality and/or interpretability.  Authors review and propose a different approach to creating predictive models that is, they argue, more usable.

Authors quantify student participation in the environment as one of 6 different variables. The authors argue that this classification of activities into different variables differentiates them from other approaches as it provides context to the interactions. Then, the authors use Genetic Programming to predict student performance (thus far unspecified). The proposed model outperforms other models in prediction rate and interpretability.

Current methods of early detection of students at risk often circle around the notion of detecting at-risk vs not at risk. However, it would be helpful to create models that assist all students → not only those at risk. This would expedite the roles of assessments.
Prior work on performance prediction has usually involved the employment of either Statistical or Data Mining Techniques, but these methods have limitations:

Interpretability is important in early-wrning systems because these are meant to be decision-support systems → meaning that the humans need to trust the model. Also, it will not be helpful for the purposes of telling professors what should be changed in order to improve the prediction. Without these, the authors argue that it would be impossible to explore personalized education.

As mentioned, previous work often involves the use of non-interpretable models and a very simple binary flag of at-risk vs not at-risk. This causes concern because these early-warning systems can soon become tools to intervene only on those who fail→punitive interventions. Another common limitation is the lack of theory embedding in the models, both the models used for prediction as the ones used for e-g- feature selection.
This is seen by the authors as a huge flaw, calling for the development of a principled, theory-based method for the synthetizing of factors from factors.

The authors intend to use a Genetic Algorithm for predictions, arguing that it offers a good tradeoff between predictive power and interpretability. To provide interpretability to actions, authors draw from Hratsinski model of online learning as essencial towards learning. For that, the authors label activities according to 6 different possible aspects. The 6 aspects are then gfiven as inputs to the GA.

The endpoint of the work is a Predictive Model that achieves good performance using 6 different features and that allows educators to understand the causes for a specific prediction.

Data sources for early warning systems  often involve the use student information, learning management systems and/or financial information system. With time, the encoding of this data may change → encoding of learning activities for example.

Most of the models used, such as neural networks or SVM are called black-box models, as they focus on predictive power and not interpretability. There are, however, some studies with white-box models with a much higher emphasis on understanding reasons behind a prediction: for example Bayesian networks. However, these pale in comparison to Decision Tree-based or Rules-based models.

Still, non-parametric models tend to require a lot more data than parametric ones, which is an increased hardship in educational contexts (likely with the exception of MOOCs). GP appears as an elegant alternative that provides interpretability while working reasonably well on smaller datasets. Thus, the authors attempt to use GP to balance multiple constraints:

Knowledge is a construct that is manifested not only in minds but communication, interaction, theories and models. Online participation specifically is regarded by Hrastinski as the most important variable in online learning. As such, he proposed a theoretical model for online learning participation containing the following variables:

A framework that links individual and social behavior. According to it, learning is a joint activity of learners, tools and tools. Learning is thus a result of social participation, not mere practice. It involves 6 constructs:

Analysis of the logs of a math course comprised of 4 modules. In total, the course had 122 students. All activities took place in a Computer-Supported Collaborative Learning environment. The logs from GeoGebra categorizes data in different event types: Awareness, Geogebra, System, Chat and Whiteboard. From these logs, the authors created new measures of participation:
From these 4 categorizations, the authors make a subsequent classification into the 7 constructs of Activity Theory:

These 6 new constructs are then the features for the predictive model, which as mentioned before, will be the students’ performance resulting from the clustering approach. An preliminary look at the final form of the features is presented below:

GP tests different candidate prediction models, in this case Rules-based models, and assesses their fitness. Then, it iterates over multiple generations in order to minimize the fitness of the candidate solutions. The authors consider the use of 3 genetic operations: cross-over, selection and mutation.
The authors draw from previous work on a model call Genetic Programming Interpretable Classification Rule Mining. This model presents 3 advantages:
Fitness Function
The fitness function uses 2 metrics: Sensitity (Recall) and Specificity (Recall, but for negatives).
To provide a better balance, the final form of the Fitness function is as follows:
The final algorithm can be summarized in the following steps:
The authors test the GP-IRCM against other classifiers:
Nearest-Neighbor Generalized Exemplars - a sort of rules-based nearest neighbor algorithm
Random Tree
Naive Bayes
Perceptron
Logistic Regression
The scores on each construct give an immediate understanding to the instructor into which dimensions each student is struggling more (as loing as they are familiar with Activity Theory).

The results show that the GP ICRM algorithm outperforms all other options on all metrics, at the level of overall predictions. Moreover, it is only superseded in the identification of students at risk by the Naive Bayes classifier.

The authors followup with a sample showcase of a model built using GP IRCM as it is the model that generated less rules and the simpler rules (even though IMHO the interpretability of these constructs hinges so much on theoretical understanding of the constructs that it sort of looses its mark).

references.title: Using institutional data to predict student course selections in higher education

extracted.Data sources: Course and Instructor Scores, Student Preferences
extracted.Initialization Method:
Qualifier tags are built from the evaluations of a course using an ontology. 

When a course has not been taught before (and has no evaluations), essencially the algorithm provides it with an “unknown” constant ranking.
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Model recalibrated using cross-validation and variable weighting of AHP criteria (course difficulty, GPA, popularity, interest)
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
– De-identification of student-level data

– Aggregation of preferences by GPA group and course type– Normalization of grades [0–1]

– Pairwise comparisons for AHP matrix consistency check
extracted.Recommendation types: Prediction of most likely course enrolments by student (ranking)
extracted.Evaluation: {Accuracy: 0.64, RMSE: 0.072, Pearson r: 0.69}}
extracted.Comments:
– AHP effectively integrates identifiable + non-identifiable data to respect privacy constraints.

– GPA and course difficulty are dominant predictors of course selection.

– Model matches accuracy of prior identifiable-data approaches.

– Emphasizes self-efficacy as a psychological factor in academic decision-making.
extracted.Limitations:
– Limited generalizability (single program dataset).

– AHP assumes stable weighting preferences across cohorts.

– Manual feature scaling and expert-defined hierarchy.

– Lacks behavioural, temporal, or motivational data.

– Not a “true” recommender—predicts enrollment likelihood, not utility.

annotations.text: There are enormous conomic benefits to draw from automating course-selection and many current predictive models actually try to predict the number of students that may enroll in a given course. Multiple issues exist with this approach:

Thus, authors propose a recommender system for courses only using information available in the School’s institutional system. The approach starts with the extraction of features and their use with Analytical Hierarchy Process to predict course selections. Tests done on a sample of 1061 higher education students.

Students who procrastinate more are more likely to do badly in the course.

annotations.text: Authors use a 2-stage system with data collected from the first year of a program to predict whether the students will be successful or not. In this context, success is defined in 2 terms:

More than developing a predictive model and testing it on data collected throughout 12 years, the authors also propose a segmentation of students. annotations.text: Giving effective feedback and monitoring its effects in an harduous task. Thus, in order to understand the effectiveness of feedback, the authors compare the behaviors and learning performance of two groups of students:

Samples uses 49 undergraduates. Differences in learning performance were assessed using t-test and ANCOVA. Diffferences in behavioral features were evaluated with lag sequential analysis and Hidden Marov Chains.

Results show that students who received feedback tended to be more engaged than the control group → e.g. they completed more elearning assignments. Moreover, the analysis showed that, while all students used all LMS systems, students in experimental group were more active in task and discussion views and posts, updates on tasks and group performance viewing. Moreover, all students tended to increase their engagement as the semester advanced, but the increase was more noticeable in the experimental group.

Students with Stable Weekly Study Patterns (n=158) - entropy of recurrence in weekly activities remained stable and high throughout the course.

Students with Steep Chances in Weekly Study Patterns (n=40) - highest entropy of recurrence at the start of the course.  These students were the ones with the lowest performance on the course.

Students with Moderate Changes in Study Patterns (n=90): Slight decrease in recurrence over time.

Measure is sensible to window size. ptimizing for window size may be an interesting approach,

Authors collect student trajectories and cluster them. 3 patterns were identified:
Learners with steep changes achieved lower performances on exam. The authors also tie back their results to existing SRL theory.

annotations.text: The authors analyze 17 courses with 4898 students. To do that, they create a set of course-specific and course-agnostic models predictive models. Results show a lot of variability from course to course but, generally speaking, the course-specific models outperform the course-agnostic model, suggesting low portability of models between diferent courses and instructional conditions.

A second interesting conclusion is derived from early prediction using logs and in-between assessment grades. Authors argue that the introduction of these intermediate grades pretty much takes out any additional value logs might have as predictors of performance.

Over the yearas, institutions have started to bet more and more in LMS and VLE to deliver online or blended courses . LMS store interactions, which means that this data can be used to analyze and improve learning and teaching. One of the main applications is the identification of students at risk of failing.

Previous studies have attempted this. However, data from the LMS is far from being something that is theoretically sound. Instead, it is merely a sequence of clicks. Moreover, most works work on sample samples, thus limiting the ability to draw general conclusions. In addition, the creation of a general would also be harmed by individual differences between courses and/or institutions.
This work analyzes an early warning system that is meant to be portable between different courses. To dop that, the authors only consider predictors that are available for all courses.

Most previous works follow a frequentist approach without much consideration for theory. Other studies have focused on analysis under the frameworks of SRL. Under constructivism, learning is an active process → learners seek and acquire information. Instruction is a process that supports learning. Under this view, LMS are a tool that allows students to access all of the materials and resources they need to learn, streamlining the process.

Under SRL, learners plan their actions, execute them and then evaluate their progress. This is affected by task conditions (resources) and cognitive conditions (beliefs, motivations, etc…). Other theories exist and should also be explored such as connectivism or situated learning.

Data used in predictive modelling of student performance may measure different constructs such as student characteristics, dispositions and demographics. Recently LMS have also grown in popularity. Howver, due to a generally small sample size, the generalizability of any potential conclusions is harmed.

Pages read, content pages viewed, etc… have been reported to« be significant predictors of student performance in some previous studies. Moreover, students who dropout tend to be substantially less engaged with the LMS than students who complete the course. Depending on the authors, different predictors are found to be significant: forums messages for example. Even moreso, there are differences in the way certain variables could be accounted for (e.g. whether the data is analyzed dichotomously or as a fully-fledged count). Common variables include:

The variability in the results obtained can be explained by differences in the instructional conditions between different courses and contexts. This leads to inconsistencies when it comes to predictors, importance and even effect. Thus, the authors raise questions relative to the generalizability of course-agnostic predictive models.

The issue of portability stems from the OAAI, which aims to scale current predictive modeling solutions for a greater context. A previous work that combined Purdue and Marist universities found some success in identifying similar predictors and relationships between variables. However, works by other authors do not find the same relationships (with Gasevic 2016 being a good example). The analysis of 9 courses on different contexts did not reveal this fabled ability to generalize as the course-specific models tended to underperform the general model.  The current study expands on Gasevic work by working on 17 b-learning courses with similar contexts.

Observations on early prediction also returned interesting results: one work found that users who used the LMS on the first week tended to outperform students who have not used it. Other works have argued that predictions at later stages tended to be better than predictions done earlier. Moreover, adding predictors such as grades on midterms greatly increased the predictive power of the models used. This performance data has been shown to be a significant predictor on multiple studies which ahs caused researchers to argue for the ideal moment of prediction to be done as soon as possible but after the first assessment.
Thus, the authors also attempt early prediction an look to analyze the impact of intermediate grades.

The authors collected data from Moodle of Technologyu Ubiversity of Eindhoven throughout 2014-2015. During this time, 42 courses were using the LMS, out of which 17 fulfilled the condition of having more than 75 students. The final sample included 2,913 unique students, totalling 4,989 student-course pairings.

All courses ran throughout 8 weeks. Courses in Quarter 2 were formally considered 13 weeks as there was a 2-week Christmas Break followed by 2-weeks of exams. All courses were delivered in b-learning format having between 3 to 6 hours of face-to-face followed by a significant amount of resources being available on Moodle.
Data collected included data available on the LMS AND the grades on assessments → intermediate and final. A notable difference is that, in this context, the final exam grade was the target variable.
As far as features go, most of the features used were previously used in the literature. However, the authors also introduce new variables:

The analysis involved the extraction of logs from Moodle and their transformation into different features. In between assessments were considered: available on 16 out of the 17 courses used. All assessments were considered to be available by the end of week 5.

There were 4 predictors based on total online participation. Reasoning comes from the obsevation that engagement tends to be positively correlated with success and higher grades. In addition, the authors also extracted variables associated with motivation and attendance.

Some assumptions had to be made for this to work. For example, a session was defined as having the length between first click in a while and all following clicks that do not have a gap between clicks greater than 40 minutes (definition of session end). Moreover the authors also included predictors measuring the regularity of study.

The authors conducted a correlation analysis - both at a general level but also in a course-specific manner. Then, they created multiple linear regressions using OLS estimation. Moreover, the selection of features was performed considering Backward Feature Elimination. Results for both regression and classification were assessed using 10-Fold Cross-Validation.

Most variables, 21 out of the 23 to be specific, were found to be correlated with the final exam grade (exceptions were irregularity of study time and discussion posts). Between assessment grades were the only feature found to have a significant effect on all courses. Other more general measures of effort suych as resources, sessions, quizzes were fgound to be significant on 75% of the courses with the remaining ones only being found to be important in between 30% to 60% of the courses.
In short, the authors observed the same issues that their review reported on → different predictors have different effects in different courses:

The regression analysis using Hierarchical Multiple Linear Regression (in a way that accounts for the fact that the data is clearly clustered by course) shows that most of the effects are present at the student level (48% of the variance) and very little (8%) at the course-level. This means that, while the results thus far are not great when it comes to portability → the fact that most of the variance comes from the student actually is a point for possibility of course-agnostic models.
The representation also allows for an analysis across 2 points of view:

Variables such as total number of clicks, online sessions, views, grades and irregularity of study time all had a significant positive effect on the final exam grade according to both points of view. This suggests that it may not only be important to compare students with others, but also with themselves on other courses.

The authors argue that, combined, these results suggest low portability: only a very small subset of variables has consistent effects across different contexts → a very big oversimplification. However, the importance of individual measurements suggest that including performance in other courses may be interesting → not in a sense to design a course-agnostic model but to include performance in other courses as a predictor for new courses.

The authors do away with the regression that uses courses as dummies and instead try to perform a linear regression for each course. Explained variance for these models varies greatly from 0.08 (no significant predictors) to 0.37, which had 4 predictors at the 0.05 significance level. The results show that a general interpretation of conclusions about specific predictors should be limited to a very small subset of variables: more online sessions, lower standard deviation of the time between the sessions, and less time until the first session (i.e., starting early) all go with a higher grade.

The previous reults refer to end-of-course, which does nothing to assist on early prediction. Thus, the authors extract data to make a prediction at the end of every week only using the most basic variables. It was found that some of the more complex variables were neither not able to be computed, or not meaningful yet.  The authors report on a regression per week (using separate courses as dummies).
Exam Grade Predition

Predictive performance of the Regression increases with the advancement of time. In week 0 (before course starts), none of the variables are found to be significant. AS time advances, performance increases: without considering the between-assessment grades, the Adjusted R-Squared increases from 0.14 on the first week to 0.22 on the last week. Including the between assessment grades greatly increases model performance (adjusted R-Squared) to 0.43 by the end of Week 5. In essence, all predictors are pretty much worthless when compared with in between assessment grades.
Pass-Fail Probability Prediction

When converting the problem into a classification one, the authors found similar success (or in this case, lack thereof). Accuracy of the logistic regression over time went from 60% at Week 0 to 69% at the end of the course, even after accounting for all predictors such as in-between assessment grades. Week 3, where the model achieved 67% accuracy, seems to represent the best moment of prediction when it comes to the tradeoff between performance and actionability.

For intervention, the authors argue that getting more students at risk is more important than intervening on students who do not need it. To intervene on 95% of the students at risk, the auithors would need to make an intervention on 85% of all students in the sample, which is clearly impractical.
In conclusion, LMS data does not seem to be an very accurate measure of student performance on the exam.

The authors conclude that there does not seem to exist a general set of predictors of student performance, aside from the most basic ones. Moreover, the existing predictors also vary between courses/contexts substantially both in terms of scale or direction of effect. In fact, only the inbetween assessment data showed to be correlated with the exam performance on all courses.

Thus, portability seems to be low, even if it is only on data from the same institution and LMS. Other preprocessing decision such as only using variables that exist on all courses also did not seem to improve generalizability.

As future work, the authors propose exploring more data sources and, eventually, diving deeper into course specific activities in order to extract variables that could better explain the learning phenomenom.

references.title: Equality of Learning Opportunity via Individual Fairness in Personalized Recommendations
references.journal: International Journal of Artificial Intelligence in Education

extracted.Recommender System Type:
Hybrid 

– Matrix Factorization: GMF, NeuMF

– Collaborative: UserKNN, ItemKNN (cosine)
extracted.Initialization Method: Each base algorithm as in the literature (e.g. random seed for Random; 100 neighbors for KNN; 10 latent factors for GMF/NeuMF)
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: After base lists are generated, a fairness reranker adjusts ranks by optimizing a weighted equality–consistency objective controlled by λ
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
– UTF‑8 lowercase tokenization of titles/descriptions
– TF‑IDF vectors for content‑based
– Ratings binarized for implicit CF
– Graph‑based: P3‑α, RP3‑β with fixed α/β, k=200 neighbors
– Hybrid: CoupledCF combining item+category embeddings
– Negative sampling for MF
extracted.Evaluation: Global (λ = 0.01) metrics : • NDCG@10: Random 0.000, TopPopular 0.035, UserKNN 0.012, ItemKNN 0.021, P3‑α 0.001, RP3‑β 0.000, GMF 0.008, NeuMF 0.010, ItemKNNCB 0.042, CoupledCF 0.013 • Consistency: Random 0.586, TopPopular 0.516, UserKNN 0.618, ItemKNN 0.615, P3‑α 0.578, RP3‑β 0.572, GMF 0.662, NeuMF 0.652, ItemKNNCB 0.699, CoupledCF 0.717 • Equality: Random 0.872, TopPopular 0.795, UserKNN 0.885, ItemKNN 0.891, P3‑α 0.850, RP3‑β 0.847, GMF 0.917, NeuMF 0.906, ItemKNNCB 0.959, CoupledCF 0.969
extracted.Comments:
Introduces a fairness‑aware post‑processing layer on top of existing recommenders to balance accuracy with equality and consistency across learners in an online course platform.

A model‑agnostic reranking framework that can wrap any base RS.A λ‑weighted objective combining two fairness metrics—Consistency and Equality—under three allocation strategies (Global, User‑proportional,Per‑learner). Demonstration that small accuracy sacrifices can yield large fairness gains.

Dataset & Split: COCO platform snapshot (6.56 M ratings; fixed‑timestamp split 2017‑06‑08; train ≥ 4 ratings/user; test ≥ 1).Base Algorithms (10): CF (UserKNN, ItemKNN), MF (GMF, NeuMF), graph (P3‑α, RP3‑β), content‑based (ItemKNNCB), hybrid (CoupledCF), plus random & popularity baselines.Reranking Strategies: Apply reranker post‑hoc under three λ allocation schemes, varying λ to trade off NDCG vs. fairness.Metrics: NDCG@10 for accuracy; Consistency & Equality for fairness.

Accuracy (λ = 0.01): ItemKNNCB tops NDCG@10 at 0.042; CoupledCF at 0.013; baselines near 0.00–0.035.Fairness: CoupledCF achieves highest Consistency (0.717) and Equality (0.969); ItemKNNCB also strong (0.699/0.959).Trade‑offs: Under User‑ and Per‑learner strategies, modest NDCG drops (≤ 0.01) yield substantial fairness improvements (Δ ≥ 0.1).Key takeaway: A lightweight reranking step can substantially improve fairness with minimal impact on recommendation quality.
extracted.Limitations:
– Equal max‑weight for all principles (no learner customization). – No live user evaluation; offline only. 
– Metrics ignore position‑sensitivity (top of list matters). 
– Only positive feedback modeled.
– Reranker requires choosing λ—stakeholder decision with no guidance. 
– Fixed static split; no temporal/adaptive modeling of user changes. 
– Assumes consistency ≡ fairness—other fairness notions unexamined.
 – No assessment of computational cost or scalability for post‑processing at large scale.

annotations.text: Learners from an online ERS should have the same opportunities. The authors propose a set of possible avenues that could be considered towards ensuring equal opportunity in ERS, among them being a new metric by which to evaluate ERS.

The framework starts by the outlining of principles that the ERS should follow and expands into how sticking to those principles can be put into practice. Experiments (backtested on MOOC data) show that their system makes recommendations in a more equal way with negligible loss of personalization.

references.title: Visualizing patterns of student engagement and performance in MOOCs

annotations.text: MOOCs are becoming more and more widespread and universities are interested in knowing the learning process of students in this format. The authors explore 2 distinct MOOCs and find common patterns when it comes to initial motivation and end of course attrition. However, when using more elaborate visualization the authors claim to have found meaningful distinctions between the courses.

references.title: CoBERT: A Contextual BERT model for recommending employability profiles of information technology students in unstable developing countries

annotations.text: The authors propose CoBERT, a fine-tuned BERT implemetation used in an item-based filtering Recommender System that is meant to provide employment recommendations to students and graduates. The model uses information that is demographic, relationship between employer and graduate and overall competency of the graduate in IT.
The authors evaluate their model using 4 metrics: Normalized Discount Cumulative Gain & Maean Average Precision (Offline evaluation) and Novelty & Diversity (online evaluation).

references.title: Semester-level Spacing but Not Procrastination Affected Student Exam Performance

extracted.Courses: 4 editions of an Introductory Programming Course
extracted.Features: [Spacing, Procrastination, Exam Scores, Studied Amount, Course Easiness, GPA, Sex, Ethnicity, Non-Native English Speaker, Level, Program, Pass/Fail]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The choice of SEM was done to address the problem of confounding co-variates. For example: students who study more tend to study in more days - so have higher spacing (but success can be attributed to the study and not specifically to the spacing itself). 

The SEM model tries to predict the final exam score from other variables. In total , 6 equations are created. 

All variables are standardized.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Comparative Fit Index: 0.998, SRMR: 0.006}
extracted.Cluster Description:
Authors find that Semester-level spacing and procrastination are independent from one another. 

Spacing increases exam scores buuuut procrastinations does not have a signifcant effect.
extracted.Implications:
Authors propose a new distinction between procrastination and massing: 

Procrastination is studying later or closer to the deadline

Massing is studying in fewer sessions.

While procrastination itself does not have an impact on exam scores, Massing does.
extracted.Comments:
Authors use SEM to identify the relationship between spacing, procrastination and exam scores. 

Spacing usually leadds  to better grades, procrastinationg behavior, by itself does not seem to have an impact.
extracted.Limitations: Small sample size.

annotations.text: It is possible to study regularly and still procrastinate, especially if the students waits until to late to increase the amount of studying. The authors examine the interactions of 674 student swith an eBook of introductory programming.

When using SEM modelling, the authors find a small positive correlation between spacing and procrastination but, more importantly, find that there is a positive relationship between regular study intervals and final exam scores. No significant effect between procrastination and exam scores was found.

references.title: Learning Analytics on Student Engagement to Enhance Students’ Learning Performance: A Systematic Review

annotations.text: Prisma review of 42 papers. There is a limited number of studies performing a multi faceted analysis on online student engagement. It draws from the Redmond et. al 5 types of online engagement.

Student engagement is a form of student involvement in activities that are likely to result in higher quality learning and evidence suggests that more engaged students tend to perform better and achieve better conceptual understanding. Online engagement can follow 5 components: social, cognitive, collaborative, behavioural and emotional. Missing aggregate analysis and common issues related with lack of focus and motivation in online learning motivate the study.

It is important to ensure engaging and collaborative environments in online learning environments. Learning analytics allow for collection and analysis of online data and has the potential to improve student learning online.
RQ1- What types of online engagement have been used in LA?
RQ2- Purpose of LA in online learning?
RQ3- Effects of LA in student engagement

Student engagement can be measured from the LMS and there has been an established relationship between engagement and performance. Learning content must be designed in a way that promotes 5 types of engagement: social interaction, cognitive skills, behavioral engagement, collaborative learning and emotional engagement.
Social engagement: the forums and other tools that facilitate communication and networking by the students.
Cognitive engagement: understanding complex ideas. It is an active learning process demonstrating participation. Online materials should promote it by encouraging critical thinking.
Collaborative engagement: it is promoted by activities that reflect communication between students. This may come in the form of forums or joining students in groups.

Behaviour engagement: showing interest in learning. This comes in two types: dynamic behaviour and passive behaviour. Active students tend to have positive attitudes towards learning tasks and try to participate in it. This sort of behaviour can be measured using online activities.

Emotional engagement: This domain reflects the attitude towards learning. Attitude, enthusiasm, curiosity, anxiety and pleasure in learning. It has been shown that emotions can promote or distract thinking and decision makig in online environments.

Learning analytics has been here for a long time and its use has mainly been focused on higher education. Applications include informing curriculum design, improvements of learning and teaching environments, analysis of engagement and learning performance, thus making LA an interesting option to unobtrusively measure engagement online. Another application is the measurement of student performance - which can also be adapted to develop strategies to influence student performance.
The motivation for this study is to perform a systematic literature review of studies about student engagement and student-engagement aspects in online-learning environments.

Used PRISMA on empyrical studies, written in English and using LA in Higher Education using keywords such as “student engagement”, “learning analytics” and “online learning” - 159 papers and, at the second filtering stage I would go 42.
Most articles have been published since 2016 and most studies are from US, Canada and UK, with China and Malaysia also showing a couple of good results.
RQ1 - Types of engagement
7% of the works under review focus on more than one of the aspects of engagement in online learning.
Social engagement is measured from time on task, lectures, comments, networking and spectrum of engagement.

Cognitive engagement: feedback and interaction with peers and it has been argued that sharing information in online platforms reflects cognitive engagement. Studies combining multiple perspectives of engagement have tended to lead to more comprehensive measures. With time and more literature, the number of studies including multiple perspectives have been ever increasing.
RQ2 - Purpose of LA studies of engagement
There are 3 major domains of application of LA in the context of online learning:
Learning analytics in the online context allow professors to measure and track the progress of the learning of their students.
RQ3 - Effects of LA on student engagement
Most researched type of engagement was behavioral. Some authors have seen the performance of their students increase after receiving interventions flagged by early warning systems.

Three types of interactions have also been shown to promote cognitive engagement: discussion of questions, quality of student responses and the general learning community. Learning analytics can be used to promote this type of interactions and measure how they are received by the students. However, all of this also reflects caveats from instructional design and learning instructions.

Other authors have used the time spent watching a video to assess their learning outcomes. Other authors figured that participation (again, engagement) positively relate with student success and performance.

While the positive relationship between engagement and performance seems to be ever-present, it should be interpreted with caveats. Logs are only a partial picture and there are many other potential factor involved in learning. Moreover, it is possible to achieve better performances while interacting less with the systems.

42 total papers, with 26 having distinctive and multifaceted engagement and 15 used LA to enhance learning performance. There are multiple factors that are related with engagement in online activities. People with more active approaches to learning tend to do better. Teacher instructions promote cognitive and behavioral engagement, but not emotional engagement because students tend be distressed by the overlapping of instructions. The authors did not find any studies that evaluate engagement across the five dimensions and argue for more multi-faceted works.
An interesting application can be intelligent tutoring and adaptations - in other words personalized learning.

No studies on the 5 types of engagement identified by the authors. Main applications of LA are predicting of performance to enhance student outcomes. Important gap in terms of student engagement in the context of online learning and a more multi-faceted approach can lead to mor comprehensive solutions to the most common issues with online learning.
42 papers fully focused on online learning, meaning other common and proeminent pedagogical approaches were not included in this work.

references.title: Student Answer Forecasting: Transformer-Driven Answer Choice Prediction for Language Learning

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Predicting the Option Selected by Student in MCQ Scenario
extracted.Features: [User Generated Transactions, Questions (and Options), Topic Taxomony for German and Maths]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Most approaches that are SoTA usually focus on whether the answer is correct or incorrect, not the learner’s specific answer - something which can now be better used with attention-based models.
extracted.Novelty of Model: The proposed model uses LLMs to understand content and context of the answers to create student embeddings based on their question answering history.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Users are first organized into learning sessions. Each learning session includes a the sequence of answers given by a student. 

For each question, the options are binary encoded so that it becomes possible to identify what was the answer given by the student.

Authors created 22 different embeddings: 1 with MLP, 16 with LSTM, 1 with LernnaviBERT and 4 with Mistral7BInstruct

Authors create a normal KT model from sequences of answers but subsequently incorporate the student embeddings. Main distinction between the models is related to how these embeddings are integrated: 

MCQStudentBertCat → concatenates with model outputs before classification, 

MCQStudentBertSum → Sums embeddings at input stage
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {MCC: 0.579 - MCQStudentBertCat with Mistral Embeddings, Accuracy: 0.797 - MCQStudentBertCat with Mistral Embeddings, F1-Score: 0.782 - MCQStudentBertCat with Mistral Embeddings}
extracted.Comments:
Authors compare the 2 different models: MCQStudentBertCat and MCQStudentBertSum using multiple different forms of learner embeddings:

- Dummy (no user embedding), MCQBert, MLP, LSTM, LearnnaviBERT and Mistral 7B.

Testing with the holdout method, the authors found that the representation that concatenates the user embeddings before prediction are the betterr form of introducing hte embeddings. Moreover, the best embbeddings are, unsurprisingly, the ones obtained with Mistral.
extracted.Limitations:
Limited interpretability of embedding-based models

Single context - Lernnavi dataset.

Holdout method

annotations.text: ITS predict likely student answers and provide individualized feedback but, most Knowledge Tracing tasks focus on getting question right or wrong and do not focus explicitly on knowledge states of the learner.

Authors propose a novel answer forectasting model called QStudentBert. This model integrates LLMs to understand student context and answer history. The idea is to create embeddings of student context and previous answers and use them as the inputs for a fine-tuned BERT.
Tests on a dataset featuring more than 10000 students. REsults versus baseline are not revealed.

references.title: Do Not Trust a Model Because It is Confident: Uncovering and Characterizing Unknown Unknowns to Student Success Predictors in Online-Based Learning

extracted.Students: 292 Algebra, 216 Functional Programming and 147 Parallelism and Concurrency Students
extracted.Courses: 3 Courses of a Computer Science and Communication Systems BSc degree
extracted.Features: [22 Control Variables, 13 Effort variables, 7 Proactivity variables, 3 Regularity variables]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Indicators were extracted per week. Normalization was then applied. 

Nested CV was used and models are course-specific
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Balanced Accuracy: 0.60 - RF across the 3 courses}
extracted.Comments:
Authors use a multi-tiered system to identify what information may be missing from student performance prediction models. 

Essentially, the prediction modules output a probability of passing or failing. These will sub-sequently be used as inputs for a clustering model to map differences in model perception vs reality (identify potential unknown unknowns.)
extracted.Limitations:
Course-Sepcifc Models. 

Small number of algorithms tested.

extracted.Features: [22 Control Variables, 13 Effort variables, 7 Proactivity variables, 3 Regularity variables]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Indicators were extracted per week. Normalization was then applied. 

Nested CV was used and models are course-specific
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Balanced Accuracy: 0.95 - RF across the 3 courses}
extracted.Comments:
Authors use a multi-tiered system to identify what information may be missing from student performance prediction models. 

Essentially, the prediction modules output a probability of passing or failing. These will sub-sequently be used as inputs for a clustering model to map differences in model perception vs reality (identify potential unknown unknowns.)

Performance on MOOCs is much better than performance of Flipped Classroom settings.
extracted.Limitations:
Course-Sepcifc Models. 

Small number of algorithms tested.

extracted.Students: 292 Algebra, 216 Functional Programming and 147 Parallelism and Concurrency Students,  15394 DSP,  2423 HWTS amnd 18702 GP students
extracted.Data sources: Student Performance Model Predictions, LMS logs, Exam Grade
extracted.Features: [45 Behavioral Features, Gender and Provenience]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors created a nomenclature for different types of students based on the difference between confidence in prediction and actual label: 

Known knowns: Model is very confident and prediction matches label.

Known unknowns: Low condfidence prediction.

Unknown Unknowns: High confidence prediction of incorrect label. 

Based on this observation, the authors create different prediction models and try to characterize the different groups.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Flipped Classroom models have a higher percentage of Unknowns that MOOC models. With RF being better at identifying them.

However, MMOOCs also exhibit the existence of Unknowns. 

When characterizing Unknowns Unknowns in a Flipped Classroom setting… the authors identify multiple variables associated with frequency and engagement as relevant for the prediction.
extracted.Implications:
Scientific: 

Data is partial in nature and does not capture all student behavior. So, having a model only assess performance based on accuracy may lead to misleading results.

Techniological: 

Need for better ways of identifying unknown unknowns since the current models struggle with them.

Social:

Need to introduce interpretability for trust in implementation.
extracted.Comments: Authors create multiple classifiers for the types of mistakes of their models.
extracted.Limitations:
Course-Sepcifc Models. 

Small number of algorithms tested.

Flipped Classroom Lessons data not used.

annotations.text: Insufficient representation is a common cause of weak spots in predictive models. The paper intends to provide explanatory potential for areas where a predictive model may fail - called unknown unknowns (situations where a model is very confident but gets the prediction wrong).

Thus, the paper starts with the introduction of a framework that intends to identify what are the unknown unknowns - reasons for why a student failed and that the model was blind to. The mdeol is verified using data from flipped and online courses. It also combines quantitative analysis with instructor interviews.

references.title: Study-GNN: A Novel Pipeline for Student Performance Prediction Based on Multi-Topology Graph Neural Networks

extracted.Features: [forumng, homepage, oucollaborate, oucontent, page, quiz, resource, subpage, url, gender, region, highest_education, age_band, num_of_prev_attempts, studied_credits, disability]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Data is converted into a tabular dataset. Authors report to do feature selection, data cleaning and transformation… these steps are not specified...

On the graph network, each student is a node and the graph is created based on similarity:

- Pearson Correlation, 
- Cosine Similarity, 
Tanimoto Coefficient.
extracted.Models:
Linear Regression,
SVM, 
MLP, Graph Convolutional Network (GCN), GCN-Cosine, MTGNN
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a Graph Neural Network approach tto predict student performance.

Authors perform multiple experiments:

1. Show if model is better than other approaches,

2. Show results for early prediction.

3. Results for Multiple Topologies vs Single Topological models.

There is a comparison of results using 1 vs 1 computation of metrics. Results of Pass vs Fail are considerably “worse” than Pass vs Withdraw.

This difference is less noticeable for early prediction → best tradeoff seems to be Week 10.
extracted.Limitations:
- Analysis is performed using data from a single course.

- Aggregate metrics are not revealed.

- Uses Holdout Method

annotations.text: Student performance prediction is an important task in Learning Analytics. A significant issue with the current student performance prediction models relates with the fact that most models treat students as independent (no relationship between learners).

To circumvent this limitation, the authors propose a Graph Neural Network model that tries to reflect relationships between students. They end up treating the problem as a semi-supervised classification problem where each node represents a student and the node’s label a performance status and an attention mechanism is used to create a single graph representation.
Benchmarking results show that the approach outperforms other “traditional methods” at the task.

references.title: Personalizing Computer Science Education by Leveraging Multimodal Learning Analytics

extracted.Data sources: Student Characteristics, Academic History, Programming Submissions, LMS logs
extracted.Features: [Distance, CAO Points, MAth LC, Avg. Grade, Exam Course Year, Results Wx, Cumulative Wx, Lab Attendance Wx, Time Wx, Lab Access Wx, Hour Access Wx, Checking Wx, On/of Campus Wx, Week(end) Wx]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The different features were extracted on a course-specific perspective.

Content of exercises or assignments specifically was not considererd because the specifics differ signifcantly from year to year.

During Cross-Validation, the authors selected their top 10 features for every week.
extracted.Models: Linear SVM, Gaussian SVM, Random Forest, Decision Tree, kNN, Logistic Regression
extracted.Comments:
Author create a set of models that predicts succes in programming assignment for every week.

This was an opt-in system. Students who participated were not only part of the early warning system but also participated in a sub-sequent recommender prototype.
extracted.Limitations:
Specific to programming content and submissions.

Relatively small sample size.

extracted.Students: 133 CS2 students, 81 SH1 students, 60 PF3 students
extracted.Initialization Method: Recommendations are constructed based on a knowledge graph based on professor’s notion of important concepts.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The recommendations are a part of intervention.

At the end of each week, the system sends an e-mail notification to students with their progress and, if learner failed at submission.
extracted.Recommendation types: Most similar successful submission from top student.
extracted.Comments:
Participation in the intervention work was considered optional - After taking first exam, learners were offered the chance of receiving interventions vs not receiving them.

Authors measured “improvement” between students who opted in vs students who opted out:  All students performed badly on second exam but the decrease for students who opted-in was, at least, smaller.

Positive feedback for interventions.
extracted.Limitations:
Interventions are aimed for students flagged as likely to fail → Vulnerable to Misclassifications.

Requires more data for better recommendations.

Doers not test other equally valid recommender sstem approachs such as Collaborative Filtering.

annotations.text: The paper presents a platform called PredictCS that identifies struggling students AND makes personalized recommendations to them. The system contains a dashboard.

For the predictive modeling part, the system uses historical data, demographics, logged interactions and progress in Labs to predict end of course achievement every week. By the second part of the semester, students were given an opt-in option to receive how well they are expected to do in the course AND recommendations to tackle their difficulties.
The performance of students that received feedback vs the ones who haven’t is compared and the difference between one group and the other has been reduced.

references.title: Fuzzy Bayesian Knowledge Tracing

extracted.Students:
1825 Algebra05&06 learners,
1112 Bridge Algebra learners, 3062 ASSISTments2009 learners, 16587 ASSISTments12 learners, 1704  ASSISTments17 learners
extracted.Features: [Sequences of Scores for Previous Questions in the same Knowledge Component]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
Most of the current state-of-the-art KT models either focus prediction binary outcomes - correct or not correct, which makes these models unsuitable for continuous scores or subjective questions.

No consideratioin for a status of partial mastery of a specific knowledge component.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
The addition of fuzziness to BKT allows for the introduction of the  partial correctedness.

However, the introduction of this Fuzzy BKT also has its own drawback - model parameter uncertainty - by using type-2 fuzzifications.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: In essence, Fuzzy BKT and T2FBKT takes the performance scores as input (not a binary sequence of 0s to 1s)
extracted.Models: BKT, DKT, DKVMN, DKT+, DKT_DSC, DeepIRT, SAKT, KQN, AKT, CKT, DMN, ADMN, IADMN, FBKT, T2FBKT
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Average MAE Rank: 1.47 - T2FBKT, Average RMSE Rank:                                                                                                                                                      1.4 - T2FBKT}
extracted.Comments:
Authors explore the idea of introducing fuziness to a domain that is often characterized by working with binary sequences.

The fuzzy possiibility allows the assignment of a continuous score to each question, which should reflect a better estimate of learner knowledge than 0 - wrong vs 1 - correct.

Authors propose this approach and test its efectiveness on mulitple datasets. Proposed models - FBKT and T2FBKT seem to outperform the remaining options. 

Results between models are done using Friedman tests and Nemeyi tests. 

Either FBKT or T2FBKT beat all baseline models on all datasets and metrics.

Some abation studies are conducted in order to optimize the paramenters of these models.
extracted.Limitations: This fuzzy approach is exclusively applied to BKT - missing DKT or any of its variants.

annotations.text: Most KT models use CFA, a binary problem (the answer is correct or incorrect), which limits the possible applications for KT models. Thus, the authors propose a set of Bayesian KT Models (FBKT and T2FBKT) to address continuous score scenarios. The results show that KT can go beyond regular applications with this approach.

references.title: Knowledge Tracing Within Single Programming  Practice Using Problem-Solving Process Data

extracted.Data sources: Perfect Solution for each task, Intermediate learner solutions in the poroblem-solving process
extracted.Features: [pathLength, currentPerf, AIScore]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
BKT, DKT or Additive Factor Model (AFM) struggle in contexts of data sparsity as is the case of informa programming contexts.

More specifically, if the dataset contains a small number of activities completed by each student.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
Authors present an Approaching Index - a measurement of similarity between current solution and ideal programming solution.

The proposed approach - Process Knowledge Tracing looks to model knowledge from the sequences of Approaching Indices when solving a question. In essence, the closer an intermediate solution
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Paths that do not lead to a solution have been disregarded. Same goes with duplicate solutions.

Authors create the approaching index (AI) as a measure of simialrity between learner’s current solution AND a pre-defined optimal solution. Similarity was computed using Tree-Edit Distance (TED)

Data was undersampled for Class rebalancing
extracted.Models: LR+pathLength, LR+currentPerf, PoissonPath, DKT-P, PKT-Variables, PKT-Sequences
extracted.Comments:
In programming tasks, reaching a solution can be thought of as following an Abstract Syntax tree (AST). From a root solution, there are multiple possible pathways a student can follow. The actual process a student follows is called a programming path. 


2 different models are attempted: 

PKT-Variables, which uses 3 features for a logistic regression.

PKT-sequences, which uses the AI sequences along with path length and current performance.

PKT-Sequences and PKT-variables achieved performances close to best-performing models, especially on the harder problems (HOC18 to HOC19).
extracted.Limitations:
Limited number of tasks for evaluation.

Problems tested had one and only one optimal solution. Model as designed REQ_UIRES an optimal solution.

TED-Based similarity may not be ideal form of similarity ibetween programming tasks.

No Cross-Validation.

annotations.text: KT is difficult to do in scenarios where the opportunities for practice are small, such as big-block coding exercises. The authors propose 2 knowledge tracing models and a novel metric (Approaching Index Sequence). The programming path is represented by AISeq or AIScore (average of AISeq):

Both models are tested on two programming datasets, and the models were competitive with four state-of-the-art models on Precision-Recall, Accuracy, Specificity and Cohen’s Kappa. On a more fine-grained level, the models exhibited robustness in identifying students who fail to complete tasks.

references.title: EKT: Exercise-Aware Knowledge Tracing  for Student Performance Prediction
references.journal: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Predict the response score on next candidate exercise
extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, Answer Sequences, Question Attributes
extracted.Features: [Exercise Embeddings, Student Embeddings]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
Most previous models strictly consider sequences of previous exercises and whether they were right or wrong. xercise contents or the intervention of the learners on the process is not considered.

In addition, the models consider that next performance depends on current knowledge, not considering the possibility of possibly updating knowledge between current state and next exercise.

Models also usually struggle with the cold-start problem.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
Authors propose full representation of the data in 2 main components:

- Exercise Embeddings - a representation of the exercise from its content

Student Embedding: A representation of the learner’s knwoeldge state at each specific moment in time.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors filter the dataset to a fraction of the initial sample of students. Authors experiment 2 alternative ways of performing the prediction:

EERNNM which uses the Markov property, oor EERNNA which uses an Attention Mechanism.

This, og course requires, among other things, the creation of word embeddings from the text.
extracted.Models: IRT, BKT, PMF, FKT, DKVMN, LLSTM, LSTMA, EKTM, EKTA
extracted.Comments:
Authors perform a sequential holdout implementation while looking to optimize for % lerngth of training size (60 to 90% of each learners records).

Authors test their models in two paradigms: general scenario and cold-start scenario - new exercises.

Attention seems to outperform the Markov Rule approach.
extracted.Limitations: Only considers a single dataset.

annotations.text: For personalized education services to work, it is often necessary that the systems are able to accurately predict the score a student will get on a given exercise. This is done via latent variables extractable from materials (e.g. knowledge concepts or exercise content). Thus, the paper makes multiple proposals:
Both EERNN and EKT are tested on large-scale data and tests on general and cold-start scenarios are effective.

references.title: Dynamic Bayesian Networks  for Student Modeling

extracted.Students: 6043 Algebra learners, 7265 Dybuster learners, 77 Physics learners, 1581 Math learners,
extracted.Courses: Number Representation, Subtraction, Physics, Algebra, Spelling
extracted.Features: [Sequences of Answers]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: BKT is unable to identify relationships between skills since it requires the construction of one model per skill.
extracted.Novelty of Model: The authors embloy Dynamic Bayesian Networks to map hierarchies of skills. An advantage of the method is that skills are modeled jointly.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: The DBN uses Log-Linear weights associated to each skill and knowledge component.
extracted.Models: DBNc1, DBNc2, DBNc3, DBNc4, BKTc, AFM, PFA
extracted.Comments: Authors check whether their proposed model otperforms other approaches using RMSE and AUC metrics but, additionally, they also test thheir moddel when it comes to Reliability and Resolution.
extracted.Limitations: Model does not include ability to forget, something which is known to improve model performance.

annotations.text: In order to work, ITS require good representations of students and their knowledge, with BKT being among the most popular representations. However, BKT does not account for hierarchical relationships between skills, something that Dynamic Bayesian networks (DBN) address.
The authors introduce DBN for student modelling and test the performance of their algorithm on 5 distinct datasets, outperforming previous approaches.

references.title: Pull together: Option-weighting-enhanced mixture-of-experts knowledge tracing

extracted.Students: 100000 EdNet-KT1 learners, 118971 Eedi learners, 10000 EdNet learners
extracted.Features: [Sequence of Answers, Option Scores]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
KT methods often estimate knowledge states based on correctedness of a MCQ. 

However, selecting different options may be indicative of different knowledge states. 

Moreover, while multi-task prediction has been used in KT, it lacks a full model specific for different tasks.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
The novelty of the approach is two-fold:

The introduction of option-weighting to assign different weights to the different possibilities of MCQ. 

Secondly, the model introduces the notion of mixtures-of-experts. 

One Module Specific for KT, a second for OT and a thir for both tasks.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The authors create 2 forms of embeddings: a categorical embedding for exercises representation.

A continuous embedding to represent the learners’ knowledge states. 

The authors create mixture of experts set of embeddings. 

In short, the authors create 3 modules that are meant to mimetize domain-experts with different perspectives:

- Exercise Embeddings, 
- Exercise Analysis, 
- Learning State Recognition, 
- Interaction Parsing,
- Knowledge State Acquisiton

For this exercise, there is an expert for the KT task and an expert meant to evaluate the KT and the OT perspectives.
extracted.Models: DKT, DKVMN, SAKT, AKT, SAINT, CL4KT, DIMKT, DP-WEKT, MQFKT, ATDKT, WEKT
extracted.Comments:
Results show that both WEKT and DP-WEKT approaches achieve state-of-the-art performance on 3 of the datasets.

The impact of the experts is measured via an ablation study - both for full experts and Expert Compents as well.
extracted.Limitations:
Only works for MCQ scenarios.

Does not consider specific learner behaviors which may enrich the model’s performance.

Does not consider, at this stage, the incorporation of LLMs.

Holdout Method
