extracted.Students: 100000 EdNet-KT1 learners, 118971 Eedi learners, 10000 EdNet learners
extracted.Features: [Sequence of Answers, Option Scores]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
KT methods often estimate knowledge states based on correctedness of a MCQ. 

However, selecting different options may be indicative of different knowledge states. 

Moreover, while multi-task prediction has been used in KT, it lacks a full model specific for different tasks.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
The novelty of the approach is two-fold:

The introduction of option-weighting to assign different weights to the different possibilities of MCQ. 

Secondly, the model introduces the notion of mixtures-of-experts. 

One Module Specific for KT, a second for OT and a third for both tasks.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The authors create 2 forms of embeddings: a categorical embedding for exercises representation.

A continuous embedding to represent the learners’ knowledge states. 

The authors create mixture of experts set of embeddings. 

In short, the authors create 3 modules that are meant to mimetize domain-experts with different perspectives:

- Exercise Embeddings, 
- Exercise Analysis, 
- Learning State Recognition, 
- Interaction Parsing,
- Knowledge State Acquisiton

For this exercise, there is an expert for the OT task and an expert meant to evaluate the KT and the OT perspectives.

annotations.text: In online teaching, KT is meant to inform whether a teaching strategy should be changed according to the changes in learners knowledge states. Most KT models assume a binary target, without additional adjustments. For example, MCQ options introduce confounding concepts and, depending on the learner’s answer, it may be possible to understand the current knowledge state.

To take advantage of this phenomenon, the authors introduce an option-weighted enhanced mixture-of-experts knowledge tracing (WEKT) method. Each answer receives a specific weight and can be used to update the learners’ knowledge states. The structure combines cognitive theory and deep learning. Experiments on multiple datasets show that the model achieves its aims.

references.title: Multi-Granularity Ensemble Interaction Graph Modeling for Knowledge Tracing

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Probability of answering next exercise correctly.
extracted.Students: 4217 ASSISTments2009 learners, 1709 ASSISTments 2017 learners, 5000 EdNet learners, 118971 Eedi learners
extracted.Data sources: Sequences of Answers, Question Sentences, LMS logs
extracted.Features: [Interaction Sequences, Question Sentences, Answers]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
KT approaches often consider different knowledge concepts as independent from one another, which is unrealistic. 

Graph-based KT methods have started to introduce relationships between concepts to update learner knowledge.

However, the way the graph is built does not really consider changes in knowledge state for either dynamic or static perspectives.

Moreover, learning encompasses different temporal resolutions, something that current models do not seem to completely grasp
extracted.Novelty of Model:
Model uses a Transformer Knowledge State encoder to represent learning at different temporal resolutions - days, weeks, months, semesters.

Moreover, the authors introduce an Interaction Graph Dependency Module which that builds 2 knowledge graphs and uses them to build a more stable mapping of correlations between concepts.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Model starts with the construction of 3 embeddings - Questions, Knowledge Concepts, and Answers

The transformer for Knowledge States does a separate normalization of rows and columns to expedite training time.

Graph modules are built by measuring dissimarity between nodes of the graphs. A connection is etablished if similarity is above a specified threshold. One of the graphs is passed through a low-pass (smoothes the edges) filter while the other is passed by a high-pass filter (sharpens the edges). 

The results are concated.
extracted.Models: DKT, DKVMN, SAKT, MRTKT, DTransformer, GKT, DGEKT, MGIGKT
extracted.Comments:
Authors introduce a new model meant solve 2 problems:

1 - Consider different temporal resolutions of the learning process

2 - Encode relationships between different knowledge concepts to provide a better estimate of learners.

Some of the preprocessing decisions are made in order to reduce the training time of the model.

In addition, authors perform an ablation study and notice to check the impact of presence/absence of each of the proposed modules proposed.
extracted.Limitations: No cross-Valdiation.

annotations.text: KT analyzes historical records to infer students’ knowledge states, and deep learning methods are the current state-of-the-art. However, the current techniques tend to treat records as a single time-series AND they don’t account for possible correlations.

Authors propose Multi-Granularity Ensemble Interaction Graph Modeling for Knowledge Tracing which is meant to address the two constraints. Analysis on four datasets show that the model outperforms other approaches.

references.title: Logistic Knowledge Tracing: A Constrained  Framework for Learner Modeling

extracted.Students: 97 students at an US university, 912 ASSISTments learners, 66 Andes learners, 120 KDDCup learners, 1024 Mc-Graw Hill learners, 478 Mechanical Turk learners,
extracted.Courses: 1 Chinese Language course, ASSISTments, Cognitivite, Andes, Mc-Graw Hill
extracted.Features: [lineafm, logafm, powafm, recency, expdecafm, base, base2, ppe, logsuc, linesuc, logfail, linefail, expdecsuc, expdecfail, basesuc, basefail, base2fail, base2suc, linecomp, prop, propdec, logit, logitdec]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Most currently available models such as BKT, DKT, Additive Factor Models, Performance Factors Analysis are relatively strict in terms of the data they are allowed to take in order to obtain meaningful predictions.
extracted.Novelty of Model: LKT allows the encompassing of different forms of features, making it a flexible framework for knowledge modelling even if it may not be the best performing approach to student modelling.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors filtered out all attempts that are beyond the first attempt at an exercise. 

Exercises were divided into 5 knowledge components and each feature was created from the corresponding data sources.

Modelling becomes the implementation of a logistic regression suing specific subsets of features.
extracted.Models: AFM_1, AFM_2, PFA_1, PFA_2, PFA_3, PFA_Decay, R_PFA_1, R_PFA_2, R_PFA_3, PPE, LKT_1, LKT_2
extracted.Comments:
Authors argue for LKT as a useful framework of KT that can be used as an alternative to BKT and/or DKT. 

They make a comparison of 12 alternative models that mix Additive Factor Analysis, Performance Factor Analysis and their own LKT proposals. 

;odels predict probability of getting answer correct on first attempt which obviously is later converted into a probability.

Model propeckc+ppekc seems to almost consistently outperform the remaining options.
extracted.Limitations:
No comparison against KT models who have been more successful such as BKT and/or DKT.

Most models seem to substantially underfit (as highlighted by across the board low values of RSquared).

Hiearchy of features needs to be pre-deternmined before being given into the models. 

Limited support for random effects.

annotations.text: ITS create learner models to measure learning and propose new pedagogical decisions. The paper introduces a Logistic Knowledge Tracing model. The authors use the framework to fit 12 models to 6 learning technology datasets.

Results show that there is no model to rule them all as, depending on feature set, the best algorithm differs. Authors argue that this more general approach is benefical exactly because of these results.

references.title: Stable Knowledge Tracing Using Causal Inference

extracted.Students: 4417 ASSISTments2009 learners, 19917 ASSISTments2012 learners, 333 Statics Learners, 2000 Simulated Learners
extracted.Features: [Confounding Variables Matrix, Sequence of Answers]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Current KT models have wayu too oversimplistic assumptions, which makes them suffer from volatility:  Among thosse assumptions we have:
extracted.Novelty of Model:
Authors introduce a method that uses causal inference to predict outcome via knowledge graphs.

This is achieved by the additon of an autoencoder model.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The proposed model relies on a set of assumptions:

1- Stable Knowledge Concept Value - Ther is a stable influence that one knowledge concept can have in another.

2. Unconfoundedness - Knowledge concept layout and outcomes are conditionally independent on the data.

3. Separateness: there are 3 sets of variables: Confounding variables (X), Adjustment Variables (Z) and Irrelevant Variables (I)
extracted.Models: BKT, PFA, BKT+FSA, DKVMN, DKT, DKT+, Causal Inference KT
extracted.Comments:
The main distinction between this model and traditional DKT is the inclusion of restrictions of causal inference that enrich the feature set. 

Results show that, indeed, the inclusion of these restrictions make predictions a lot more stable than standard approaches in real datasets.
extracted.Limitations:
Does not seem to be effective in smaller sequences of answers - highlighted by the performance on the Synthetic dataset.

No measurement of additional data sources.

annotations.text: KT is a popular application of LA research and, nowadays, there are increasingly more student representations who are becoming increasingly complex. However, most approaches are inneficient at updating the learner’s knowledge state, often leading to poor predictions.

The authors employ a causal inference model to explain KT. The idea stems from the construcion of balanced weights for the different features. Experimentation on different datasets shows that model is accurate and interpretable (2 hallmarks for good KT models.

references.title: Monitoring Student Progress for Learning  Process-Consistent Knowledge Tracing
references.journal: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering

extracted.Students: 29018 ASSISTments2012 learners, 1709 ASSISTmentsChall learners, 78431 EdNet-KT1 learners
extracted.Data sources: Exercise and Answer Sequences, LMS logs, Time between exercises
extracted.Features: [Learning unit (E-AT-A), interval time, learner embedding]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
Most KT approaches achieve good accuracy from sequences of exercises, but pay little attention to effectively monitoring knowledge states → often the knowledge states are not consistent with the learning process → learners can gain knowledge whether they get a question right or wrong → what can change is the pace at which knowledge is gained.

Moreover, most approaches do not account for obvious factors of learning progress such as forgetting.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
The authors propose a framework that continuously monitors how a student progresses.

To do that, the authors formalize:

- Basic Learning unit as tuple: Exercise-answer time-Answer

- Learning Gains as normalized difference between post and pre test scores → positive effect on learning progress

- Forgetting as a temporal decay - negative progress 

- Student progress as the result of both effects.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The model is composed of 3 modules. For any given timestep:

A Learning gain module followed by a Forgetting Module and Finally a prediction module.

Each learner is represented by an embedding vector which encodes the learning progress of the student.

There is a Q matrix that encodes exercises and knowledge concepts.

LPKT-S additionally adds a set of student-specific features.
extracted.Models: DKT, DKT+, DKVMN, SAKT, CKT, AKT, LPKT, LPKT-S
extracted.Comments:
Author pros«pose a new model that assists in the continuous modelling of student progress by the addition of different modules. 

Results on the 3 datasets show that the model beats AKT which was, at the time, the state of the art model.

Authors also perform ablation studies to check the impact of the presence/absence of the different modules:

- Forgetting has noticeable negative impact in student performace.

- Modelling learning gains achieves better predictive performance than merely modelling outcomes.

- Interval time and answer time are essential information for modelling.

Authors also perform exercise clustering.
extracted.Limitations: Room for improvement in the representation of Q-matrix.

annotations.text: KT is about tracing a student’s knowledge at any given moment with the rationale that knowing what a student knows and where the knowledge is lacking allows for more effective and efficient pedagogical activities. Most methods assess the correctness of the knowledge model by checking how well the model predicts CFA. However, it does not account for whether the students’ learning process maps into the inferred knowledge state.

The paper explores Learning Process-consistent KT (LPKT), a model that monitors the learning process. The model incorporates parameters to account for learning gains and forgetting. They add a new constraint to their model to account for student-specific learning rates. Results on 3 public datasets show hat PLKT and LPKT-S outperformed State-of-the-art models.

references.title: Deep Knowledge Tracing and Dynamic Student  Classification for Knowledge Tracing
references.journal: IEEE International Conference on Data Mining

extracted.Students: 4163 ASSISTments 2009 learners, 28834 ASSISTments, 19840 ASSISTments 2014, 574 KDDCup learners
extracted.Features: [Student Ability, Sequences of Answers, Sequence of Attempts]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: DKT, and similar models, while decent at modelling student knowledge from sequences of answers, it does not consider attributes such as learning ability.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
DKT-Dynamic Student Classification looks to incorporate this missing piece by incorporating a clustering module meant to encode the learning ability on each timestep. 

The cluster is used as an additional parameter to the model for each timestep.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Data is extracted from the sequence of answers and used. Sequence of attempts is used to clustert learne rs according to their learning ability.

Sequence and Cluster are used as inputs for the model on each time-step. All results published are done on sequences of 20 attempts and 8 clusters.
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a method that incorporates the learner’s learning rate via clustering at each timestep.

Ablation study by varying Time interval and number of clusters to use are attempted.
extracted.Limitations:
Simlar to DKT, each question is associated to a single KC.

Only includes improvement to DKT, not considering effects on other KT approaches.

annotations.text: KT is a key task in ITS and DKT is one of the state-of-the-art architectures. In this work, the authors expand on DKT by previously assigning learners to different teams with an unsupervised learning technique according to their ability on regular time intervals. Model becomes better at predicting performance in comparison to other State-of-the-art techniques.

references.title: csKT: Addressing cold-start problem in knowledge tracing via kernel bias and cone attention

extracted.Students: 333 Statics learners, 574 Algebra2005 Learners, 1146 Bridge2006 learners, 4918 NIPS34 learners
extracted.Data sources: Question and Response Sequences, Knowledge Components, Difficulty
extracted.Features: [Knowledge Components, Question Sequences, Responses]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Most state-of-the-art knowledge tracing approaches, especially the ones based on attention modules are problematic when it comes to handling smaller sequences → cold-start problem.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
The authors propose an approach that makes KT more effective especially when dealing with smaller sequences.

This is achieved via 2 different mechanisms: 

1. Cone attention which converts the representation of knowledge components into an hyperbolic space.

2. A Kernel bias: a logarithmic-decay based positional encoder. This ensures that more recent questions become more important than older ones. This allows working with smaller sequences because the importance of larger (older) sequences becomes diminished.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Preprocessing involves the construction of sequences and the formulation of the kernel bias.

Maximum sequence length is defined to be 20 questions.
extracted.Models: DKT, DKT+, DKVMN, EERNN, ATKT, SAKT, LPKT, AKT, AT-DKT, sparse-KT, FoLiBiKT, simpleKT, Dtransformer, HD-KT, csKT
extracted.Comments:
Author attempt different predictions using different sequence lengths - 50, 100 and 500 questions under a cold-start scenario.

Results show that the approach using the kernel bias assigns more importance to more recent questions, which makes it more effective than state-of-the-art techniques.

Authors also perform ablation studies to see effectiveness of the different modules of the arquitecture.
extracted.Limitations:
Only tests model performance under the cold-start scenario.

Model only considers answer sequences, not accounting for other potentially predictive factors such as engagement.

annotations.text: KT has a cold-start problem. If we use the past to predict student performance,not having it may be problematic. Thus, KT models, while impressive, tend to not generate stable predictions when there still are small numbers of interactions.
Authors propose csKT, which is meant to be able to be trained on small sequences and then expand in a smoother way to longer sequences. This is done via a kernel bias and cone attention.
Results on 4 known KT datasets show that csKT outperforms other KT models in cold-start scenarios.

references.title: Integrating Temporal Information Into Knowledge Tracing: A Temporal Difference Approach

extracted.Features: [Sliced Sequences of Answers]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: BKT, while more interpretable than, for instance, MAtrix Factorizarion approaches, suffers from the problem of exclusively considering sequences of answer, but does not account for temporal aspects of the exercise solving process.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
The approach TD-BKT starts with the introduction of a congnitive inflection point detection algorithm.

The purpose of this algorithm is to identify which moment in time refers to the point where the student starts to faulter on challenging problems to the point where the student is mastering the skill.

This allows the model to slice the sequence and assign a weight to each slice.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: The main novelty of this odl is precisely in the preprocessing. Instead of using individual exercise events as members of the sequence, the authors slice the data (each slice containinng multiple questions/answers) based on IRT.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {MAE: 0.27 - TD-BKT, RMSE: 0.1 - TD-BKT}
extracted.Comments:
Authorse xplore differenct form of computing the Inflection Point: Arithmetic, Logics and Probabilistic, with the probabilistic approach reaching the lowest error.

Best performance, when comparted against baseline, is TD-BKT.
extracted.Limitations:
No exploration of DKT or DKT-based approaches.

Holdout Method approach.

One dataset only.

Does not include any additional data sources.

annotations.text: BKT has limitations related with temporal differences in data. The authors propose an improved version of BKT that incorporates temporal difference information. In other words, this model detects moments in time where there is a shift in the cognitive level of a student and incorporates that information.
Empyrical tests show TD-BKT outperforms BKT on the Junyi academy dataset.

references.title: Dynamic Key-Value Memory Networks With Rich  Features for Knowledge Tracing

extracted.Features: [user_id, problem_id, correct, attempt_count, first_response, first_action]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: DKVMN, while interesting as an approach, has the issue of not considering additional features relative to sequences of answers - the knowledge status.
extracted.Novelty of Model: The authors propose a small change to DKVMN that not only includes sequences of answersa to create both matrices, but also considers additional behavioral information in order to obtain a better value matrix.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The model starts with the prediction section of whether a question will be correct or not.

This is followed by a Clustering module that is meant to calculate learning ability and group students into one of 3 groups.
extracted.Models: BKT, DKT, DKVMN, DKT-F, DKT-DSC, DKVMN-LA
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {AUC: 0.919 - DKVMN-LA}
extracted.Comments:
Authors perform 4 distinct experiments:

1. Comparison od DKVMN-LA with other models in the literature.

2. Comparison of DKVMN-LA with DKT-LA.

3. Analysis of the model suing different segment lengths.

4. Changes with different feature splicings.

DKVMN-LA wiuth all features and segment length 5 outperforms all others.
extracted.Limitations:
Limited number of Benchmark Datasets.

Room for improvement in behavioral features being considered. 

Opportunity for refinement both at the stage of correctedness prediction AND LEarning Ability clustering.

annotations.text: Out of all models in KT that existed in 2022, Dynamic Key-Value Memory Networks (DKVMN) have been argued to be state-of-the-art. A drawback of these models is that they seem to not consider learning abilities or behavioral features collected by ITS.
Thus, to include these theoretically important constructs, the authors propose a new form of representation that improves the performance of KT using DKVMN.

references.title: Dynamic Cognitive Diagnosis: An Educational  Priors-Enhanced Deep Knowledge  Tracing Perspective

extracted.Students: 3079 ASSISTments2009 learners, 24768 ASSISTments 2012 learners, 9471 KDDCup learners
extracted.Features: [Question ID, Question/KC Difficulty, KC, Question Discrimination, Input Question Attributes]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
Cognitive diagnosis is a form of student modelling in a static setting (i.e. a student’s knowledge state does not change, or is not meant to, during a test).

Test theory methods are interpretable and often evaluate 2 perspectives: ability level or cognition level. 

These features can be valuable additions to DKT model in order to increase its interpretability.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
DCDKT looks to include the cognitive level features into a DKT model.

This is done in order to incorporate items such as: Difficulty and/or discrimination.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Questions without a Knowledge Component were not considered. Similarly, learners with fewer than 15 answers were disregarded.

Students wth more than 200 answers were divided into multiple dummy students.
extracted.Models: BKT, DKT_Q, DKT_KC, DKT_MLP, DKVMN, DIRT_1, DIRT_2, DIRT_3, DIRT_4, DNeuralCDM_1, DNeuralCDM_2, DNeuralCDM_3, DNeural_CDM_4
extracted.Comments:
Predictions were attempted using different possible models: 

DKT_Q → only using question features.

DKT_KC: Each question is represented by the KC ID

DKT_MLP: Same input as DKT_KC, but output was produced by 2 feed-forward layers.

The congnitive diagnosis section is presented using DIRT or DNeuralCDM.

Number at the end of DIRT or DNeuralCDM represents the type of features being considered for each.


1: Question ID, 
2:QID and KC ID, 
3: QID, KC ID, Difficulty, 
4: QID, KC ID, Difficulty, Discrimination
extracted.Limitations:
No cross-validation.

Add additional educational priors. 

Models considered only use GRU.

annotations.text: Deep Knowledge Tracing has been able to achieve state-of-the-art performances in the representation of students’ knowledge states. One disadvantage of this technique is the lack of interpretability of the model (especially when compared against BKT).

To address this, the authors propose dynamic cognitive diagnosis, a model that is somewhat inspired by psychometrics and combines educational priors with DKT. The authors show that their model is not only more interpretable but proper selection of biases also boosts the performance of DKT models.

references.title: Explore Bayesian analysis in Cognitive-aware Key–Value Memory Networks for knowledge tracing in online learning

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Probability of Answering Next Question Correctly
extracted.Students: 574 Algebra learners, 4151 ASSISTments2009 learners, 1709 ASSISTments2017 learners
extracted.Data sources: Sequences of Answers, Problem Difficulty, LMS logs
extracted.Features: [Student Knowledge Embeddings]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
DKVMN, which are part of the state-of-the-art of KT at the time struggles is vulnerable to longer sequences.

DKT achieves good performances, even with longer sequences but struggles with interpretation.

BKT is the most interpretable KT model but its performance is relatively subpar in comparison with other options.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
The authors introduce an LSTM module after the formation of the KV matrices.

Moreover, the outputs of the LSTM module are subsequently given to a Bayesian Module which allows for greater interpretability of the model.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
As any Key-Value Network, the model requires the building of 2 matrices: 

A Key-Matrix that associates questions and knowledge components - which is static

A Value Matrix that suffers different sets of Erase-Write operations to model the student’s actually knowledge.
extracted.Models: BKT, DKT, DKT+, DKVMN, SKVMN, enKT, BCKVMN
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose new architecture and perform multipe experiments:

Analysis of effect of different parameters (d, N, lambdas)

They also tested effects of changing the size of the training data

Clustering of exercises.

A very preliminary start at using this approach for recomendations of in-session learning paths.
extracted.Limitations:
Requires integration in order to be used for elarning apth recommendation. 

No Cross-Validation.

annotations.text: The current KT approaches either model knowledge for different concepts separately or fail at capturing longer-term dependencies in an historical sequence of questions. To address this issue, the authors propose Bayesian Cognitive-Aware Key-Value Memory Networks, a model that combines multiple popular KT architectures (DKVMN, LSTM and Bayesian theory).

In essence, longer-term dependencies are captured by introducing an LSTM layer to DKVMN. In addition, an embedding layer is used top infer question difficulty on final predictions and Bayesian theory for better parameter interpretation. Testing on different 3-real world datasets shows that the approach achieve SotA performance.

references.title: Individualized Bayesian Knowledge Tracing Models

extracted.Courses: Algebra I and Bridge to Algebra
extracted.Data sources: Answer Sequences with Length of Answer
extracted.Features: [Sequences of Answers, Student Priors]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: BKT models only consider a finite set of parameters per knowledge component without considering learner-specific parameters.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
BKT with student parameters uses negative log likelihood as the objective function.

In essence, some of the BKT parameters are specific for each student.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors extract student specific features that are used in the models: A priori knowledge of skill and learning rate of student which has impact on the probability of transitioning a state from unknown to known.
extracted.Comments:
The authors also have 2 skill models. 

Authors find that adding student-specific probability of learning is more important than probability of initial mastery.
extracted.Limitations:
Only BKT is explored. 

Limityed number of datasets.

Does not consider student-specific versions of 2 parameters: pSlip or pGuess

annotations.text: Bayesian Knowledge Tracing is popular method in Intelligent-Tutoring Systems that uses skill-specific parameters, but often fails to account for student variability. Thus, the authors propose an improvement to BKT which hinges on the introduction of student-specifc parameters.

Results show that the introduction of these parameters greatly improves the prediction capabilities of BKT on new students. In addition, they also show that parameterizing speed of learning also beats parameterizing a-priori knowledge.

references.title: Prerequisite-Driven Deep Knowledge Tracing
references.journal: IEEE International Conference on Data Mining

extracted.Student Performance Definition: NExt Candidate Question is Answerewd Correctly
extracted.Students: 1610 ASSISTments learners, 2648 AICFE-CM learners, 1451 AICFE-GE learners, 2445 AICFE-MA learners, 964 AICFE-PH learners
extracted.Data sources: Sequence of Questions, Sequence of Answers
extracted.Features: [Student-Question Matrix, Question-Concept Mapping, Ordered Pairs]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: The idea of prerequisite knowledge between different knowledge concepts is underexplored in common KT frameworks.
extracted.Novelty of Model: Model proposed treats prequesities ar ordering pairs. Essencially, the probability of mastering a concept needs to be lower than the probability of having mastered concepts that are prequisites.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The first step starts with the building of 2 matrices:

A student-question matrix indicating whether the question was correctly or incorrectly answered.

A Question-Concept Mapping intending to identify which concepts are associated with each question.

A set of pre-requisite relationships derived from ppedagogical experts. 

Modelling of these constraints is attempted in different forms:

1. Logic, 2. Boolean Factors and 3. A Conditional Probability, 4. An Ordering Pair
extracted.Comments:
The main difference of this approach relative to DKT is that it includes constraints on pre-requisite knowledge.

On all datasets, the authors show that PDKT-C is the most effective method out of all tested across all datasets.

Authors also do an Ablation study to check the effect of different parameter changes to model performance: 
-Hidden Units, Lambda, Trainig dataset size, Number oif considered pre-requisites.
extracted.Limitations:
Does not consider additional sources of data,

Pre-requisite extraction needs optimization.

annotations.text: One of the main bottlenecks to the deployment of KT approaches is the sparseness in exercise data. To circumvent this issue,. the authors propose that KT models could incorporate pre-requequisites into the KT model. Authors introduce pre-requisites in the form of a constraint and show, on 5 datasets, that this alteration improves the standard KT approaches.

references.title: Multiclass Prediction Model for Student Grade Prediction Using Machine Learning

extracted.Data sources: Academic Spreadsheets, Repository of Academic Management System
extracted.Features: [StudID, Yeear, Class, Session, Credit Hour, Course Code, Total Marks, Grade Pointer Average]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Data was collected from 2 sources to explore imbalanced multiclass classification. Authors explore the use of SMOTE to oversample the minority classes.

Authors use a combination of Filter & Wrapper Feature Selection Methods.
extracted.Models: J48, kNN, SVM, Logistic Regression, NB, Randoim Forest
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.995 - RF - SMOTE, Precision: 0.995 - RF - SMOTE, Recall: 0.995 - RF - SMOTE, F1-Score: 0.995 - RF - SMOTE}
extracted.Comments:
The authors attempt a ML pipeline to address the problem of data imbalance in multiclass classification.

While interesting, the approach suffers from some implementation flaws that lead to the extremely optimistic model predictions.

Using SMOTE leads to better predictions. However, the same cannot be said of the FS techniques. While it seems benefical, most of the FS techniques used do not significantly outperform the remaining approaches.
extracted.Limitations:
Methodologically flawed - Leakage in implementation.

Requires validation on additional datasets.

Only explores SMOTE

annotations.text: Student grade is one of the key KPIs that help educators  monitor learning from their students and many ML approaches have been proposed to predict it (despite the challenges like early prediction and imbalanced dataset). To address the imbalanced dataset problem in two different experiments:

extracted.Target of Recommendation: Courses to Register in based on the learner’s goal
extracted.Courses: 10430 courses at University of California Berkeley
extracted.Data sources:
Student’s personal course history,
Course Pre-Requisites embeddings
extracted.Initialization Method: 0/random initialization since there is no history when student registers.
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Updates are done via update to the LSTM weights based on how the learner evolves throughout the courses.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors filtered the dataset to e exclusively account for records from Fall 2016 to recommend courses for Spring 2016.

Course history is encoded in the form of embeddings.
extracted.Recommendation types: Top-10 Course Recommendations - computed and sorted in descending order  by the probability of achieving an A.
extracted.Evaluation: {Accuracy: 0.5765 - Model 2 on B Treshold}
extracted.Comments:
Authors do a complete work that follows a sequence of different steps: 

1. There is a prediction of course performance, which is enhanced by a prediction of performance in a using information from pre-requisite courses.

2. The LMSTM predicts the probability of achieving an A for every available course in the following semester and recommends the 10 in which the poorobability is highest.

Since this is a backtest, authors consider a successful reccomendation if the student took a course that is listed in the top-10 recommendations list.
extracted.Limitations:
Recommender only takes into account courses taken in previous semester. May benefit from working with a longer sequence.

Imbalanced dataset.

annotations.text: Supporting student decision making is becoming increasingly important when more and more information is becoming available. This work builds on the literature of Deep Learning-Based Recommender systems meant to recommend courses meant to prepare them for target courses of interest.

Authors test their model by using tests of grade prediction and the recovery of pre-requisites as articulated by the university. On a third step, authors compare behavior of students in the semester preceding a course versus a simulated version where students would have taken the recommended courses.

references.title: A Self-Attentive model for Knowledge Tracing

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Student is able to solve exercise at step t+1
extracted.Students: 4000 Synthetic learners, 4417 ASSISTments09 learners, 19917 ASSISTments15 learners, 686 ASSISTmentsChall, 333 Statics learners
extracted.Features: [Sequence of Question-Anser pairs]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: DKT approaches use either RNNs or RNN-derived structures which makes thems vulnerable to deal with sparser datasets and/or longer sequences.
extracted.Novelty of Model: The authors introduce a Transformer architecture which, at the time, was the state-of-the-art form of addressing sequential data.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The model involves 2 forms of embeddings: A question embedding and an interaction embedding.

The authors also employ a normalization layer at the end of each layer: be it feed-forward or self-attention layer.
extracted.Comments:
Authors also perform an Ablation study:

The loss in performance coming from positional encoding is not very pronounced.

Results show that not only model performs better but is also1 ordder of magnitude faster.
extracted.Limitations:
No Cross-Validation.

Could explore more features and data from other sources.

annotations.text: Knowledge Tracing estimates the performance of a student in different learning activities, allowing it to model the student’s mastery of different knowledge concepts. The current state-of-the-art methods are BKT, DKT and Dynamic Key-value Mmory Networks and all have the issue of poor generalization in the rpesence of sparse data.

The authors propose an approach that estimates Knowledge Components from past activities (sort of using preceding knowledge components to estimate current ones?).  This approach is Self-Attention Based (SAKT). Results on multiple datasets show that SAKT beats other alternatives by an average 4.43% on the AUC score.

references.title: Analyzing Student Procrastination in MOOCs: A Multivariate Hawkes Approach

extracted.Features: [L, D, A]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Students labelled as dropouts were not considered for this course.

Each row represents a student-module pairing. Multiple feature sets are created:

L → activities associated with video lectures, 

A - Activities associated with assignments

D - Discussion-Related Activities
extracted.Cluster Description:
Group 1: Assignment to Video Leacture and Assignment to Discussion activities is much lower for this group than the other - Non-dealy group.

Group 2 - Delay Group → participation in activities is much more dictated by assignments.
extracted.Implications:
Dealines are big motivating factors for assignment-related activities. 

Non-delay learners have more frequent and less dense activities → suggesting a relationship between performance and being able to to tasks more frequently.
extracted.Comments:
Authors create a set of attempts at modelling procrastination. The authors use k-means clustering to identify 2 groups…. Non-delay vs delay.

Analysis of the results based on performance shows that there is a negative correlation between delay and performance.
extracted.Limitations:
Disregarding of Dropout students, as procratination is a decent predictor of dropout.

Only a single metric being evaluated.

limited number of predictors in a single context/dataset.

annotations.text: Procrastination is harmful effects to student well-being and ability to learn and its effects are even more pronounced in online settings (MOOCs). Modelling procrastination has been attempted before and the most relevant representations are either static or as parameters of constant-rate temporal models (usually discrete events).

Authors attempt to model procrastination as part of a continuum by using a Multi-dimensional Hawkes approach. Essentially, the authors use this approach to model exogenous events (deadlines) and endogenous events (responses within and between types of learning materials).
Results show the existence of relationships between past and future, meaning that this representation of procrastination can improve the explainability of student models.

references.title: Motives for dropping out from higher education—An analysis of bachelor's degree students in Germany

extracted.Courses: 4 majors: Engineering, STEM, Law, Economics and Social Sciences, Linguistics and cultural Sciences
extracted.Data sources:
Student Interviews. Data is divided into main categories:

1. Interest/ Expectations

2. Performance/ Requirements

3. Financial Aspects

4. Study Conditions

5. Job Alternative/ Career

6. Personal/ Family Aspects
extracted.Theoretical Grounding: Vincent Tinto’s Model for Student Attrition
extracted.Features: [Drop Interest Field, Drop Expect, Drop Wish Practice, Drop No Practice, Drop Exam, Drop Require, Drop Material, drop Perform, Drop Activ, Drop Financial, Drop Moneymaking, Drop Anonym, Drop Overcrowd, Drop Orga, Drop Tuition, Drop Job, Drop Suitability, Drop Interest Job, Drop Opportunities, Drop Child, Drop Family, Drop Ill, Drop Pregnant, Drop Abroad]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: The NEPS dataset conducts interviews with their students and the authors use this dataset. While there is some worry with pre-processing such as identification of different types of Missing Value, authors use PCA on the feature set to reduce dimensionality.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Silhouette Score: 0.634 - 8 Clusters wih reduced Feature Set}
extracted.Cluster Description:
Clusters 1 and 2: The largest clusters with a mix of all reasons for dropout. Difference is that Cluster 2 has a big prevalence of Family Reasons while Cluster 1 doesn’t.

Clusters 3 and 4: These students reported a combined of 3 or 4 motives for dropout. Cluster 3 specific is mostly comprised of second and third year droouts who argued for study-related reasons.

Cluster 5: Students who reported interest, expectations and performance rrelated problems - mainly early dropouts.

Cluster 6: Cited study conditions along wqith family/personal motives for dropping out.

Cluster 7: Mostly lack of interest and unfulfilled expectations.

Cluster 8: Early dropouts who leave because of mainly personal or family reasons.
extracted.Implications:
Programmes may focus on addressing the most relevant reasons for student dropout: 

1. The gap in expectation vs reality.

2. Take care of possibly unrealistic expectations on study requirements. 

Both could be achieved by greater comunication between university and Highschools.

Moreover, depending on program type, htere are differences in Dropout. Thus, the emasures that should be taken for distinct programs should probably be accounted for - STEM students foten leave for personal/financial reasons.
extracted.Comments:
Authors look to cluster different motives for Dropout. 

Authors do a series of discussions of motives for dropout according to different individual characteristics. Clustering is used afterward to cluster dropout motives using all predictors.
extracted.Limitations:
Panel attrition and missing values on motive for dropout.

Small sample size, that could benefit from more instances.

Exclusive use of Hierarchical Clsutering without attempting other approaches.

Subpar results on entire feature subset. Usage of PCA to bypass feature selection/Feature engineering.

annotations.text: Dropout is important to control for, so authors analyze the different reasons that may justify dropout from a higher education institution. The main reason authors found were interest and performance in an undergraduate program. Authors look at dropout using hierarchical clustering and identify multiple motives behind it.

references.title: Can we Take Advantage of Time-Interval Pattern Mining to Model Students Activity?

extracted.Features: [Sequences of Activities in the LMS]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Minimum support of 0.1, meaning that only sequences that happen at least 10% of times are considered.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Activities of temporally close events: solving exercises, labs - the typical activity one would see when preparing for an exam. 


Activities made up of Ih time-intervals: Often exercise sheets that take between 1 hour and one day → this tyype of activity ios noticeable when the student is going deeper into a specific activity,

Other intervals
extracted.Implications:
Since different activities have their own rythm, it is possible to monitor whether the behaviour of the learner maps onto the expected behavior. Moreover, it also allows educators to understand different learning styles.

Better ability to predict the next action of a student, which is vbery helpful in the discovery of optimal learning pathways,

Timing of the last gap in student activity can be regarded as strong indicator of a learner’s next activity,
extracted.Comments:
More than merely looking at sequences of activities, the authors attempt to consider the time that passed bettween consecutive activities to mine patterns of student behavior. 

Authors evaluate what temporal resolution to use based on what they call the fitting ratio: Ratio between non-empty intervals and total number of intervals - this is a measure to minimize the number of irrelevant timesteps.

Close to 99% of the sequential patterns mined using PrefixScan were disregarded by the incorporation of time-intervals.

Outputs are groups of patterns of activities,

When looking at the skeleton of activites at different temporal resolutions, we can see that they are very different → each activity has a rythm.
extracted.Limitations:
Small sample size within the context of a single course.

Most behaviors do not have a stable timing, which lked to the loss of more than 99% of all sequences of behavior.

Temporal granularity of the interval may need refinement.

Irregular occurences and potential noise - students don’t always stick to the typical timing.

Computational complexity.

annotations.text: Time between activities is an often underexplored construct when analyzing student activities. Thus, authors look to explore intervals of time spent between different activities using data from 113 first-year students. Human-times (hours, minutes, seconds) seem to be the most relevant.

Time-interval pattern mining brings more information than sequential pattern mining. Also, some students exhibit regular intervals, suggesting that order of resources is not the only determinant to model student behavior. Interestingly, differences in time-intervals also seem to be predictive of different sequences of learning activities by the learner.

The paper by Dermy and Brun explores using time-interval pattern mining (via the I-PrefixSpan algorithm) to model student learning activities. Instead of looking only at the sequence of resources a student accesses, this approach also considers the time gaps between events, yielding time-interval patterns (ti-patterns) that include temporal information. The main findings from their study on LMS log data (113 first-year university students over ~1 year) can be summarized as follows:
The research provides a direct comparison between traditional sequential pattern mining and the time-interval pattern approach:
Incorporating time intervals into pattern mining has notable implications, as directly highlighted by the authors:
While the results are promising, the paper also discusses several limitations and challenges in using time-interval pattern mining for student activity modeling:

This work offers a fresh perspective in the educational data mining (EDM) domain by emphasizing the temporal dimension of student behavior. A few additional points help contextualize the significance of the findings:

In summary, the I-PrefixSpan time-interval pattern mining approach proved effective in uncovering temporal dynamics in student learning behavior. The main results showed that only a subset of student activities have consistent time gaps, but those that do can be very informative. Including time intervals made it possible to discern different student strategies and to more confidently predict future actions (reducing uncertainty by around 40% on average). The work also highlights important limitations: most learning patterns don’t have fixed timing, and choosing the right time granularity is critical. Despite these challenges, the research demonstrates clear benefits to modeling

when learning events occur. This added temporal dimension enriches our understanding of student activities and opens up possibilities for more responsive and personalized learning systems that account for not just what students do, but when they do it.

annotations.text: Work uses multiple data mining techniques on data from 98 undergraduate students. Authors distinguish between active and passive learners and then extract features for predictive modeling.

Instructors rely on LMS to provide basic data in the interactions between student and the contents. This makes this sort of data especially appealing. However, at the time of the paper, there is limited research going into the data generated by LMS. However, the potential of the statistical analysis of these logs is recognized.
Research Questions:
Undergraduate Business Software Applications course as part of a 4 year vocational track. The course follows a PBL approach. In total the interactions of 98 students across 6 weeks was recorded.
Authors extract multiple features and then pass the data over 2 distinct types of analysis:

Distribution of login frequency and frequency of access to course materials resembled a normal distribution. Moreover, there was positive skew in some of the other variables. Most important variables were found to be - NP, NR and FAC.
Clustering results show 3 clusters:
For daily learning patterns, the authors used ARM. For students who made daily logins rule 1 was that more than 50% of activities were reading course materials and announcements.

The decision tree shows that frequency of accessing course materials was the most important variable. This was a regression approach using a Decision Tree. The authors however never test the performance of the model on either this set or a separate set at all. Instead, they merely focus on interpreting the factors that contribute to one outcomes vs the other.

references.title: Predicting Behavior Change in Students With Special Education Needs Using Multimodal Learning Analytics

annotations.text: The work uses Multimodal Learning Analytics to gain insights into how students with special needs respond to behavior analysis therapy. The ML models used achieve outstanding performances when it comes to predicting behavioral change. Moreover, the authors discuss how the multi-modal nature of the data improves model performance relative to the usual educational-only data.
Results come a model that is deployed in an Intelligent Intervention Learning System on a sample of 500 students with special needs.

references.title: Higher Education Student Dropout Prediction and  Analysis through Educational Data Mining

annotations.text: Dropout is worrysome for academic institutions and preventing it should be regarded as important both for the institutions and students. Authors present a model that uses Naive Bayes to predict dropout. The predictive model is followed by a feature analysis.

references.title: Academic motivation and self-regulation: A comparative analysis of undergraduate and graduate students learning online

annotations.text: The paper makes a comparison between the Self-Regulated Learning traits exhibited by graduate versus undergraduate students, specifically when it comes to motivational beliefs, deep processing strategies and motivational engagement.

Results show that graduates tend procrastinate less and have better developed critical thinking skills. Conversely, undergraduates assign more-value to specific tasks and are more motivated to enroll in more online courses.

references.title: Supporting actionable intelligence: Reframing the analysis of observed study strategies

extracted.Features: [Count of learning actions (based on activity), Time between first attempt and and overall first assessment, time distance between learner submission and overall last submission, Number of sessions, Median and MAD of action counts per session, Median and MAD session length, Entropy of session counts epr day, Days with at least on session, delay of first session, Number of sessions per learning strategy]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The logs contain around 44 types of learning activities, which are aggregated into the 7 fhigher level forms of aggregation which are subsequently used to extract the different features.

Data is aggregated from the start to the moment of prediction, which is done every week. 

The authors use expectation maximization to create learning tactics.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model:
Within Cluster Square Distances to select k.

Performance distinctions were assessed with Kruskall-Wallis followed by Man-Whitney U test.
extracted.Cluster Description:
MIX_SIP_LCA_AS - a variety of learning actions, primarily and roughly equally distributed across passive social interaction (SIP), learning content access (LCA), and assessment (AS) actions. 

MIX_LCA_AS - a variety of learning actions, with learning content access (LCA) and assessment (AS) having the largest presence. 

LCA_AS - dominance of learning content access (LCA) with notable presence of assessment (AS) and minimal presence of informing/orienteering and/or monitoring. 

AS_LCA_MM - dominance of assessment (AS), with a notable presence of learning content access (LCA) and monitoring (MM) actions. 

AS_INF_MP_LCA - sessions focused primarily on assessment (AS) with notable presence of informing (INF), planning (MP) and learning content access (LCA). 

sh_AS_INF_MM - short (sh) sessions with dominance of assessment (AS) and notable presence of informing (INF) and monitoring (MM) actions. 


sh_AS_INF_MP - short sessions with dominance of assessment (AS) and notable presence of informing (INF) and planning (MP) actions.
extracted.Implications:
Need and benefit of communicating with students why a specific strategy is not only benefical, but also important to the task at hand.

Need to understand the reason for why students consider changing learning tactics throughout a course.
extracted.Comments: Authors identify 7 “learning tactics”, which subsequently are divided into 4 learning strategies.
extracted.Limitations:
Multiple features seem to have the same name, hindering interpretability.

No insight into student motivation, which leads that log interpretation requires assumptions.

Need for generalization to other courses and contexts.

extracted.Features: [MIX_SIP_LCA_AS, MIX_LCA_AS, LCA_AS, AS_LCA_MM, AS_INF_MP_LCA, sh_AS_INF_MM, sh_AS_INF_MP]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The logs contain around 44 types of learning activities, which are aggregated into the 7 fhigher level forms of aggregation which are subsequently used to extract the different features.

Data is aggregated from the start to the moment of prediction, which is done every week. 

The authors use expectation maximization to create learning tactics.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: Done via dendogram
extracted.Cluster Description:
Strategy A: Active, regular, comprehensive - a learning tactic that mixes multiple forms of resources regularly

Strategy B: Active, Regular, Selective - high (lower than A) levels of activity → low presence f monitoring activities

Strategy C - Marginally active and regular, highly selective: low levels of activity - low activity an low regularity of study. When it comes to learning resources the focus is learning materials and assessments. 

Strategy D: Inactive, Irregular, Selective - Even lower levels of activity than C.
extracted.Implications:
Need and benefit of communicating with students why a specific strategy is not only benefical, but also important to the task at hand.

Need to understand the reason for why students consider changing learning tactics throughout a course.
extracted.Comments:
Analysis is done over time, but the 4 clusters appear consistently over time. After obtaining the clusters, authors focus on examining the learning pahtways of different students → whether they are changing their strategy of not and see effects in performance. 

Authors conclude that being consistent in Strategies A or B tends to result in the best performances. On the other end of the spectrum, being consistent on poor strategy tends to lead to the worst performance.

Interestingly, when it comes to tactics, successful students are not only active but tend to follow varierd forms of tactics, while the least successful tend to reduce the variety of tactics as the semester advances.
extracted.Limitations:
Multiple features seem to have the same name, hindering interpretability.

No insight into student motivation, which leads that log interpretation requires assumptions.

Need for generalization to other courses and contexts.

annotations.text: Translating analytics into outcomes is a problem that the authors aim to address. For that, the authors focus on developing an interpretable predictive model for student performance. Analisys on data from an undergraduate course shows that there is a relationship betweeen observed study strategies and course-success.

annotations.text: EDM and LA are useful disciplines to extract insights from educational databases. Most of themethods that predict student performance use income or assets, but often neglect spending or personal information.

The authors use data from Pakistani universities to create a model that predicts success on a the degree using these often overlooked features. The approach outperforms outperforms other models that do not consider these new feature sets.

references.title: PredictCS: Personalizing Programming learning by leveraging learning analytics

extracted.Courses: Not Applicable - students belong to Engineering in Foreign Languages School
extracted.Initialization Method:
Learning Style is inferred from Questionnaire. 

Sparsity problem → requires that there is at least one student with each learning tool to have selected a tool as preferrable.
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: The learning style is continuously updated based on the interactions of the user with the system.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Similarity between items is computed using Jaccard Similarity.

Representation of users used demographic data AND Learning Style as derived from the LS questionnaire.
extracted.Recommendation types:
Shortcuts: based on static information

Suggestions: incorporate learning style and user interactions
extracted.Evaluation: {Satisfaction: 12/20 students reported to be likely to use the recommendder again}
extracted.Comments:
Methodologically, the work can be summarized into 2 sections:

1. Obtaining the learning styles from the questionnaire AND a rating of tools based on choices upon registration.

2. Using a Collaborative Filtering approach to make recommendations.

To mitigate the sparsity problem, recommendation matrices are computed seaparately for each learning style.
extracted.Limitations:
- Results on accuracy of LS evaluation or Recommendations is not reported.

- Items whose score does not surpass a pre-determined threshold are never shown.

- Cold-Start problem

- Evaluation metrics considered (accuracy)

- Survey evaluation for recommender based on sdatisfaction-

annotations.text: PredictCS automatically flags students at risk and sends them feedback useful for early intervention. It uses multi-modal data as inputs for multiple predictive models - prior academic performance, carachteristics, logs and student progress. The model generates predictions every week.
There is also an opt-in to receive feedback - programming suggestions to resources that help fill knowledge gaps.

extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.9281 - PLR Stage 3, F1-Score: 0.9283 - PLR Stage 3, Precision:  0.9255 - PLR Stage 3, Recall:  0.9313 - PLR Stage 3}
extracted.Comments:
Authors create a 5-stage logistic regression to predict succes at next assignment in 5 different stages of course completion: Week 2, Week 4, Week 6, Week 7, Week 10.

Overall, the models created seem to be especially effective at Stage 3: week 6. 

Different features are important at different stages.
extracted.Limitations:
No model assessment strategy → Data leakage issues.

No algorithms tested beyond Logistic Regression.

Some questionable preprocessing decisions: Pearson correlation and Label Encoding for non-ordinal variables.

annotations.text: Most of the literature on early warning systems for students at risk does not use probabilistic approaches. To fill this gap, the authors use a five-stage Logistic Regression model trained on Moodle logs, demographic information and student assessment data to predict the probability of a student failing an upcoming assessment.

Prior performance was established to be the primary predictor, with the remaining feature sets loosing significance after incorporating data from this source. Moreover, the authors established that good predictions could be achieved as early as week 6, but with uncertainty decreasing at later stages of prediction.

Learning actions were grouped in learning sessions and these were given to a first-order Markov Model, which subsequently were sued as inputs to an EM model.

Authors define 30 minutes as the threshold that separates 2 learning sessions.

annotations.text: There is a research stream focusing on extracting learning strategies from multiple sources and multiple learning settings and checking how these different learning strategies map into overall performance. However, tow gaps are apparent: doing this for MOOCs is still relatively under-explored AND connections between uncovered strategies and psychological constructs is usually not performed.

The paper uses process and sequence-mining techniques to detect learning strategies from MOOC logs and tries to map them to the Big Five personality traits. Results show 4 distinct learning strategies that were predicted by conscientiousness, emotional instability and agreeableness.

Authors make a distinct analysis between students who succeeded in first assignment and students who have not. 

Analysis shows

annotations.text: The Open University has invested in early warning systems that flag and allow students at risk of failing the module. The paper goes over methods to analyze students in a VLE (Markov Chains and GUHA). While both are effective, Markov Chain analysis allows for the modelling of temporal dependencies.

references.title: SGKT: Session graph-based knowledge tracing for student performance prediction

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Probability of getting the next candidate exercise correct
extracted.Students: 3852 ASSISTments09 learners, 27485 ASSISTments12 learners nad 5000 EdNet learners
extracted.Features: [HRG embeddings, SG embeddings]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
Graph-based KT has already been used. Current approaches either ignore relationship between exercises and skills. GIKT or PEBG-KT ignore relationships between exercises. 

Also, all Graph KT models ignore the graph when answering questions.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
SGKT introduces 3 structures meant to address the flaws of the previous models:

an HRG embedding which encodes the exercise-to-exercise and skill-to-skill information.

An SG embedding model which encodes the learner’s knowledge state.

A prediction model which takes the previous representations and predicts the probability of getting the new exercise correctly.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Both HRG and SG are combined using a self-attention mechanism.
extracted.Models: BKT, DKT, DKT+, KTM, DDKVMN, GIKT, SGKT
extracted.Comments:
The authors create a new KT model based on Knowledge Graphs.

The model allows the measurement of the evolution of skills over time - interpretability.

Authors also do experiments to address cold-start scenario. SGKT ourperforms DKT and DKT+ on both of the scenarios tested - but eventually
extracted.Limitations:
No cross-validation.

Limited studies on cold-start scenarios.

Requires model optimization efforts.

annotations.text: LSTM is commonly used in DKT to analyze sequences of exercises done by students and predict whether the next exercise will be done right or not. However, LSTMs tend to give too much importance to short-term dependencies and often overlook longer-term dependencies. Authors look to create a Graph-Based Knowledge Model (SGKT). This model creates a representation for each exercise session and attempts to model the relationship between exercises and skills.

The knowledge state is obtained via a gated graph NN and uses CNN to obtain embedding representations of exercises and skills. Moreover, there is a second mechanism to obtain embeddings of knowledge states. Finally, an Attention mechanism is used to make predictions.

references.title: CNN-Transformer: A deep learning method for automatically identifying learning engagement

annotations.text: Previous research established a relationship between engagement and emotional investment of learners. Authors focus on using a model called CNN-Transformer to measure engagement from learner position and facial expressions. Model is able to predict engagement of new participants with an accuracy of 93%.

references.title: Variational Deep Knowledge Tracing for Language Learning

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Getting next word in the sequence correct
extracted.Students:
6000 language learners on SLAM dataset

Unmentioned number on HLR
extracted.Courses: 3 Languages on SLAM: English, Spanish and French
extracted.Features: [Sequence of Preceding Words]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
DKT approaches learn from raw data, meaning that they cannot capture latent student behaviors.

BKT is able to do that, but the model, on a results-based approach underperforms DKT.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
The model introduces a seq-to-seq form of knowledge modelling. While advantageous relative to DKT or BKT (theoretically), the sequence model has a drawback of being deterministic.

The authors also propose Variational Knowledge Tracing which adds a latent variable to each time step.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: In this experiment, each word in the sequence is understood as an exercise. Thus, the prediction task becomes predicting if the next word in the sentence will be correct or not.
extracted.Models:
SDKT,
VDKT,
Self-Ensemble of SDKT, Self-Ensemble of VDKT, GBoost Ensemble of SDKT, GBoost Ensemble of VDKT
extracted.Comments:
Work proposes a new approach that uses sequences to see if students understand gramatical rules. 

As a probabilistic model, VDKT encodes the understanding of these rules as a latent variable.
extracted.Limitations:
No Cross-Validation, 

Could be generalized for other contexts. 

Latent variables only capture information extractable from the learning trajectories - something other data sources could probably capture.

DKT approaches learn from raw data, meaning that they cannot capture latent student behaviors.

Word discovery is understood as a learning section. Thus, the authors save the “sequence” of the previous exercises done.

Models use a Encoder-Decoder architecture.

Work proposes a new approach that uses sequences to see if students understand gramatical rules.

annotations.text: DKT is a state-of-the-art technique that predicts a learner’s performance based on observed learning history. However, learning history does not account for the whole picture: guessing, partial understanding, etc… To include a measurement of these elemente, the authors propose Variational Deep Knowledge Tracing (VDKT), which uses latent variables to incorporate their stochasticity.
OL a KT task, VDKT beats seq-to-seq DKT baselines and other KT models on different language learning datasets across different metrics.

references.title: Learning from Non-Assessed Resources: Deep Multi-Type Knowledge Tracing

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Getting the next assessed learning activity correct on timestep t
extracted.Students:
1000 learners from EdNet

2063 learners from Junyi2063 and 1564 from Junyi 1564
extracted.Courses: Each dataset often refers to a single course.
extracted.Data sources:
Description of Activities Undertaken.

Learner interactions and performance on graded and ungraded assessments.
extracted.Features: Knowledge of the learner in each knowledge concept.
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
Current KT approaches do not take into account non-assessed learning resources even though interaction with these types of resources is instrumental towards knowledge acquisition.

In other words, since there is usually no latent space of knowledge concepts that are being evaluated in graded exercises, doing that exercise for non-assessed resources is even more challenging.
extracted.Novelty of Model: Authors claim non-assess learning resources is pivotal. Thus, the proposed model DMKT which not only estimates knowledge gains between different assessed exercises but also tries to do the same between non-assessed and assessed exercises.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Each learner is represented by a memory matrix which stores the knowledge states of each student.

After each question, the memory matrix is updated using a erase-followed-by-add mechanism.

For a given knowledge concept, different resources have different importances/weights. Attention mechanisms are especially useful for this specific step.

Initialization of both Memory Matrix is done via Gaussian initialization. 

Sequence of actions is optimized to be of length 50.
extracted.Models: DKT, DKVMN, DeepIRT, SAKT, SAINT, AKT, MVKM, MLP-M, DKT-M, DKVMN-M, SAKT-M, SAINT-M, AKT-M, Deep Multi-Type Knowledge Tracing
extracted.Comments:
Authors use a different form of representation of learners adapted from DKVKT.

It uses a key matrix to store knowledge concepts. In addition, it uses an Attention mechanism to represent the ungraded resources → these are an embedding representation of the concepts from each ungraded exercise. 

Moreover, it has a Memory matrix meant to represent each learner and his current knowledge on different knowledge components. At different timesteps, the learner interacts with components and questions, which leads to the update of the memory matrix. 

Thus, for Question at time t, the input is the knowledge state of the learner and the knowledge concepts that exercise evaluates. 

Discussion of interpretability is very much welcomed, as the notion of correlation between resources can help identify similar types of resouces.
extracted.Limitations:
AUC as only classification metric being considered.

Small numbe of students - despite high number of interactions.
