annotations.text: Non-assessed learning resources are often ignored in KT even though they generally contain multiple materials that are necessary for students to acquire knowledge. The paper introduces Deep Multi Knowledge Tracing (DMKT) that models transitions between assess and non-assessed learning activities.
Comparison with State-of-the-art on 4 datasets shows that the model is good at predicting performance and respresenting student knowledge.

references.title: Deep Knowledge Tracing with Convolutions

extracted.Students: 4151 learners in ASSISTments2009, 19840 learners in ASSISTments2015, 333 learners in Statics2011 and 4000 synthetic learners
extracted.Courses: Each dataset refers to its own context
extracted.Features: [Sequences of Answers]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
Current KT models consider the whole sequence of answers and not specifically the most recent answers. 

All methods used the embedding of answers directly into the model.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
Authors introduce convolution to identify local interactions and patterns in the sequence of answers:

The vector is converted into a matrix.

CNNs are used to reduce dimensionality and locally encode potential patterns.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Sequence vectors are reshaped into a Matrix, which is given as argument to a Convolutional Layer, followed by an average pooling layer.

These are then given as iput to a LSTM.
extracted.Comments:
Authors develop a new apporach at knowledge tracing. Models are followed by a set of optimization attempts. 

Authors argue that this KT approach can help professors and learners track the learning progress of learners and check exercise realtionships.
extracted.Limitations:
Model evaluation should use ranking instead of only showing performance results.

More metrics of evaluation should be considered.

annotations.text: KT looks at past responses from students and uses them to create a model of the students’ knowledge. This model can later be used to predict the probability of getting a new question right. Good KT models can help students identify where a student is lacking in terms of knowledge, which can also be used by professors to better prepare new materials.

Deep learning models in KT seem to be the state-of-the-art but seem to underestimate the contribution of the most recent answers for the current knowledge state, which is counterintuitive. To address this, the authors propose a Convolutional KT model, that uses 3D convolutions.
CKT is argued to achieve the new State-of-te-art performance in predicting performance on ASSISTments2009 and ASSISTments 2015 datasets.

references.title: Optimizing Bayesian Knowledge Tracing with Neural Network Parameter Generation

extracted.Students:
4217 ASSISTments 2009 students, 
3310 KDD-Cup Algebra learners, 
46674 ASSISTments 2012 students, 6043 Bridge and Algebra learners
extracted.Features: [Knowledge Components for learner, Sequence of Previous Answers]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
BKT, while interpretable, has issues with convergence in local minima regardless of optimization technique. 

DKT and SAKT get better predictive performance but are not interpretable.

Goodd interpretable approaches such as DLKT or Deep-IRT introduce interpretability because they separately moddel knowledge and difficulty, but they are “definitive” and lack probabilistic context.
extracted.Novelty of Model: Authors propose not a model, but a neural network-based optimization technique - OptiNN - that improves BKT.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Observations with no skills attached were not considered. Missing values were filled with mode.
extracted.Comments:
Authors compare 3 well-known KT models: BKT, KT-IDEM and ILE on 4 distinct datasets. 4 forms of parameter optimization are done for each model. 

OptiNN optimization shows the best performance consistently across all experiments on models.

Varying the psarameters of OptiNN shows some robustness of the model.
extracted.Limitations: Holdout method.

extracted.Students:
4217 ASSISTments 2009 students, 
3310 KDD-Cup Algebra learners, 
46674 ASSISTments 2012 students, 6043 Bridge and Algebra learners
extracted.Features: [Knowledge Components for learner, Sequence of Previous Answers]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
BKT, while interpretable, has issues with convergence in local minima regardless of optimization technique. 

DKT and SAKT get better predictive performance but are not interpretable.

Goodd interpretable approaches such as DLKT or Deep-IRT introduce interpretability because they separately moddel knowledge and difficulty, but they are “definitive” and lack probabilistic context.
extracted.Novelty of Model: Also, they authors also propose TransformerKT to beat state-of-the art models in a hyper-parameter insensitive manner.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Observations with no skills attached were not considered. Missing values were filled with mode..

BKTransformer is an extension of SAKT that also incorporates BKT parameters for knowledge component.
extracted.Comments: BKTransformer achieves close to state-of-art performance on most datasets, being only surpassed in AST12 by DKT.
extracted.Limitations: No full interepretability of attention networks.

annotations.text: Bayesian Knowledge Tracing, while useful and well-established, is known to have some convergence problems that lead it to converge in undesirable local minima (parameter degeneracy). Deep Knowledge Tracing achieves better performance but is often not interpretable - a significant issue in student response correctedness scenarios.

To address the problems with BKT convergence, authors propose a new form of optimizing BKT OptimNN which is meant to use a NN to train BKT parameters. This is later extended into BKTransformer, which generates parameters for BKT that evolve with time.

Authors show this approach to outperform both BKT and DKT with minimal parameter tuning, with the advantage of mantaining the BKT interpretability that comes with probability estimates of skill, student mastery and question correctedness.

references.title: XKT: Toward Explainable Knowledge Tracing Model With Cognitive Learning Theories for Questions of Multiple Knowledge Concepts
references.journal: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering

extracted.Students:
3841 learners on Assistments
5000 students in EdNet
6043 on KDD
2086 on WUD
extracted.Courses: Each dataset refers to its own context
extracted.Features: [Memory Matrix]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: The current Knowledge Tracing Models based on IRT are too simple to integrate prediction of answers and connect them to the theoretical constructs of knowledge modelling.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
The new model XKT includes 3 components that together look to make this integration:

Multi-Feature Embedding: a component meant to model questions.

Cognition Processing Network: Thew knowledge tracing model that encodes the different knowledge components.

MIRT Neural Predictor: The layer that outputs predictions and parameters.


A big distinction between this model and the previous is that Each knowledge concept is represented by a vector.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Prediction of Next Question CFA is done by subdividing the task into 2 components:

1. A Cueing Retrieval Network which estimates the learners’ ability based on the its current memory matrix.

2. A Question difficulty model that estimates how simple/hard the question will be for the learner.
extracted.Models: DKT, Deep-IRT, DKVMN, CKT, AKT, simpleKT, LPKT-S, XKT
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a new model called XKT which is not only expected to outperform current KT models but also provide explainable parameters.

The authors show that model outperforms baselines. Moreover, the authors also explore how this model addresses the cold-strat problem for both learners and questions:

For learners, XKT starts outperforming BKT for sequences of exercises that are greater than 35 questions.

XKT also outperforms the remaining models when simulating coldd-start for questions.

From an interpretation perspective, the transitions between knowledge states in XKT are smooth.
extracted.Limitations: Only AUC results are shown.

annotations.text: DKT has an issue with interpretability, despite its well-document success at modelling student knowledge. Authors draw from Multi-Dimensional Item-Response Theory and Cognitive Learning to create XKT, an explainable Knowledge Tracing Model.
XKT has 3 components:
Experiments in 4 real-world datasets show XKT achieves better performance than other KT methods while also showing good ability to identify key predictors of student performance.

references.title: Unpacking help-seeking process through multimodal learning analytics: A comparative study of ChatGPT vs Human expert

extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, Eye-Tracking Data, Conversational transcripts
extracted.Features: [Diagnosing Question, Asking Help Instrumental, Asking Help Executive, Asking Help Avoidant, Asking Help Clarifying, Asking Help Understanding, Evaluating Help Positive, Evaluating Help No Feedback, Evaluating Help Negative, Processing Help Accepting, Processing Help Neglecting, Processing Help Returning]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Data collected from the different sources is aggregated into each construct of help-seeking. 

The processes are measured in relative terms.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model:
Differences between HE group and ChatGPT is done using a Shapiro-Wilk test. Effect size of AI presence is done using Cliff’s delta.

FOMM rules needed at least a 5% threshold of transitions.
extracted.Implications:
Students wityh access to AI have shown a much higher propensity to ask for help than their peers. LEarners without access to it are much more likely to critically try to engage with questions before going through with asking for help.

Moreover, students without access to AI are much more likely to follow through with the standard linear  help-seeking process. Access to AI often skips critical steps: Diagnosing Question and Evaluating Answer.
extracted.Comments:
Work does a comparison between help-seeking behaviors for essay writing of learners using ChatGPT vs students who do not have access to ChatGPT.

Groups were set experimentally Apriori.
extracted.Limitations:
Difficulty in measuring cognitive processes exclusively using behavioral data.

Human interactions were not done in a naturalistic classroom setting. 

Small sample size in the context of a single course.

No analysis of context of quality of the help provided (authors measure the act of seeking help). 

Requires validation for learning tasks beyond essay writing.

annotations.text: There is close to no research highlighting differences in help-seeking behaviour when it comes to asking help to humans or to GenAI. Authors use multi-modal data to compare help-seeking between 2 groups of students. Comparison is done using Statistical tests and process mining.

Results show that Human help-seeking is linear, whilst AI group follows a more non-linear approach to help-seeking. Also, in human help-seeking there seems to be more thought put into the analysis of the feedback that is received, showing that it is possible that student over-rely on AI when it is available (since they ask pretty much everything).

references.title: Footprints at School: Modelling In-class Social Dynamics from Students’ Physical Positioning Traces

annotations.text: Position Tracking is a potentially useful source of educational data. The paper explores how using physical positioning data can be used for Social Network Analysis purposes. Authors perform an analyzis on primary school data (98 students and 6 teachers) across 8 weeks.
Results show that position can helpo identify which pedagogical approaches promote in-class interactions or isolated students.

references.title: Knowledge Tracing Machines: Factorization Machines for Knowledge Tracing

extracted.Students:
536 Fraction Learners, 
757 TIMSS learners, 2922 ECPE learners, 4217 learners, 1730 Berkeley learners, 58939 Castor learners
extracted.Features: [Users, Items, Skills, Attempts, Wins and Fails, Extra Side Information - all One Hot Encoded]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Most approaches precedding this model students or questions using unidimensional parameters. Moreover, most approaches are unable to model side information per question (such as multiple skills).
extracted.Novelty of Model: The Factorization machines approach not only allows a higher dimensionality representation of students/questions but also shows side information is more effective to improve model performance than increasing the latent dimensions.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The first difference stems from how data is represented: ASSISTments and Berkeley have a temporal representation while the remaining datasets are not. 

Feature Encoding requires the consideration of the following dimensions: 

Users, Items, Skills, Attempts, Wins and Fails, Extra Side Information - all One Hot Encoded.

Sequences are represented as triplets.
extracted.Models: AFM, PFA, IRT, MIRTb10, MIRTb20, KTM(iswf0), KTM(iswf20), KTM(iswfe5)
extracted.Comments:
The introduction of side information seems to increase the performace of the model. This is increase is more noticeable than augmenting the number of latent dimensions being considered for the knowledge model.

Ultimately, KTMs are presented as testbeds for the introduction of additional features.
extracted.Limitations:
Encoding (OHE) may need refinement.

Not all models are considered for all datasets.

annotations.text: KT is described as a sequence prediction problem, where the goal is predicting performance on question (usually in the form of CFA). The authors propose the use of Factorization Machines to address the KT problem (that traditionally uses latent variables or factor analysis).
Results on seven datasets show that FM performs well even in situations with sparse data, recomming it to be a good testbed to improve existing models.

Only course DDD is considered, specifically. Moreover, the authors perform an implementation of the holdout method. 

Multiple attempts at optimization were performed. 

Multiple feature sets are considered and the authors identify that engagement and performance achieve, on their own, much better predictions than demographic data.

annotations.text: The paper does an overview of the supervised learning algorithms that attempt to predict exam performance of students in both classification and regression scenarios. The empirical results show that the models using engagement and past performance data used as input to an Artificial Neural Network achieve the highest precision. Demographic data does not seem to have a large effect at predicting exam performance.

Relçevant messages were labelled into 4 PBL-steps:

Representation-Relevant, Solution-Relevant, Justification-relevant & Evaluation-relevant
extracted.Implications: Students are more likely to engage in discussion in discussion with their peers than any other activity (i.e. answering questions). Educators hsould make an effort in fostering an environment where helping peers is rewarded.
extracted.Comments:
Participation of study was voluntary.

ML models ware meant to measure whether a specific forum entry is relevant or irrelevant for the course → which can be used as a hallmark of engagement of procrastinatory behavior. 

The predictions of this model are subsequently used as inputs for the Hierarchical Linear Regression.
extracted.Limitations:
Selection bias → only includes learners who have signed in to participate in a specific course.

Small sample size (student-wise and number of courses-wise) should be addressed in future works.

extracted.Courses: 1 Online Course in Social Work and Problem-Solving
extracted.Data sources: Student Reports, Discussion Forums, LMS logs, Questionnaires
extracted.Features: [Gender, Prior Knowledge, Self-Regulation, Posted, Replied, Words, Representation-rlvt, Solution-rlvt, Justification-rlvt, Evaluation-rlvt]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The predictions of the ML classifiers are used as features for this model. 

The idea is to identify which steps or phases of the PBL process are relevant towards better performance.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Adjsuted R-Squared: 0.682 - Regression using all features, R-Squared: 0.713 - Al Features}
extracted.Cluster Description:
Results show that the predictions obtained from the ML models are better representations of peer-learning than exclusively using logs. 

Self-Regulation and peer-learning variables were found to be relevant predictors of PBL performance.
extracted.Implications:
Adequate labelling consistency from human evaluators may contribute to creating models able to predict engagement. 

Multi-modal learning analytics may be used to keep track of the stages of the PBL proces learners are into and provide adequate supoport to them.
extracted.Comments: Hierarchical linear regression results show that the addition of the peer-learning variables obtained from training the different ML models results into better overall predictions of performance in PBL than common constructs such as prior knowledge or gender.
extracted.Limitations: Selection bias → only includes learners who have signed in to participate.Small sample size (student-wise and number of courses-wise) should be addressed in future works.

annotations.text: Multimodal learning analytics has garnered a lot of interest among Problem-Based Learning researchers. Within this context, there is a gap in using text data generated from learner interactions with their peers. The authors explor MMLA on a sample of 104 fourth-year students to classify text in online discussions. Self-Regulation, messages and engagement were found to be predictive of online PBL performance.

The study not only makes predictions but also delves into discussing pedagogical policies and guidelines that can be derived from these early predictions.

LSTM, BOW and Course2Vec.

Holdout Method implementation follows a temporal logic to split the data. 

Both courses  and students are used to create embeddings.

The sequential information of the semesters relevealed itself to be extremely valuable as it led to the best performance.

Work is, by no means, exclusively using data for this purpose, but none of the other tasks fall into the objective of this work.
extracted.Limitations:

No evaluation of more classical models

Evaluation metric for task of predicting grade is not ideal (especially in multiclass contexts).

annotations.text: More availability of data has contributed to a proliferation of research on predicting next course. The paper provides a comprehensive revieww of data sources and models for different prediction tasks.
Authors evaluate different sources and models to predict:

Results show that enrollment-based graph embeddings perform better at predicting course pre-requisites. In addition, descriptions, syllabi and course2vec combined achieve the best predictive capabilities for course similarity.

references.title: Designing for serendipity in a university course recommendation system

extracted.Students: 164196 students in offline data; 70 undergrads in user study
extracted.Data sources: Enrollment histories + catalog descriptions + course meta-info
extracted.Initialization Method: User gives one favorite course; models use learned embeddings (no special cold-start beyond that)
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Models/versioned on GitHub and refreshed 3× per semester after data refresh
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Remove generic sentences, stop words & punctuation; lemmatize/stem; tokenize; build TF, binary, tf-idf vectors
extracted.Evaluation: Offline: mean/median rank & recall@10 (equivalency); recall@1/@10 (analogies). User study: serendipity = mean(unexpectedness, successfulness); 6 dimensions rated
extracted.Comments: BOW (div) scored highest in serendipity/novelty; course2vec best on analogies; RNN lacked novelty and often repeated requirement/major courses
extracted.Limitations: Students rely on catalog text; neural concepts may be hidden; hard to see commonality when descriptions are distal; real-time enrollment lag; future work: add latent semantics/syllabi

annotations.text: Collaborative Filtering algorithms tend to over-optimize for past behaviour, often leading to what is called a filter bubble. Authors try to address the issue in a course recommendation system at a HEI. This is done by using catalog descriptions (BOW) and enrollment histories (course2vec) to recommend novel and different courses.
Comparison is done with offline validation sets against a RNN-based recommender, with the bag-of-words based recommendations being the more serendipitous.

references.title: Investigating students' use of self‐assessments in higher education using learning analytics

extracted.Courses: 1 Course in a Bachelor's program in Economic and  Business Education at an European University
extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, Self Assessment Results, Questionnaires
extracted.Features: [Ratio of last self-assessment  attempt against best self-assessment possible, ratio of processeed tasks of each self-assessment, attempts per self-assessment, procesing time per self-assessment]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Data was cleaned from the LMS during self-assessments (11 in total) - 44 features in total.

All were standardized.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: 2 Clusters were selected based on WCSS and Sillhouette Score
extracted.Cluster Description:
High engagement (n=51): higher engasgement with self-assessments throughout the semester

low-engagement (n=107): lower engagement throughout the semesters.

Both groups have similar engagement for the mid-term test.
extracted.Implications:
Students seem to use self-assessment at specific moments in a semester AND studdents who are more engaged seem to obtain better performance than their peers. 

Learning analytics dashboards should not only draw relationship between engagement and performance and instead should provide process-oriented feedback.
extracted.Comments:
Authors use k-means on a set of students and then check whether there is agreement between the observed data for engagement against:

1. Overall engagement with resources - there is.

2. Alignment with Self-Reported data - there is as well.
extracted.Limitations:
Single course.

Limited number of self-reported variables weere cconsidered. 

Self-Assessments were limited to specific lessons and not framed in a hollistic manner.

No analysis of learner progression.

annotations.text: Formative assessments are a tool that exists but that is often underexplored. The study investigates learner engagement with self-assessments to see if and how they affect student performance. Approach is tested on a sample of students taking a BSc in Economic and Business Education and, throughout the semester were asked to do multiple self-assessments.

Authors used unsupervised and process mining approaches on the self-assessment records and found that most formative assessments were explored in preparation for the summative assessments. In general, 2 main clusters emerged and the obtained results were consistent with the self-reported strategies adopted.

references.title: What Time is It? Student Modeling Needs to Know

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Prediction of completing the next task successfully
extracted.Target: Finish Assignment within 1 hour: Successful (0) vs Unsuccessful (1)
extracted.Features: Extacted using EFD
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Time is often not considered in models for KT in either self-paced or tutor-based education -time to be understood as time elapsed between sequential events.
extracted.Novelty of Model: T-LSTM introduce the ability to model time intervals and incorporate into the prediction
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Students who had requested hints were eliminated from the sample.

Code traces are timestamped snapshots of student code.

Features are extracted using a form of AutoML called Expert Feature Detector.
extracted.Models:
ZeroR,
Logistic Regression, 
Recent Temporal Pattern,
LTSM,
T-LSTM
extracted.Comments:
BKT was not considered for this open-ended task since there are no specific knowledge components or predefined steps that need to be followed.

Task on iSNAP is defined by the learners being able to solve a homework: a snapshop of their screen is taken every 2 minutes and this sequence is used for prediction.
extracted.Limitations:
Only a single modeling task per learning environment.

Small sample size.

No alternative methods to assess time-awareness.

extracted.Features: Answer sequences
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Time is often not considered in models for KT in either self-paced or tutor-based education -time to be understood as time elapsed between sequential events.
extracted.Novelty of Model: T-LSTM introduce the ability to model time intervals and incorporate into the prediction
extracted.Models:
ZeroR,
Logistic Regression, 
BKT, LSTM, T-LSTM
extracted.Comments: Task on Pyrinees was to predict learning gains based on the sequences of answers given in a test.
extracted.Limitations:
Only a single modeling task per learning environment.

Small sample size.

No alternative methods to assess time-awareness.

annotations.text: Some of the most well known methods for KT are BKT and LSTMs. The problem with these approaches is that sub-divide time into discrete time-steps and not continuous. However, time between events  varies significantly and, fot that reason, authors decide to use Time-Aware LSTM for KT.

Authors explore T-LSTM in 2 domains. First, self-paced learning, wherein authors compare T-LSTM vs other standard classifiers to predict success. On a second-approach, they follow an instructor-paced setting and try to predict learning gains.
Results show LSTM beats all others in the self-paced setting and perform on par with LSTMs on instructor-paced learning.

references.title: Modelling Math Learning on an Open Access Intelligent Tutor

annotations.text: The authors evaluate student knowledge states in 2 courses from Arizona University using ALEKS. The authors look at unsupervised approaches and social network analysis to create embedding representations.

references.title: Should Learning Analytics Models Include Sensitive Attributes? Explaining the Why

extracted.Data sources: Academic History, Student Demographics, LMS logs, Attendance lgos
extracted.Features: [network_fundamentals, problem_solving, it_fundamentals, design_thinking, num_core_enrolled, core_average, gender_code, age, citizenship_status, home_language, disability_exists_flag, forum_contribution, site_visit, last_login, sms_opted_in_flag, Attendance mode]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The data includes 4 putative targets that are reduced to 2. Non-binary categoricasl were binarized and numerical features were standardized. 

The authors also created 4 specific variables whose inclusion/exclusion were tested.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy - Unpublished, TPR, TNR}
extracted.Comments:
Authors use AutoML to determine what would be the best algorithm and identify GBoost as the best one.

Authors do not fully publish the metrics epr se, but do publish a T-Score which measures the different

annotations.text: Dropout prediction is a common Learning Analytics task and, in may scenarios, institutions have models put into place to predict it, raising questions on whether sensitive attributes should be used or not.

Authors create models using data from an Australian university and realize that sensitive attributes brought marginal gains to model performance and, when there is an increase in model effectiveness, it comes more of as a function of how the attribute is related to the target.

references.title: Modeling Knowledge Acquisition from Multiple Learning Resource Types

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Score on Next Graded Learning Material
extracted.Courses: Each dataset refers to one course
extracted.Features: [Sequences of Activities]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
Current knowledge models tend to avoid relationships between different types of learning materials. 

Current measures (such as time spent on specific resources) may indicate contradictory realities.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
The proposed model identifies relationships between different types of learning resources while, simultaneously, building a domain knowledge model that allows a better mapping of materials to their respective knowledge components. 

This model follows an essential assumption: the knowledge provided by different types of learning materials can be described using the same latent space → meaning transferrability between types of learning materials.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Each learning material as pre-assigned to a specific knowledge components.

Learners are represented by a tensor of 3-lower dimensional representations:

1. Latent Features

2. A temporal dynamic knowledge tensor

3. A mapping between materials and different knowledge concepts.

The preoduct between Knowledge Tensor T and Latent Feature _Matrix S represents the student’s knowledge in each concept before an attempt.
extracted.Models:
Individual BKT,
DKT
Deedback-Driven Tensor Factorization, 
Tensor Factorization Without Learning,
Bayesian Probabilistic Tensor Factorization, 
Average of All Predictions, 
Multi-View Knowledge Model
extracted.Comments:
For each model, the probability is assumed to be the predicted score on each exercise.

To do that, the authors benchmark their models in 6 distinct datasets: 3 synthetic datasets and 3 real datasets.

Tests include some form of hyper-parameter tuning via Grid Search. Model includes constraint for learning and forgetting materials. Results show that these constraints, while not universal, are important constructs to consider in knowledge modelling,
extracted.Limitations: All datasets are done on a MOOC/course specific analysis.

annotations.text: Students learn from multiple resources and an effective learner representation should probably account for them as it would allow for an effective detection of knowledge gaps and recommendation of learning materials. KT traditionally only uses problems for this modelling, ignoring other forms of materials.

The paper proposes a KT model that incorporates data from multiple resource types - Multiview Knowledge Model (MVKM) using tensor factorization - which is meant to be able to reduce dimensionality. The authors perform multiple tests for predictive accuracy and curiously the authors found multiple similarities across different types of learning materials.

references.title: Learning Behavior-oriented Knowledge Tracing
references.journal: Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Answering question at Step t+1 correctly
extracted.Students: 3006 ASSISTments 2009 learners, 22397 ASSISTments 2012 learners, 1000 Juniy learners
extracted.Features: [Sequences of Answers, Spped, Attempts, Hints]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Previous models tend to look at KT as sequences of answers, leaving behind the different types of learner behavior that are relevant to model a learner’s knowledge state.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
LBKT has 2 modules: 

Differentiated Behavior Effect Quantifying: it looks at 3 specific learning behaviors (Speed, Attempts and Hints). 

Fused Behavior Effect Measuring (FBEM): Behaviors are not independent from one another. For that reason, the authors use a separate module to measure dependencies between behavioral features.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Learners are represented as matrix that is updated with more and more exercises. The update of knowledge states includes a forget gate and a knowledge acquisition vector.
extracted.Models: DKT, DKT_concat, AT-DKT, DFVMN, DDMKT, SAKT, AKT, LPKT, LBKT-DB, LBKT-FB, LBKT-Forget
extracted.Comments: Authors create a new methodd that looks to introduce Learner Behavior. Model is validated on 3 public datasets. Multiple Ablation Studies are also performed to measure sensitiviy of the model to different parameters.
extracted.Limitations:
No Cross-Validation,

Limited number of learner behaviors considered in approach.

annotations.text: Most of the KT work that exists is based on predicting whether a student will get a question right or wrong, often disregarding relevant indicators such as answering speed or number of attempts. Authors propose LBKT which is meant to explore these constructs.

Authors start with explanation of the constructs and proceed with their quantification. Benchmarking on public datasets shows that the model outperforms other approaches in the literature. Code is also publicly available.

references.title: Adapting Bayesian Knowledge Tracing to a Massive Open Online Course in edX

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Next Question is Correct on First Attempt
extracted.Features: [Activities associated with a specific problem]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
In intelligent tutoring systems there is a connection of the Knowledge Components associated with each Question in the System.

Getting experts to map questions to knowledge components is not trivial in a MOOC context. Moreover, in a MOOC setting, KC are often treated in isolation, leaving little chance to see evolution of KCs over time.

MOOCs also have a different form of accepting an answer as correct and /or incorrect - ITS use first answer, MOOC often consider last attempt.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
The authors’ implementation of BKT includes a variant that allows the observation of multiple subparts at once (IDEM). 

It also includes a parameter to measure the number of attempts needed to get the correct answer (Count).
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Logs were separated by user and the representation of each user is the sequence of activities associated with a problem.
extracted.Comments:
Fitting of BKT model was done via Expectation-Maximization. 

Models are divided into types of materials. Authors analyze the results in terms of problem types: Homework problems, Lccture Problems and finally Exam Problems.

The model is percurssor for KT in MOOCs with 2 that address 2 challenges:

1. Lack of a KC model for MOOCs,

2. Unpenalized Attempts Counts
extracted.Limitations:
Authors only publish results using AUC score.

Comparison with ASSISTments scores is soewhat unnecessary as interpretation of contexts is substantially different.

Subpart separation is not ideal to predict success throughut the course.

A single dataset and course.

No extension to DKT framework, focusing only in BKT.

annotations.text: MOOCs are becoming more and more prevalent in higher education. However, this proliferation follows the traditional educational paradigm in many regards. The paper shows tha BKT can be adapted to a course on edX. The experiment tests BKTs success on 3 distinct challenges: lack of knowledge component models, unpenalized multiple attempts for quizzes/tests and diverging learning pathways through the system.

references.title: Numbers Are Not Enough. Why e-Learning Analytics Failed to Inform an Institutional Strategic Plan

annotations.text: LMS have shown to foster a sense of community and progress among the student population and have consequently become a cornerstone of higher education institutions. Authors take data from one institution and analyze the usage patterns of the LMS in relation to student outcomes.
Moreover, the paper delves into how these analytics are used at an institutional level (often they aren’t) and discusses possible reasons for this.

references.title: Analyzing Student Strategies In Blended Courses Using Clickstream Data

extracted.Courses: 1 Computer Science Course at Carnegie Melon University Spring 2019
extracted.Data sources:
LMS logs, 
Homework and Exam Grades, 
Student-level lecture and attendance logs
extracted.Features: [n-grams of sequences of activities with length 3, 4 or 5]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors identify 37 types of clicks.^Clickstreams are divided into assessment weeks. 

Authors create an embedding of the click types using a skip-gram model.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: Coherence score between sequences.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Group 1 - Looks at lecture notes

Group 2 - Look at Homeworks

Group 3 - Looks at recitation materials, 

Group 4 - Catch up on news, 

Group 5 Active HW Engagement, 

Group 6 - Active Homework Engagement

Group 7 -Library Documentation

Group 8 - Tries Practice Exam

Group 9 - Look at practyice exam
extracted.Implications:
NLP-approaches can be used to analyze student’s learning strategies. 

Activity tends to increase before exams/homeworks, with students.
extracted.Comments:
Authors start by using pattern mining to extract sequences of activities. Similarity between patterns is computed using Jaro-Winkler distance.

Clusters generated are imbalanced. Smallest clusters have around 2 or 3 candiddate patterns.

Authors create a predicitve model that predicts assessment type based on the interactions of students → showing beihavior differs between homeworks and exams.
extracted.Limitations: Needs generalizability with more data/ techniques.

annotations.text: Descriptive modelling is under-explored in comparison to the creation of models that predict performance. Authors draw from pattern mining and NLP to extract the most frequent learning strategies from a blended course.

Results show that, depending on the assessments the student is preparing for, the patterns of interaction with the LMS differ considerably. The results show that the hybrid NLP approach may generate decent descriptions of student behavior and be used to predict performance.

references.title: Machine Learning applied to student attentiveness detection: Using emotional and non-emotional measures

annotations.text: Tracking student attentiveness in e-learning scenarios is a big challenge. The work tries to use webcam footage as a means to perform attentiveness prediction. Using a sample of 7 learners, the authors use EAR, YAR, head pose and emotional states to extract features from video. Then, they use these features as part of an ML pipeline meant to predict attentiveness.
XGBoost outperforms the other algorithms, achieving and OVR AUC of 92%.

references.title: Clickstream Knowledge Tracing: Modeling How Students Answer Interactive Online Questions

extracted.Features: [In-Question Activities]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Most KT approaches only consider answers to model learner knowledge without considering how they got to the answer, which provides a naive and incomplete picture of a learner’s knowledge.
extracted.Novelty of Model: Model proposed is an expansion of DKT that considers the clickstream of the in-question activities as an integral part of the knowledge model.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Answer states are stored in JSON.

All data is normalized and, if necessary, One-Hot Encoded. 

Timestamps are converted to time intervals (in logathmic form) and each partial score is considered as categorical. 

Data is used tp build an embedding representation using an Autoencoder.
extracted.Models: Clickstream Knowledge Tracing-PT, CKT-FT, CKT-E2E, DKT
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model:
Math Unit:

{AUC: 0.8313 - CKT-FT, }

Question ID:

Math Dimension:
extracted.Comments:
CKT is presented under 3 paradigms:

Frozen Pre-trained weights - CKT-PT

Fine-tuned rpe-trained weights - CKT-FT

Trained from Scratch - CKT-E2E

Authors experiment their results based on 3 forms of indexing for knowledge components:

1. At the Question ID level, 

2. At the Math Unit, 

3. At the Math Dimension

Authors also identify what is the minimum number of questions a learner needs to answer to address the cold-start problem and find that CKT-PT reequires between 5-8% less questions than DKT.
extracted.Limitations: Comparison to DKT only, discarding other potential approaches.

annotations.text: KT often revolves around using previous questions to predict whether the next question will be correctly answered. One of the main drawbacks of the traditional KT approaches is that it only considers correctedness of previous questions, but not the rationale that led to a specific answer.

Authors use unsupervised learning on clickstream data to identify different patterns of question-answering behavior. This is followed by Clickstream KT, a model that is standard KT that also considers clickstream data within its parameters. Results show that inserting clickstream leads to better performance of the KT models.

references.title: Affective States and State Tests: Investigating How Affect and Engagement during the School Year Predict End-of-Year Learning Outcomes

extracted.Data sources: Labelling of Affect and Behavior Observations, LMS logs,
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Labelling of Affect and Behavior observation was performed by 2 experts by combining field observations with

annotations.text: Authors hypothesize that there is a relationship between student affect, engagement and their learning outcomes. Authors look into ASSISTments data and found boredom during exercises to be negatively correlated with performance. However, boredom during a tutoring session was positively correlated with performance.
On a second experiment, authors use affect and disengaged behavior on the tutoring system to predict performance. Model achieves good correlation with standardized exam scores.

references.title: Credit hours is not enough: Explaining undergraduate perceptions of course workload using LMS records

extracted.Features: [Average size of original posts, Numberr of Original Forum Posts, Number of OPriginal Top Forum Posts, Original Forum Posts per Student, TA/Instructoir Posts per Student, Average Forum Reply Time, %of students with replies, Average Size of Submission Comments, Number of Submission Comments per Student, %Submissions with Comments, Assignment Spread, Parallel Assignments, Course Assignments, Graded Course Assignments, Graded Course Assignments per Week, Most Graded Course Assigments per Week, Submission Time to Deadline, early Assignment Availability Ratio, early Assignment Availability Time, Dropout Ratio Q1, Q2, Q3, 4, Satisfied Prerequisites, Student Course STEM Match, Course credit Hours, Course GPA Spring 2021, GPA Standard Deviation for Course, Course was STEM, Number of Course Pre-Requisites, % Pass and Satisfactory among Non-Letter Grades, Course had No Letter Grades, No LMS Assignments, No LMS Assignments with Due, No LMS Assignments with Due + Unlock, No LMS Forum, No LMS Submissions, Multiple LMS Courses Concatenated]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Data is collected from all sources and aggregated. Final dataset has each row refer to a learner-course pairing. 

Analysis starts with the creation of different linear models with different feature sets:

Individual Student Features from Enrollment, Course and Pre-requisite based features, Features based on prior student GPA, Feautres based on prior course GPA.

Dropout status of students was included as feature in a model.

Assignments were filtered in order make asure all assignments considered had, at least, one submission. Authors also created a set of control variables.

STEM status for all courses was encoded manually.

Variables whose Variance Inflation Factor was below 10 were excluded.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {R-Squared: 0.45 - LMS + Individual Features}
extracted.Cluster Description: NA
extracted.Implications:
Authors argue that ECTS are a bad predictor of the effort put by students into their studies. Instead, the authors argue that other indicators, such as the number of deliverables in the course should be used:

The more deliverables, the more the perceived effort the studuent needs to put into the course.

Time load is a determinant for elective course selection and should be treated accordingly.
extracted.Comments:
LMS features re collected at the course level.

3 Mixed Linear models were created:

- Time load, 
- Mental Effort,
- Psychological Stress

The authors show that the models containing these feature sets far surpass the expectations of effort students have about course load relative to ECTS.
extracted.Limitations:
Limited access to other likely predictors of course load such as lecture materials or Zoom calls.

Course load evaluations were done post-hoc, which may not be an accurate representation → peak-end rule.

Need to validate in additional cultural contexts. 

Sample was collected during Covid era - whuch may not be representative of workload in face-to-face contexts.

annotations.text: This study measures the degree to which student perception of the time they need to spend on a course actually maps to the credit hours expected for a specific course. Authors compare whether credit hours explain student effort (don’t seem to), as the number of deliverables student need to do actually is a much better predictor. Authors argue for the adoption of a new construct of course load than credit hours.

annotations.text: LMS and Learning Analytics have made it possible to pinpoint and identify potentially dilatory behavior from learners such as procrastination. Authors attempt to use ML to predict procrastinatory behavior by comparing 2 different feature sets:

Models using Logs consistently outperform models self-reported measures, with a combination of both actually achieving marginally better results (Accuracy around 70%). Depending on feature set, the best alorithm also differed, putting emphasis into performing model selection when using these machine learning approaches.

references.title: Beyond Predictive Learning Analytics Modelling and onto Explainable Artificial Intelligence with Prescriptive Analytics and ChatGPT
references.journal: International Journal of Artificial Intelligence in Education

extracted.Courses: All courses in an Australian University
extracted.Data sources: Admission Data, Academic History, Course Descriptions
extracted.Features: [Basics For Admission Description, Has Previous Tertiary Studies, Higherst Schoool Qualification Description, Current Full Time Status, Current Student Mode Numeric, Current Prior Activity Description, Age Description, Gender, Grade MArk Mean, Grade Mark Max, Grade Mark Deviation From Class Mean, Papers Failed for Student Academic Year, Online Learning Has Passed Assessment Count Z-Score, Total Qualification Percent Completed, Online Learning Submitted Assignments Z-Score, Papers Withdrawn For Student Academic Year, Online LEarning Pages Viewed Count Z-Score, Online Learning Quiz Taken Count Zcore, Online Learning Forum Post Created Count Zscore, Online Learning On Time Submission Count Zscore, Programme Title Programme Credits Required]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Students were represented per year. In essence, this means that the same student who completed a 3-year program would feature 3 times in the dataset, always with the flag completed.

Features can be summarized by 4 overarching categories:

Academic performance , learning behavbiors, immuatable learner characteristics, Program Characteristics.

Instead of working with absolute counts, authors opted to relativize their variables among their co-hort. Data was standardized. 

Missing Values were filled using 0. Categorical Variables were subjected to Binary Encoding.
extracted.Models: CatBoost, Random Forest, SVM, Logistic Regression, DT, GBoost, kNN, Naive Bayes, Random Guessing, ZeroR
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {F1-Score: 0.945 - CatBoost, Accuracy: 0.92 - CatBoost, AUC: 0.883 - CatBoost, Recall: 1 - ZeroR, Precision: 0.925 - CatBoost}
extracted.Comments:
Peescriptive Modelling part shows only hypothetical scenarios. Predictive modelling follows a formative and a summative perspective.

During th formative perspective, the models evaluate outcomes for specific thresholds of course completion. On the Summative Perspective, the authors attempt to predict end-of-program completion.

Hyper-Parameter Tuning for Candidate Models was executed via a Random Search. Feature interepretability was done using SHAP.

This work is positive in the sense that it provides a picture into what can be done after predicting learner success.
extracted.Limitations:
Lacks deployment of Prescrictive Model Part.

Only results from the summative approach - predicting at the end of the program - are shown.

Imbalance between classes is not addressed meaningfully.

Feature set considered is limited to Actionable Features, which hinders the rpedictive performance of the models.

annotations.text: Most of the work in Learning Analytics focuses on making predictions and not as much in interpreting the causes that lead to a certain prediction, as Explainable AI has only recently become a thing. The work proposes a framework that combines XAI and prescriptive analytics, with the added caveat of having a LLM communicate insights to learners.
The authors use a dataset of around 7000 students ranging from 2018 and 2022 meant to identify learners at risk of qualifying in their program.

38 students for “testing” purposes

extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Logs were categorized into different SRL constructs which the authors called Observed SRL Tags. 

MSLQ answers are clustered using Hierarchical Clustering.

LMS logs were filtered in order to only account for the ones relating with the assignment and the logs were aggregated into sessions.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Weighted Cohen’s Kappa: 0.74 - Agreement between self-report and SRL tags}
extracted.Cluster Description:
Cluster 1: High SRL learners, 

Cluster 2: Moderate SRL learners

extracted.Implications: Being able to observed students’ SRL behaviors in the context of an LMS could be pivotal towards developing better assignments and learning activity structures.
extracted.Comments:
Authors evaluate self-report measures and how they map with Log behavior in the context of one/more than one assignment.

After tagging behavior to different SRL dimensions, the authors check the agreement between what was observed behavior AND Self-Reported Behavior.

Results show that it is possible to achieve a level of partial agreement based on sequences.
extracted.Limitations:
Self-selection bias-participantes were rewarded with participation.

LMS are a partial and incomplete picture of learner behavior.

annotations.text: Authors use instructional design to create SRL measures that are capturable from a LMS. Authors valdiate their approach on 2 distinct learning activities and compare how well their SRL measures actually map to SRL constructs obtainable from Self-Reports. Results show relatively high agreement between the authors methods’, but sometimes it does seem to generate a bias in the results.

annotations.text: Work uses EEG to record physiological information from learners to predict SRL traits during online learning. The authors also propose a form of providing feedback to train online learner brains to improve their SRL skills.

references.title: GIKT: A Graph-based Interaction Model for Knowledge Tracing

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Probability of getting the next candidate exercise correct
extracted.Students: 3852 ASSISTments09 learners, 27485 ASSISTments12 learners nad 5000 EdNet learners
extracted.Features: [Question, Skill and Exercise Embeddings]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Previous models often compute the learners’ current state as a weighted sum aggregation of all historical states based on correlation between current question and historical questions → all previous knowledge states are considered.
extracted.Novelty of Model: The model GIKT uses attention to select which previous states should be considered for the computation of new question performance. Moreover, it is, to the authors’ knowledge to evaluate question-skills relationships via Graph Neural Networks.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The model creates 3 embedding matrices that represent questions, skills and answers.

Question-skill relationships are modelled using a Knowledge Graph. 

The model incorporates the concept of mastery of a question by the student. This mastery is determined from the history of interactions with similar questions.
extracted.Models: BKT, KTM, DKT, DKVMN, DKT-Q, DKT-QS, GAKT
extracted.Comments: Authors propose a new model and validate it using 3 datasets. The authors perform a set of Ablation studies to show how parameter optimization impacts the performance of the models.
extracted.Limitations:
No testing on Cold-Start Scenario.

No Cross-Validation.

annotations.text: KT in online settings has some differences to other contexts, as online questions are often associated with a smaller set of skills but tend to be more and more common. Thus, KT works in online settings could explore problems that sparse dataset cannot - such as question-skill correlations.

To solve this limitation, authors propose a Graph-based Interaction model for KT. The model uses Graph Convolutional NNs to include question-skill correlations. Essencially, the model can identify the key questions for skill mastery and consider how it evolves from the moment the key questions were done to new questions related with this skill. Results show that GIKT achieves new State-of-the-art performance.

references.title: Lessons Learned from Creating, Implementing and Evaluating Assisted E-Learning Incorporating Adaptivity, Recommendations and Learning Analytics
Lessons Learned from Creating, Implementing and Evaluating Assisted E-Learning Incorporating Adaptivity, Recommendations and Learning Analytics

annotations.text: Implementing different learning analytics systems is often described in isolation, but often, the combined systems that can be integrated are not discussed holistically. The authors describe their Experience API in Learning Tools Interoperability. The authors discuss challenges regarding multiple aspects, such as organization, methodology, content didactics and technology.

Paper presents an overall framework that meant to combine different learning analytics activities in a single framework. The authors focus on inter-operability standards and discuss different challenges relating with application and inter-operability.

annotations.text: Learning analytics to perform early identification of students in need is underexplored in medicine. The study looks at online activites from mediical students and checks whether they correlate with performance.

The authors extracted logs from 133 students and extract general counts of activities and 5 indicators. The authors were able to predict the final grade with 65% accuracy and a recall of 54%. Predicting at the course’s halfway point, accuracy was actually 42%.

references.title: Open-Ended Knowledge Tracing

extracted.Features: [Student Knowledge State]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Most KT models only analyze and predict binary-valued learner responses to questions → making it difficult to use for open-ended questions.
extracted.Novelty of Model: OKT presents a framework that is able to model and predict knowledge from open-ended questions.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The model creates a continuous embedding representation to encodde the open-ended answers. 

Knowledge updates follow the same logic as other KT models.

Student is represented, for each timestep, as a triple: question-answer-correctedness.

Embedding of questions is done by GPT-2 and answers are of ASTNN.

The knowledge state of the student is updated by a LSTM AND is the input of the code generation.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {CodeBLEU: 0.565 - OKT-AKT, Dist-1: 0.396 - OKT-AKT, Test Loss: 0.133 - OKT-AKT}
extracted.Comments: OKT does not measure wether the answer is correct or not. It predicts the learners’ open ended answer → which could later be tested for correctedness. Since this is not a common task in KT, ther are no baselines to compare against. To address that the authors test multiple variants of OKT: using other frameworks for knowledge update.
extracted.Limitations:
Does not address the standard KT task of predicting correctedness.

Limited number of explored Generative Moddels for Code Generation.

Small dataset.

annotations.text: One of the problems with KT is that they treat answers as correct/incorrect. While valuable, this information is somewhat incomplete and likely could be complemented if questions were “open-ended,” - especially when the goal is to create a model of learner’s knowledge.
The paper presents open-ended Knowledge Tracing. They lay out the specifications and evaluation metrics and conduct experiments to test the open-ended knowledge tracing on a real world dataset.

references.title: Incorporating Rich Features into Deep Knowledge Tracing

extracted.Features: [Exercise Tag, Correctedness, First Response Time, Attempt Count, First Action]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Previous DKT models only consider Question/Skill and correctedness as individual features.
extracted.Novelty of Model: Authors look to improve DKT by adding additional feature sets and an Undercomplete Auto-Encoder to reduce dimensionality and improve training speed.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Features are represented as a one-hot encoded sparse vector.
extracted.Comments:
Authors add multiple differetn types of feature sets to traditional DKT.

Results from CV show that adding cross-features of skill/correctedness andd time/correctness tend to lead to better performance (both in terms of AUC and R2).

The presence of the Auto-Encoder also improves the performance of the model.
extracted.Limitations:
No model optimization attempts. 

Only focuses on DKT while not considering other viable approaches (at the time- e.g. BKT).

annotations.text: CFA is an important reearch interest, especially in the context of ITS, generating models such as BKT, PFA and DKT (which uses RNN). The problem with the DKT apporach is that it considers only a subset of features related with the knowledge components of a problem.

Thus, the authors’ proposition is 2-fold. First they want to add more features to the problem level. Second, they introduce an Auto-Encoder to reduce training time and resource requirements. Results show that new model obtains higher accuracies than the regular DKT approach.

annotations.text: The paper looks at data from 772 students and tries to predict student performance at the end of the semester using video learning analytics and ML. The authors use data from SIS, LMS and mobile applications, use PCA and Genetic Search for feature selection and compare 8 distinct algorithms. RF achieved 88.3% accuracy and outperformed all other algorithms.

43 highschool students in Dataset 1,

501 students of undisclosed level for Dataset 2
extracted.Courses:
5 different courses in dataset 1 

8 Courses in dataset 2

Student Grades in Different Tests

Student Information

Authors use two datasets with different feature sets.

CAISP is a model that works in 3 stages:

annotations.text: Student performance prediction is used to leverage existing information about students to predict future learning performance. Authors follow a predictive approach to predict course grades. Most studies look into different aspects of learning but fail to account for how different factors (features) affect different learners.

Authors propose Complexity-based Attentive Interactive Student Performance (CAISP). The model uses attention networks. When tested on 2 real-word, datasets, CAISP outperformed two state-of-the-art solutions.

references.title: What is wrong with deep knowledge tracing? Attention-based knowledge tracing

extracted.Students: 4417 ASSISTments09 learners, 19917 ASSISTments15 learners, 686 ASSISTmentsChall learners, 333 Statics learners, 2000 Simulated learners
extracted.Features: [Concatenated Learner Embeddings]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Sequential models, such as DKT, while effective, often fail to capture longer-term dependencies → the larger the input sequence, the more noisy the model will be.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
Authors employ multi-head attention which was shown to be better for modelling longer-term dependencies in sequences. 

However, the problem of attention is that the sequential information is somewhat lost, which is the reason why the authors propose KTA-LSTM.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors do dataset specific models. Weight initialization comes from Xavier iniatialization.
extracted.Models: BKT, PFA, DKT, DKT+, KTA, KTA_LSTM, SAKT, AKT, RKT, DKVMN
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose 2 KT models using Attention mechanisms.

The model using Attention and LSTMs outperforms the remaining baselines on most datasets except the synthetic one.

The evaluation is done at 2 levels: First comparison of KTA with other models, which KTA_LSTM outperforms the remaining models.

Secondly, the authors make a comparison of these models against other Attention-Based Models - AKT, SAKT, RKT, DKVMN, which are also outperformed by KTA.

Finally, the authors do an Ablation study to see the effects of hyper-parameter tuning on model performance.
extracted.Limitations: Does not consider behavioral data.

annotations.text: DKT, while effective, is a much more data-driven approach than a knowledge-driven approach. However, its results tend to fluctuate due to not requiring elaborate representations of student knowledge. The authors find that DKT cannot properly establish long-term relationships and, to circumvent this issue, introduce a set of attention-based models. Benchmarking on 5 datasets shows that both models achieve state-of-the-art performance on KT tasks.

references.title: Unveiling the Differences in Early Performance Prediction Between Online Social Sciences and STEM Courses Using Educational Data Mining

extracted.Features: Sum of Clicks per activity type.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Each activity type was aggregated from the start to current week (in this case, there are 20 activity types, so each model has 20 features representing the number of clicks in each activity.

Data was oversampled using SMOTE and Standardized (I’m guessing this is what StandardNormalization.
extracted.Models:
DT
Logistic Regression
GaussianNB
KNN
LDA
SVM
AdaBoost
Bagging
ExtraTrees
Random Forest
XGBoost
MLP
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.79 - Random Forest & ExtraTrees, Mean Rank: 10.04 - Random Forest}
extracted.Comments:
In total, the authors created 10824 models, one per course iteration and Week.

Authors publish results in terms of Accuracy, Mean Rank  and Friedman’s test. Most of the results shown are averaged out over the full period or aggregated by some week thresholds (Week 10, Week 20, Week 30, Week 40).

At a second level of analysis, the authors look to distinguish differences in Predictive capabilities between STEM and Social Science Courses and find that the models in STEM achieve better scores than the ones for SS. 

Recommendation of authors is to perform early prediction for STEM at Week 10 and for SS at week 20.
extracted.Limitations:
Each model is course-specific, making it impractical for implementation.

No analysis of casuses for difference in performance between STEM and SS. 

Accuracy is the only model performance metric considered.

annotations.text: Using learner logs to create early-warning systems is, while useful conceptually, highly dependent on knowledge-domain (because different disciplines tend to require different forms of engagement which, in turn, often leads to differences in interaction patterns).
Authors focus on creating a EWS for Social Sciences and STEMa and use data from 32593 students (seems to be OULAD). Results show dedcent accuracies starting at the 10th week.

annotations.text: Around 11% of EU young adults end up droppoing out of tertiary-education, thus prediting early should be regarded as a priority. Deep learning models, powered by CNNs have been shown to contribute significantly to more accurate models. The study in question takes administrtative data from 6000 students and results show an accuracy of 67% in dropout for first-year students and 94% for third-year students.

annotations.text: ML pipelines are essential to predict students in need and make necessary recommendations often using behavioral logs or demographic data stored in the SIS. This strategy is vulnerable to concept drift or spuriousness.

Authors show that Lasso outperforms multiple models in both scenarios. Moreover, a linear regression with 2 new features (handcrafted) outperforms multiple more complex ML models. Authors propose their results as an interesting benchmark for future studies.

references.title: Dynamics of self-regulated learning: The effectiveness of students’ strategies across course periods

extracted.Courses: 41 courses at a Dutch STEM university
extracted.Features: [Announcement, Discussions, Assignment, Submission, Learning Materials, Mandatory Quiz, Optional Quiz, Metacognition andd Course Browsing]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors aggregated different types of activities in the 9 categories described as the features.

Moreover, the authors defined learning sessions, considering that two clicks were in the same session if they were within 12.1 minutes and cannot have more than 111 clicks/learning events.

Data was filtered to remove outliers,  leading to 5110 students in 34 sessions.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model:
Log-lokelihood,
Bayesian Information Criterion,
Akaike Information Criterion
extracted.Cluster Description:
Assignment-Focused tactic: Most common tactic. Dominated by Assignment Pages and Mandatory Quizzes

Material-Focused tactic:  More than half of the clicks are on materials. Second most common tactic as well.

Mixed Tactic: More diverse set of actions with no clear dominant action.

Quizz-Focused Tactic: Least common tactic in which students opt to do mandatory quizzes or optional quizzes.
extracted.Implications:
The different learning tactics are the backbone of the learning strategies covered layer. More than just characterization of the clusters over the features, authors also describe clusters in terms of most likely transitions in actions.

For example, the Mixed Tactic revels itself to be the most common way of a student starting a course.
extracted.Comments:
Selection of Groups number was done via the Expectation-Maximization Algorithm.

The first level of clustering was for learning tactics, the second for learning strategies.
extracted.Limitations:
Survivorship bias (only learners who were present over the 2 years were considered).

LMS are a partial and incomplete picture of learner behavior.

No specifics on the internal conditions of each course.

extracted.Courses: 41 courses at a Dutch STEM university
extracted.Features: [Quizz-Focused Tactic, Mixed Tactic, Material-Focused Tactic, Assignment-Focused Tactic]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors obtain the clusters of learning tactics from the Logs using EM and then use the model outputs as the features for the second clustering algorithm of learning strategies.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: “Best fit”
extracted.Cluster Description:
Balanced Strategy: No dominant tactic, as all are employed relatively often. 

Resource-Oriented: Most of the actions are accessing resources. This learning strategy is the second largest and is ranked thrid in terms of number of sessions.

Grade-Oriented: Most of the time is spent on grade menus (largest cluster). 

Flexible: Adaptation to different courses, buyt not equal “balanced” engagement in all learning tactics.
extracted.Implications:
Different strategies suggest different levels of engagement with Surfave vs Deep approaches to learning. 

Temporal level of granularity of analysis also impacts the analysis of transitions between strategies.

All strategies are found to have a significantly different impact on grade as showcased by a Kruskall-Wallis test. 

Results also show that being consistent in terms of learning strategy ois more effective than changing strategy midway.
extracted.Comments:
Authors also look at how learners change their behavior (strategy) during a course - often considering both at a half or at a quarter.

End-result: Adopting a consistent deep-level approach to learning is the best option to succeed. However, transitioning between strategies is better than consistently adopting a surface-level approach to learning.
extracted.Limitations:
Survivorship bias (only learners who were present over the 2 years were considered).

LMS are a partial and incomplete picture of learner behavior.

No specifics on the internal conditions of each course.

annotations.text: Logs are a good form of detecting Learning Strategies, but not only that, transitions between log behaviors are also helpful. Thus, the authors use a dataset of 41 courses to see how in-course behavior changes affect performance. Authors use Markov Models to examine behavioral shifts (changes in strategy).

Results show that it is possible to identify clear clusters with measurable impacts on student performance. Surface approaches tend to underperform deep approaches over time. Secondly, more interestingly, being consistent with one approach is better than being irregular.

annotations.text: Around 30% of students that are registered in the first year do not return for the second. The authors take a step to understand why by analyzing data from the University of Washington. Authors find that it is possible to identify early predictors and, not only that, that it is also possible to predict dropout when a single term of academic data (end of first-semester equivalent).

references.title: The impact of social capital on self-efficacy and study success among first-year university students

extracted.Courses: All courses in the Behavioral and Social Sciences Department of the University of Groningen
extracted.Data sources:

extracted.Features: [First-Year Student, Moving Out, Financial Support, Mentor Support, , Peer Support, Friends, Help/Advice Seeking, Collaboration, Self-Efficacy, Weighted Average Mark for Fall Semester,  Weighted Average Mark for Spring Semester, Weighted Average Mark for First Year}
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors employed Path Analysis to explore the best way in which to fill missing values. 

In addition, the authors measure correlation between the different predictors.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: Model was found to be statistically significant to describe student performance.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Low Achievers

Average Achievers

High achievers: Having friends shows a positive relationship with success.
extracted.Implications:
Peer and Faculty capital contribute to explaining student success.

Family support was not shown to be relevant at this level, likely because students without family support often fail at reaching the undergraduate level. 

Being in a small group with similar peers does not seem to be benefical for either loiw or average attainment students.

First semester success is highly indicative of later success, which means that using this as an early-warning system may be relevant.

Authors argue for the buiding of fostering development of social connections between different levels of achievers in a way that helps low achievers find the social capital that helps them perform.
extracted.Comments:
Authors do the separation at the level of the dependent variablein 3 categories.

Effect of social circles and having friends was remarkably higher for high-achieving students → suggesting that social interactions are more benefical in this context than they are for lower achieving students.
extracted.Limitations:
Study focuses only on access to social capital via number of friends and not social networks themselves.

Use of self-reports to measure support without any direct behavioral data.

Homogenous sample of Behavioral and Social Sciences students.

annotations.text: Authors touch on the concept of Social Capital as a driver that fosters academic performance. They explore how social capital influences performance in first-year undergraduates. Authors use path analysis to evaluate how different forms of social capital influence performance.
Results show that peer capital and faculty capital contribute positively to study success. Moreover, high-achievers have a positive influence in low-achievers if they are friends.

annotations.text: Dropout prediction is a very popular learning analytics activity - after all, detecting students who are less likely to complete their programs is essential to prevent churn. The work uses data from first-year students attending a European University at two different moments of prediction: the beginning of the course or the end of the first semester.

Different classifiers were used, and predictions at the end of the first semester outperformed the beginning of the course. Moreover, Logistic Regression was good enough as a baseline, but was outperformed by other ML techniques.

references.title: Predicting University Students’ Academic Success and Major Using Random Forests

extracted.Courses: All courses at University of Toronto
extracted.Features: [StudentID, Number of Credits attempted- Divided by Department and average grade across Department ]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Each dataset has 142 features since there are 71 Departments at University of Toronto.

Target variable is computed according to the rule: Registered for 5 credits, did not succeed in at least 18 and did not take a course in 3 semesters were defined as dropouts. 

Authors also perform a feature importance analysis via Permutation Descrease Importance.

Permutation is preferred since it does not show to be a reliable indicator of variable importance in different types of predictors.

Authors fiund RF to be the best model even though they attempt 3 forms of RF.
extracted.Limitations:
Use of Grades to predict success. 

Does not consider option of a student being in multiple majors or specializations. 

Inneficient addressing of Nans.

Holdout method is split in 90%-5%-5%, which is unorthodox.

annotations.text: The authors take 10 years worth of data from a Chinese university and create 2 Random Forest models. The first model is meant to predict end-of-program success just from using data from the first 2 semesters. The second RF is used for interpretation purposes as it is used to understand in which courses the successful students first registered.

Authors create 6 SVR models (one after eaxh moment of evaluation).

Authors examine two modelling frameworks. The first is sequencial: M1 refers to Assignment 1 and all subsequent models have the addition of the next assignment.

In framework 2, the authors start with Exam grade and start adding each assignment grade).

Authors do feature interpretation using LIME and SHAP and find that Exam is, unsurprisingly, the most important predictor of the final grade.

Early prediction is possible as early as Week 2.
extracted.Limitations:
Any model past Model 6 cannot be used for eacly prediction since it uses all elements of evaluation that are factored in to compute the final grade.

Model optimization strategy and computyational cost of the techniques selected.

Needs additional predictors from other sources and generalization across institutions.

annotations.text: Authors use 5 assignments and one exam mark as predictors to overall scores of students. The novelty factors from their combination of Tree-Structured Parzen Estimation and SVR. Authors evaluate their approach against other models on a sample of 492 students taking a general science course. Models are also interpreted using LIME and SHapley.

Results show that their model outperforms all others used, achieving the lowest RMSE, RRMSE, highest Wilmott’s index, Legates-McGabe index (LM) and Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency. LIME also shows that exam grade is the most important factor.

In terms of interpretability, authors identify that students who achieve higher test scores are more likely to continue with their studies and not dropout.
