Model results showcase that using Logs does not fully capture all learning activities, as learning activity and regularity explain very little differences in final grade. 

Same pedagogical model (PBL in this case) may not be portable across different couses. 

Learner characteristics (Internal conditions) explain most of the variance in performance rather the external conditions being considered (couyrse random effect and logs).
extracted.Comments:
A lot of the curriculum relies on Problem-Based Learning Approaches.

There is a conscious effort to only include rpedictors obtainable from the LMS.

Authors create multiple mixed effect linear models on differente feature sets to select the most important predictors for a final model. User and Course were random effect.

LALA features marginally get better results on Considtional RSquared but, overall, the feature sets seem relatively equivalent as performance does not vary significantly.


Only a very small portion of the variance (3% to 5%) in the final grade could be explained by the model.
extracted.Limitations:
Homogenous set of courses (PBL).

Would benefit from additional data sources than just LMS alone.

Data only captures a portion of the PBL learning process.

annotations.text: Early work on early warning systems focused on course-agnostic and portable models. However, with time, the flaws in this approach have surfaced more and more. This work attempts to do early prediction on a subset of similar courses while simultaneously considering differences between the courses considered. This iis done using mixed effect linear regression models.

Authors look to explore how different predictors explain differences in final grades and, more specifically, what is the role of the courses internal and external conditions. Results show that behaviour on the LMS explains a small portion of of variance and learner’s internal conditions account for the most part.

references.title: A Machine Learning Approach for Tracking and  Predicting Student Performance in Degree Programs
references.journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Signal Processing

extracted.Courses: 811 courses (core and elective), focus on core courses from Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering at UCLA
extracted.Features: [Pre-college academic performance, course and grades obtained in each quarter in the program (using course clustering approach and excluding summer)]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors perform course-clustering to identify what are the most relevant courses for each student. 

More than pre-processing, learning is done in 2-ways: 

First, a set of base classifiers is trained offline, while a second tier of ensemble models follows online learning.

For a new student, the model makes a prediction for each course he/she is likely going to take and uses these as features to rpedict GPA.
extracted.Models:
Ensemble-Based Progressive Prediction,
Linear Regression, 
Logistic Regression,
Random Forest,
KNN
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Mean-Squared Error: EPP - All Benchmarks}
extracted.Comments:
The authors introduce a bilayer predictive model combining: (1) base predictors trained on course clusters and (2) a progressive ensemble layer that incorporates predictions over time. This allows  GPA prediction at each term. 

The key innovation is the sequential ensemble that adapts using past and present predictions, making the model scalable and interpretable. 

Course relevance is discovered via probabilistic matrix factorization, grouping similar courses to reduce noise and dimensionality. This outperforms traditional groupings (like prerequisites or same-department only). 

The ensemble model outperforms all individual base predictors across terms, with MSE decreasing as student history grows. 

Insights include: (a) SAT Math is more predictive than HS GPA; (b) early prediction is feasible but less accurate; (c) course clusters can guide curriculum and advising.
extracted.Limitations: Predictions limited to core courses only. Elective courses and course recommendations are not included. Performance generalization to other programs not tested. Data is from a single department and institution.

annotations.text: Early warning systems allow the development of interventions. Most studies focus on course-level, but end-of-program performance is often not as studied and has its own challenges:
The authors propose an ML method that predicts end of program success by addressing the previous challenges. It works in 2 ways:
Simulations on undergraduate data show that model outperforms the benchmark approaches.

Authors use a set of classification methods to identify the profiles of students who are more likely to dropout.

references.title: Knowledge tracing: Modeling the acquisition of procedural knowledge

annotations.text: KT is a method that aims to model students’ ever-evolving knowledge during skill acquisiton. For that, the authors empirically evaluate tyheir hypothetical model using a tool called APT. The model estimates the probability of the student having learned each of the rules necessary for the ideal model, which allows tutors to present exercises that foster the mastery of specific rules.
The study is followed by a series of evaluations to validate that the model works. Authors show that model is, at this stage, quite good at predicting test performance.

annotations.text: Most of the previous KT models used have shown inconsistent results. They argue that, in large part, these inconsistencies come from dataset size. Thus, authors explore how enemble methods could perform on a large dataset. They compare the performance of 8 student models and 8 ensembles and see that the best ensemble outperforms the single models by close to 10 percentage points.

references.title: A three learning states Bayesian knowledge tracing model

extracted.Students:
912 students - Assistments Math 2004-2005

3136 students - Assistments Math 2005-2006

5046 students -  e Assistments
Math 2006–2007
extracted.Features: [D-KC, G-KC, M-KC, N-KC, P-KC]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: BKT is based on Hiden Markov Models, where the observations are the answers to previous questions (correct or incorrect).  This bi-partition is not always clear.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
The authors introduce a tripartite of representing each element of the BKT sequence:

Unlearned State/Learning State/Learned State
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Students with fewer than 100 learning processes were excluded.

Each learner is usually represented by the sequence of exercises within a knowledge component - a binary of learned state and unlearned state.

Authors simulate the bipartite vs the tripartite data representation using synthetic data - with results being clearly better for TPS.

Each learner is ultimately represented by the sequences in each of the 5 considered knowledge components.
extracted.Comments:
Before going into the real datasets, the authors test the performance of both traditional BKT and TLS-BKT on random subsets of the datasets.

One model is created for each individual knowledge component.

Here, they consider multiple metrics beyond the AUC score: Distinction, Accuracy, RMSE and Standard Deviation.

TLS-BKT outperforms BKT on every benchmark test.

Need to validate, but it seems that there is one model per knowledge component, which would make such a model very difficult to create in practice.
extracted.Limitations:
Lacks generalization to environments outside Assistments Math.

Evaluation on the full real data does not consider all metrics used for the randomly selected data data.

annotations.text: Traditional BKT has two-learning states and the authors propose a third learning state. This means that they define the learning process into 3 sections. This small change to BKT shows superior results in comparison with BKT both in quantitative and qualitative terms.
Representation of the bipartitate state of the sequences vs the tri-partite state.
Model Performance (AUC) on the 3 datasets

references.title: Deep Knowledge Tracing

extracted.Features: Sequence of Answers
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
BKT is based on Hiden Markov Models, where the observations are the answers to previous questions (correct or incorrect).  

This not only assumes that, once learned, a skill is not forgotten, but the binary representation is also flawed.

Most exercises do not require knowledge of a single concept, something BKT assumes.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
RNNs use a continuous latent space, which (as in the case of LSTM) allows the proper modelling of longer sequences. 

DKT allows the modelling of relationships between exercises
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Every pair of exercises is assigned an influence factor which shows the probability of getting exercise j correct if they alkso got exercise i as correct.

In small feature spaces, a learner is represented by the OHE sequence of Correct/incorrect answers.

In larger feature sets, the authors assign a random vector to each input tuple. This creates a lower dimensional projection that mantains the representation in a lower dimensionality space.
extracted.Models:
Marginal
BKT
Optimized BKT
Deep Knowledge Tracing
extracted.Comments:
Authors attempt the deploymnt of DKT as Agnostic on knowledge components or “siloed” - sequences of answers on same topic.

RNNs hav been shown to outperform other aprproaches in many application, so authors adapt LSTM to KT under the name BKT. 

Tests on multiple Benchmark datasets show that DKT outperforms other popular KT models.
extracted.Limitations: RNNs require a lot more data than an Hidden Markov Model (such as BKT). Thus DKT should be more suitable for online environments and not as much classroom environments.

annotations.text: KT is meant to model student knowledge as the student interacts with a computer environment. The paper uses RNN to perform the task, as they do not require so much feature extraction. Usage of these models greatly improved performance on multiple datasets.

extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors removed grades from students who have de-registered from a course. To address the class imbalance between success and insuccess, authors made 2 copies of every observation that registered a negative mark on a course. 

Course interestes were extracted by creatinfg vector embeddings of the course descriptions in courses in which a student registered in.

Course description embeddings were created using BERT. Similarity between students was computed using cosine similarity. Similarity between students when it comes to course performance was computed using Adjusted Euclidean distance,

Data was normalizesd.

Authors introduce a new method that can be a percursor of an elective course recommender system. 

The method takes the history of students that have attended the course and take the course descripions (embedded using BERT) as a proxy for learner interests.

Authors use kNN as the algorithm to compute the expected grade on the elective course based on the current student’s history and their interests based on the the history of courses completed by the student.

Authors also include a fallback for first-semester students (cold-start scenario).
extracted.Limitations:
- Oversampling technique selected is fairy simple compared to other alternatives (e.g. SMOTE).

- Interestes are a proxy of courses taken by the students.

- Only kNN is used as a predictive algotrithm.

Only tested in a MSc dataset.

annotations.text: Paper uses NLP to predict the performance of students at the end of a course. The model uses historical enrollment data and course descriptions to predict the potential grade of a student in a course. The authors use BERT to analyse the text and create course description embeddings.

Moreover, the author creates a student knowledge profile that is based on the descriptions of the courses the student has already attended AND the student’s respective performance on that course. The approach had soime promise but still has plenty of room for improvement according to the authors.

annotations.text: The paper introduces Data Mining of forum-related data at a specific course of Computer Science. Authors focus on comparing multiple variations:

Classifiers are compared against other classifiers, some unsupervised techniques are also tested. Authors find that it is possible to use Clustering and Association Rule Mining to extract specific learning pathways. Moreover, making a selection of features also seems to contribute to greater predictive accuracy.

Discussion forums arte among the most widely used web-based tools to promote collaboration and learning among students. Educators could hopefully analyze the forum behaviors and contributions to make an assessment of a learner’s behavior and/or learning. Data Mining techniques may be useful in assisting make sense of this data →  something which is naturally very difficult to do manually.
The paper uses data from a first-year course in Computer Science, more specifically discussion forum usage patterns to predict success. The goal is to find:

The task of predicting student performance is extremely complex and multi-faceted. Nowadays there are many possible targets to use and a multitude of possible predictors from many sources. The rise of EDM has been, in great part, fostered by the proliferation of LMS which themselves are a great source of educational data. One of the most important LMS activities is online discussion forums.

The literature features some distinct approaches when it comes to analyzing the relationship between online forums and student performance. Specifically, there are approaches that merely focus on usage patterns → the amount of times or frequency in which the resource is used. There are also approaches that focus on the content itself. Finally, there are approachs that are based on Social Networks formed in the forum.

The authors start with collection of data from the forums at 2 distinct moments: End of the Course and Halfway Point of the course. This is followed by a standard Data Mining Framework that involves instance and attribute selection. Finally, the authors use the processed data to create models to predict student performance with both Supervised and Unsupervised methods.

The data removed belongs to 3 perspectives: Quantitative (for usage patterns), Qualitative (a score of content messages), and Social (Social Network Analysis) data. Data passes through an instance/attribute selection process .
Instance Selection - Use all messages or exclude the invalid messages

Attribute Selection - Ranking approach based on different variable selection algorithms - these start with a Pearson correlation index to measure redundancy of attributes. They are followed by a voting process of multiple feature selection algorithms provided by WeKa.

When it comes to the algorithms, the authors make reference to the fact that interpretability is an important element of predictive algorithms. This interpretability is something shared by decision trees, rules-based classifiers and centroid-based unsupervised techniques such as k-means. Association rule mining also has potential.
Datasets used:

Among the Unsupervised methods, EM was the best algorithm. Also unsupervised methods seem to perform on par with supervised methods when using data for the entire course. Interestingly the authors make 1 to 1 comparisons between EM and Some of the supervised algorithms SMO and Naive Bayes.

The second question involves knowing the predictors. The authors use a Wilcoxon Signed Rank test to compare the models running all features vs running a subset of the features. The results show that, in some circumstances, running models with Messages, Words, AvgScoreMsg, centrality and Prestige, outperforms all features in a statistically significant way. For others, these differences were not substantial enough.
Finally, early prediction at the halfway point of the course seems possible as the models used outperform other attempts at early prediction in the literature.

references.title: Dynamic Key-Value Memory Networks for Knowledge Tracing
references.journal: Proceedings of the 26th International Conference on World Wide Web

extracted.Students:
4000 Synthetic learners, 4151 ASSISTments2009 learners, 19840 ASSISTments2012 learners,
333 Statics learners
extracted.Features: [Sequence of Questions]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
BKT and DKT (the at the time state-of-the-art) have problems with the way they represent knowledge concepts:

- BKT treats each knowledge concept as its own dimension and they are modelled separately,

- DKT aggregates all KC into a single, not interpretable hidden vector
extracted.Novelty of Model:
Memory augmented Neural Networks are a foprm of enural networks with 2 modules:

1. A memory matrix that sotre information
2. A Controller module that reads and/or writes oin the matrix → changing it.

MANN can be adopted for KT as it represents a theoretical improvement to LSTMs, but still has an issue with the fact that reading and writing is done on the same matrix.

DKVMN addresses this by using 2 matrices: a static one that encodes information about questions and a second dynamic one that encodes knowledge on different knowledge components.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Each dataset is treated separately and, in each, each question is associated with a knowledge concept apriori.

Input exercise data is provided as a one-hot encoded vector.

Key is updated during training but becomes fixed for the testing process.

For the value, the starting point is the difficulty of each concept.
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a new model and test it on 4 different datasets. 

Authors do some hyper-parameter tuning to identify what would be the ideal combination of hyper-parameters.
extracted.Limitations:
Does not perform cross-Validation.

Only takes into consideration answer sequences, leaving aside any representation of content or any supporting priors.

annotations.text:
Authors propose DKVMN, a framework that models learner’s knowledge by mining a large amount of exercise records. This approach addresses problems from previous models by creating 2 separate matrices:

a static matrix: the Key that stores information about different knowledge concepts
a dynamic matrix: the Value that encodes the learners’ mastery level of different concepts.

DKVMN outperms DKT on multiple datasets.

Work combines the detection of learners at risk with recommendations. 

For that, it integrates 3 main fields of study: AI, Federated Learning and Cloud Computing.

All reported results are for classification performance of early warning model (ID2QN). No empirical evaluation of the recommendation module (Att-CapsNet). Gains over baselines are very large but methodology for split/validation is unclear.

annotations.text: DRL has contributed to significant improvements to personalized learning systems even though there still are some issues related with privacy, bias and data limitations.

The authors propose EFedCloud, an adaptive environment that automatically tracks student progress and makes personalized recommendations. The system makes a weekly evaluation of student engagement. This, along with other attributes are then given as input to a DRL early warining system that predicts performance of non-engaged students. 

After a prediction is made, the students are categorized based on expected performance on the final exam. Then, it takes all of these datapoints to make a personalized recommendation to the student on what contents to visit next, with sensitive data being encrypted using LWEA.

references.title: GritNet: Student performance prediction with deep learning

extracted.Students: 1853 students in ND-A and 8301 ND-B students
extracted.Features: Weekly Event embeddings
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
As a Deep Learning Model that uses a Bidirectional LSTM.

Tuning was done via GridSearch.
extracted.Comments:
Authors present their architecture called GritNet.

This embedding was compared against Logistic regressions.
extracted.Limitations: Only compares GritNet againt baseline.

annotations.text: The authors start from the perspective that there are very few attempts at using Deep Learning to address the student performance prediction problem. The authors, thus, propose a Bi-Directional LSTM based model that treats the problem of student performance prediction as a sequential event prediction problem.

While working with real Udacity data, authors show that GritNet outperforms Logistic Regression in the overall task of predicting student success. Moreover, the differences are more pronounced in the first few weeks of the course.

references.title: Ontology-Based Recommender System for a Learning Sequence in Programming Languages
references.journal: International Journal of Emerging Technologies in Learning (iJET)

annotations.text: Personalized learning promises an educational experience catered to a student’s knowledge level. To address this, authors believe recommender systems can be very helpful, especially if the feature extraction (creating the student representation) is ontology-based. Thus, they propose a Recommender System for Personalized Learning Sequences in Programming (PSLP).

The system includes a knowledge model that is ontology-derived and the recommender has 3 stages based on semantic rules and ontology representation. Testing of the system is done on 32 learners and results show that participants using the system needed less time to find a suitable learning path than students using non-recommender approaches.

references.title: OU Analyse: analysing at-risk students at The Open University

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Being at risk of failing at next assessment
extracted.Target:
Not at Risk (0) vs At Risk (1)

Note: Dashboard points to target possibly being Submit vs Not Submit assessment.
extracted.Students: Unspecified number should be in hundreds or thousands
extracted.Features: [Age, Previous Education, Gender, Number of Credits, Previous Attempts, Number of clicks in different resources]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The work does not consider student performance in other courses. 

Materials are labeled according to role they play in learning process. 

Selection of attributes to predict success at next assignment is also done manually.
extracted.Comments:
Authors developed an EWS and show the results on a pilot-test done on 2 courses at OU. 

The system considers demographics and VLE from previous editions of course to predict success in next assessment. 

Every week, there is a prediction that results from Vote of the 4 classifiers trained in parallel. 

Results on experimental courses show that Precision increases with time, but Recall and F1-Score decrease with time.

Current work focuses on extending to more courses and that requires multiple pipeline optimization efforts.
extracted.Limitations:
Does not consider student academic history

Target is binary label on next assessment

Current pipeline uses Excel sheets which is unfeasible for scale.

Manual labelling and selection of VLE Activity Types for next assessment.

On an implementation view, even though formally parallel, the models are being trained sequentially. 

Insufficient data at beggining

Vulnerable to possible changes in course design.

annotations.text: OU Analyze is a system that intends to identify students at-risk in a VLE. The model uses 4 distinct learning algorithms. Pilot tests started in 2014. The system features prediction and a Dashboard to show course overview and view of individual students.

Early identification of students based on interactions with VLE can be helpful for courses in an online setting. The Open University is a british Online University that allows students to participate in degree programs or do isolated courses. The system has its own VLE and a dedicated team to student support. As the number of students for each course can go into the thousands, providing personalized support to students in need is a difficult endeavour.

Previous research has focused on the use of ML to identify students at risk. These models are often trained on what the authors call legacy data (previous academic performance) and, despite the naturally high failure rate seen in online education, it is possible to save some students in need. Another way of looking at the problem involves using VLE interactions or demographics.

The work of these authors follows up on previous work conducted at the OU. Initially, the authors used DTs on behavioral data and assessment scores. More recently, demographics have also started to be incorporated.

In order to make a prediction, the authors consider the behavior and performance of a student in the current course, student demographics and prior performance of other students in the same course. Academic history of a student is not considered. Goal is identify, as early as possible, if a student will or will not be at risk in a given course → Models are course-specific.
Data is collected from the warehouse and transformed in order to be suitable for an Analytics Dashboard - 11 courses at time of writing. Data is collected for every student:
Role of VLE

As the course advances, the weight of demographic data decreases: very important at the start when there is nothing to go with → less and less important as more VLE interactions are made with the system. Predictive performance increases with the addition of VLE data.
For each course, the authors identify what types of interaction are to be considered the most meaningful → Bayesian analysis. The data is then taken to 4 distinct predictive models that are parallel:
Decision of classification is based on the vote of the 4 algorithms.

Models are used every week to assess whether a student will be at-risk or not. Currently deployed on 18 courses → Empyrical results of the pilot test done in 2 distinct course - ABC and XYZ. In this case, it is possible to see that, as different assessments advance, the model becomes increasingly more precise even if at the expense of other metrics.
This makes sense, as the idea is to have a support team reach out to students in need, the you better make damn sure that the students are actually at risk.
The authors have developed an analytics Dashboard. The dashboard includes a Course view page that includes overview of assessment results and predictions for the next assessment.
Also, there is student view page. The page shows student’s performance and compares it against scores of the cohort. It also includes an activity recommender.

Scaling up to 18 courses. The authors are finding some challenges with scale which required some redesigns. One of the main bottlenecks is the cleaning and pre-processing pipeline which is somewhat inefficient. 

The labelling and selection of activity types is also a point being addressed as the authors are looking to automate it with Minimum Redundancy Maximization Algorithm. In the prototypical implementation models are trained in a sequence (even though formally they are parallel).
Another concern is starting predictions for new courses for which there is no prior data. In here, the authors consider 2 scenarios:

extracted.Novelty of Model: The proposed model integrates a language model in the KT task - using the text of questions and answers.

Authors propose an LKT approach on 2 Datasets and show that, for one of them, their LKT approach (using a language model to include the text of the questions, answers and knowledge components.

Moreover, when addressing the cold-start problem, the authors show that the model outperforms DKT on when the sequence is small.

annotations.text: Current KT approaches faulter in actually representing knowledge states, often due to a lack of ability to semantically interpret text and concepts. The authors use pre-trained Language Models to give a semantyc meaning to text, making this now possible to use in KT modelling.

The authors validate their hypothesis that integrating semantic meaning to knowledge tracing improves the current state of the art for these methods, as indicated by the fact that LKT outperforms other KT models on the benchmark datasets. Also, this semantic knowledge seems to be a viable solution to address the cold-start problem often found in KT tasks. Also, the model is argued to be more interpretable due to its use of rich text data.

In total, the authors identify 3 main clusters.

This is followed by a subsequent regression analysis that tries to “explain group membership vs grade. Best students were the ones in Cluster 3.
extracted.Limitations:

Extraction of general patterns from a very diverse subset of courses.

annotations.text: The abstract starts by criticizing the fact that the showcase of new models in LA is to show how well they perform in a single course. The authors attempt to replicate a temporal model of SRL taxonomies with the caveat of generalizing it to 411 students in 19 courses. Techniques used combine hierarchical clustering with Hidden Markov Models.

The findings show that that some patterns of SRL can, indeed, generalize between different courses and it is even possible to find some relationship between interaction and performance. However, the generalization to other contexts requires a reduction in complexity of the models, whihc may lead to a situation where all you have is too much of a simplification.

Results show that students who were part of the heteroogenous groups not only performed better but were seen to perceive more effort on the part of their group. Authors propose new opportunities for group formation implementations.

annotations.text: This work is divided into 2 different parts. The first is the prediction of undergraduate student performance at the end of a 4-year program. The second is the grouping of students, out of which 2 groups emerge: low and high achieving students.

Authors also found that focusing the prediction on a smaller subset of courses may be a good early indicator of student performance. Student performance to be understood, in this context, as success at the end of a program.

Educational Data Mining seeks to make use of the enormous amounts of data that exist nowadays in universities and classification is among the most common tasks in EDM. Moreover, these models can work at different levels:

Another common EDM task is clustering, with special emphasys on grouping students with similar behaviors together. A third application refers to the distillation of data for human interpretation (e.g. educational dashboards).
The work intends to answer the following 3 research questions on a set of students pursuing a 4-year program in Information Technology:
To do this, the authors will perform 3 main tasks:
The literature review is divided into 3 main components:

Prediction of success at the degree level uses multiple predictors also found in prediction at the course-level such as demographics. However, these models tend to also include other predictors that often not seen in course-level models. The include:

These models have been applied in the context of either one or more than one educational institutions. Different algorithms have been used: Decision Trees, Bayesian Networks, kNN. General targets for prediction are GPA or binary (Fail/Pass) or multiclass constructs extracted from GPA (Fail, Fair, Good, Very Good).

General metrics used is accuracy, which tends achieve better results in the binary classification case. Still, the use of early grades is essencial in order to be able to obtain what can be considered good predictions. A second consideration is that many works use cross-validation to assess the performance of their models. The authors will train the model on one co-hort and evaluate the performance of the model on another cohort.

A preivous study has identified typical learning progressions on K-12 education, something the authors adapt in their work for undergraduate education. The authors will use hierarchical histograms to show the resulting clusters.

The authors review some of the data mining methods they intend to emply in this work: classification and clustering. The metrics they identify as relevant for classification are Accuracy and the kappa coefficient (an indicator that takes into account that correct predictions could occur by chance). There is also an important distinction between models that are black box: ANN, NB, RF from the ones that are more interpretable such as Decision Trees.

Clustering in this paper is defines as a process of grouping similar objects. Similar objects are usually defined as being closest using a certain metric, which in many situations is the euclidean distance. Common algorithms include k-means and the one used in the wok, which is going to be x-means. Both are centroid-based algorithms with a key distinction being that x-means attempts to find the optimal number of clusters using a Bayesian Information Criterion.

The data uses 4-year information technology BSc degree in Pakistan, more specifically data 210 undergraduate students in the years 2007-2008 and 2008-2009. These wil go through a process of feature selection to select the most important marks to use. 

Target variable is the marks at the end of the program: 10% of first year average exams, 20% for second year, 30% for 3rd year and 40% of 4th year. Student who failed after the 4 years are not considered in the study, with the remaining 5 grade classes (A, B, C, D and E) being the relevant classification targets.

The authors employed multiple classification algorithms. From the analysis osf the sample, there are barely any A students while C is the most popular class. Majority class predictions would achieve 51% accuracy. More specifically, the authors look at the grade distribution to identify ideal intervals for prediction: ending up being grades B to E. Features considered end up being pre-admission marks and marks on years 1 and 2.

To address RQ2, the authors look into identifying which factors between courses from the first and second year that are predictive of student success. For that they explore the different feature selection techniques that are available in RapidMiner. Most did not improve performance of DTs (because DTs make it themselves). So, the authors turn to looking at the feature importances of the DT.

Variables that appear in at least 50% of the created decision trees are considered important. Then, the authors focus on decision paths that lead to successful outcomes (A and B) or unsuccessful outcomes (D and E).

To study the progression paths, the authors employ clustering on multiple levels. For each year, they create vectors containing the marks obtained in each year. For each year, students are clustered using the Xmeans algorithm. Progression is then defined as a 4 element tuple containing the cluster labels for each year.

The results on 10 different classifiers show that it is possible to predict student performance by training the models on one cohort of students and applying it to a second cohort. Interestingly, the model that achieves the best score is a Naive Bayes classification algorithm (84% accuracy and 0.73 kappa coefficient. By looking the confusion matrices, it is possible to see that only Decision Tree based algorithms are able to predict students who achieved an E.

The authors rely on Decision Tree interpretability to determine which courses are the most important towards determining the final grade of the program. More particularly, they found 2 courses from first year and 3 courses from the second year to be the ones with the best predictive potential (HS205/206, MS-121, CS-251, HS-207 and CT-255).
On the second level of selection (pathways), the authors identified 3 pathways:

The final marks for each student in each year were used to determine progression. Clustering of cohort 1 on the first year shows that 3 clusters were found in the first year: Low, Intermediate and High.

When considering all four years, the same pattern emerges… some students have generally low grades, others keep in the intermediate and a final subset is on the high end of the grades. Most students in cohort 1 are intermediate throughout the coure excepting year 2 which has an abnormal amount of high marks.

Observations highlight that cohort 1 tends to include better performers than cohort 2, as noted by the clustering results in years 2, 3 and 4. Another observation is visible, which is that good students tend to remain good while bad students tend to stay bad.

The green bars shows that students that are performing betters in the first year, also tend to perform well as they advance in the course. Similarly, the red bars, also highloght the opposite in that most of the students who start performing worse tend to continue to be performing worse.

Some students do not follow these trends, but they are quite few. These students can only be detected from using the progression as it is what allows these measurements: clustering on all courses together does not highlight these students.

The paper proceeds with a discussion of a would be pragmatic policy of using the pragmatic policy of using the marks of the first 2 years to identify students as “likely to have A or B” or “likely to be D and E” which intends to catch, as early as possible, both high performing students and students who are struggling. For that, they analyze the progressions of the students. Results show that applying this pragmatic policy would is cautious: when in doubt, flag students in need.

annotations.text: Learning analytics is being constantly integrated in learning environments. However, most of the markers for student success derive from self-reported measures. Study compares the effectiveness of logs vs self-reports as effective representations of achievement goals.

The authors create goal clusters using latent variable mixture modelling and the results show that there is a misalignement between both sources of data. More specifically, self-repoorts before learning do not translate into learning relevant behaviour measurable by learning logs. More specifically, self-reports seem to be overly optimistic measures of student motivation and engagement.

annotations.text: The work explores 3 algorithms that may be used to form groups for computer-supported collaborative learning in ITS aimed at K-12 students. The algorithms use multidimensional views of student ability comprising multiple hundred attributes.

Comparison of the 3 algorithms is done against different baseline approaches - random and grouping based on course score. Simulating the results on data from previous years shows that the grouping algorithms could lead to better results on the evaluation metrics than the alternative methods.

references.title: A recommender agent based on learning styles for better virtual collaborative learning experiences

extracted.Courses: Not Applicable - students belong to Engineering in Foreign Languages School
extracted.Initialization Method:
Learning Style is inferred from Questionnaire. 

Sparsity problem → requires that there is at least one student with each learning tool to have selected a tool as preferrable.
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: The learning style is continuously updated based on the interactions of the user with the system.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Similarity between items is computed using Jaccard Similarity.

Representation of users used demographic data AND Learning Style as derived from the LS questionnaire.
extracted.Recommendation types:
Shortcuts: based on static information

Suggestions: incorporate learning style and user interactions
extracted.Evaluation: {Satisfaction: 12/20 students reported to be likely to use the recommendder again}
extracted.Comments:
Methodologically, the work can be summarized into 2 sections:

1. Obtaining the learning styles from the questionnaire AND a rating of tools based on choices upon registration.

2. Using a Collaborative Filtering approach to make recommendations.

To mitigate the sparsity problem, recommendation matrices are computed seaparately for each learning style.
extracted.Limitations:
- Results on accuracy of LS evaluation or Recommendations is not reported.

- Items whose score does not surpass a pre-determined threshold are never shown.

- Cold-Start problem

- Evaluation metrics considered (accuracy)

- Survey evaluation for recommender based on sdatisfaction-

annotations.text: More is not necessarily better. Survey data shows that students need more guidance to access materials than when they had less choice. Thus, the authors propose a new collaborative filtering recommender that also finds a student’s learning style. The system produces 2 types of recommendations and the authors showcase an example of its integration in a VLE.

Nowadays, there are new forms of education that have not existed before: continuous education, online educations, etc… Meaning that it no longer is knowledge transfer from professor to students. Computer Supported Collaborative Learning (CSCL) is among the most challenging educational opportunities, requiring good SRL skills to be successful.
An important construct to consider is personalization. The authors propose a collaborative filtering method for personalization of a CSCL environment.

The authors consider that personalization can be defined as fostering specific learning behaviors. However, e-learning personalization differs from traditional classrooms substantially, bringing forward new challenges related to the fact the e-learning tends to be self-directed.
E-learning recommenders are not as developed or matured as their e-commerce counterparts. Moreover, they have recognized drawbacks, but still should be regarded as mainly advantageous:
Improving learning with recommendations

One popular form of personalization is what is called learner-centered: approaches that make the learner have a defining role in the learning experience (often frowned at by students at the start, but accepted later). Professors in learner centered approaches are not giver’s of content and more coaches or facilitators.

Recommendations can be understood as those made by a professor or done by a technology. The authors discuss two distinct conceptual models of recommendations/feedback applicable to the context of professors and proceed to discuss recommender systems in the context of personalization.
A recommender is defined as an agent that, starting from a very large pool of possible resources, gives a user the approapriate resources and guidance to achieve their learning goals.
The main advantage of a recommender is related with its decisiveness: they make a decision for someone who may not have the expertise or the maturity to know where to go next.
Still, recommenders in VLEs are not straighforward:
Learning Styles as a Possible Input to a Recommender Agent

For personalization and recommendations to work, it is pivotal to ensure that there is an effective user model (representation is key). Common attributes used in such representations include personal details, learning experience, goals or style. However, creating a representation that is optimal for a specific application is de facto representation a challenge. Empahsys on Learning Style.

Again, Learning styles themselves have multiple different conceptual models for Learning Styles and the authors explore the relationship between the different learning styles and any relevant teaching/learning methods to make recommendations.

Such cases include Protus (v1 and 2.0) which use Learning Style to recommend educational content. Oscar is a ITS to learn SQL that also uses learning styles to recommend the most suitable content to deliver.

There is an interesting niche on making recommendations on specialized online search and learning tools (for some reason, students do not follow recommended bibliography). The authors created a recommender and added it to their VLE - U-Learn.
Rationale
Authors surveyed 35 people from different backgrounds in order to understanding how they access  and use bibliographical resources:
The responses informed the creation of the e-learning recommender on the following features:
Features
The user profile is saved into a preference tree model with a dynamic part and a static part.
Similarly, the recommender offers 2 types of recommendations:
Description of Recommender
Collaborative filtering approaches tend to use rankings (e.g. stars). Thus, similarity is generally computed from the ratings, not specifically behavior. Formally, the algorithm works as follows:
An example:

The initial matrix is a user-tool matrix, the inverted matrix is a tool-user matrix. This is followed by the computation of a Tool-Tool matrix based on Jaccard Similarity (a similarity matrix basically).
Then, for each tool it is possible to use these similarities to compute the score for each tool:
Indexing Learning Styles

So, for students who share a specific learning style, the algorithm works as described previously, which requires classification. The first step for identification is the questionnaire answered by the users. Then, they are classified according to the Index of Learning Styles (ILS): active/reflective, sensing/intuitive, visual/verbal, and sequential/global.
Based of the assessment of learning style, the recommmender considers what types of items to recommend to the students.
Technological Choices
The platform U-Learn was developed in Java. The platform is accessible via browser. The platform saves all user information into a MySQL database. This part is a lenghty description of the platform.
When it comes to functionality, the platfomr includes 5 parts: View Article, Add Article, View Tools, Add Tools, Suggestions and Shortcuts.
User can access recommendations by clicking suggestion. To use the platform, users must create an account and follow a set of steps:
This allows the system to make recommendations. Successful recommendations are considered as such if the user shares them.
The authors do a pilot-test of their prototype on a group of 25 students to validate the recommenders efficiency and effectiveness.

To do that, the authors developed a feedback questionnaire that measures: Ease of use, learning style accuracy, usefulness and recommendation accuracy. Most students mentioned to be satisfied with the system in utilization and would recommend its use. However, no actual mention of the performance of the recommender.

references.title: An Integrated Framework Based on Latent Variational Autoencoder for Providing Early Warning of At-Risk Students

annotations.text: Prediction of student performance is one the most relevant tasks in LA. However, traditional methods are designed for balanced datasets which is somewhat difficult in datasets intended to detect students at-risk. To address the imabalance, the authors propose a framework that combines an auto-encoder with a Deep NN.

The auto-encoder is intended to generate new instances of students at-risk first, the neural network is meant to make predictions. Experiments of performed on benchmark dataset show that the model actually is a possible alternative to generate samples that make the datasets more balanced. Confirmation tests validate effectiveness and robustness of the designed approach.

references.title: Mood detection ontology integration with teacher context

annotations.text: Ther are recommender systems that suggest pedagogical activities to professors. However, the acceptance of these suggestions is not always popular. The recommender proposed in this paper is ontology-based.

references.title: Uncovering Learning Styles through Eye Tracking and Artificial Intelligence

extracted.Features: [Fixation, Whole Fixation, Visit, Glance, Total Duration, Modiefied Total Duration, Number, Modified Number, Time to First, Duration of First, Minimum/Average/Maximum duration, Last AOI viewed]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Surveys reveal target while eye-tracking dimensions wiill be used as features… eacxh element on the LMS is mapped to an AOI Grouping: Table of Contents, Summary, Illustrations, Keywords, Theory, Exercise, Example, Supporting Text, MCQ, Additional Materials.

Outliers were removed. Authors, in fact, do different pre-processings and test the different preprocessings on different algorithms.

extracted.Implications: The results show that predicting learning styles from eye-tracking alone is challenging, but the introduction of pre-processing steps seems to improve model performance.
extracted.Comments:
Authors attempt to use supervised Learning to predict each of the 4 dimensions FSLSM. To do that, the authors use the ILS survey for the target variables and test different algorithm/preprocessing configurations. 

Results show that ML from Eye Tracking is not particularly effective since iit does not achieve an average F1-Score of 0.8 (the author’s established target).
extracted.Limitations:
- Limitations inherent to eye tracking tool or action classification.

- No hyper-parameter tuning.

- No data augmentation techniques used.

annotations.text: The paper proposes the utilization of eye-tracking data to identify learning styles on learners. The authors attempt to use multiple data extrtaction methods and make predictions on a plethora of differetn potential classifiers.
Authors use data from 100 students to publish their findings but study results are unspecified.

references.title: Informing Expert Feature Engineering through Automated Approaches: Implications for Coding Qualitative Classroom Video Data

annotations.text: Authors make a comparison between expert-derived features versus automatically engineered features (AutoML) to predict group interactions from classroom videos.

The results show that combining both feature extraction methods outperforms the use of exclusive manual expert feature engineering. Authors follow-up with an analysis of feature importance even though, at this stage, they make no explicit mention on what were the moist important features.

references.title: Difficulty aware programming knowledge tracing via large language models

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Predicting next programming-task success from historical submissions
extracted.Features: Problems, submission responses, CodeBERT embeddings, LLM-generated KCs, TD, KD
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Prior programming KT insufficiently models difficulty, especially TD vs KD
extracted.Novelty of Model: Difficulty-aware programming KT with GPT-4o + CodeBERT + GAT + update gate
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Max sequence length 50; split or zero-pad sequences; 5-fold CV; Adam, lr 0.001, batch 32, epochs 50
extracted.Models: DKT, DKVMN, SAKT, EKT, Code-DKT, DKT+, DIMKT, QIKT, TCKT, THKT, DPKT
extracted.Comments: Distinguishes text difficulty from knowledge difficulty, which is the main conceptual contribution
extracted.Limitations: Needs externally generated difficulty labels; long-term state modeling and richer behavioral features remain future work

annotations.text:
KT techniques, while good at achieving good accuracies on different benchmark datasets, have often not focused on the impact of difficulty in knowledge modeling. Authors propose a Difficulty-Aware Programming Knowledge Tracing via Large Language Models (DPKT) that aims to improve at this component that is so important for open-ended questions such as programming challenges. 

Results show that Model performs excellently across different Language Datasets.

references.title: Modelability as a Strategy for Improving the  Generalizability and Scalability of Predictive Models

extracted.Target: High achievers (A- to A+), Moderate performers (B- to B+), At-risk students (C+ or lower)
extracted.Courses: 2 introductory statistics courses at 2 public research universities in California
extracted.Data sources: CourseKata online textbook trace data, embedded formative assessments, chapter summaries, final letter grades
extracted.Features: [Reading-related: sess_count, sess_avg_dur, sess_max_dur, pages_per_sess, read_time_total, engage_ratio; Formative-assessment-related: quiz_score, first_attempt_acc, final_attempt_acc, avg_attempts, summary_word_count]
extracted.Moment of Prediction: Chapter-level early prediction throughout the course; models evaluated after each completed chapter
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors engineered 11 chapter-level features for each of 12 chapters (132 total features). Reading traces were segmented into sessions using a 10-minute inactivity threshold; sessions under 1 minute and sessions from end-of-chapter quizzes were removed from reading-feature construction. Data were standardized with z-score normalization using training-set scalers only. 5-fold stratified cross-validation, repeated 5 times, was used. Training data were balanced with SMOTE. For early prediction, the authors created an augmented dataset containing all prefixes of each student’s chapter sequence, masking later chapters as missing.
extracted.Models: Random Forest (RF), sequence model based on RNN/GRU, chapter ensemble of RF classifiers
extracted.Assessment Strategy: Within Course 1: 5-fold cross-validation repeated 5 times. Cross-institution generalization: train on Course 1, test on Course 2. Models compared across chapter-by-chapter early prediction.
extracted.Comments: Main contribution is showing that a system designed for modelability can support predictive models that generalize across institutions. The strongest model was the sequence model with augmented data, especially in the cross-institution test. The study is also interesting because it uses behavioral-only textbook interaction metrics, not demographic or prior-grade predictors, and still achieves strong early prediction.
extracted.Limitations: Only 2 institutions and both were public research universities in California, so broader generalizability still needs testing. Sample size is modest, especially for Course 2. The target is an ordinal 3-class grouping based on letter grades rather than raw score prediction. Features come from a shared CourseKata environment, so transferability may depend on similarly instrumented platforms.

annotations.text: Generalizability across different contexts is a known flaw of the current Student Performance Predictive Models. Authors present their framework and show how it would work in an online environment called CourseKata. Results show that modelability is a promising avenue for generalizability across contexts and potentially institutions.

references.title: An Outcome-Based Educational Recommender  System

extracted.Recommender System Type: Outcome-Based Educational Recommender System (OBER); method-agnostic framework with Fixed trajectory, Collaborative Filtering (CF), and Knowledge-Based (KB) filtering
extracted.Target of Recommendation: Learning items / next items in a learning trajectory to maximize mastery of outcomes
extracted.Students: Over 5,700 learners total; experiment groups: CF 1856, Fixed 1963, KB 1932
extracted.Data sources: Learner profiles, learning items, interaction logs, outcome tree, item–outcome alignment mappings, mastery scores
extracted.Initialization Method: Learning outcomes first established with domain experts; outcomes aligned to items; in NamazApp, an LLM produced initial item–outcome alignments, later checked and edited by the team; mastery computed from best result on verifying items
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Recommendations delivered during normal app usage; framework supports swapping recommendation methods without changing logging or mastery calculation
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Minimal entity-relation schema with Learner–Item–Outcome entities; item-outcome alignments labeled as promotes or verifies; mastery formula uses learner’s best result on items verifying each outcome; total mastery is sum over outcomes
extracted.Evaluation: Two-week randomized split test comparing Fixed, CF, and KB. Metrics: Retention, Relevance (CTR), and Mastery. Results: CF had highest retention (6.16) and relevance (0.3033), but Fixed achieved highest mastery (0.4641), followed by KB (0.4212) and CF (0.3899)
extracted.Comments: Links recommendation directly to learning outcomes rather than only engagement. Contribution is mainly methodological: adding outcomes and assessment into the RS schema so different methods can be compared on pedagogical impact. The page-3 schema and page-6 evaluation flow make the framework especially clear
extracted.Limitations: Findings are specific to NamazApp and non-formal learning; larger/longer experiments needed for stronger significance claims; generalizability to other educational contexts remains open

annotations.text:
Most ERS suffer from the problem of being evaluated with pedagogically unmeaningful metrics. Authors introduce OBER which introduces outcomes and assessments into the schema → allowing the evaluation of learning gains. 

Authors A/B/C tested their framework on a set of 5700 students over 2 weeks using 3 methods - Fixed, CF and KB. Fixed path maximized mastery, while CF maximized retention.

references.title: Future-Proofing Programmers: Optimal Knowledge Tracing for AI-Assisted Personalized Education

extracted.Recommender System Type: Knowledge-tracing-driven personalized recommender / AI copilot for educational resource allocation; combines BKT-style mastery estimation, signal processing, convex optimization, and generative AI chatbot delivery
extracted.Target of Recommendation: Personalized next-step learning support: educational resources, review materials, learning activities, reminders, Q&A help, and adaptive intervention guidance
extracted.Students: 203 university students in a controlled study; 4 experimental groups of 40–41 students plus 40 in control
extracted.Courses: University AI course; also implemented on programming-learning platforms My Code Weapon and Nemobot
extracted.Data sources: Assignment submissions, quiz/exam responses, participation logs, platform activity, chatbot queries, feedback surveys, OJ submission data (scores, attempts, errors, timestamps, resource usage)
extracted.Initialization Method: Student state initialized through BKT-based mastery probability P(Lj,i)P(L_{j,i})P(Lj,i) using submission histories and contextual features; policy parameters optimized for personalized resource allocation; chatbot uses these outputs to deliver recommendations
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Iterative flywheel: new signals are smoothed and aggregated, mastery is updated in real time, recommendations are regenerated, and model/policy parameters are refined continuously
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Signals treated as time-series; multimodal analytics pipeline includes smoothing, normalization, sampling, feature embedding, and multimodal aggregation; in OJ setting, verdicts, error types, execution time, memory, attempts, difficulty, and timestamps are used
extracted.Recommendation types: Resource recommendation, proactive reminders, conversational question handling, tailored learning activities, foundational vs advanced exercise suggestions
extracted.Evaluation: Controlled study: COTUTOR achieved highest assignment score (73.2%), post-course survey score (72.8%), activity participation rate (73.9%), and prediction accuracy (67.4%) versus control, BKT, DKT, and Code-DKT. Compared with control, assignment score improved from 68.4% to 73.2%
extracted.Comments: It operationalizes KT into resource allocation and chatbot-delivered recommendations. The chatbot screenshots on page 12 help make the recommendation role concrete
extracted.Limitations: Recommendation effects are shown through group outcomes, but the paper does not isolate which specific recommendation type produced the gains; one-course university study; some effects versus stronger baselines are modest; framework is broader than a classical item recommender

extracted.Target: Correct vs Incorrect; mastery probability P(Lj,i)P(L_{j,i})P(Lj,i)
extracted.Courses: TOEIC preparation in Santa ITS (benchmark setting)
extracted.Data sources: Santa ITS (benchmark setting)EdNet KT1 interaction logs + metadata + skill labels / Q-matrix
extracted.Features: Timestamped interaction sequences, user/item IDs, answers, skill labels, smoothed contextual features, submission/performance signals
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Traditional BKT is interpretable but limited; DKT handles temporal complexity but is less interpretable and data-hungry; existing systems often do not integrate richer learning signals for real-time adaptivity
extracted.Novelty of Model: COTUTOR extends BKT with signal processing and convex-optimization-based updating/planning, integrating smoothed multimodal learning signals for more adaptive and interpretable KT
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Remove duplicates; remove missing user answers; remove items with undefined skill tags; construct binary Q-matrix from metadata; filter students with fewer than 10 interactions; exponential smoothing applied to contextual signals
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.843 - COTUTOR, AUC: 0.717 - COTUTOR, PR-AUC: 0.741  - COTUTOR, RMSE: 0.229  - COTUTOR, NLL: 0.594  - COTUTOR}
extracted.Comments: benchmarks learning-state estimation against KT baselines on EdNet. Best gains are clearest in Accuracy, RMSE, and NLL; AUC/PR-AUC gains over baselines are smaller but still best overall
extracted.Limitations: Exact benchmark subset size is not fully specified; COTUTOR is not a pure KT model only, but a larger AI-assisted framework; comparison is limited to a small set of baselines

annotations.text: Authors present CoTutor, which builds on BKT with Signal Processing to deliver adaptive feedback and strategies in an adaptative manner. CoTutor has been deployed to universities and authors report that it outperforms other approaches.
Another figure… this is interesting because it looks something similar to what I want to do in my PhD

references.title:
GCKT: Context-Aware Gating of Heterogeneous Learning
Features With Transformer for Cognitive Knowledge
Tracing in Intelligent Tutoring Systems

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Predicting future cognitive performance from historical interactions
extracted.Target: Cognitive level class (0–6); also binary correctness in auxiliary evaluation
extracted.Features: Exercise, response, response time, interval time, topic, concept, cognitive level
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Prior KT models use simple concatenation or fixed weighting of heterogeneous signals
extracted.Novelty of Model: Transformer KT with context-aware gated fusion of heterogeneous features
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Student-wise 7:3 split; temporal order preserved; 5-fold CV; padded/truncated to length 500; QQQ-matrix smoothing γ=0.03
extracted.Comments: Main contribution is better fusion of temporal + hierarchical knowledge features; also somewhat interpretable via gate-weight analysis
extracted.Limitations: Needs rich cognitive annotations; cold start and very long sequences remain issues

annotations.text:
Most KT models struggle with different types of signals that are generated during learning. Authors present Gated-full features Transformer Cognitive Knowledge Tracing which promises to be able to integrate these heterogenous signals via a fusion gate (instead of the general concatenation or weighting used in other architectures). 

The model embeds topics and concepts which is later concatenated with with exercise, time, correctedness before passing through this fusion-gate which generates weights that hopefully provide a better representation of the knowledge State. Tests on multiple Public Datasets show that CGKT beats other KT models.

references.title: Analysis and optimization of student learning paths based on CRNN and sequential data

extracted.Recommender System Type: Learning-path recommender / path optimization framework using CRNN + Transformer + Reinforcement Learning (PPO)
extracted.Target of Recommendation: Personalized learning paths / tailored learning routes / intervention suggestions
extracted.Students: Empirical deployment reports 1,200 participants across 3 institutions; benchmark datasets also include large public educational datasets
extracted.Courses: OULAD university courses, OLI Engineering Statics, ASSISTments math, EdNet online learning
extracted.Data sources: Student learning history data, timestamps, course progress, learning duration, interaction records, question/problem logs, skill labels, knowledge-point graph, student outcomes

extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Yes — recommendations are iteratively updated through PPO based on real learning data and reward signals such as completion rate and test score improvement; hourly recommendation updates are mentioned in deployment description
extracted.Preprocessing Details: temporal feature extraction from sequential logs; predefined knowledge graph; nightly batch feature extraction and hourly recommendation updates in empirical deployment; incremental learning setup mentioned
extracted.Recommendation types: Personalized learning routes, recommended paths, predicted knowledge gaps, intervention suggestions, dashboard-based path recommendations
extracted.Evaluation: 15% increase in completion rate and 12% improvement in average test score over baseline methods; empirical comparison also reports baseline vs CRNN framework on completion rate (68% to 83%) and test score (75.3 to 87.6)
extracted.Comments: RL makes the recommendation layer explicit. The LMS plugin description on page 20 reinforces practical recommender deployment.
extracted.Limitations: Some reported empirical results are described in a broad framework-level way and are not always tightly tied to a clearly separated recommendation baseline; reliance on predefined knowledge graphs; possible demographic bias in training data; public-dataset experiments and empirical deployment are somewhat mixed in presentation

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Modeling student knowledge states and predicting learning-path related future behaviors from sequential interactions
extracted.Target: Implicitly successful future learning path / interaction relevance;
extracted.Courses: TOEIC prep platform (EdNet/Santa), math problem solving (ASSISTments)
extracted.Features: Timestamps, question/problem IDs, answers/correctness, skill labels, elapsed time, course progress, learning duration, interaction records; sequential temporal features extracted by CRNN
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Existing approaches struggle to model temporal dynamics and knowledge-point relationships effectively; prior methods use isolated CNN/RNN-style modeling and weaker knowledge association / reward design
extracted.Novelty of Model: Hybrid framework combining CRNN for sequential feature extraction, Transformer for knowledge-point association, and RL for path optimizationHybrid framework combining CRNN for sequential feature extraction, Transformer for knowledge-point association, and RL for path optimization
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Adam optimizer; batch size 32; early stopping; CRNN with 3x3 filters + LSTM (128 hidden units); Transformer with 4 heads and 256-d embeddings

extracted.Limitations: Not a pure KT model; target is less cleanly defined than “next answer correctness”; metrics used for KT experiment are F1/HITS@K, not the more usual AUC/RMSE despite the text mentioning them elsewhere; student counts for experimental subsets are not clearly specified

annotations.text:
Most approaches at learning path recommendations struggle with properly incorporating knowledge modeling and temporal dynamics. Authors propose a new framework that uses CRNNs, Transformers and Reinforcement Learning for dynamic learning path optimization. 

Tests show that the model is effective at promoting learning gains and completion rates. Authors also finish with an Ablation study.

references.title: AI-Driven Personalized Learning and Remedial Recommendation Through Knowledge Concept-Centric Evaluation

extracted.Recommender System Type: AI-driven remedial recommender / adaptive concept-based recommender using knowledge-gap identification, LLM-generated remedial content, and adaptive follow-up evaluation
extracted.Target of Recommendation: Weak knowledge concepts; recommendations include learning objectives, review topics, remedial explanation, practice activities, and concept-gap rationale
extracted.Courses: Procedural Programming using C (evaluation setting); broader curriculum processing spans 70+ CSE courses
extracted.Data sources: Curriculum PDFs, OBE exam data from AMPLE LMS, learner-question performance, concept/outcome mappings, instructor-defined attainment rules
extracted.Initialization Method: Initialized from curriculum processing and concept extraction; instructor validates concepts and defines thresholds/targets and question rules; weak concepts are identified from learner performance
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Iterative closed loop: after studying recommendations, learners complete self-assessment; results are fed back and the system generates refined recommendations until target mastery is reached
extracted.Preprocessing Details: PDF-to-text conversion, RAG-based retrieval, embeddings/vector DB, semantic deduplication for follow-up questions, multi-model cross-verification of generated remedial content
extracted.Recommendation types: Personalized remedial recommendations + review guidance + practice tasks + adaptive follow-up question
extracted.Evaluation: Recommendation-module results should be the focus here: after recommendation, Not Competent/Beginner decreased and Intermediate/Expert increased across all five concepts (Fig. 7); pre/post gains were significant for all concepts (p < 0.0001), with mean gains of +22.47, +18.50, +15.97, +13.21, and +1.28 depending on the concept; learner survey showed strongest ratings for relevance and structure of recommendations (>3.3/5) and weakest ratings for personalization to individual mistakes/needs (<3.0/5)
extracted.Comments: Best framed as a remedial intervention recommender rather than a classical item recommender. Strong point: the paper evaluates the effect of recommendations directly with pre/post learner improvement and user perception, not only underlying model performance
extracted.Limitations: Single-institution study; recommendation quality is not compared against alternative recommender baselines; personalization was perceived as weaker than clarity/relevance; relies on human/instructor oversight and cross-verification

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Whether learners have attained the required concept level
extracted.Target: Concept mastery probabilities; weak concepts are those below OBE target levels
extracted.Data sources: Historical OBE exam data from AMPLE LMS; learner-question responses mapped to concepts; curriculum and course-outcome structure
extracted.Features: Learner interaction sequences, correctness, exercise/question content, concept mappings, OBE attainment labels
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Earlier KT approaches either rely mainly on response sequences or do not sufficiently incorporate exercise semantics/content. The paper argues this limits fine-grained concept-level gap detection for OBE-aligned personalization
extracted.Novelty of Model: Uses Exercise-Aware Knowledge Tracing (EKT) to combine learner response history with semantic content of exercises, then uses predicted concept mastery to identify weak concepts for personalized follow-up
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Curriculum PDFs converted to text and aligned to concepts; learner-question attainment mapped to concept-level outcomes; 75/25 train-test split; 12 epochs; embedding dimension 100; LSTM hidden size 100; dropout 0.3; Adam optimizer 0.001; batch size 32; binary cross-entropy loss
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {AUC: 0.9334 - EKT, RMSE: 0.1828 - EKT}
extracted.Comments: Good fit for KT table because KT is not peripheral here: it is the mechanism used to estimate concept mastery and trigger downstream intervention. Figures 4–6 show model comparison, learner knowledge-state evolution, and concept-wise mastery visualization
extracted.Limitations: Single-institution OBE dataset; results tied to one engineering program; only holdout evaluation reported; paper emphasizes downstream application more than deep ablation of the KT component

annotations.text:
The authors propose an architecture that includes 4 key components: Knowledge Gap Identification, Adaptive Question Generation, Personalized Evaluation and Remedial Recommendation. EKT continuously monitors the learners’ knowledge states while RAG is used to create MCQ for the learners to practice. 

Recommender is tested.

references.title: A Question-centric Multi-experts Contrastive Learning Framework for Improving the Accuracy and Interpretability of Deep Sequential Knowledge Tracing Models
references.journal: ACM Transactions on Knowledge Discovery from Data

extracted.Features: Chronologically ordered interaction sequences with question, KC set, response, and timestamp; question embeddings; concept embeddings; question-level and concept-level interaction encodings
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Existing deep sequential KT models struggle to model individual question information, especially for less interactive questions, which can cause overfitting of question embeddings. They also lack sufficiently interpretable prediction results for teachers
extracted.Novelty of Model: Q-MCKT explicitly models student knowledge acquisition at both question and concept levels, uses mixture of experts for more robust knowledge acquisition states, adds fine-grained question-centric contrastive learning for less interactive questions, and uses an IRT-based interpretable prediction layer
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Followed the preprocessing protocol in pyKT: removed student sequences shorter than 3 attempts; maximum input sequence length 200; five-fold cross-validation; within each fold, 80% of student sequences used for training/validation and 20% for evaluation; trained up to 200 epochs with early stopping and Adam; hyperparameters searched with Bayesian optimization
extracted.Models: DKT, DKT+, DKT-F, DKVMN, ATKT, GKT, SAKT, SAINT, AKT, SKVMN, DeepIRT, qDKT, AT-DKT, SimpleKT, Q-MCKT
extracted.Comments: Strong KT paper with a clear dual emphasis on accuracy and interpretability. The visualizations on pages 22–23 show both concept-level knowledge-state evolution and how the final prediction is decomposed into interpretable question and concept acquisition scores
extracted.Limitations: More complex than standard KT baselines; interpretability is at the prediction-result level rather than full model transparency; benefits of contrastive learning are smaller on datasets with low average interactions per question

annotations.text:
DKT is a common way of solving the Knowledge Modeling problem. However, DKT is not good at identifying learning from questions that include multiple knowledge components (since the gains on the different KCs may be different) - which in large questions banks may even be worse due to the lack of sufficient QA pairs. 
A second problem is interpretability - the predictions should be interpretable at least. 

Authors propoe Q-MCKT, a framework that intends to address both of these drawbacks by using experts technique to find a better representation of knowledge for both question and concept-level prediction. 

Tests on different benchmark datasets show that Q-MCKT outperforms other Deep Learning-based models in multiple tasks.

references.title: LLM-based Literature Recommender System in Higher Education - A Case Study of Supervising Students' Term Papers

extracted.Target of Recommendation: Literature references and feedback for term paper topic / research question development
extracted.Students: ~70 students in prior supervision analysis; 56 term paper examples in pilot evaluation
extracted.Courses: Bachelor of Arts program in Culture and Social Sciences; term-paper supervision context rather than course recommendation
extracted.Initialization Method: Topic extraction and categorization from student proposal using LLMs; sentiment analysis used to help trigger recommendation mode; literature selected from static corpus with star-based sorting over concept/reference links
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Recommendations generated from each submitted proposal; iterative supervision use case, but no clear automated online updating / retraining reported
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Moderation check; LLM extracts top-k topics with explanations and assigned categories; topic filtering via in-context learning; retrieval from concept-linked external resource corpus; final natural-language feedback generated by LLM
extracted.Recommendation types: Literature recommendations + personalized natural-language feedback / explanations
extracted.Evaluation: Expert evaluation with 2 raters on 56 examples using relevance, citation accuracy, citation consistency, usefulness; Cohen’s kappa: relevance 0.5105, accuracy 0.4746, consistency 0.3805, usefulness 0.4683
extracted.Comments: Interesting because it shifts recommendation from course/resource consumption to academic idea development. Emphasis is on inspiring students, supporting topic refinement, and reducing teacher workload rather than automating writing. Current implementation is only the knowledge-based engine, despite the broader conceptual framework.
extracted.Limitations: Open-ended recommendation setting; no uniform item corpus; small dataset; only pilot expert evaluation; current “star” matching is semantically limited; repeated recommendations / possible popularity bias; creativity evaluation remains unresolved

annotations.text: Authors introduce an LLM-based Recommender for Literature - LRS4TP. The goal of the paper is to formulate research topics and questions to help them in their Papers. It DOES NOT WRITE automatically… instead it acts as supervisor. A test on a Bachelor of Arts program suggests promising results - high quality feedback while freeing time from the professors and teaching staff.

references.title: Competency and Skill-Based Educational Recommendation System
references.journal: International Journal of Artificial Intelligence in Education

annotations.text: Automatic scoring often uses ML to generate a score but often ignore how well the answer maps to the original question. Authors’ approach identifies cognitive difficiencies and subsequently makes recommendations for remediation. Authors present a case study of their system.

references.title: Dropout Prediction in MOOCs: Using Deep Learning for Personalized Intervention

extracted.Target: Dropout vs Active in the following week
extracted.Courses: 1 Project Management MOOC on Canvas (about 8 weeks; 11 modules, 14 discussion forums, 12 quizzes)
extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, Quiz Scores, Discussion Forums
