extracted.Preprocessing Details: Data came from Canvas trace logs plus JSON data retrieved through the Canvas API. A flat feature structure was used. Features from each week were cumulatively added to the previous weeks’ features, so the model at week t used all information up to week t. Data were split into 70% training / 30% testing, and 10-fold cross-validation was used to reduce overfitting. Deep learning models were customized for each week and trained with forward/backward propagation and batch gradient descent.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {AUC: 0.968 average – Deep Learning; Testing Accuracy: 0.930 average – Deep Learning}
extracted.Comments: Main contribution is combining weekly temporal dropout prediction with individual dropout probabilities to support personalized and prioritized intervention. Deep learning outperformed KNN, SVM, and Decision Tree in both predictive performance and stability. Regression tests showed that the dropout probabilities produced by deep learning aligned best with actual dropout timing (p = .000, adjusted R² = 0.0687), supporting its use for personalization.
extracted.Limitations:
Based on a single MOOC course, so generalizability is limited. 

The intervention mechanism is still hypothetical and was not validated in a live intervention study. 

Feature engineering is relatively simple and uses a flat cumulative structure. 

Personalization is statistically supported, but not tested for actual impact on dropout reduction.

annotations.text: MOOCs have very high dropout rates. It is possible to use Analytics to create models that predict whether a student is likely to dropout or not. However, the models available do not have the finesse to enable personalized interventions.

In order to address this, the authors propose a temporal DL model that predicts the probability of a student dropping out or not. The model outperforms other baseline algorithms tested while also returning a probability → meaning that educators can start by trying to help students flagged as more likely to drop out relative to other colleagues.

references.title: Architectural Adaptation and Regularization of Attention Networks for Incremental Knowledge Tracing

extracted.Features: [Sequence of Answers, Questions and Concepts]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
Attention-based KT models are the curent state-of-the-art on multiple datasets. However, most of them assume that the pool of questions does not change after the training of the model. 

Adding questions in online settings would lead to an increase of the input dimension.

Moreover, the changes in behavior of the learners introduce distributional drift.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
The proposed model uses an attention mechanism for questions and concepts that increases dynamically while preserving the information provided by the pre-existing pool of questions. 

In addition, the model weights are regularized.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
This model is based on AKT with a change in the question-concept mapping. 

This approach involves the ability to increase the dimensionality of the attention module whether it is because of the introduction of new concepts or the introduction of new questions.

This is done by copying the weights of the previous question while initializing the new weights for the new question using Rasch embedding.
extracted.Models: J-AKT, D-AKT, ARAIKT, ARAIKT without reg
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a change to the attention representation of AKT and show that it outperforms AKT on the tested dataset. 

Authors also perform an ablation study to showcase the improtance of having regularization or not.
extracted.Limitations: Needs valdiation on additional datasets.

annotations.text: A limitation of the current embedding based representations of KT models is the fact that the number of questions is often fixed. Thus, when EdTech platforms add new questions, the current models cannot follow this update.

Authors propose a model that adapts to growing pools of concepts and/or questions. The model, called ARAIKT rpeserves previous representations while being able to adapt to increasing concepts and question banks.
Testing of the model on portions of the ASSISTment dataset showcase effectiveness of the approach.

references.title: Prerequisites-based course recommendation: recommending learning objects using concept prerequisites and metadata matching

extracted.Data sources: Course metadata, Concept Corpus and Pre-Requisite Relationships,
extracted.Preprocessing Details: The first part of the work is the association between course concepts and concept pre-requisites.
extracted.Comments: Authors present an improvement to traditional educational recommender systems by proposing contents meant to address the course pre-requisites for learrners.

annotations.text: Educational recommender systems have problems with cold-start but also require learners to have pre-requisite knowledge to fully comprehend the received recommendation. The approach starts with creating pairs between concepts and pre-requisites and making this form of labelling using ML.
The similarity between pre-requisites and course metadata are the mechanism used to determine the recommendations. Results of experimental tests reveal promising Accuracy scores.

references.title: Predicting student performance in a blended learning environment using learning management system interaction data

extracted.Courses: 6 Lab Classes in a Simulated Learning Environment
extracted.Features: [Session, StudentID, exercise, activity, Start_time, end_time, idle_time, mouse_wheel, mouse_wheel_click, mouse_click_left, mouse_click_right, keystroke, mouse_movement]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Raw session CSV files were merged into a single dataset. Data from all six sessions were aggregated per student into a dimensional vector. Null, empty, and negative values were removed. Only students attending all six sessions and the final exam were retained and verified through attendance logs. Final numeric marks were binarized into Pass and Fail (62% pass, 38% fail). Feature selection was supported by exploratory correlation analysis via heatmap.
extracted.Models: Random Forest, J48, NBTree, Decision Stump, AdaBoost
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.857 – Random Forest with AdaBoost; Precision: 0.857 – Random Forest with AdaBoost; Recall: 0.857 – Random Forest with AdaBoost; F1-Score: 0.843 – Random Forest with AdaBoost; RMSE: 32.7 – Random Forest with AdaBoost}
extracted.Comments: The study proposes an ML-based educational decision support system to identify at-risk students from LMS interaction data. Random Forest was the best-performing classifier both with and without boosting, and boosting improved all models’ performance. Authors argue the approach supports near real-time or timely intervention, though the actual dataset is aggregated across all sessions.
extracted.Limitations:
mall sample size. 

Course/context description is limited and based on a simulated e-learning environment.

Prediction is framed as early identification, but the operationalization aggregates data across all six sessions.

 Only a small set of behavioral LMS features is used, and the outcome is reduced to binary Pass/Fail.

annotations.text: Student attrition is a significant issue in higher education and analysis of behavioral data in the LMS may be helpful for that.
The paper uses a publicly available dataset to identify student attrition with ML. Findings show that it is possible to use ML to identify students at risk with 85% accuracy (RF).

references.title: Personalized and Automated Feedback in Summative Assessment Using Recommender Systems

annotations.text: Authors use a recommender system to provide automated feedback after summative assessment. The feedback is given in the form of a set of questions that would be benefical for the student in question.b This requires, first, the construction of a knowledge model of the learner and second the identification of the gaps in that knowledge.
Authors do recommendations using multiple popular algorithms. Results on secondary education level show that the recommendations can be done reasonably well without a lot of computational complexity.

references.title: Constructing a personalized learning path using genetic algorithms approach

extracted.Students: 164,196 students in offline data; 70 undergrads in user study
extracted.Data sources: Enrollment histories + catalog descriptions + course meta-info
extracted.Initialization Method: User gives one favorite course; models use learned embeddings (no special cold-start beyond that)
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Models/versioned on GitHub and refreshed 3× per semester after data refresh
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Remove generic sentences, stop words & punctuation; lemmatize/stem; tokenize; build TF, binary, tf-idf vectors
extracted.Evaluation: Offline: mean/median rank & recall@10 (equivalency); recall@1/@10 (analogies). User study: serendipity = mean(unexpectedness, successfulness); 6 dimensions rated
extracted.Comments: BOW (div) scored highest in serendipity/novelty; course2vec best on analogies; RNN lacked novelty and often repeated requirement/major courses
extracted.Limitations: Students rely on catalog text; neural concepts may be hidden; hard to see commonality when descriptions are distal; real-time enrollment lag; future work: add latent semantics/syllabi

annotations.text: Traditional learning has the drawback of being a one-size fits all solution where, despite their differences, all learners follow the same path → and the materials usually recommended follow this linear approach to learning, which may hamper a student’s performance. And, while there is literature on personalizing learning, most of it ignores relationship between concepts or difficulty (focusing on preferences).

The paper uses a Genetic-Algorithm based model that aims to generate optimized learning pathways that account for these constraints. Results show that the quality of the GA approach is higher than traditional learning paths.

references.title: Early Warning System for Online STEM Learning—A Slimmer Approach Using Recurrent Neural Networks

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Passing the course in a specific week
extracted.Features: [Video_views, Number_posts, Did_Midterm, Did_FinalExam, Finished_assignments]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Data is collected every week and concatenated to the previous sequence.
extracted.Models: SRN, LSTM, GRU, MLP, CNN, Logistic Regression, SVM, Decision Tree, Random Forest
extracted.Comments:
Authors do everyweek prediction using a dataset from an online course.

Authors observe that predictive performance tends to increase as the number of weeks advances (which is expected).
extracted.Limitations:
Considers a general education course

Small dataset

annotations.text: CNN models are the most common models used for prediction of learning outcomes (?). Authors argue that RNN based models are more adequate for the task of identifying students at risk.

Authors use data from an Online course in a Taiwanese university. Authors extract multiple features and train their RNN models and benchmark it against other alternatives: SVM, LR, DT, RF, CNN based model and a standard MLP.
Results show that RNN surpasses conventional classifiers and achieves similar performance to the CNN model. Moreover, identification is passive after a third of the semester has passed.

references.title: An Adaptive Learning Path Builder based on a Context Aware Recommender System

extracted.Courses: 1 Fundamentals to Informatics and 1 Costume Design courses
extracted.Data sources:
Questionnaire, Social Networks, 
Contextual Information,
Professor
extracted.Initialization Method:
Item Profiles are created and labeled by professors nd subsequently vectorized.

Student specific data is extracted from the questionnaire after discretization.
extracted.Updates to Recommendations:
Success in modules is recorded by the Knowledge Base Module after obtaining ratings on a specific module.

If the user does not answer the questionnaire, the ratings are updated via inference.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Users and Items have the same dimension fixed in advance and cannot have negative elements.

Users are clustered using cosine similarity.

Context (Location and Day of Week) are added in the Knowledeg-Base Module.
extracted.Recommendation types: Learning Path that addresses student difficulties
extracted.Evaluation:
The test of the system was conducted via survey. Participants reported:

Utility, Reliability, Recommendation, Usability and Performance. These were evaluated in a Likert Scale
extracted.Comments:
In this short paper, the authors create a recommender system framework meant to recommend exercises and materials using “contextual information”.

The system is designed into separate modules:

Data Management & Knowledge Base: The stages at whixh the authors do the data collection and preprocessing.

The RS Moduule and CARS Modules: The RS Module is the main dataflow for the creatioon of the of the PathBuilder. If it fails, then the data goes thrrough CARS which does not include the contextual features. 

Path Builder Module: Does the recommendations.
extracted.Limitations:
Validation via self-reporting.

Small sample size for evaluation of system. 

Unclear form of recommendation.

No assessment of model performance.

annotations.text: There is a big increase in tools and services that now create novel learning pathways for learners. In this scenario, the challenge becomes recommending the correct pathway to the correct student. The paper presents a recommender system that introduces recommendations via multimedia content.
The recommender seems to follow a content-based filtering approach and is tested in 2 courses with different characteristics.

references.title: Early Detection Prediction of Learning Outcomes in Online Short-Courses via Learning Behaviors

annotations.text: Work focuses on short-courses with a single outcome at the end meaning that the courses only have a final evaluation at the end and no intermediate assessments. To address the lack of data, authors rely on Social Network Analysis to obtain new features.
Study retains 3 key observations:

references.title: Transformer Encoder Model for Sequential Prediction of Student Performance Based on Their Log Activities

extracted.Data sources: VLE logs, Learner Characteristics, Course Information
extracted.Features: [id_student, code_module, code_presentation, activity_type, score, is_banked, weight, sum_click, date]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The original table was arranged in a way that the original table was converted into a 4-dimensional tensor: 

(N, D, T, F)

D accounts Days and is of length 270 (full duration). 

Authors also attempt a weekdly aggregation instead of a daily one. 

Authors use 2 layers of Transformational encoders with 100 units each. 

Another notable distinction is the use of a Weighted Loss Function instead of the Standard Binary Cross-Entropy

Authors propose a new architecture to do prediction of End of Course Performance. The proposed architecture uses a Transformer model.

Results on different binary tasks show good results, with daily representations winning. However… these results seem low compared to other methods I’ve seen using OULAD.
extracted.Limitations:
• No other architectures are tested to benchmark against the one used 

  • One Dataset

  • No multiclass setting

  • Only considers Activity Features

annotations.text: The authors propose a new deep learning model to predict student performance using the sequential aspect of interaction logs between a student and an LMS in the hopes of providing support as early as possible.

The authors use OULAD to perform multiple early classification tasks and their proposed model achieves, at the very least, 76% when performing early prediction. When comparing the performance of the Transformer-Encoder model, the authors found that their proposed method outperformed the baselkine LSTM models by 3% to 7% on F1-Score.

Authors also test the effect of different pre-processing options such as positional encoding. In OULAD, the best options had no positional encoding and, in some cases, worked better with weeky feature aggragation and weighted loss functions.

references.title: Effects of Internal and External Conditions on Strategies of Self-regulated Learning: A Learning Analytics Study

extracted.Data sources:
LMS Logs, 
MESH Questionnaire, Pre-Test, Pre-Survey, Post-Test, Transfer Test, Post Survey
extracted.Features: [17 Learning Actions]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors use the MESH questionnaire to see how they perceive different strategies to be effective or not. 

IIn total, the authors identified 24053 instances of SRL processes in their sample. 

These were made to compute the transition probabilities between different actions.
extracted.Comments:
80 participants from the original 253 student sample were excluded.

FOMM were exclusively used to extract sequences of learning behaviors and mapping them to the SRL Processess.

These were subsequently clustered.
extracted.Limitations:
• Findings on external conditions may be biased due to the amount of removed participants.

  • Unclear student motivations to perform task (i.e. learners may have intended to just get a good grade).

extracted.Data sources:
LMS Logs, 
MESH Questionnaire, Pre-Test, Pre-Survey, Post-Test, Transfer Test, Post Survey
extracted.Features: [SRL Processes as Identified by FOMM]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: The SRL processes were identified and mapped according to COPES.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Cluster 1: Read First, Write Next: - 91 members: Reads materials first and then starts composing essays. They rarely go back to read the instructions. These ere the ones that performed better (even if slightly).

Cluster 2: Read and Write Simultaneously - 48 members:  Tended to alternatte more often between reading description and writing materials (to make sure).

Cluster 3: Reading with Quiz: Reads materials with intention of passing on the Quiz.
extracted.Implications: Encouraging learners to Read First and Write next mildly promote the development of knowledge transfer.
extracted.Comments:
80 participants from the original 253 student sample were excluded.

Authors check differences between groups using the ANOVA test and, when normality is not ensured using Kruskall-Wallis
extracted.Limitations:
• Findings on external conditions may be biased due to the amount of removed participants.

  • Unclear student motivations to perform task (i.e. learners may have intended to just get a good grade).

annotations.text: Interpreting SRL attributes merely from behavior is challenging due to potential differences in task and context. Authors propose a new method to extract SRL processes to a dataset of a multi-source writing task.
Authors relate the strategies to outcome, internal and external conditions. Ultimately, they identify 3 learning strategies.

references.title: Predicting Secondary School Students’ Performance Utilizing a Semi-supervised Learning Approach

annotations.text: Authors use 2 wrapper-methods for Semi-Supervised Learning to predict performance of High-School students in their final examinations. Authors argue that the SSL approach significantly increases prediction accuracy, thus leading to more reliable predictive models.

references.title: Analysing User Experience of Dynamic Group Formation (DGF) in Intelligent Tutor Collaborative Learning (ITSCL) using Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis

annotations.text: Dynamic group formation (groups that adjust) is helpful in computer supported collaborative learning settings. The work explores how groups are formed in an ITS designed for collaborative learning and evaluate the user experience of their users. Feedback is collected via a questionnaire.
Results show that users are receptive to the dynaimic group formation feature of the ITS as users report to be satisfied with the system’s flexibility, adaptatibility and interactivity.

references.title: When the Past != The Future: Assessing the Impact of Dataset Drift on the Fairness of Learning Analytics Models

extracted.Features: [Gender, Age, Citizenship Status, Home Language, Last LMS login, Medium of Contact, Study Period, Course Accesses, Resourcee Acesses, Assignment Accesses, Quiz Accesses, Forum Accesses, Folder Accesses, Page Accesses, URL accesses]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The authors decide to do feature selection using the top-5 features for each year unsing the RF feature importances.

To do this, the authors rank the feature importances in each year and select the top-5 moist important ones.

Authors also subset their analysis across distinct demographiic segments:

- English vs Non-English speakers
- Citizens vs Non-Citizens
extracted.Comments:
Most datassets in the real world are “not stationary”.  A fair algorithm trained at time t has no assurance of remaining fair at time t+1…

The authors m,ake an assessment of dataset drift in Learning Analytics student success models in 2 perspectives… how performance is affected from one academic year to next (it is…) and also how this is affected in terms of model fairness when comparing generral student populations (citizens orr english speakers vs other demographics).
extracted.Limitations:
- Small number of courses in STEM.

- Vulnerable to changes in instructional conditions from year to year.

- Only tested on Randoom Forest

- No early prediction

annotations.text: The authors analyse the drift in features (covariate drift) and drift of the target. The results show that the features are the most responsible for unfairness of the models in the dynamic context. Moreover, there is also a proportional relationship between drift and model unfairness. Finally, there is no guarantee that a fair model will remain fair → requiring models to be robust before deployment.

references.title: A learning analytic approach to unveiling self-regulatory processes in learning tactics

extracted.Features: [Task Analysis, Goal Setting, Working on Task, Coordinating Learning Task Sequences, Evaluating]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors defined that a session would consist of an whole day. 

The labelling of the learning actions was not specifically focused on just counting of different actions on the LMS and, instead, mapped actions to specific learning actions, which would then be used to identify micro-level SRL processes. 

Micro-level processes were then associated to macro-level SRL behaviors.
extracted.Cluster Description: Authors obtain a matrix with the probabilities of transitions between learning actions. This will be the input
extracted.Comments: The first part of the work is to use FOOM to identify common sequences of actions. Because of information provided by instructors, the matrix is generated considering a whole day as a session.
extracted.Limitations:
Quality of the trace data

Definition of learning sessions

extracted.Features: [Session Matrices from FOMM]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors defined that a session would consist of an whole day. 

The labelling of the learning actions was not specifically focused on just counting of different actions on the LMS and, instead, mapped actions to specific learning actions, which would then be used to identify micro-level SRL processes. 

Micro-level processes were then associated to macro-level SRL behaviors.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: Gap Statistic
extracted.Cluster Description:
Tactic 1: Reading wuth Quizz - Reading oriented with an additional emphasis on Quizzes → the main tactic based even on the way the course was designed.

Tactic 2: Assessment and Interaction - Opposite of the first, almost 0 Reading. Mostly used to participate in assessments and interact with peers and teachers. 

Tactic 3: Short Login and Interact - The most frequent tactic of the course, with the shortest learning sessions → students login and check announcements.

Tactic 4: Focus on Quiz - Quiz related activities.
extracted.Implications:
Informing seems to be a relevant construct for all Learning Tactics that were identified.

Most micro-level processes adopted by students were part of engagement and evaluation… with planning being considerably less frequent. 

Learning actions can be converted into learning processes. 

Adoption of SRL-focused approaches may bridge the gap of “context-specific models and assist in the creation of more universal models”.
extracted.Comments:
The first part of the work is to use FOOM to identify common sequences of actions.

Authors obtain clusters and map the micro-SRL processes to the different sequences of actions. A MANOVA Test is conducted to show that all 5 micro-processes are statistically significant.
extracted.Limitations:
Quality of the trace data

Definition of learning sessions

annotations.text: Trace data has historically been used to identify different learning tactics. Authors propose a learning approach to identify and compare learning tactics. Authors identify 4 learning tactics in data from 728 learners and theorize five micro-processes.

Authors analyzed how those processese were activated when engaging with the 4 learning tactics. Analysis between learning strategies shows signifcant differences on the 5 micro-processes, showing that trace data has the potential to highlight different types of behaviors.

references.title: Promote collaborations in online problem‑based learning in a user experience design course: Educational design research

annotations.text: Authors propose an online educational design for collaborative learning in Problem-Based Learning using Group Contracts and Peer-Evaluation. Both conscructs were found to be helpful in helping group members achieve their goals, providing useful insights for online learning UX design.

references.title: Building Knowledge Graphs and Recommender Systems for Suggesting Reskilling and Upskilling Options from the Web

annotations.text: The availability of resources that now existes makes it really challenging for prospective learners to select the best sets of resources available to them. Authors collect data from providers and use Named Entity Recognition and entity linking to extract information about pre-requisites, learning objectives, etc…

These representations are incorporated into a Knowledge Graph that is used as a basis for a recommender system for career path and upskilling suggestions. Model is evaluated using CareerCoach 2022 gold standard.

references.title: Enhancing Automated Scoring of Math Self‑Explanation Quality Using LLM‑Generated Datasets: A Semi‑Supervised Approach

annotations.text: Despite its effectiveness in math education, self-explanations seem to be an underused source of data about student learning processes. However, with new advancements in NLP it may now be possible to use them. Thus, the authors propose the use of an LLM to automatically grade mathematical self-explanations and provide personalzied feedback based on them.

Authors use 5 distinct datasets for validation. Results show that combining LLM explanations with amthematical material improves the accuracy of such an auto-grader model. Authors also find that there is a cap in synthetic self-explanations before the model starts to overfit.

references.title: Semi-Supervised Classification in Educational Data Mining: Students’ Performance Case Study

annotations.text: The authors attempt to perform semi-supervised learning using WEKA. Authors use preprocessing and feature selection and, ultimately evaluate performance using accuracy.

references.title: An automatic group composition system for composing collaborative learning groups using enhanced particle swarm optimization

annotations.text: Collaborative learning intends to maximize learning performance for all students. Understanding levels and interests of students are two grouping criteria usually used. However, as the number of students increases this problem becomes larger and larger.
The works starts with formulating a group formulation problem and using Particle Swarm Optimization to address it. Results show potential in the adopted approach.

Computer Supported Collaborative Learning has become increasingly popular due to being a more convenient experience for both instructors and students. Well structured groups tend to work better than poorly structured groups.

Grouping criteria are multi-faceted as they involve multiple factors such as learning achievement, learning style and/or interpersonal relationships. The former 2 are understood to be real-world classroom problems by instructors. Grouping based on knowledge can encourage better discussions and interactions. Also, different interests also have been shown to enhance learning.

Group formation is a NP-hard problem which means that finding an optimal solution scales with the data. Since, in practice, finding optimal groups is not possible, the general options are random assignment OR letting students choose which group to belong to. This of course has consequences has groups may never be enough to learn OR have all members not learn anything themselves.
This brings us to PSO, an algorithm that, in 2010 was considered recent and being used to address these NP-Hard problems.

Based on the promising signs shown by Swarm Intelligence, the authors propose EPSO to use student interests and levels of understanding to form groups. The approach is compared against other competing methods of group formation.

PSO is derived from social behavior. Animals such as birds or fish seem to behave intelligently when in a complex system such as being part of a group. Thus, the main idea of Eberhart and Kennedy was to propose an algorithm where an individual’s behavior is not only determined by itself, but also based on the behavior of the remainder of the group.

Thus, PSO does not use individuals but, instead, uses the notion of a swarm of particles that are distributed throughout the problem space. Each particle is an hypothetical solution. PSO optimization uses a fitness function that uses the particle’s location and then applies a velocity and direction.
In short parlance:
Thus, training a PSO involves, for each particle, computing the direction and velocity that it should take towards the best solution. This is repeated iteratively.
Grouping students can be formulated as a problem addressable accoring to 2 distinct criteria:
Working under the assumption that a course teaches k topics and there are n students.
2 constraints:
This required some encoding in EPSO:
Essencially, each particle will feature a list of all students and each student will be assigned to a group and a specific position on that group: example:
The fitness function is defined as a minimization problem. The goal is to minimize:
This PSO uses elitism to ensure that the best particle survives.

Authors create the groups on a LMS called ANTS. The authors created an automatic group composition system. The study proposes a 4-step approach to creating groups in a copllaborative learning environment:
The authors proceeded to analyze the effectiveness of their EPSO approach. The experiment starts with a set of 9 simulated datasets comprising 15 to 2000 students.

The level of understanding and interest in each topic were randomly initialized for the purposes of the experiment - in a real world, there would be a diagnostic test to set the prior knowledge in each of the topics.
The authors decided to run EPSO with 5-20 particles and iterations. Optimal value of fitness function was computed by averaging 10 runs on the 9 datasets - best results found with 10 particles.
Comparison against exhaustive method on 2 dimensions: value of the fitness function and computation time. Exhaustive method finds better fitness but becomes computationally unfeasable.
Random Search vs EPSO. In this method, EPSO clearly outperforms in terms of fitness even if it becomes computationally molre expensive than the random method very early on.

Authors look at the standard deviation of the fitness function in order to measure the dispersion of the optima found for each run of the EPSO. This was compared against that obtained by the random method:
The tests were run on different numbers of students. EPSO tended to remain robust (i.e. with a small standard deviation) for all numbers of students considered (excepting at 15 iterations).

Experiment was repeated by setting the number of students as fixed and ewqual to 500 while the number of topics changed. Results tended to be similar, with EPSO showing very small standard deviations when it comes to the values of the best soilution for the fitness function.

references.title: Evaluating Sentence‐BERT‐powered learning analytics for automated assessment of students' causal diagrams

annotations.text: The authors compare 2 embedding models combined with standard ML algorithms in terms of indicators of automated diagram assessmet reliability. The goal is to estimate the correct position of a student diagram in a causal chain.

Thus, the authors want to use the fact that students create causal diagrams for studying to create automatic assessments on that causal diagram. This is a specific case of learning where students use causal relation diagrams to consolidate their knowledge.

references.title: Fuzzy-based active learning for predicting student academic performance using autoML: a step-wise approach
references.journal: Journal of Computing in Higher Education

extracted.Courses: 3 courses: Physics III, Phjysical Chemistry I & Analtical Chemistry Labs
extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, Intermediate and Final Grades
extracted.Features: [Number of Views per module]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Features are extracted from the logs themselves using a plugin.

These are subsequently given to a set of fuzzy learning algorithms.

At the second stage authors 4 distinc strategies:

Uncertainty Sample with LC, Hinted SVM, QUIRE and Random Sampling.
extracted.Models: FMARR, MFPC AIWA/OWA, FPC GA/LGA, PT, PTTD, FPC OWA, Naive Bayesm IBk-5NN, Random Forest, GBoost
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Friedman Aligned Rank Accuracy: 11.2 - PT,  Friedman Aligned Rank AUC: 15 - PTTD, Friedman Aligned Rank AUC: 15.1 - PTTD}
extracted.Comments:
Authors use AutoML to do hyper.-parameter tuning od different fuzzy learning algorithms to predict a student’s ability to pass our fail a course.

Even after comparison using Standard ML algorithms, the rank performance of the Fuzzy Classifiers beats the remaining models.

Tests using the different forms of resampling show that Hinted SVM or QUIRE are decent techniques.
extracted.Limitations:
No consideration of adding other data sources.

No explanation of learning using resources not available in the LMS.

Uncreative feature engineering and extraction.

annotations.text: While ML to predict student performance is popular, there is a growing need to create models that are accurate but at a low-cost, something fuzzy-models can do. Then, it is important to choose optimal method and optimal parameters for a model. Authors explore the potential of AutoML to predict student performance. Multiple experiments seem to show approach is accurate.

references.title: Leveraging LMS Logs to Analyze Self-Regulated Learning Behaviors in a Maker-based Course

extracted.Features: [Goal Setting, Making Personal Plans, Working on a Task, Reformulation, Evaluation, Reflection]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors take logs and associate different activities as part of wider SRL constructs. In total, there were 63716 logged events, out of which 48602 were labelled.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: Mininum support of 0.40 and minimum confidence of 0.6.
extracted.Cluster Description:
High PErforming Students:

Students tended to reflect more on their SRL behaviors. Specifically, these students have a higher proneness of Reformulation→ Reflection than their peers. 

Low-Performing Students

Less prone to reflect on their SRL behaviours.
extracted.Implications:
High performance students are often better at planning and reflecting their peers.

Low performing students often focus on fulfilling academic obligations rather than promoting their work and development.

Instructors can provide specific prompts to “force” their students to reflect more on their SRL behaviors.
extracted.Comments: Authors use Sequential Pattern mining to identify different transitions of learning behavior.
extracted.Limitations: Only uses one very general course.

annotations.text: The work explores the usage of Logs in maker-based pedagogy to generate evidence of SRL. The authors use frequencies and sequential patterns for that using a mixed-method approach. They developed a log-based analytic framework for development of SRL phases for maker activities.

Dataset uses 48602 logged events and 131 excerpts from student reflections. Segmentation is done on High-Performance vs Low-Performing students. High-Performing students exhibited SRL behaviours more frequently.

references.title: Algorithmic group formation and group work evaluation in a learning analytics-enhanced environment: implementation study in a Japanese junior high school

annotations.text: The authors propose a learning analytics framework for collaborative learning which they coin as Group Learning Orchestrated Based on Evidence (GLOBE) which requires algorithmic group formation. The authors test their framework on 12 collaborative learning activities - testing both homogenous and heterogenous group formation.

The groups formed algorithmically were shown to perform better than random grouping. Moreover, the peer satisfaction  and self-perception were higher for students in the homogenous groups for the idea exchange tasks. In the reading tasks, there were no significant differences.

references.title: Supporting self-regulated learning and personalization using ePortfolios: a semantic approach based on learning paths
references.journal: International Journal of Educational Technology in Higher Education

annotations.text: The literature supports the use of portfolios and semantic technologies to improve the pedagogical aspects of the electronic tools being used. The paper presents a conceptual model for such a system, including the desing of learning paths based on a learner’s current level.
Learning paths are rules-based (onthologically defined).

references.title: Multi-Modal LA in Personalized Education Using Deep Reinforcement Learning Based Approach

annotations.text: Technology has brough many changes to educational landscape. The authors propose KNIGHT, an AI system that is meant to revolutionize personalized education.
The system incorporates multi-modal data integration and deep Reinforcement Learning for Analytics. Some ethical considerations are also discussed.

references.title: A Course Agnostic Approach to Predicting Student Success from VLE Log Data Using Recurrent Neural Networks
references.journal: Data Driven Approaches in Digital Education

extracted.Features: [Moodle Accesses in each week]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Extraction of the weekly number of logins on Moodle from the logs. Data comprises exam sittings across 5 years.

Dummy “Average”
LSTM
Random Forest
LSTM - 0.2 D
LSTM - 0.5 D

extracted.Comments:
Work uses RNNs to predict final exam grade at each course’s halfway point. Specific criteria on target interpretation are not present.

Model Assessment Strategy is not specified, but assumed to be Holdout given that this is a Deep Learning approach.

Dropout on LSTM models does not result in a significant difference to other LSTM models.
extracted.Limitations:
Interpretation of Target Variable is not clearly defined.

Model assessment strategy is not specified.

Ambiguous rules for determination of best model.

Obtained R-Squared is verly low despite outperforming baseline model.

Exclusively uses LMS, not considering other data sources.

annotations.text: Authors use RNNs to predict whether a student will succeed or not. Experiment is performed on all students of a university, regardless of the course. Authors claim to achieve good performance even though performance is unspecified.

The purpose of the work is to use logs to do early prediction on the final exam at an institution-wide level. The reason for this approach is the small sample size of the individual courses, even considering that the data includes 5 years. The initial sample considers 255659 exam sittings.

The reason for a course -agnostic approach comes from the fact that going course-specific would require cutting courses and any new course would not be able to start with a model already available. The paper, thus, proposes a RNN-based course-agnostic model to perform the task.

The work is inspired by Course Signals, a system that identifies students in need and then informs faculty to help students. The advantage of using an architecture that works with sequences is that it does not require extraction of meta-features from the logs.

The authors decide to use RNN-based models to make the prediction. They also decide to make a comparison with other models, specifically a regressor that predicts the average grade on exams and a Random Forest. Results show that the LSTM models are the ones who achieve the lowest MSE.

references.title: Capacity Tracing-Enhanced Course Recommendation in MOOCs

extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, Course metadata, Responses for Cognitive Diagnosis, Knowledge Topics,
extracted.Initialization Method:
– MIRT static fit: estimate initial learner capacity uᵢ₀ and course difficulty dⱼ via maximum likelihood on ideal response qᵢⱼ


– Implicit response pᵢⱼ computed as geometric mean of completion ratio aᵢⱼ and relative duration tᵢⱼ/ Dⱼ
extracted.Updates to Recommendations:
– After each new interaction aᵢⱼ, update learner capacity uᵢ,ₜ by Δuᵢ = pᵢ,ₜ·λᵢ·ReLU(dⱼ – uᵢ,ₜ₋₁) (Eq 4), where λᵢ is per-learner growth rate

– Form embeddings: lᵢ,ₜ = concat(g(uᵢ,ₜ₋₁), l′ᵢ), cⱼ = concat(g(dⱼ), c′ⱼ), then predict with NCF or GRU by optimizing cross-entropy loss
extracted.Preprocessing Details: – Online setting: no offline train/test split for model fitting.– Compute pᵢⱼ via Eq 2; filter out records with missing aᵢⱼ or unknown
extracted.Evaluation: {Precision@10: 0.0715 - CT-E NCF, Precision@20: 0.1149 - CT-E NCF, Precision@40: 0.1827 - CT-E NCF, Hit_Ratio@10: 0.1605 - CT-E NCF, Hit_Ratio@20: 0.1290 - CT-E NCF, Hit_Ratio@40: 0.1025 - CT-E NCF}
extracted.Comments:
– Combines explainable MIRT capacities with CF.

– CT-E NCF yields highest Precision & Hit gains over all baselines.

– Capacity tracing correlates strongly with static MIRT estimates (ρ ≈ 0.79–0.99)
extracted.Limitations:
- No user study; offline only.
- Static dⱼ (no difficulty update).
- Assumes weak interactions suffice for pᵢⱼ.
- No specialized cold-start beyond MIRT init.

- Assumes completion ≈ mastery (may misestimate short sessions).

- No modeling of temporal drift beyond immediate updates.

annotations.text: Online features a very high dropout rate and, for that reason, tools for proper recommendations and tutoring are welcomed. Authors explore how item-response theory can be integrated into a recommender.

Authors use mutidimensional IRT and KT techniques to be able to diagnose a learner’s skills (so, first step is the creation of a knowledge model). Then, this model based on MIRT is used as input to a collaborative filtering algorithm for course recommendations.
Experiments on a real dataset show that approach not only improves effectiveness of MOOCs, but its explainability as well.

references.title: Elements of Success: Supporting at-risk student resilience through learning analytics

annotations.text: Many learning analytics applications for students at risk now show information to students, which may be even more discouraging for students who receive a notification that they are at risk. Authors use the platform elements of success to show students how they are progressing on a weekly basis.

Paper examines relationship between student a risk usage of Elements of Success and their performance. Not only did the authors find that there was no relationship between knowing to be at risk and student withdrawal, the authors also found that students who checked the system actually were more likely to earn a passing grade relative to peers who never checked how they were doing.

annotations.text: Analysis of logs requires the transformation of the time-series-esque set of data into meaningful SRL constructs. The authors explore a different way of mapping interactions to SRL construct: Trace Parsing Methodology. They attempt to use 4 different forms of trace parsing and conclude that the choice of parsing method clearly affects process identification and sequencing.

references.title: Genetic Algorithms for Optimizing Grouping of Students Classmates in Engineering Education
references.journal: International Journal of Information and Education Technology

annotations.text: Use of genetic algorithms to group students of engineering. There have been previous utilizations of such a methodology to create groups in lab assignments or other parts involving collaborative learning.

Authors evaluate the impact of their approach about the impact on collaborative performance, processes and perceptions. REsults show that there is a positive impact and that students tend to see this approach as “fair”.

Student grouping should incorporate a multi-variate and multi-faceted approach. Students have their individual technical capabilities but other traits such as motivation to learn and/or character development should also be considered. This brings us into 2 distinct forms of grouping students:

It is important to have good classes in order to avoid situations where the best students are all bunched up into a class or another that cannot progress as well as others because it lacks “someone at the level” which can make the gaps between different classes become increasingly larger. The authors then transition into associating genetic algorithms with the constructivist perspective of learning (which I won’t deny, is definitely odd). Regardless, the paper aims to use genetic algorithms to create distinct classes that are:
Explanation of what GAs are follows:

Objective of the paper is to make better classes with GA. Better classes in this context refers to creating classes that enhance academic achievement, promote participation and engagement and foster diversity within groups.

Definition of population: students will be selected from a random study programme - in this case Computer Engineering with 114 students. Students will be classified according to 11 criteria: Entrance, School of Origin, Gender, IPA value, IPS score, Language Score, Math Score, GPA, hometown, religion and Parent’s Income.

Genetic Algorithm: Big initial blunder mentioning that Darwin discovered Genetic Algorithms. But in general, Genetic algorithms have different individuals be a potential solution and these individuals are continuously crossed with one another until we get new individuals and new individuals. Each individual then represents a potential solution.

Other studies have already used GAs in education. Moreno used them to create well-balanced student groups according to academic performance. The fitness function for that test was an ANOVA - a statistical method meant to compare means between groups. The findings suggest that GA was better than the remaining methods at generating wel-balanced groups ( so, minimizing between group differences??).
So, how does this genetic algorithm work?

Generate 100 random individuals. Each gene refers to the criteria possessed by the students individidual that is part of the class whereas each chromossome refers to the combination of individuals to be used - lenght of 16.
At this stage the authors compute the fitness values of each chromossome. If criteria for optimality are met stop, otherwise form a new generation.

Fitness is going to be computed as a measure of diversity. The higher the diversity in fitness values, the “more fit a chromosome” is going to be. Thus, the fitness function can be understood as a “count of unique genes”.
Selection involves hill climbing - individuals with the highest fitness are kept.

The students were divided into 12 classes of 16 students each → problem formulation. These students are assigned randomly before the start. From this random assignment, the total fitness of this solution is 703.

The process, involves assigning a class code to each individual. Then, there is a score called ATT that is computed as a measure of uniqueness of the individual. The fitness function is the count of this ATT. Authors employ crossover to prevent premature convergence.

Final solution achieves a Fitness value of 701. When comparing this solution against that of a k-means clustering, the authors argue that there is a lot more diversity on the grouping done by genetic algorithms in comparison with the one obtained via k-means (I’ll be honest, I don’t understand how you can look at these graphs and conclude anything, but ok).
My guess is that the bars on the GA solution are not as “even” means that there is more in-class variability??? Is that it?
Findings “demonstrate - very loose term” that genetic algortihms are an effective way of producing diverse groups of students - thus promoting inclusive learning practices.
Limitations of GAs:

references.title: Classification of educational videos by using a semi-supervised learning method on transcripts and keywords

extracted.Comments: Paper focuses on using transcripts on educational videos to label them according to discipline. In essence, the paper offers a way of automating parts of the Recommender System Creation.

annotations.text: In a context where new resources are always becoming available, it becomes increasingly difficult to index them in search or recommender systems.

Authors use a semi supervised topic modelling approach to classify educational videos. Idea is to automate the process of labelling of the videos, which will make them easier to find in search or recommender systems. Results show that approach proposed with this method beats other alternatives.

references.title: Student’s performance prediction based on an improved multi-view hypergraph neural network
references.journal: Journal of Experimental & Theoretical Artificial Intelligence

extracted.Students: 3563 students from an University in Zhejiang
extracted.Data sources: Course Performance History, Once-Card Consumption, Library Usage, Cellphone Use Time, Vacation Information
extracted.Features: [Student Information, Breakfast Behavior, Lunch Behavior, Dinner Behavior, Shopping Behavior, Library Behavior, No-Cellphone Behavior, Vacation Behavior]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors combine data from multiple sources to create a dataset. For each one of hte features, the authors extract multiple features: Frequency, Shannon entropy, Average Time, Standard Deviation, Mode, Min, Max, Q1, Q3, Median, Latest, Location, etc…

Authors select eatures using ANOVA and Pearson Correlation. Additionally, the class imbalance is addressed using oversampling: SMOTE, Borderline-SMOTE and class_weights.

The data is combined to create a multiview hypergraph meant to find similarities (differences) between learners - using an adjacency matirx.
extracted.Models: SVM, kNN, Decision Tree, MLP, GCN, GTN, HGNN, IMHNN
extracted.Comments:
Authors create a predictive model of GPA prediction that involves the creation of hypergraphs.

Authors collect data from the first 5 months and create multiple datasets for multiple moments of prediction. The model, which uses the hypergraph as input to a neural network outperforms all other baselines used on all datasets.

The authors perform an ablation study on the different elements of the network. Accuracy is higher (but not that much) in comparison with ResNet and Egat_Conv.
extracted.Limitations:
- No temporal representation

- Does not account for important predictors related with learner characteristics.

- Data used refers to a very specific context

- Very specific to a university that has the means to extract (and use) a lot of sensitive information.

annotations.text: Current models to predict academic performance focus on attributes of the students but not the relationships between the students themselves. Authors propose a multi-view Hypergraph neural network to address this issue:
Comparison of this model against other models ion the task of predicting student performance shows that the model outperforms baseline.

references.title: A learner oriented learning recommendation approach based on mixed concept mapping and immune algorithm

extracted.Target of Recommendation: Sequence of Learning Objectives meant to achieve the learner’s goals
extracted.Initialization Method:
Learners are initially modelled by survey answers.

Learning Objectives are defined by basic variables and Expert Experience and Previous Feedbacks
extracted.Updates to Recommendations:
Done using an Immune Algorithm:

Optimization done via a fitness function at the end of each recommendation.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Relationship between Learners and LOs is done via Mixed Concept Mapping - uncovered via Association Rule Mining. 

Mixed concept map is updated by learner interactions with the system.
extracted.Recommendation types: Top-1 Learning Objective Learner should follow
extracted.Evaluation:
Fitness function: 
0,06 (IA)

Number of Recommended LOs: 
144 LOs (IA)

Time on Recommended LOs: 137 (IA)

Evolution Time (s): 4.7 (IA)

Satisfaction Survey:
IA achieved highest gobal satisfaction score, especially when it comes to Flow experience Evaluation and Pertinence.
extracted.Comments:
Optimization of the recommender algorithm is done via the Immune Algorithm.

Comparison is done versus groups of students who received recommendations done by other algorithms: GA, Ant Colony Optimization (ACO);
PSO, Traditional Teaching Metods.

Model trained via IA outperforms all other across all metrics tested: students achieve better performance by learning from less materials and spending less time on them.
extracted.Limitations:
Requires learners to fill questionnaire for user intialization.

Small sample sie (requries validation on other datasets).

annotations.text: Heuristic recommender systems have shown some effectiveness in the educational context but often lack the ability to generalize. Thus, the authors use a 2-tiered approach:
The authors conclude that their approach presents high adaptability and efficiency in e-learning recommendations.

In an unstructured scenario of learning such as MOOCs, it often becomes challenging to enhance the learning experience which results in high dropout and low acquisition of knowledge. Personalized learning can assist in the creation of proper learning objectives that are suited to the learners.
The authors cite that recommender systems can be grouped into 4 categories:

The authors focus heavily on content-based filtering and discuss that these systems can be effective in the generation of learning paths that match the learner’s goals. However, this involves creeating an adequate Constraint Satisfaction Problem that matches competency, Learning objectives and other constraints - a very complex task.

Previous work has focused on identifying learner needs and making recommendations that would address those difficulties - sometimes making adaptations along the way. However, there are also other factors that influence learning that have not been accounted for.

In this work, the authors start with the application of concept mapping to get a sense of the learner and the Learning Objectives. The novelty comes from the fact that it is a mixed concept mapping that take the learner’s attributes and learning goals to combine them into a LO concept map. Genetic Algorithms have also been used for this task, but the authors argue that it is vulnerable to parameter setting.

Other evolutionary options exist but the authors argue that Immune System approach is probably the best for this type of setting - and there is already some research in the area. One such case is the use of Immune Algorithms to create a nearly optimal test sheet in order to meet multiple assessment criteria.
Adapting the IA approach to recommendations is challenging. The author’s approach uses learner preference carachteristics into immune mechanisms and vaccine inoculation.

Modelling learners and their style is a complex problem, with many possible categorizations. There are a lot, and I mean A LOT of approaches that allow for the representation of learners: heuristic-based, clustering, Bayesian…

Thus, the authors developed their own questionnaire to identify learning styles based on the following constituents. Some of the constituents are straighforward - Look at the past and see how the student behaves to get a better sense of what the learner’s preferences are:
Point 4 - Learner attitude evaluation is more of a pickle for me. At least for now it is unclear how this is measured.
The latent subjective attributes are obtained from a 69 question questionnaire that combines other sources.
Learner representation - Formally:

The authors put a clear emphasis on matching learning objectives with the learning style. The reason for this is straightforward: if a learner’s learning goals are not mappable to a learning objective then there is more incentive for the learner to not learn - and possibly fail the course.
Learning objective modelling also considers two other carachteristics:
Mixed concept mapping is used to establish the relationship between learners and the learning objectives:

Following the rules mined often leads to a better learning experience and, consequently, a better learning outcome. Straying away from it is argued to lead to tiredness or depression. This form of association rule mining allows the identification of a pathway of a student based on his/her attributes. However, it may be considered very inneficient computationally.

To address the inefficiency, the authors also created a dynamic k-means approach that takes as input the learner attributes as centroids to cluster the learning resources. So… you take information about the students and use them as centroids - Then, try to find which learning resources are “more similar” to the current centroids (this part is somewhat unclear to me, may need to revise it if important).

The problem of scalability identified previously is addressed via what the authors call a Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP) that is meant to serve as a fitness function. This function should, essencially, be interpreted as:
Minimize weighted sum of penalties across multiple dimensions:
P1 - mismatch between the recommended LO difficulty and the learner’s attitude while taking into consideration the importance of the LO’s).
P2 - Difference between competency of student and the LO’s importance and difficulty
P3 - Difference between time available (planned time) and the time required to complete the LO
P4 - Difference between media type of the resource and the learner’s preferred media type
P5 - Intended to measure situations where the content of recommended LO DOES NOT match the learner’s preferences - when students are required to make the chores essencially,
P6 - Exists when the recommended LO cannot satisfy previous or post-sequence constraints
P7 - Penalty for when highly correlated resources are not recommended jointly
The authors consider LO recommendation can be treated as a problem of partition and permutation of all possible LOs. All possible permutations are represented using IA and integer coding.
The authors use two antibody evaluation mechanisms - antibody fitness and antibody concentration.
REcommendations are based on the materials of modules 4-6 (out of 10). The course starts with traditional method of delivery (1 to 3) and also ends in traditional method (7 to 10).
The modules under review can be subdivided into 11 different subjects and 235 Learning Outcomes. There are also some sequential constraints into play that make the problem a little harder.
The evaluation of the results was done in a two-fold manner:
IA seems to generate pretty satisfactory results from the author's POV.

Students who had recommendations done from this method had more free time, checked less resources and were still among the top performing members at the end of the semester. Overall, IA also was the option that was more positively received by the student population.

The experimental approach of using the Mixed Concept Mapping and the Vaccine innoculation also showed promising results as the algorithm seemed to not only converge fast, but to also converge closer to optimal solutions.

In general, the study is a good showcase about the buzz around personalized learning. When leaving students unattended on what to study or how to study it they tend to follow what can be considered a lot of inneficient and/or redundant paths which may lead to a low feeling of progress on a larger amount of time dedicated and, consequently, higher degrees of frustration and demotivation.

The authors propose a different way of representing students that considers their knowledge but also their motivations and preferences. The relationship between learners and resources was uncovered using a mixed concept mapping that, among other things, uses Association Rules.

Making, for every student, a recommendation based on the association rules uncovered is a computationally impossible task, which brings us to the use of Optimization Algorithms to make those recommendations. The authors design a special Immune Algorithm and compare how well it performs against other optimization algorithms such as PSO and GA.
Future work will consider improvements to the performance of the algorithm and the way student knowledge is represented.
