annotations.text: Paper uses data from interactions with an e-learning system to predict the final exam grade of a couse. Work involves a specific minng tool from Moodle. Models use the Plugin to extract logs, preprocess them and compare different predictive models.

Authors also look at the effects of discretization and rebalancing of the data and its impact on Model performance. This is followed by some examples and a discussion of the importance of accuracy and interpretability of these models for decision making.

Learning Management System are becoming more and more important in higher education. These systems bring multiple advantages such as fostering communication and sharing between participants. Moreover, the LMS collect A LOT of educational data which is good → more data allows for more analysis.

However, these large amounts of data also become close unmanageable as the systems themselves do not have intuitive means of extracting and analyzing this data. Data mining may be useful and, at the time of writing, was starting to gain popularity for multiple educational applications → most of them involving the prediction/classification of student performance.
The authors make a discussion of targets and models used:

Thus, goal of work is to compare different algorithms to see which one is the best at classifying students (predict final grade) using Data Mining techniques. In total, the authors will try and compare the performance of 21 distinct algorithms.
The authors developed a Data Mining tool that would be oriented to 2 distinct types of users:
The Moodle used by these authors is made out of 145 tables. The pipeline is divided into 3 steps:

The authors make 2 distinct experiments to validate the performance and usefulness of their classification algorithms. For that, the authors use data from 438 students attending 7 Online Engineering Courses - Universitylevel but unspecified whether Graduate or Undergrads.

The authors then make a comparison of the performance of their different algorithms to predict the final grade of a student. Authors select the moment of prediction as the likely end of the courses because that is the moment when there is more data.

Results show that using the entire dataset seems to generate the best overall accuracy, with the results being much poorer when cutting rows and slightly poorer when removing some features. 14 algorithms (out of 21) have better performance on all features and data.

On a second experiment, the authors introduced new changes to the data: Discretization of the features (using equal-width rules to split the features into 3 distinct categories) and Resampling (using Random Oversampling).

Essencially, the best overall accuracies are obtained using either the numerical or categorical features and not rebalancing the data. However, to achieve, on average, better results on the Geometric Mean of Accuracies, then training the models on Rebalanced Data seem to achieve better results.

Due to the sensitive nature of educational data, the authors discuss the importance of interpretable models with Decision Tree based models being at the forefront of this interpretability. These are followed by Rule Induction Algorithms and Fuzzy Rule Algorithms. Finally, Statistical and NN methods are considered the less interpretable.

references.title: Automatic Group Formation in a MOOC Based on Students’ Activity Criteria
references.journal: Data Driven Approaches in Digital Education

annotations.text: The effectiveness of collaboration in large courses such as MOOCs is under-researched. MOOCs rarely require the formation of groups or, even if they allow it, educators often can not put criteria for formation.

The paper compares a group formation proposal (activity-based) against a baseline grouping function of the platform. Approaches are compared in terms of group performance, activity and student satisfaction.

references.title: Online help-seeking occurring in multiple computer-mediated conversations affects grades in an introductory programming course

extracted.Courses: 1 CS1 course from a private american university

annotations.text: Online help-seeking is a complex phenomenom with many intricacies that ultimately have an effect on student performance. However, when studying the phenomenon, researchers often treat help-seeking as a factor to be isolated from remaining context.
The analysis of the behavvior of a set of 301 CS students shows that there are, in fact, differences in help-seeking behavior:
Findings highlight the potential of using help-seeking behavior as a m,arker for potential student performance and the re-design of educational resources in ointroductory programming.

references.title: Contrasting prediction methods for early warning systems at undergraduate level

extracted.Features: [Resource Usages per week]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors exclusively use the fine-grained data from the LMS of interactions with the folders on both Weekdays and Sunday.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: BIC
extracted.Cluster Description:
Cluster 1 (n=6): Lower total views in general and, correspondingly, lower final result.

Cluster 2 (n=69): More views on Weekdays than on Sunday.

Cluster 3 (n=61):
More views on on Sunday than Weekdays, even though not by a substantial margin. 

Performance is indistinguishable for both of these clusters.
extracted.Implications: Cluster show that more activity tends to lead to better performance. However, whether the levels of activity come more during weekdays or weekends does not seem to have a significant impact.
extracted.Comments: Clustering via GMM is introduced as a new approach to feature generation rather than what is usually used.
extracted.Limitations:
ingle course Data

May not generalize well to outside STEM

extracted.Data sources: LMS Logs, Institutuional Data, Learner Results
extracted.Features: [Demographics, Resource Usages per week]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors collect information from the Student Registration System and  of the Student Interactions.

Each week has a specific folder that contains the materials meant for the week → thus there is, at max, a total of 15 stages.

Authhors also compare 4 feature configurations:

  1. Background, Continuous Assessment and Logs, 
  2. Background and Continuous Assessment,
  3. Background, Continuous Assessment and cumulative activity for each folder,
  4. Cluster variables
extracted.Models: Random Forest, BART, XGBoost, Principal Components Regression, SVM, MLP, Adaptive Regression Splines, kNN
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {MAE: 6.5 -  Week 6 BART with Cumulative Variables and Cluster Variables}
extracted.Comments:
Authors attempt do early prediction not only using the usual traininig paradigm on multiple features sets, but also try new approaches → cumulative variables and cluster variables on a Statistics course.

Results show that it is possible to obtain good predictions by week 6/15 using this approach,
extracted.Limitations:
Single course Data

May not generalize well to outside STEM

annotations.text: This work compares 8 distinct predictive algorithms to predict student performance (grade) in a statistics course. Authors also identify weeks 5-6 (pretty much halfway point) as the optimal time to make a prediction. Best model (BART) achieves 6.5% of MAE. Also, authors publish their code in a github repository.

references.title: Can Feature Predictive Power Generalize? Benchmarking Early Predictors of Student Success across Flipped and Online Courses

extracted.Features: [CompetencyAnticipation, WeeklyPropWatched-Avg, RatioClicksWeekendDay, ContentAnticipation, CompetencyStrength, RegWeeklySim-M2, WeeklyPrpInter-Avg, StudentShape, NumSubmissionsPerCorrect, WeeklyPropReplayed-Avg, FrequencyEvent-VideoPlay, QCheck-QCheck-Vload, VPlay-VPause-VLoad, RegPeriodicity-M3, RegWeeklySim-M1, VStop-PCheck-VLoad]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors use nested CV to assess performance of their models.
extracted.Models: RF-Akpinaretal, RF-Boroujenietal, RF-ChenCui, RF-LAlleConati, RF-LemayDoleck, RF-Mbouzaoelal, RF-Mubaraketal, RF-Wanetal, RF-Marras, RF-EnsembleMarras, RF-EnsembleAll
extracted.Comments:
The main distinction of this paper is that it keeps the same algorithm and tests multiple distinct feature sets. 

Authors identify that an emsemble of their own feature set is better for early prediction.

Authors then follow-up with a generalization study over a total of 4 curricular units - 2 FP and 2 MOOCs. 

The feature sets selected in one and the other are different.
extracted.Limitations: Course-specific models

annotations.text: The intent behind early predictors of student success is to help students and the state of the art has evolved considerably with time. The paper does a 2-part split. First, it reviews early predictors and, then, it analyzes 8 feature sets that have been successful in recent years. Then, the authors introduce another feature set. Results are compared using a RF and an analysis of the feature importances is subsequently performed.

references.title: The power of Deep Learning techniques for predicting student performance in Virtual Learning Environments: A systematic literature review

annotations.text: Deep Learning has grown in popularity in educational research and prediction of student performance is no exception. Review uses 46 papers using Deep Learning for the prediction of student performance.
Authors grouped features used by DL algorithms into 4 categories: demographic, previous performance, current performance and learning behavior.

Most common models are DNN and CNN-LSTM and, for the most part, these models have been shown to outperform other alternative methods in predictive performance (specifically accuracy). Along the datasets used, OULAD is the most popular one.

references.title: An improved adaptive learning path recommendation model driven by real-time learning analytics

extracted.Courses:
1 Programming and Data Structure course

1 Data Mining Course
extracted.Data sources:
LMS logs
Learner Characteristics
Learning Objects
extracted.Initialization Method:
StaticParameters are charged into the system 

The learning path is initialized from the students choosing an LU and SU → these become the first node of the graph.

New learners are assigned to a natural learner group via clustering based on similarity with existing learners-
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: - Real‑time: after each LO interaction update: • Learner ability Aᵢⱼ (Eq.1) • LO difficulty Dⱼ (Eq.2) • Recompute optimal path via DFS + LO ranking (Algorithms 1–3)
extracted.Preprocessing Details: - Build two‑layer KG (topics → LOs)Extract static vs. dynamic learner/LO parametersNo offline train/test split (online setting)No negative sampling
extracted.Evaluation:
- Accuracy: RMSE & R² on predicted vs. actual time/score (first 8 iterations)
- Adaptivity: mean Euclidean similarity (Eq.6)
- Diversity: path dissimilarity (Eq.7)
- Satisfaction: learner rating share (Figs. 6–9)
extracted.Comments:
- Compares four methods: SeqSt, SeqDyn, PathSt, PathDyn (proposed)Leverages both ontology and analytics

Satisfaction of users is used for evaluation
extracted.Limitations:
- Initial LO metadata set by experts (not personalized)
- Limited topic/LO coverage
- Assumes learners know all prerequisites
- Some records discarded due to data errors
-Initial training was performed on simulated data.

annotations.text: Dealing with content overload is prolematic for e-learners, which suggests that a recommender system an effective measure to mitigate this issue. Authors propose a new recommender system based on knowledge building and performance analysis.

The recommender estimates the knowledge level of the learner and makes recommendations based on learner preferences and knowledge level and predicts the time it will take the learner to learn and how he/she will perform. Performance of the model is assessed using R-Squared.
Model is tested with MOOC data from 530 LO and 96 undergraduate students and is shown to outperform other alternative methods.

references.title: Enhancing Educational Data Mining Techniques on Online Educational Resources with A Semi-Supervised Learning Approach

extracted.Initialization Method: - Co-training of a precedence classifier.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The wikipedia articles were filtered to:

1. Remove articles shorter than 100 words,

2. Remove mentions of dates, disambiguitions and category pages

The data filtered
extracted.Evaluation: Coherence score with expert formulated pre-requisites
extracted.Comments:
Authors show the power of semi-supervised learning to create a classifier for course precedences:

- The target is defined by experts
- SSL approach is compared against BOW and Explicit Semantic Analysis

The idea of this classifier is to be incorporated into a recommender of learning paths.
extracted.Limitations:
- Learning path optimization is not main scope of paper

Indirect Recommendations

annotations.text: Abstract starts with distinction between EDM and LA, arguing that the former is a lot more automated while the second is more “domain knowledge” driven. Then, the authors go into the task of generating pre-requisite rules from a set of online educational resources. In essence, the authors aim to automate the process of figuring out what pre-requisites should a student have before studying some materials.
They propose a concept-based classifier with an explicit semantic analysis for that task. They build a prototype.

references.title: Enhancing Programming Knowledge Tracing by Interacting Programming Skills and Student Code

extracted.Students: 300 Codeforces users with at least 10 submissions
extracted.Features: [Timestamp, Required Programming skills, Submitted code, Judging results]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
Programming exercises require the simultaneous display of multiple different skills. However, most KT models usually treat skills as independent and assign one skill to each exercise. 

Integrating code into a predictive model is challenging and most models simply concatenate exercise embeddings with the ID and the Correctedness.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
Authors propose the utilization of an Attention network to extract the features of the sills: Skills become queries and code representations become keys and values.

The introduction of the attention mechanism to incorportate the code submission is the de-facto improvement.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The model ios largely based on DKT. In this case, each exercise submission is treated as a 4 element tuple containing Exercise ID, Programming skills required by the exercise, Code submitted and code correctedness. 

Code is embedded using either GGNN, code2vec and ASTNN.
extracted.Models: GGNN-concat, code2vec-concat, ASTNN-concat, GGNN-attn, code2vec-attn, ASTNN-atnn
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {AUC: 0.7381 - code2vec-attn}
extracted.Comments:
Authors do Hyper-parameter tuning using 5-fold CV. 

The comparison is performed by concatenating code embeddings to the usual vector vs the new approach which is using an attention mechanism to query for skills and code submissions. 

Results show that this approach outperforms the standard DKT representation - regardless of the method used for the creation of the code embeddings.
extracted.Limitations:
Time consuming and computationally intensive to train the code-embeddings.

Not tested on any of the regular KT benchmarking datasets. 

Only considers an adjustment to DKT even though DKT has not been SoTA for a while at the time of publishing.

annotations.text: Online judges are often used to help in programming training, but often do not evaluate the degree of mastery of programming skills (often focusing on works vs doesn’t work). Deep knowledge tracing models have been used, but often assume a task requires one skill only.

The authors propose an attention-based model to create embeddings of learner code AND a separate set of embeddings encoding skills. Then authors combine DKT to show this representation outperforms the traditional approach.

references.title: Identifying university students’ online academic help-seeking patterns and their role in Internet self-efficacy

extracted.Courses: Diverse Majors in Science, Humanities and Social Sciences
extracted.Features: [Infoormation Searching, Formal query, Informal query, Usage, Sharing, Communication, Verifying, Metacognition, Application, Learning]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: OAHS questionnaire is a 5-point Likert Scale, ISE is also a 5-point Likert Scale
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: ANOVA and Scheffé Tests
extracted.Cluster Description:
Cluster 1 - Adaptive: More diverse experiences across OAHS experiences and higher confidence across ISE scales. 169 participants

Cluster 2 - Socially Afilliated: Higher scores on Formal and Informal Query than  Query than self-reliant. On ISE - more sharing. 134 participants

Cluster 3 - Self-Reliant: Better at Information Searching. Also, theses students have also shown higher confidence in ISE Usage.
extracted.Implications:
- System developers could set-up multiple help-seeking channels since different students have different habits

- Develop adaptive help-seeking detection and regulation mechanisms in computer environments.

- Organaize tasks and pedagogies across new learning paradigms (e.g. PBL) to form a trusted online learning community - something the authors argue is bvaluable.
extracted.Comments:
Authors do 2 surveys to 366 undergrads across different taiwanese institutions.

From the surveys the authors cluster the students into 3 distinct clusters using k-means.

Results show that there seem to be 3 main kinds of online help-seeking behavior and each has different preferences and psychological attributes.
extracted.Limitations:
- Only  considers Taiwanese Context

- Only uses surveys as source of information

- Only considers 3 OAHS patterns.Including more could probably lead to the creation of other categories

- There does not seem to be made a normalization for different fields - groups may be overly general.

annotations.text: The paper explores online help-seeking on a set of taiwanese HE students. Learners were asked to answer 2 surveys. Authors create 3 clusters - Adaptive, Socially-afiliated and self-reliant.
Clusters were later evaluated from the perspective of Internet Self-Efficacy.

references.title: Enhanced Transformer: Knowledge Tracing with Incorporation of Temporal Features

extracted.Target: Probability of Next Question being Correct
extracted.Features: [Questions, Time of Interactions (Response Time, Lag Time, Explanation Time, Video Lecture Time), Answers]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
Most of the current KT approaches do not consider the time spent on different learrning activities. 

Moreover, the knowledge is often estimated from sequences of answers (no formal assessment of prior knowledge). Forgetting is also not usually explained.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
The Enhanced Transformer arquitecture does uses multihead attention wherein:

- Queries are the question embeddings

- Keys are embeddings of the sequences of interactions of the students

- Values: Embedding that considers the correctedness of the previous questions.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors extract from logs the time spent on different learning activities. 

These are given as input to a Multihead attention layer.
extracted.Models: DKT, DKVMN, SAKT, SAINT, SAINT+, Enhanced Transformer
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.8319 - E-Transformer with all time features, AUC: 0.8489 - E-Transformer with all time features}
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a new architecture called the Enhanced Transformer.

The model uses multihead atention to receive Questions, Time Spent on Activities and Answers and, when comparing the model against other KT models in the same dataset, the model outperforms all others.

Ablation study to check the effect different types of temporal features shows that they do, indeed, increase the performance of the model.
extracted.Limitations:
- Holdout method

 - Executes test in a single dataset

- Could consider additional temporal features

- Utilization of forgetting behavior does not consider any form of pedagogical grounding.

annotations.text: Knowledge Tracing involves estimating the knowledge state of a student and using that knowledge state to understand whether they wil get a question right or wrong. However, an underlooked aspect of KT is temporal aspects and forgetting behavior.

To address this limitation, authors propse a new-transformer based KT model that incorporates the aspects of time and forgetting behavior in order to create a more accurate estimation of a learner’s knowledge state. When benchmarking the results using the EdNet KT3 dataset, the authors find their proposed architecture outperforms all baseline models significantly both in terms of accuracy and AUC.

references.title: A novel approach for group formation in collaborative learning using learner preferences

annotations.text: Paper about Group formation. Key idea behind this specific paper is considers learner preferences in terms of group composition. Authors use k-means to obtain what would-be considered learner profiles and go into their charachterization.

references.title: An Approach to Semantic-Aware Heterogeneous Network Embedding for Recommender Systems

annotations.text: Heterogenous Information network embedding-based Recommendations face challenges related with the heterogenous nature of the way users or items are represented. The authors propose a new form of recommendations SEMH4Rec that is meant to create better representations of users and items and feeds them to a Matrix Factorization algorithm.

The first representation is Co-occurrence representation learning which follow a random-walk and skip-grams to identify meta-paths. The second representation is semantic-aware representation learning. Both are combined to be fed as input to a matrix factorization algorithm. The approach is tested on multiple public datasets with other HIN recommenders.

references.title: Accurate, timely, and portable: Course-agnostic early prediction of student performance from LMS logs

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Being at Risk of Failing in a Course
extracted.Students: 1606 students from a Information Management School in Western Europe
extracted.Features: [Total Clicks, Clicks (%), Online Sessions, Clicks/Session, Clicks/day, Forum Clicks, Discussions Viewed, Forum Posts, Folder Clicks, Resources Viewed, URLs Viewed, Course Clicks, Assessments Started, Assignments Viewed, Assignments Submitted, Submissions (%), Total Time Online, Average duration of session, Largest Period of Inactivity, Days with 0 clicks, Days with no clicks (%), PercCourse_NLogin (N= 1, …, 10)]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
All data is obtained from LMS logs. Each row represents a student-course pairing. For each pairing, the authors first decide what date would represent specific course curation thresholds and create a model for each dataset. 

All features were normalized to range [0,1], authors also perform Feature Selection.Hyper-Parameters used were selected from literature.

Class imbalance is addressed via SMOTE, without generating better results.

LSTM uses a sequential representation of a single feature instead of the whole feature set.
extracted.Models: ZeroR, Naive Bayes, CART, Logistic Regression, MLP, SVM, kNN, Random Forest, AdaBoost, GBoost, ExtraTrees, LSTM
extracted.Comments:
Authors do a model for each course duration threshold. Selection of best algorithm is done by comparison of The AUC score.

Then, the classifier is evaluated in terms of comparison against a ZeroR and vs a version of the model trained using oversampling via SMOTE.

Finally, these models are also compared against a different model trained on a different feature set - a sequence of numbers of clicks. While the LSTM does not outright beat the traditional classifiers, it does achieve remarkably competitive performances despite being trained on a single feature.

Approach is also tested by training the same models on a second dataset. Results do not beat original author’s approach.
extracted.Limitations:
Exclusive use of LMS logs. 

LSTM approach does not consider additional features.

LSTM approach was not shown to generalize well to different datasets.

Dataset was collected during a Covid-Era so warrants generalization to the back to school context.

Under-explored Feature Extraction Strategies.

No Model Hyper-Parameter Tuning asidde from LSTM.

Selection of 25 equal-length timesteps for LSTM warrants justification.

extracted.Students: 1606 students from a Information Management School in Western Europe
extracted.Features: [Total Clicks, Clicks (%), Online Sessions, Clicks/Session, Clicks/day, Forum Clicks, Discussions Viewed, Forum Posts, Folder Clicks, Resources Viewed, URLs Viewed, Course Clicks, Assessments Started, Assignments Viewed, Assignments Submitted, Submissions (%), Total Time Online, Average duration of session, Largest Period of Inactivity, Days with 0 clicks, Days with no clicks (%), PercCourse_NLogin (N= 1, …, 10)]

annotations.text: EWS can identify both students at risk and excellent students. Study uses a year-long dataset and tests 2 types of representation in a course-agnostic manner using various stages of course completion:

Findings show that it is possible to obtain somewhat reliable models at 25% course completion. LSTM got worsde performances but its use may be benefical since the difference is smaller at earlier stages and, given that it only uses a single feature, may be more practical to obtain.

references.title: Recommendation Systems for Personalized Learning: A Data-Driven Approach in Education
references.journal: Journal of Computer Engineering and Technology (JCET)

annotations.text: Paper discusses the implementation of resource recommender systems. Algorithm explores the algorithms and techniques used in recommender systems with a specific emphasis between personalization and privacy. Paper finishes with a discussion of implications of deploying this type of recommenders.

references.title: Selection of the Right Undergraduate Major by Students Using Supervised Learning Techniques

annotations.text: Selecting an approppriate undergraduate degree is of the utmost importance to high school students. The authors use multiple interpretable classifiers to predict the most approppriate undergraduate degre for a given student.

The statistical results show that higher student marks in higher secondary (hsc_p), university degree (Degree_p), and entry test (etest_p) play an important role in the student’s area of specialization, and we can recommend study fields according to these features.

references.title: Learning log-based automatic group formation: system design and classroom implementation study
references.journal: Research and Practice in Technology Enhanced Learning

annotations.text: Managing group works is often-times overwhelming for professors. The research presents a system that focuses on forming groups and allow usage of machine learning models to learning data to facilitate group formation.  A pilot-study on primary school is done and shows a positive effect of collaborative learning for disciplines such as math.

references.title: A Genetic Algorithm Approach for Inter and Intra Homogeneous Grouping Considering Multi-student Characteristics
references.journal: International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications

annotations.text: Paper proposes a GA that minimizes a two-objective objective function: inter-group homogeneity (fairness) and intra-group homogeneity (low gap). Experiments on generated data show that the algorithm is efficient at generating groups. Moreover, the approach seems better than other alternatives at fulfilling the objectives functions - with uniform corssover based on random keys also being the most effective form of crossover operators.

references.title: Heterogenous Network Analytics of Small Group Teamwork: Using Multimodal Data to Uncover Individual Behavioral Engagement Strategies

extracted.Data sources: Headset, Positioning Sensor, Video, Evaluation of Team Performance, Post-Activity Survey
extracted.Features: [Frequency of interaction with environment, Frequency of Communication, Number of times student in a specific communication type]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The problem is framed as tripartite network that for each student and each area of the learning environment characterizes the student relative to the different features. 

Difference between groups is assessed using the Mann-Whittney U test
extracted.Cluster Description:
Cluster 1 (n=31): Students who exhibited higher satisfaction with the learning activity - as well as containing the largest proportion of high performing students.  

There 6 patterns that were considered above random - focus on the primary working area and communication focused on exchanging information.

Cluster 2 (n=27): Transitioned between more working areas - primary and secondary.
extracted.Implications:
Less movement and more stable patterns of communication behavior contribute to more satisfaction of nursing students.

Potential of multimodal experiments to provide insights on more complex learning tasks.
extracted.Comments:
Work proposes a tropartite network model to uncover realtionships in collaborative learning. 

The serusts are subsequently clustered and authors find 2 clusters of behavior with distinctions in terms of satisfaction and performance.
extracted.Limitations:
Limited context (Healthcare)

Small sample size.

annotations.text: Engagement is an important measure to track in collaborative learning, but little research is dedicated towards exploring multi-modal sources of data to understand behavior in face-to-face settings.

The authors explore this issue by forming 15 teams of 4 students and analyze their spacial and vocal engagements to find 2 distinct clusters who exhibited significant differences relative to a multinomial null model.

references.title: Mining Code Submissions to Elucidate Disengagement in a Computer Science MOOC

extracted.Target: Submitting at Least 70% of Graded Assignments (-1) vs Attrition (1)
extracted.Features: [Ak, Bk, Ck, Dkm, Ek, Fk, Gk, Hk, Ik, Jk, Kk, Lk, Mk, N, Oq, P, Q, R, S, T]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The 20 extracted features are aggregated into into 5 distinct categories:  Performance, Participation, Persistence, Interaction, Timing

Hyper-Parameter Tuning is done using 5-fold CV
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.76 - Logistic Regression, Precision: 0.91 - Logistic Regression, Recall: 0.77 - DT, F1-Score: 0.82 - DT}
extracted.Comments:
Authors in study 2 try to predict attrition of a MOOC at the end the of the 3rd out of 8 modules of an introductory programming course. 

The first study involves tracking the submission history while the second involves the creation of a supervised model to predict attrition.
extracted.Limitations:
Only considers submissions and no additional data sources. 

Only considers one course. 

Small number of algorithms.

extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors use features from mulitple assignments. In essence, they monitor the submission, grade, etc… for each assigment k in a total of 27 assignments.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Assignments 21 and 22 were found to be assignments where students tended to fail the most and this was, in fact, later confirmed by the teaching staff.  

The sequence also allows the visibility of specific attrition points: 33% quit after Assignment 2, and only 35% participate in the final assignment.
extracted.Implications: Identification of points of hurt can be used by educators to improve the curriculum.
extracted.Comments: The work is divided into multiple Studies. The first study involves the creation of a set of submission trajectories and creating a graph network.
extracted.Limitations: The algorithm used to create the trajectory graph was not specified. Likely a Markov Model.

annotations.text: MOOCs in programming are carachterized by a high rate of attrition of students. The authors of the study use data from MOOCs (logs and code submissions) to predict student disengagement. For that purpose, the authors use multiple classic ML algorithms.
Results show that further research should be devoted to understanding and developing betters hand on experiences in online learning systems.

references.title: A prediction model of student performance based on self-attention mechanism

extracted.Students: 20000 undergraduate students of Xi’an Jiaotong University
extracted.Data sources: Library Access Control, Transaction Data, Attendance Data, Enollment Data, Student Characteristics, Academic History
extracted.Features: [Student Number, Province, Entry Type, Gender, College, Graduate High School, Year of Enrollment, Course Category, Class number, Schedule Week, Credits, School Year Term, Grades, Credits, Dynamic Data Sequences from Campus Card]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The model includes a set of different modules: 

- Behavior Feature Extraction module. This module does 3 forms of aggregation in an hierarchical manner: daily, to weekly, to term - the highest resolution level.

The outputs of this module are fed as input to a mode difference module, which checks the similarity of between the current time step and the next time step for a specific feature. 

Finally, the ouptputs of these are given to a module that is meant to make the correction.
extracted.Models:
Logistic Regression, Random Forest,
GBoost, SVM, MSH-MD LSTM, MSH-LSTM
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Precision: 0.54 - RF, Recall: 0.7413 - MSH-MD-LSTM , F1-Score: 0.5831 - MSH-MD LSTM}
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a new model for the prediction of student performance based on 2 tasks: Classification and Regression.

To do that, the authors propose a deep learning architecture with multiple new modules: 

1. ! Module meant to extract the temporal features from the data source (card usages). These are attempted at diufferent levels of temporal granularity (day, week, term).

2. One module meant to deal with sparsity of information that measures the difference in mode between one feature on the previous period and the current period (called the day-before feature extraction module.

These results are concatenated before being given as input to the prediction module. 

The work compares the introduction of the 2 new models and compares their performance against that of other representations - MSH-MD LSTM outperforms all baselines.

Ablation study shgows that the mode difference module is relevant.
extracted.Limitations:
- Single Dataset

- Unhelpful for early prediction since requires data from the term to be fully taken

- Holdout Method

- Difficult to generalize to different institutions - card data is extremely specific.

The work compares the introduction of the 2 new models and compares their performance against that of other representations - MSH-MD LSTM outperforms all baselines.

Ablation study shgows that the mode difference module is relevant.
extracted.Limitations:
- Single Dataset

- Unhelpful for early prediction since requires data from the term to be fully taken

- Holdout Method

- Difficult to generalize to different institutions - card data is extremely specific.

annotations.text: The paper attempts to perform prediction for 2 distinct targets:

Contribution starts with the introduction of a novel way of representing behavioral features from campus card data. Second, the authors use a multi-head attention mechanism to automatically select the most important high-order combination features. Third, the authors propose a new predictive model that retains accuracy and robustness.
Empyrical experiment is performed on data collected from a system in a chinese university. Model outpertms baseline models for both tasks.

references.title: AutoML Feature Engineering for Student Modeling Yields High Accuracy, but Limited Interpretability

extracted.Target: Efficient (positive class) vs Not efficient in block B
extracted.Students: 2464 8th-grade students; 1232 labeled training instances and 1232 holdout instances
extracted.Data sources: Assessment interaction logs from block A
extracted.Features: AutoML and expert-engineered log features. TSFRESH: time-series features from seconds per action, seconds per problem, seconds per item, and seconds per action in the last 5 minutes. Featuretools: hierarchical/relational features aggregated at student level from row-, item-, and student-level tables. Expert features: time per item, number of actions, answer-popularity features, first/last-5-minute features.
extracted.Moment of Prediction: Prediction made after block A to predict behavior in block B; separate models for first 10 minutes, first 20 minutes, and full 30 minutes of block A
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Organizers provided only block A logs, while labels were derived from block B. Data were split into train/holdout. Authors created matched 10-, 20-, and 30-minute versions of the training data. TSFRESH default feature extraction yielded 467/516/460 features; Featuretools yielded 2980 per time length; expert features yielded 644/647/990. Authors proposed a multi-phase feature selection procedure: one-feature CART models with 4-fold CV and AUC filtering, removal of features predictive of train-vs-holdout membership, then correlation-based redundancy removal. Final selected feature sets had 413, 517, and 530 features.
extracted.Assessment Strategy: Competition holdout evaluation; internal 4-fold cross-validation on training data; Bayesian hyperparameter optimization; final submission scored on held-out NAEP competition leaderboard
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Final leaderboard: Cohen’s kappa = 0.2116, AUC = 0.6653, Aggregate score = 0.5422; Public leaderboard: Cohen’s kappa = 0.2799, AUC = 0.6676, Aggregate score = 0.6351}
extracted.Comments: This is a student-modeling / behavioral prediction paper rather than a classic academic-performance paper. The outcome is explicitly time management skill, not assessment score. The paper’s main contribution is methodological: comparing AutoML feature engineering approaches. TSFRESH features outperformed Featuretools and expert-engineered features on average, but were also the hardest to interpret.
extracted.Limitations: Outcome is not a conventional academic success variable. Potential overfitting/generalizability concerns are suggested by the gap between public and final leaderboard results. AutoML features may be dataset- and software-specific. Interpretability analysis is exploratory and based on only six experts.

annotations.text: Authors use AutoML to extract features in context of the NAEP data mining competition. In particular, 2 different packages are tested: FeatureTools and TSFRESH.

Thus, Bosch creates predictive models using the features extracted via the AutoML packages and compare how they perform against the features extracted from expert knowledge. The approach got 3rd on final kaggle scores and first in the public data.
More specifically, TSFRESH revealed to be the package that got the best features, even if at the expense of interpretability.

annotations.text: Authors explore the use of predictive model on the prediction of student performance with Logistic Regression. Experiment is performed in multiple ways:

Authors find that course-specific models achieve better predictive scores than course-agnostic model. Moreover, the general model does not seem to exhibit meaningful types of interactions with the LMS, suggesting that the approaches for predictive modeling should take into consideration instructional conditions.

There are open questions on whether predictive models of learning analytics are generalizable between different educational contexts. Getting this right is pivotal to devlopment and interpretation of learning analytics models.

Most common approaches LA modelling involve the use of data mining techniques on data extracted from either an institutional SIS or LMS. Predictive models for the identification of students at risk or the prediction of a student’s final grade are common applications. Sources for these models (often Logistic Regressions at the time, but with data mining practitioners using other algorithms) are:

The idea of these models is to extract variables that predict academic success in a way that is generalizable between different undergraduate contexts, ogten overtly. However, thee types of studies are often the exception and not the rule → most works are often used in one or a small subset of courses. Raising the question → how generalizable is it, really?

The reason for this observation comes from the fact that there seems to be mismatch between the determinants of academic risk found in learning theory when comparing against the assumptions made for these predictive models. Regardless of learning theory framework, context matters, and the models are somewhat designed to ignore context.

The literature is filled with mixed results due to differences in context: for some courses forums are important while being unimportant for others. Likewise, the relationships found to be significant for individual courses often do not hold for general models → underexploration of contextual variables is a major contributor to this.

The authors argiue that aligning learning theory with moidelling contribute to validating previous educational research and leading to better and more actionable insights. For that, the authors follow a constructivist approach to learning, according to which learners acquire knowledge by exploring the available educational tools. This makes them argue that learning analytics must account for conditions.
Thus, the authors follow the model of Winne and Hadwin, specifically pointing at instructional conditions as a key factor to determine success.

In total, the authors explore 9 separate first-year courses, each having different instructional conditions. Total sample size is 4134 students attending the courses in 2012. All courses were taught using a b-learning method and, since each course had different activities, there was little overlap between the courses in terms of Moodle Activities.

When it comes to target, the authors identified 2 possibilities: a regression based on the final grade or a multiclass label distinguishing between passing/failing or withdrawing (removed due to small number of withdraws - 88).

When it comes to features, the authors focused on features extracted from the institutional system and features extracted from Moodle. Features extracted from Moodle were initially extracted as continuous but, if heavily skewed they were eventually discretized.

The authors looked at the differences between courses using ANOVA and Chi-Square statistical tests. Moreover, they create differente models: course specific and course agnostic. Moreover, there is also the indication of 2 types of models considered by the authors:
Regression models were evaluated using R-Squared while the classification models were evaluated using the AUC of the ROC curve.
The authors identified statistically significant difference sin student characteristics across courses,l mentioning that these alone are an argument against general models.

Likewise, when looking at the trace data, the authors identified statistically significant differents in the end of course performance in different courses (using Bonferroni test). Math and accounting students were the ones who failed the most percentage-wise.

Notably, when comparing general models and specific models, the authors found that the general regression models exclusively using characteristics explained 5% of the the variance in final grades. The results are also not that appealing when adding LMS as the total R-squared rose 16%. In course-specific models, R2 for Model 1 varied significantly, going as low as 9% in GRAP to 74% in COMM. Again, context matters, and different instructional conditions also play an important role in determining whether a specific variable will or will not be considered important.

As far as classification goes, similar observations hold. Model 1 tended to underperform Model 2 both in a general sense and in a course-specific sense. However, the differences were not uniform from course to course - the alsrgest improvement was observed in mathematics.

Relationships found to be meaningful for one specific course often did not generalize either to other courses or to a general mode. E.g. in CS more logins tended to be negatively related with failing. However, this relationship was not found for any other model.  Model performance also varied significantly, as AUC for model 2 ranged from to 0.765 in Accounting to 0.915 in COMM.

At the end of the day, the authors show that performance on course-specific models tends to outshine the performance on a general model that is course-agnostic, leading them to explicitly argue for more course-specific models.
The authors also divide LMS features into 5 categories:
The conclusions become elementary from this:

extracted.Students: Whole-institution GAR analysis: 608 courses after cleansing, with 240,929 records for training and 85,198 for testing. Case study: 537 students in a first-year Computer Fundamentals course
extracted.Courses: Whole institution (UOC) for model analysis; case study in 1 first-year Computer Fundamentals course in Computer Science
extracted.Data sources: Institutional data mart, continuous assessment registry (CAR), student grades on assessment activities
extracted.Features: [Sequence of Assessment Activity Grades]
extracted.Moment of Prediction: From the first assessment activity onward, updated gradually after each newly graded activity
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Data from the institutional data mart covered 3 semesters. 2016 Fall and 2017 Spring were used for training; 2017 Fall for testing. Courses whose number of assessment activities changed across semesters were removed, leaving 608 courses. Predictive submodels were generated course by course according to the number of assessment activities. Results were distributed across the semester timeline and smoothed using LOESS regression for institutional analysis. A threshold of 70% was defined to identify low-quality models and adapt interventions.
extracted.Comments:
Authors attempt to do prediction of performance of the student at the start of the academic year.

This is Model B, the one using the feature sets of “immutable data”.

Authors also use Bayesian Models with default parameters. Results are little unintelligible though.Main contribution is an early warning system combining a simple course-specific predictive model, dashboards for students and teachers, and an automated personalized feedback service. A distinctive feature is that students are shown the minimum grade needed in the next activity to retain a good chance of passing. The warning system uses Green-Amber-Red (plus Black for likely dropout in the teacher view). The first case-study activity used only Yellow because the first submodel was below the quality threshold. The system also gathered student feedback and reasons for poor performance / non-submission.
extracted.Limitations:
The GAR model relies only on grades, so it ignores LMS interaction, demographic, and other potentially useful features.

It becomes invalid if the assessment structure changes across semesters; 281 courses (31.6%) were excluded for this reason.

 TPR is weaker in early stages, partly because of class imbalance and the presence of dropout behaviour. 

Some courses had underfitted models due to small historical samples. 

The impact of the EWS on retention/performance cannot be firmly established from one semester alone.

annotations.text: Good old study that suggests data mining tecniques as a possible way of early identification of struggling students. Authors start with the creation of a model from the logs. In total this model uses 15 variables on a logistic regression.
The authors also performed a network analysis on the data to identify peers and student groups. So, the contribution os two-fold: the model and the network analysis based on the LMS data.

Higher education has an ever-increasing number of constraints and that make it difficult for professors to demonstrate effective and innovative teaching practices. In this context, with more and more students every year, educators struggle with being able to properly identify students requiring support.

Identifying students in need of support has become increasingly challenging under the new paradigm: summative assessment makes it too late (no chance to provide support). Moreover, more students means more individuals that profesors would need to look out for → more likely for someone to slip through the cracks.

This brings us to LMS and online learning interactions. Over the years, authors have consistently argued that monitoring activity on the LMS could be an early indicator of academic achievement. Thus, the goal of the project in which this work is part of is to develop a dashboard-like visualization tool for educators.
What makes an EWS

LMS are part of the most important technologies for higher education and, even in the 2010s, had already shown massive adoption rates by institutions. The popularity of LMS is noticeable in both Online but B-Learning and Face-to-Face contexts. Moreover, LMS such as BB Vista, automatically collect user activity and interaction data, making them very useful tools for analytics.
Academic Analytics

Over the years, the rise of academic analytics has been focused on analysing the effects of demographics on student performance → goal of improving student recruitment and retention. Little to no work is devoted to improvement of teaching practices.

Despite this, educators have looked into educational theory and accepted that there are benefits to educational practices based on socio-constructivist principles. Thus the authors point to 4 key developments that may allow for the development of better EWS:
Thus, the RQs are:

Data from a fully-online undergraduate course in Biology at British columbia university. This course is considered very important as it provides the foundations for a plethora of other possibly health-related courses such as micro-biology or biochemistry. The data used only encompasses the 118 students who completed all the courseworks.

Extraction of behavior was done from the LMS. The authors extracted whole term counts for the different variables being analyzed. Goal is to predict student success. On a second goal, the authors took data from a subset of data (the 36 students with discussion forum data). From here, the authors perform a social network analysis on the data to uncover ppotential network relationships.
Correlation between LMS variables with grade

The first part of the work revolved around the analysis of scatterplots showing the relationship betweem variable values and student final grade. And, more quantitatively, 13 out of 22 variables actually exhibit statistically significant correlations with final grade, most with medium effects and the other 6 with small effects.
Regression

The authors make an analysis discriminated by Quartile of course achievement. For example, students on the first quartile tend to spend more time, while students of the last quartile of performance tend to spend less time online per week. Q2 and Q3 show no significant differences.  Ultimately, the authors argue that there is no consistent pattern between time spent and performance.
Classification

Moreover, the authors also use a Logistic Regression for classification purposes. The authors not e that there were Type II error - Predict not at risk that were at risk and 16 Type I errors. Authors argue, reasonably, that Type II errors are more important for this specific context.
Network Analysis

Ego network Analysis shows that students who perform worse have a social circle (on forum) of fewer students who happen to be low performing students. Conversely, students who perform very well interact with more students on the forum and, more specifically, those students tend to also be better performing.
Authors argue that learning is a social process and that engagement with peers is an indicator of success. Also, predictive models should be done at the course-level.

extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Variable Extraction required the definition of sessions and the computation of time online.

Many similar activities were summed together into a variable. These aggregate variables’s effectiveness was analyzed and compared against the individual features.

Feature Selection was performed using the Pearson correlation with Final Grade - 14 variables survived.

Regression analysis was performed and 4 variables survived for best model.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.813 - LR}
extracted.Comments:
4 variables used

In addition, while the Linear REgression did follow cross-validation, the confusion matrix hints at no explicit model assessment strategy being used. After all, the model made predictions for all students in sample.
extracted.Limitations: Classification problem was evaluated on the data used to train the Linear Regression.

annotations.text: Standard Analytics work of predicting Performance from LMS logs in a blended-course. Authors extract 29 variables from the LMS and use it to predict student final grade using a Stepwise Multivariate Regression. R-Squared analysis on the 14 significant variables explains 52% of the variance in the student’s final grade. Abstract makes no mention on early prediction.

Modern universities have the ability to create online learning experiences. These experiences allow learners to learn at their own pace and collaborate with other learners. web-based modules also allow to design more student centered content delivery methods. Remote learning also has challenges: educators cannot take emotional cues from face-to-face classes to understand struggling students. Thus, educators need to devise learning activities that promote engagement and SRL skills to be successful.

Online enviroments have the capacity to allow the delivery of contents in different types to cater to different learner preferences while also allowing learners to be more self-directed in their learning. However, online courses still have considerably higher rates of dropout than their face-to-face counterparts.

Contexts that combine standard Face-to-face with online learning platforms have shown to be at least as effective as full face-to-face. The interaction with the teacher is seen as the likely cause for this observation. Because there is more data, the author argues that the b-learning workflow should include analysis of online student activities - in the LMS.

Tracking of LMS activities has been previously explored by this time to predict student performance. Current challenges involve choosing correct set of predictors → in many cases this decision is ontology-basedbut lacking validation on real data. To address this issue, the author extracts 29 variables from the LMS and found 14 to be significant to creating a predictive model that predicts Final Grade.

Common sense says that full-time online students have higher dropout rates than their peers. Still, there is potential as the maturation of e-learning tools allows for the design of differnt learning experiences to suit learner needs.
B-Learning Course Design

B-Learniong combines F2F with online in a transformative manner → not only as repository for slides for example. More than just knowledge transmitters, professors in this paradigm take more of a role of facilitators or mentors. According to the author, B-Learning supposes Active Learning. Guidelines on effective course design involve dividing materials into chunks and providing multiple forms of content delivery (videos, audios, etc…).  Regular feedback is also a part of the mix.
LMS and B-Learning

LMS centralize and automate the delivery of learning materials, facilitating communication, assessment and, if need be, administration. They also have built-in mechanisms to facilitate discussion between students and other stakeholders → professors, groups, peers. The asynchronous and synchronous tools that exist in LMS allow for access to learning materials and/or collaboration. Due to the flexibility, student satisfaction with B-Learning approaches is rather high.

When it comes to data, LMS are rich sources as they record the logs for every interaction done with the system. When there is enough data, teachers can leverage those records and automate systems to predict e.g. student performance. Key variables being measured by researchers include engagement, participation and effort.
Instructional Design and Participation

Not all teaching methods are conducive to having interactions with the LMS represent actual learning activities, especially if there are face-to-face components taking the brunt. When the interactions occur is also telling: some authors identified that foruym posts increased dramatically in weeks when an assignment was due.
Social Learning Analytics

There is an inherent difficulty in properly modelling the learning process in tertiary education due to amount of variables and factors that determine learning. Learning can be understood as a combination of SRL skills, behavior, motivation, cognitive ability and learner preferences. This however is not the only perspective → attitudes towards learning in group can also play a role in this.
The study at hand intends to replicate the work by MacFadyen and Dawson in 2010 but in a B-Learning Setting.
The work is divided into 3 stages:

The course is an Introductory Java Course that is fully organized to suit B-Learning… Materials, forums, etc… are all available. The authors extracted the logs for whole semester and created 29 features from the logs.

Sessions and time per activity were defined under specific rules. In addition, a variable called RePoMessages represented the sum of all reading and posting of messages on all media. All aggregate variables were tested and compared against the individual variables that made them. Subsequently, these variables were given to the models: Multiple Linear Regression and LR.

Results for Correlation Analysis resulted in 14 variables being selected. Interestingly most aggregate variables were mantained while individual variables were removed as they did not reach the p-value < 0.05 threshold. Out of 29 actions/variables examined, one demonstrated a strong effect size (R = 0.60–0.79), two showed a moderate effect size (R = 0.40–0.59) and eight had a weak effect size (R = 0.20–0.39)
Results from Stepwise Regression actually show that, despite 14 variables being enough, the best regression was created using only 4 predictors:

This was followed by the Logistic Regression, that does not seem to have an explicit model assessment strategy. Due to the fact that the model generates predictions for all observations in a sample and does not have a separate validation or test set.

he work had some unexpected results when it comes to variable importance. The 52% RSquared shows that only 4 variables explained a significant portion of the final grades’ variability. The composite variables (sum of multiple variables representing similar activities) played a major role in the model.

Engaging with quizzes also had a positive effect in performance which goes contrary to previous literature where it is usual to see a negative effect. Assigment submission was found to be a good univariate predictor, but not in a multi-variate way.
Learning analytics may be viable strategy to implement for B-Learning contexts as it allows professors to continuously monitor student progress. LMS are important tools for that.

references.title: Predicting at-risk university students in a virtual learning environment via a machine learning algorithm

extracted.Data sources: Module presentation, LMS logs, Student Demographics
extracted.Features: Unclear
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors develop a SVM model that takes a OULAD dataset. 

The important thing to understand about RTV-SVM is that it aims to reduce the number of observations to consider for SVM:

It starts with identifying the multivariate normal distribution of the data for each class and selecting the N obervations with the lowest probability of belonging to the distribution (because they would be near the margin). Then, it calculates a ratio to decide how many central points would can be removed safely - The Tier-1 elimination of training stage.

Then, it draws an imaginary line between the center of class 1 and of class 0 and projects each point according to this line. Points away from the boundary are eliminated. 

It then Runs a standard SVM on the remaining observations.
extracted.Models:
RTV-SVM-no elimination

RTV-SVM-T1 elimination

RTV-SVM-T2 elimination

RTV-SVM-T1+T2 elimination
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Sensitivity: [0.921-0.940] - RTV-SVM-T1+T2 elimination, Specificity: [0.921-0.936] -RTV-SVM-T1+T2 elimination, Accuracy: [0.924-0.938] -RTV-SVM-T1+T2 elimination}
extracted.Comments:
Authors use an interesting approach to reduce the number of observations in a SVM problem.

Results show that this approach mantains the the predict performance while significantly reducing the time required to train a SVM algorithm.
extracted.Limitations:
Single dataset usage

No optimization of the SVM

No relevant for small datasets

annotations.text: It is important to identify students who will fail to graduate, so identifying students at risk in a course is of paramount importance. The authors propose a model that not only makes the prediction of students at-risk, but also that removes redundancies to reduce training time and support vectors. For that, the authors use RTV-SVM.

In their empyrical experiment, the authors resort to 33 thousand students in 7 courses. The proposed model managed to a 60% reduction of vector size without compromising predictive performance. Moreover, the proposed model also achieved 90%+ accuracies for the identification of at-risk or maginal students. Also, the authors seem to use OULAD.

Abstract does not explicitly mention the metrics used for evaluation but it does seem that DT was better (possible typo). Moreover, the authors have also identified 7 possible factors that affect student performance.

references.title: Cluster-Based Performance of Student Dropout Prediction as a Solution for Large Scale Models in a Moodle LMS

extracted.Features: [Flag Evaluations, Speed Coefficient, Consultation Coefficient, Count Events]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The authors consider 2 distinct datasets → one for student behaviour containing the Student logs and a second for clustering that contains information about the different courses.

Courses are clustered by different characteristics of behavior…

Each cluster will have its own classifier of dropout. This approach makes sense because different courses have very different ways of working.

Log events were aggreagated into different constructs and were subsequently scaled using RobustScaler.
extracted.Models: Logistic Regression, Random Forest, GBoost, xGBoost, AdaBoost
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Precision: 0.567 - 25 clusters, Recall: 0.289 - 5 clusters, F1-Score: 0.416 - 5 Clusters, Accuracy: 0.95 - 12 Clusters, AUC: 0.642 - 5 clusters, Log-Loss: 0.653 - 12 clusters}
extracted.Comments:
In total, the authors obtained 25 clusters, 12 clusters and 5 clusters and for each cluster there were 5 candidate algorithms → 210 models being trained. 

In total, the authors created and deployed 42 models → one per cluster.

Metrics show that performances do not vary significantly regardless of the number of clusters considered, but 5 clusters is the winner on most metrics.
extracted.Limitations:
No hyper-paramenter tuning is done.

Very specific online context (Covid-Era)

Does not consider all resources available. 

Does not consider information outside of Moodle.

annotations.text: LA serve multiple purposes in modern educational contexts and many of the predictive models for dropout usually work better on a smaller subset of courses or a single course, with few works actually working with large numbers of courses.

In this work, the authors use an automated system to predict student dropout. First, they start with clustering courses together based on their design and similarity. Secondly, a predictive model of dropout is specifically created for each cluster.

references.title: Classification and Regression Trees (CART) for Predictive Modeling in Blended Learning
references.journal: International Journal of Intelligent Systems and Applications

extracted.Features: [Messages exchanged, Group wiki content contributions, Files viewed, Quiz efforts]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: IStudent online interactions were extracted from Moodle logs into Excel. VBA macros were used to compute, for each student, the number of files viewed, content contributions, quiz efforts, and messages exchanged. The activity table was then merged with a second file containing student IDs and course grades. CART was applied with four predictors and one binary target.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 99.1% - CART; Recall_Fail: 98.2%; Recall_Pass: 100.0%}
extracted.Comments: Messages exchanged and collaborative content creation were the two most important predictors. Two main rules emerged: students with messages ≤ 172 were predicted to fail; students with messages > 172 and content contributions ≥ 13 were predicted to succeed. The resulting tree is very small and interpretable.
extracted.Limitations:
Single course in one context. 

Prediction is based on full-semester behavior. 

Very high accuracy may reflect context-specific patterns and may not generalize to other courses or institutions. 

Only four LMS features were used, with no demographic, prior-achievement, or offline learning

annotations.text: Author uses data collected from Moodle to predict student success in a course using CART. Model focuses on detection of students at-risk based on 4 distinct activities: message exchanging, group wiki content creation, course file opening and online quiz taking. Accuracy is close 99%.

extracted.Preprocessing Details:
All data is obtained from LMS logs. Each row represents a student-course pairing. For each pairing, the authors first decide what date would represent specific course curation thresholds and create a model for each dataset. 

The features extracted are calculated in relative terms (since different courses have different durations.

All features were normalized to range [0,1], authors also perform Feature Selection and Hyper-Parameter Tuning.

Class imbalance is addressed using multiple Resampling Techniques.
extracted.Models: ZeroR, NB, CART, LR, MLP, SVM
extracted.Comments:
Authors do a model for each course duration threshold. Selection of best algorithm is done by comparison of The AUC score.

Then, the classifier is evaluated in terms of comparison against a ZeroR and over the F1 and F0.5 scores.

All models are meant to perform early prediction and for that reason, the authors never look at the performance at 100% course duration.
extracted.Limitations:
Exclusive use of LMS logs. 

Limited interpretability of features due to potential differences in instructional conditions.

It is very likely that the model implementation suffers from data leakage (RFECV in a Holdout-Method).

Authors do not have access to Final Grades and compute a proxy based on Assignment marks available on Moodle for each course.

extracted.Cluster Description:
One clustering solution was obtained for each moment of prediction (10%, 25%, 33% and 50%). While the number of clusters remains the same, there are differences:

QAT - - : Cluster oif students who answe quizzes a lot faster.

QAT ++: Opposite of previous previous

UAA- : Students who check external resources earlier than their peers.

CIR++: Students who consistently procrastinate in course contents and resources.

MOA-CIR+: Assignment ew valuations below average while having above average views of resources.

Average: Average for all aggregate variables.

Students who answer quizzes the fastest have higher average performance while  the lowest performing student are learners who procrastinate.

The largest CIR++ (real procrastinator) achieve the second lowest average performance.
extracted.Comments: One clustering solution was obtained for each moment of prediction. The statistical tests identify at least 4 out of the 6 clusters created to have a correlation with performance.
extracted.Limitations:
Inferred Grade.

Lack of Additional Data sources.

Loss oif interpretability satemming from the Aggregate variables without a proper use of the remaining variables.


Clustering using a different approach would have been awesome.

annotations.text: Early detection could allow students at risk to receive timely feedback. Likewise, early identification of excellent students could allow for the delivery of more personalized content.

Many works either make models using data from the whole course or, in many cases, make early prediction on a single course. For those reasons, the authors use LMS logs to create a set of course agnostic models that predict student success at different thresholds of course duration: 10%, 25%, 33% and 50%.

Models are trained using the full logs of the Univeristy of Oviedo in the years 2013 to 2014. Different Classifiers are tested and MLP emerged as the best model throughout, achieving 80% accuracy at 10% course duration and 90% at the end of the course.
On a second analysis, the authors performed clustering and found 6 groups and, in 4 of the 6, there is a strong correlation between interaction patterns and student performance.

references.title: Semi-supervised learning for big social data analysis

annotations.text: Authors present a semi-supervised approach to perform knowledge distillation from multi-modal Big Data. This novel approach exhibits good results for multiple tasks such as emotion recognition or polarity detection → all things that may be useful for an educational context.

references.title: Optimal learning group formation: A multi-objective heuristic search strategy for enhancing inter-group homogeneity and intra-group heterogeneity

annotations.text: Collaborative learning benefits from clustering students into appropriate learner groups. However, most group optimization is performed via single-objective optimization, not accounting for multiple constraints. To address this, authors propose a multi-objective Genetic algorithm, NSGA-II.

This method successfully satisfies multiple criteria that are important for group formation, especially the intra-heterogeneity and inter-heterogeneity. Results show that single-objective optimization often leads to sub-optimal groupings since it does not account for all criteria. Another variation is that this model is able to check the fitness of each group distinctively. Quality of results also suggests potential for generalizability to other domains.

references.title: Early prediction of undergraduate Student's academic performance in completely online learning: A five-year study

extracted.Features: [Total_logins, N_access_forum, N_addedd_messages_forum, N_access_didactic_units, N_access_glossaries, Total_assignments, N_assignments_consulted, N_assignments_submited, N_access_questionnaires, N_attempts_questionnaires, N_answered_questions, N_questionnaire_views, N_questionnaires_submitted, N_reviews_questionnaire, Days_first_access, N_entries_course, Age, Sex, Degree, Grade Course, GPA_12_13, GPA_13_14, GPA 14_15, GPA_15_16, GPA_16_17]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
All courses in UDIMA share a similar structure, which makes it easier to preprocess the data. All variables were measured at the end of the Academic Semester. GPA was computed (not provided by the system).

Variables were subjected to PCA to perform Exploratory Factor Analysis, which lead to the selection of 3 Factors.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {RSquared: 0.456 - 4 Variables - Course, Adjusted RSquared: 0.455 - 4 Variables - Course, RMSE: 0.126 - 4 Variables - Course}
extracted.Implications:
Short-term success is often determined by Task, Access, Questionnaire and Age. 

Factors that influence long-term sucess, however are the same (but without task).

extracted.Preprocessing Details: Students with missing values on more than 25% of features were dropped. Continuous numerical variables were standardized by centering to the median and scaling by the IQR. Remaining missing values were handled with multivariate imputation. Click features were computed from the first half of the course, while the target was long-term GPA in the following academic year.
extracted.Assessment Strategy: Course-level leave-one-group-out cross-validation across the 10 courses. For each feature-set/outcome combination, the classifier with the highest F-score was retained. Evaluation considered Accuracy, FPR, and FNR, with subgroup fairness analysis via disparity ratios and one-sided two-proportion z-tests.

Fairness analysis showed persistent underestimation of disadvantaged groups when institutional data were used, and combining data sources did not fully remove bias.
extracted.Limitations: Only 10 online STEM courses at one institution. Long-term success is operationalized relative to classmates via median split rather than raw GPA. Fairness was analyzed at the data-source level, but no debiasing intervention was tested.

annotations.text: Learning analytics predictive models are not always fair to all student subgroups. To show this, authors compare the effectiveness of features extracted from 3 different data sources: SIS, LMS and Survey. The analysis is made in a 2-fold manner:

Authors found that LMS and Institutional data are, indeed, predictive of student performance and that performance is increased when both sources are combined. However, the authors also identified that under-represented groups tend to have their performance be under-estimated, meaning that these students may, under the current regimen, be more likely to slip through the cracks of a warning system.

extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The work creates sequences of features. Authors attempt 4 distinct intervals:
daily, every 3 days, weekly and monthly.

For each period, boolean indicators are created mentioning whether a resource is accessed or not. Moreover, 2 to 5-grams are also created to mirror sequences of clicks in a specific activity.

Authors create boolean flags for sequences of different length. Moreover, the first 2 questions focus on prediction at the end of the course, with the 3rd focusing on early prediction.

Work does not aim to find best model and best hyper-parameters. Instead it focuses on optimizing preprocessing strategy.

Authors point to the models created having minimal explanatory power even though they are decision trees. 

Model assessment is also rough:
1 - test performance on same course and likely same data
2. Holdout where test set is third offering of a course.

Models clearly overfit. Still, optimal time to make early predictions seems to be after 21 days, when the FPR tends to decrease substantially.

Models are course-specific but meant to generalize across different editions.
extracted.Limitations:
No feature selection was performed.

Does not use any algorithms beside DT.

Unverified assumption that previous editions work similarly to later editions of a same course.

annotations.text: Need for new methods of predicting achievement that are cheaper and generalizable to increasingly large courses (e.g. MOOCs). Paper presents a novel approach of feature extraction from logs → Features extracted using proposed methodology are built from interactions between learners and resources.

Current models for rpediction of student performance often depend on domain knowledge and pedagogical constraints which makes these models only effective in situations where the technologie or pedagogical strategies being measured are used. Thus, there is a need for more generalizability.

The authors intend to build a model from interactions that is generalizable and not course-specific. A factor motivating this approach is the increasing availability of LMS systems that collecte clickstream data. Altough there are many approaches for this, trhe authors specifically focus on time series interaction analysis with the goal of predicting summative evaluations on MOOCs.

Historically, prior performance data is more predictive of student performance than clickstream data. However, due to their availability, clickstream summaries have been more and more popular. For example, in MOOC, institutions often have very little knowledge on a student’s performance in previous courses. Thus behavioral data is the primary dsata source to form a predictive model until the end of examinations (at least in MOOC).

There are multiple perspectives when it comes to modelling cognitive skills or learning. The basis for many ITS is the ACT-R model that postulates that cognitive skilsl can be described as if they were mere production rules. Another theory argues that learning comes from making mistakes. Moreover, there is contact with materials or communication with peers.
This work intends to model learners based on their interactions with a LMS - specifically resources available in the LMS.
There are 5 main intervenients:
How to create TS Features:
The creation of Time-Series Features involves first defining specific time-frames and then creating boolean flags that reflect whether a specific resource was interacted with in a given dataframe.
In total, each student enrollment had 1221 features: 480 boolean features, 21 summative counts of accesses in distinct days of the week and 717 n-gram related features.
Tests were done over 3 RQ, with only the third one focusing on early warning systems per se:

To test the feasibility, the authors opted to use a J48 DT. on 4 different MOOCs from Michigan University on Coursera. The models will be evaluated across the numbers of correct and incorrect predictions and Fleiss’s kappa. This metric has some constraints related with its interpretation that will be addressed later by the authors.
RQ1: The first decision tree for post-hoc analysis shows 3 viable paths of classification:
RQ2: The model trained on the balanced datasets of course editions 1 and 2 and tested on third offering. Multiple predictions are made over time. It stands clear that the models clearly overfit:

Predictive performance measured by kappa clearly increases as the number of days increases. Optimal tradeoff for classification seems to be after 21 days, when there is a significant drropoff in the FPR.

On the other hand, students who lagged behind tended to focus more on studying materials they already knew.
