references.title: Detecting Learning Strategies with Analytics: Links with Self-Reported Measures and Academic Performance

extracted.Features: [seq.cluster1, seq.cluster2, seq.cluster3, seq.cluster4, seq.total ]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The SPQ made 20 questions and allowed the emasurement of 4 sub-scales: Deep Motivation, Deep Strategy, Surface Motivation and Surface Strategy (only 144 students actually answered).

The LMS data was collected from weeks 2-13 (only students who answered the SPQ were considered).

Consecutive learning events were considered as part of the same learning session if they occured within 30 minutes.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: Variable differences via de Mann Whitney U test.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Clusters 1 and 2 are characterized by being more active than the 3 and 4 counterparts. These students engage in much more meaningful deep learning activities (especially cluster 1). 

Clusters 3 and 4 follow a much more surface level approach to learning, with cluster 4 being less active throughout and cluster 3 being active when it comes to summative assessments. 

Unsurprisingly, clsuters 1 and 2 outperform 3 and 4 both at the midterms and final exams.
extracted.Implications:
Differences were found when it comes to approaches deep approach and deep strategy. However, the logs do not provide ways of distinguishing motivation subscales or identify specifical surface approaches. 

Professors can invest in delivering contents in a way that promotes deep learning approaches with more flipped classroom and active learning.
extracted.Comments:
The clustering approach is done in a twop-fold manner:

1. The learning sequences were clustered first. 
2. The learning sequences were used as inputs to cluster the students.

Clustering of students creates 4 clusters that are sub-sequently grouped into 2 groups (Deep learners vs surface learners). Mapping the approaches to SPQ answers, it is possible to see that deep learners have a much higher DA scale on the SPQ.
extracted.Limitations:
Questionnaire was administered at start of the course.

Susceptible to self-selection bias - students who answered are more active and more likely to invest in their course.

Small sample size.

annotations.text: Few works have compared the SRL variables extracted from logs with the SRL variables extracted from questionnaires. This work explores exactly that by lloking at a sample of 144 students attending a course that follows a flipped classroom approach.

Authors found that the logs allow for the distinction between deep learning approaches and surface learning approaches. The results also show that there were some associations between deep learning strategies identified in LMS vs the other methods. The same, however, cannot be said in regards to surface learning or motivation. The authors also found that students following deep approaches performed better.
While the early days of LA often involved the predictive modeling of student success, the use of advanced machine learning techniques to model students has gained significant traction.

Multiple authors have argued that proper modelling of students, their learning processes and behaviors requires backing by educational theory. The reason for this is two-fold: First, it allows for better interpretation of the models while, simultaneously, allowing for their validation and updating. This opens opportunities for the creation of new predictors and facilitate interpretation of results.

There still is some tension on theory-driven techniques used in learning analytics, with the reliance in self reports being a considerable point. Moreover, a problem comes from the fact that trace data and self-reports often cannot be overlapped → learners to over-estimate their tactics. Trace data has shown to be less biased. This brings forward the notion of combining both self-reports and trace data.

The apper explores learning strategies measurable from self-reporting and trace data and their effect on student performance. Existing research shows that learners often engage in unproductive learning strategies and inneficieient use of the tools available. Unsupervised learning techniques have generaaly identified between 3 and 6 learning strategies. Results often showed that it is possible to discern differences in behavior and that it is possible to identify relationships with performance → more engagement tends to lead to better performance.

When it comes to historical readings from students, the literature often shows that deep learning approaches often tend to lead to better student outcomes than more surface-level approaches to learning. Obsevation shows that surface approaches are more common in knowledge transmission classes while deep learning approaches were often more common in conceptual change classes.

Thue authors use a self-report instrument that tracks different approaches to learning on 4 sub-scales: Deep Motive, Deep Strategy, Surface Motive and Surface Strategy. The research questions for this study can be written as follows:

The course includes a fllipped classroom first-year course that lasted for 13 weeks and enrolled 290 students. The preparatory lessons included videos, MCQ, reading materials and problem sequences (the latter being used as summative assessment). Students also had access to a LA dashboard.

3 sources were used. Study Process Questionnaire which was answered by 144 students. The second source were the LMS logs created during the whole duration of the course: weeks  2 to 13. The highlights the preparation for the flipped classroom lectures by the students who answered the SPQ. Exam scores were also extracted: both mid-term and final exam scores.

As far as the data goes, the learning sequences were clustered and used subsequently to cluster students. In both cases, the authors used agglomerative clustering with ward distance. Clustering of learning sequences used Levenshtein distance (edit distance).

The learning sequence clustering gets 4 clusters, and the clustering of students is based on the number learning sequences that belong to each of these clusters seq.cluster_i. The fifth feature is the total number of learning sequencves by the student.
Comparisoin of student along the 6 constructs of relied on the Man Whitney U test.
From the clustering of learning sequences (6196), the authors found 4 clusters:
As far as student clustering goes, the results also show 4 clusters:

There is a distinction between approaches to learning: deep approaches come from intrinsic motivation and is characterized by critical evaluation and synthetization. Students in C1 and C2 are more engaged in general, especially on Seq1 - Formative assessment and Seq3. Reading materials. Thus, these clusters seem to follow more of a deep approach (with C1 even having advantage here). Both these clusters also perform better than their peers.
Cluster 3 and follow a surface aproach, with C4 being a more innactive set of students relative to C3. C3 is interpreted as trying to get good results with minimal engagement.

Cluisters 1 and 2 were grouped into students who followed a deep approach and 3 and 4 grouped into students who follow a surface approach. These 2 new clusters were compared against one another and we see that Deep Learning approaches had considerably higher scores on the Deep Approach scale than students identified as having a Surface approach.
Finally, the authors compare the mid-term and final exam scores of both groups, with the Deep Approach Group outperforming the Surface Approach Group on both.

Students in the deep approach followed a variety of possible ways to engage to with the contents provided to them. This is due to the fact that deep approaches often require the exploration of both deep and surface level strategies, whereas surface level approaches often are performance oriented (not learning).
There are some other factors that may account for the differences in results: prior knowledge.

references.title: Predicting achievement and providing support before STEM majors begin to fail

extracted.Students:
337 - Training Data
298 - Test Data 1
349 - Test Data 2
extracted.Features:
[Content Folders, Environmental Structure, 
Link to Content Area,
Lecture Notes,
Monitoring Learning,
Monitoring Performance,
Monitoring Process, 
Monitoring Progress,
Planning,
Policy]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
3 different feature representations:

1. LOs as frequency counts

2. LOs as binary variables

3. Mixed representation and aggregation by Resource Types
extracted.Comments:
All best models were obtained using with the 3rd feature representation.

Deploys early warning system for Test Sets 1 and 2.
extracted.Limitations: [Integrity of logs, Pedagogical validity of count representations,Introduction of Learning theory for feature categorization, Weekly binning]

annotations.text: Need for improvement in current early warning systems. The authors use behavioural data to anayze whether they can create an early warning system with data collected up to the first exam and follow up with the development of actual measures to improve academic achievement. Authors found that the warning system identified 75% of students who were “at-risk” and, on a subsequent sample, followed up with the development of actions meant to support students in need. Students who were give the intervention achieved better outcomes than those who did not receive the intervention.

There are disciplines in STEM that aim to “weed out” a large portion of aspiring students so that only the best students stay.  This phenomenom has led to substantial effort by researchers to try and mitigate this weeding out as, ultimately, failing around 50% of students guarantees, at best, that they will be delayed in entering the workforce.

Different teaching methods have been employed to address this: active learning, developmet of student skills and early warning systems. In the topic of early warning systems, while there is plenty of literature available, there is a limited number of studies focusing on large lecture courses. The authors have focused on adapting LMS incoporation into their course work to: i) promote active learning, 2) to actually gather meaningful data from the LMS.

Still, the literature is somewhat scarce in a couple of specific aspects: 1) interpretability of insights as most models use black box to obtain predictions and 2) even in the cases where white box approaches are followed, a very limited number of studies actually goes beyond the prediction stage and tries to address the student difficulties.
Goals:

Throughout time, different universities have made efforts to develop their own early warning system (e.g. Purdue’s Course Signals) to monitor student performance throughout the semester and warn students and instructors in an effort to give the time to perform some form of intervention. Common sources for student data use a combination of behaviour, prior performance and, to some extent, demographics. Due to its more unobtrusive nature, LMS provide an interesting opprtunity for early prediction of student performance.

There have been multiple studies employing ML to predict whether students will pass or fail a course, usually ranging between the 50% to 80% accuracy ranges. These models, of course, outperform other models based on naive predictions of everyone passing or everyone failing. As far as predictors go, theere are also plenty: demographics, finantial aid data or high-school grades. While certainly effective, these datapoints have the issue of being static and, more specifically for the demographics case, potentially stigmatizing.

Moreover, prior performance in the course is also taking into account, leading to situations where the models simply spew what they know thus far (if performance is low up to the midpoint, it will continue being poor), especially in the absence of a strategy to change student behavior. Thus, the authors argue that prediction should aslso take into acount variables that allow effective early prediction when outcomes are not foregone conclusions.

The authors identify feww studioes that are exclusively relying on data from the LMS. Baker outperformed chance by 34% (cohen kappa) and MAcfadyen & Dawson found that the logs contained 31% of the explained variance when determining whether a student should be at-risk or not, pointing towards predictive power of LMS behavioral data.

The authors will pursue the association of behavior with specific learning events as informed by learning theoretical constructs. This allow the aggregation of “similar objects” into more generalizable categories which facilitate classification. In other words, the authors claim that 2 students may engage in the same learning behavior while engaging with different resources. In addition, the models claim to value accuracy metrics as the main criteria for model selection as that reflects the identification of students in need of additional support.

In here, it is important to make a clear distinction between what allows better performances vs what is actionable. Performance in previous courses, financial situation of demographics have been shown to have positive effects on predictive performance but tell us nothing on how to assist a student in a specific course.

Using intermediate grades also poses considerable challenges. If the purpose is early intervention, these models rely on studfents failing in assessments a-priori before making the identification, which reduces the window for meaningful intervention towards course-correcting behavior, which is, at the very least, counter-productive to the aim of the model itself (you need to fail before I can help you).

The authors wish to focus their efforts tod evelop a predictive model that uses what the students can actually control (their behavior on the LMS). The authors assess the performance of their models based on whether their manipulation of data consists of dichotomous occurrences or frequency counts. Then, the authors use multiple ML allgorithms in order to figure out which one of them is the best performing one (while also giving some emphasys on interpretability.

How should the access to different contents be modelled? There is plenty of evidence that suggest that multiple types of content should be treated dichotomously (accessing vs not accessing). For example, powerpoint slides can be downloaded once and then used repeatedly throughout the semester.

Using these materials depends on how the students will actually engage with content: some students will downloead once and never access a material again. Others, will access the same materials multiple times without creating a personal collection. For this paper, the authors explored both dichotomous use and frequency counts in order to verify which is the most relevant for predictive purposes.

More than just foruns or downloadeable content, there are other events that require repeated access. There are resources that require automatic feedback methods and repeated engagement make perfect sense in the context of frequency counts. In order to better account for what each event refers to, the authors believed that it would be important to consider what they call a principled approach.

The authors take into consideration multiple facets of the learning process: dichotomous-use of learning materials, frequency counts when accessing different learning materials and, more importantly, using a principled approach to identify whether models using a data preprocessing that follows instructional design and learning theory outperform models that do not have this level of granularity.

The models sused in this work should fulfill a couple of conditions: the first is the conversion into some form of model that is able to predict in real time - which means that the envents need to be observable immediatly. A second consideration will be the interpretability of the models (so if a complex model achieves the same performance as a simpler one, the authors show preference for the simpler). To that effect, the authors present each feature set to 4 candidate models to assess performance.
1. What methods of representing learning events (i.e., features) and which statistical models (i.e., algorithms) produce the most accurate prediction of student learning?
337 from Anatomy and Physiology at an US unversity to train their models. The models were then assessed on additional samples of 298 and 349.

After selecting the best model, the authors used it to identify students that are struggling and, after that, messaged some of the students to focus them on more effective study practices. Learning support was provided on day 27 of the semester (1 week before the first exam of the course). The students who were given access to personalized messages were assigned randomly (as long as they were identified as struggling). The impact of these messages was assessed only on subsequent exams (not the first one).

Large lecture course witth support on Blackboard. All materials were provided at the start and every single resource was optional. The models only consider the first 4 weeks of the semester. Moreover, all content accesses were converted to a weekly basis, which still captures relevant patterns such as when is a student dopwnloading the contents for a specific week. Students use these logs and a binary target of success vs fail at 80% (minimum to be admitted to the next course in the major).

The models under consideration were WEKA implementations of Logistic Regression, C4.5 (J48) and J-Rip, and the Naive Bayes algorithm. Each item was aggregated into a type of resource that reflects the activity that the content is meant to support. The best models were selected using Cohen’s Kappa, supported by accuracy, recall and precision.

Authors attempted both K-Fold CV and Leave-One-Out. On the test data, the authors make use of Kappa and Recall to measure the eprformance on the test data (new sets of students that are going to receive the intervention). Students flagged as more likely to achieve worse than B were separated and randomly assigned into either control group or gorup who receives the personalized e-mail for additional support. The final step of the work is the comparison of the grades obtained by students who received the e-mail vs the students who had not received the e-mail nor accessed the supporting materials.
The analysis starts with measuring correlations and standard descriptive statistics.
General patterns involve heavy activity at the start and in the week preceding the exam.
Again, heavy emphasys on recall as one of the key indicators for model performance.
In the subsequent semesters, the authors managed to correctly identify more than 80% of students who ended up failing the course.

The authors make a split between all students flagged as struggling and measured the differences in performance between those that accessed the extra content vs those who had not accessed the content. The assignment was made in order to ensure that there would be no confounds relating to demographics or other potentially predictive aspects.

On the 4 exams following the delivery of the content, the authors collected the exam scores and used an ANOVA test to check the performance of one group against the other. Students who received support were found to out-perform and be more likely to succeed than their peers who had accessed the different materials.
Moreover, the authors also checked whether there would be a meaningful impact on the performance of students who did not need the support and dound no meaningful relationship.

references.title: Predicting student academic performance using multi-model heterogeneous ensemble approach
references.journal: Journal of Applied Research in Higher Education

extracted.Features: [Age, Gender, Ethnicity, Postcode location (deprivation level proxy), Campus of study, Mode of study (full-time / part-time), Entry qualification, Disability status, Total login time, Number of resources viewed, Number of module quiz attempts submitted, Number of module forum course views, Number of module forum discussion reads/views, Parent economic status, Work hours, Work pressure, Entry qualification (self-reported context variable), Average study hours, Family support, Course satisfaction, Technology impact, Learning style, Health status, First in university (first-generation student), Adaptation (adjustment to university), University support, Prior course knowledge]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors test different configurations of features: 

Data from SIS alone, LMS alone, Survey Alone and combinations of different data sources.

Banner data includes 8 features, LMS 5 and Survey includes 14 features

Hyperparameter tuning is achieved using 10-Fold CV
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.8295 - SVM - Survey, RMSE: 0.396 - Stacking - All Sources, Precision: 0.7962- Stacking - All Sources, Recall: 0.7586 - Stacking - All Sources, F1-Score: 0.7769 - Stacking - All Sources}
extracted.Comments: This study investigates whether combining multiple data sources and heterogeneous ensemble methods improves the prediction of student academic performance. Using data from 141 students at the University of the West of Scotland, the authors integrate demographic (SRS), behavioral (LMS), and psychological/survey variables. They compare decision tree, neural network, and support vector machine classifiers against a stacking ensemble approach. The hybrid ensemble model that combines all three data sources achieves the highest predictive performance (around 81% accuracy). The authors conclude that multi-source ensemble modeling can support early identification of students at risk of poor performance and potential attrition.
extracted.Limitations:
Unclear operational definition of performance

Temporal ambiguity

Small sample size

Single-institution context

Limited transparency on course scope

No early prediction

annotations.text: Goal of paper is to compare performance of student performance prediction models across different data sources and predictive models.

Authors considered 141 students and, for those, relied on 3 distinct data sources: Student Record System, LMS and survey data. Then, the authors attempt 3 base learners and a stack of different base learners. Ensembles trained with data from multiple data sources outperforms the remaining options. Models trained use accuracy. Also, no mention of a specific moment of prediction.

references.title: Student performance analysis and prediction in classroom learning: A review of educational data mining studies

annotations.text: Authors make a systematic literature review of over 140 studies in the area of student performance. Main aim of the paper is to identify what are the predictors, methods, time and aim of the prediction.

Authors also make a distinction between studies that aim to perform analysis of student performance on classroom based educational contexts. Msin gap that is identified is the lack of studies that focus on prediction of performance before the start of the course.

references.title: CLGT: A Graph Transformer for Student Performance Prediction in Collaborative Learning

extracted.Features: [Vertices (students), Edges (interactions between vertexes)]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors create a set of graph nodes to represent learners and their interactions in a collaborative learning environment.

The first part is related with the definition of interaction degrees, which leads to the generation of an Interaction Matrix - that represents the vertexes and interactions.

From the interaction graph, the model predicts the grade that the learner should get that week → based on contributions and/or interactions.

Then, it follows a standard ML pipeline: 

- Generation, 
- Variable Selection,
Modelling
extracted.Models: AdaBoost, R-GCN, GTN, Graph Trans, CLGT
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.7392 - CLGT, F1-Score: 0.6675 - CLGT, AUC: 0.9057 - CLGT}
extracted.Comments:
Authors create a new Graph Transformer and compare its performance versus other models used.

Essencially, the goal of the models is to make a prediction for every Node (each week’s grade and/or final grade) and the model outperforms (on average) the remaining tested models.
extracted.Limitations:
- Single Course Study

- Specific to Collaborative Learning Settings using GitLab

annotations.text: There are few works that attempt to use interactions between elements in a context of collaborative learning to predict student performance. Authors use data from a PBL-course with a final group project. Authors build an interaction graph based on the activities of students in their teams.

Then, the authors present a Graph Transformer Network for Collaborative Learning for the prediction of student performance. Authors claim that their approach outperforms the baseline models used for the task of predicting student performance when in a collaborative environment. Moreover, the authors also claim that there is an important degree of interpretability in the knowledge graphs, which allows educators to better identify students who are struglling and provide personalized support.

references.title: Learner Modeling and Recommendation of Learning Resources using Personal Knowledge Graphs

extracted.Data sources:
– Lecture slides (concept text, extracted keyphrases)

– Student DNU feedback logs 

– External APIs (Wikipedia + YouTube)
extracted.Initialization Method:
– Personal Knowledge Graph (PKG) nodes initialized from lecture concepts

 – Concept & resource embeddings via SBERT (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) 

– Relation weights set using cosine similarity
extracted.Updates to Recommendations:
– PKG updated each time a learner flags new DNU concepts 

– Concept and neighbor embeddings re-propagated through LightGCN layers 

– Re-ranking by similarity between learner-PKG and resource graph
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
– Keyphrase extraction: SingleRank + DBpedia Spotlight – Entity linking: Wikipedia/DBpedia URIs 

– Text normalized, stop-word removed 

– SBERT embeddings generated for slides & external texts 

– Graph edges built: {relatedTo, subcategoryOf, hasCategory}
extracted.Evaluation:
Online
{Precision@5: 0.68 - PKG-Keyphrase, MAP: 0.78 - PKG-Keyphrase, MRR: 0.81:  - PKG-Keyphrase}
extracted.Comments:
– Introduces Personal Knowledge Graphs (PKGs) that learners build themselves by marking DNU concepts. 

– Combines semantic embeddings (SBERT) with graph propagation (LightGCN) for richer contextual recommendations. 

– Transparent & scrutable learner model: students can see and edit their PKG. 

– Demonstrates significant improvements in accuracy and user trust compared to content-only approaches.
extracted.Limitations:
– Small sample size (N = 31, 1 course). 

– Concept extraction errors (e.g., non-academic or ambiguous terms). 

– YouTube API limit restricts query length (≤ 5 concepts). 

– No direct measurement of learning gain. 

– Manual DNU entry burden on learners; automation needed for scale.

annotations.text: Creating a representation of a learner’s knowledge state is important (more than just on KT) in the design of educational recommender systems. However, current recommenders have problems with transparency in the learner model and how it relates with the learning materials. The paper proposes an approach using Personal Knowledge Graphs.

The knowledge graph is created from users labelling contents as “did not understand” as they interact with the materials. This labelling is a precursor for the construction of the learner model and uses this representation to suggest external learning resources. The approach is tested on a sample of 31 students and shows promising results on user-centred metrics such as accuracy, novelty, diversity, usefulness, satisfaction and intentions.

references.title: Artificial Intelligence for Assessment and Feedback to Enhance Student Success in Higher Education

annotations.text: Paper seems to be a review about assessment strategies and how AI can be used to automate this task and improve student outcomes. The work starts with a review of assessment strategies, it seems, and follows up how AI can be used as part of them.
It also “tries to capture” an outline of ML algorithms used to support student success and discuss how assessments can be used to enhance learning outcomes.

references.title: Predictive Modelling of Student Reviewing Behaviors in an Introductory Programming Course

references.title: A General Purpose Anomaly-Based Method for Detecting Cheaters in Online Courses

extracted.Features: [m.vtp, std.vtp, perc.vtp.watched, Guttman Error, ZU3, Guttman Error on response-time]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors extract features from videos and assessments and subsequently use LASSO regression on one of the versions of the Physics course. It returned 5 features as ideal and all models for all datasets were subsequently trained on this set of features.

annotations.text: Work focuses on identifying cheating behaviors in online courses. It treats cheater detection as a combination of anomaly detection AND supervised ML. Authors create behavioral features using literature and try to find anomalies. Results show that classifiers trained on data of cheating behabviors becomes effective at identifying cheating behaviors across different types of cheating and acrooss different courses.
Authors run their 2 models on 5 runs of MITx MOOC. The contributions of paper are:

references.title: Predicting At-Risk Students Using Clickstream Data in the Virtual Learning Environment

extracted.Features: [Weekly collection of 20 attributes]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Raw OULAD tables were processed into a week-wise sequential format. Students were identified uniquely per course presentation. Repeated students and repeated course attempts were excluded. Distinction and pass were merged into a single pass class; fail remained the other class. Withdrawal cases do not appear to be part of the final binary dataset. Final analytic dataset contained 22,437 instances. Weekly vectors were padded to equal sequence length, and padded values were masked before input to the LSTM.
extracted.Comments: Main contribution is showing that week-wise sequential clickstream modeling with LSTM improves early prediction of at-risk students compared with non-sequential baselines using aggregated data. The paper argues that the sequential format captures learning behaviour better than flat aggregated vectors. The pass group consistently showed more VLE interaction than the fail group, as illustrated in the week-wise average-click chart on page 4.
extracted.Limitations:
The study does not analyze repeated students or course-repeat behaviour. It also does not perform course-level analysis in depth, despite using multiple courses. 

The dataset transformation excludes some potentially relevant complexity, and future work is suggested to add assessment scores and textual feedback data. 

Withdrawal is not retained as a prediction class in the final setup, which simplifies the problem.

annotations.text: The authors explore the effectiveness of LSTM to predict end of course performance in an online University. Results obtained on OULAD dataset show that this approach greatly outperforms the baseline Logistic Regression model, achieving 95.2% accuracy, 93% precision and 76% Recall.
Moreover, the authors show that it is possible to use a week-wise granularity to make such predictions, with the model being abel to achieve 90% accuracy whithin the first 10 weeks (out of 38).

references.title: Deploying multimodal learning analytics models to explore the impact of digital distraction and peer learning on student performance

extracted.Data sources: Questionnaire, Publications on Social Media, Student Characteristics
extracted.Features: [Male, Age, Prior Knowledge, Facebook Stay Time, Facebook Friends, Digital Distraction, Peer Learning Orientation, Peer Learning Engagement]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Prior knowledge comes form a 33 MCQ test done at the start.

Digital distraction was done using Perceived Attention Discontinuity subscale. 

Peer learning was extracted using the MSLQ. 

The work involves the of predictive models from 2299 interaction artifacts to predict peer learning. It starts with identifying whether the posts were statistics related or not. 

Statistics important posts were analyzed using different NLP representations and subsequently used for prediction using RF, SVM, LSTM or Fully Connected Neural Networks.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {R-Squared: 0.58 - Model 5, Adjusted R-Squared: 0.49 - Model 5, AIC: 314.76 - Model 5, BIC: 332.67 - Model 4B}
extracted.Cluster Description:
The best performing model in terms of hierarchical regression was the one that considered the results of the peer learning predictive model.

Objective peer learning engagement was still a significant predictor of performance in PBL.
extracted.Implications:
Prior knowledge activation or process orientation feedback can prevent  learners from incorrectly learning. 

Digital distractions negstively impact performance. 

It is benefical to introduce timed tasks to help learners focus.

Predicted peer learning was fouond to be more relevant than the perception. 

Low number of posts associated withs tatistics was a signifcant predictor of poor performance.

Mu.ltimodal Learning Analytics can be sued to identify distractions and predict poor performance.
extracted.Comments:
Authors use an hierarchical regression model to understand factors behind peer learning. 

In the process, they extract data from multiple sources and compare whether peer learning reported in questionnaire is more predictive than observed behavior.
extracted.Limitations:
Only recruited learners from one specific course. 

Limited number of ML algorithms and NLP representations were tested. 

Coding as statistics-relevant vs not -relevant, while interesting, could be explored with additional layers.

Only considers peer learning as part of a facebook group.

annotations.text: Social media is simultaneously indispensible for learning and one of the main causes of digital distraction and poor peer learning engagement. In this work, authors use a PBL approach with LA. LA model is meant to identify 3 things:

Authors use data from learner discourse in FB groups and qureestionnaires, learner characteristics, and multiple latent variables related with distraction and engagement. Authors found that performance and perceived peer engagement were correlated. However, they also found that the use of ML to predict peer engagement generates features that are more predictive of performance than the self-reports.

references.title: Analytics of Planning Behaviours in Self-Regulated Learning: Links with Strategy Use and Prior Knowledge

extracted.Features: [Monitoring Orientation, Elaboration, Planning, First re-reading, Re-reading, Evaluation]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors take interactions of users with LMS to make a taxonomy of actions that, together can be subsequently divided in SRL processes.  

Authors find 3 typical sequences of behavior that lead and translate to 3 patterns of learning behavior. These were compared using the McNemar-Bowker test, with Bonferroni correction being applied to control for family-wise error rate.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Group 1: Read and write simultaneously - students who tented to exhibit the highest rates of re-readiing while continuoulsy re-directing their writing to meet the specifications. 

Group 2: Write Intensively, Read Selectively - They start to write early and dedicate substancially more time to writing than their peers. 

Group 3: Read-first, then write -
extracted.Implications:
SRL is more dynamic than initially expected. One of the reasons for this sort of behavior relates with the fact that learners often fail to fulfill on their own promises and expectations. 

This may stem from the fact that learning conditions change and from it learners my update their behavior.
extracted.Comments: Authors use FOMM to create 3 clusters of behavior when it comes to planning and execution of a writing task.
extracted.Limitations:
Task was time-limited.

Small number of submissions of changed plans.

However, the area of the inflkuence of prior knowledge in learning strategies has received little attention from the research community.
Authors collect log data and analyze it to understand plan-making processes and whether they were followed through. Analysis on a sample of 202 studentsd shows that:

annotations.text: Peer assessment is a popular form of assessing learner ability but becomes more and more unmanageable as the number of students increases. This work uses item-response theory to improve the peer assessment step. Results show that approach is not significantly better than a random group formation.

references.title: Analysis of Student Behavior and Success Based on Logs in Moodle
references.journal: International Convention on ICT, Electronics and Microelectronics MIPRO

extracted.Courses: 41 courses at a Dutch STEM university
extracted.Features: [Announcement, Discussions, Assignment, Submission, Learning Materials, Mandatory Quiz, Optional Quiz, Metacognition andd Course Browsing]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors aggregated different types of activities in the 9 categories described as the features.

Moreover, the authors defined learning sessions, considering that two clicks were in the same session if they were within 12.1 minutes and cannot have more than 111 clicks/learning events.

Data was filtered to remove outliers,  leading to 5110 students in 34 sessions.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model:
Log-lokelihood,
Bayesian Information Criterion,
Akaike Information Criterion
extracted.Cluster Description:
Assignment-Focused tactic: Most common tactic. Dominated by Assignment Pages and Mandatory Quizzes

Material-Focused tactic:  More than half of the clicks are on materials. Second most common tactic as well.

Mixed Tactic: More diverse set of actions with no clear dominant action.

Quizz-Focused Tactic: Least common tactic in which students opt to do mandatory quizzes or optional quizzes.
extracted.Implications:
The different learning tactics are the backbone of the learning strategies covered layer. More than just characterization of the clusters over the features, authors also describe clusters in terms of most likely transitions in actions.

For example, the Mixed Tactic revels itself to be the most common way of a student starting a course.
extracted.Comments:
Selection of Groups number was done via the Expectation-Maximization Algorithm.

The first level of clustering was for learning tactics, the second for learning strategies.
extracted.Limitations:
Survivorship bias (only learners who were present over the 2 years were considered).

LMS are a partial and incomplete picture of learner behavior.

No specifics on the internal conditions of each course.

extracted.Courses: 41 courses at a Dutch STEM university
extracted.Features: [Quizz-Focused Tactic, Mixed Tactic, Material-Focused Tactic, Assignment-Focused Tactic]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors obtain the clusters of learning tactics from the Logs using EM and then use the model outputs as the features for the second clustering algorithm of learning strategies.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: “Best fit”
extracted.Cluster Description:
Balanced Strategy: No dominant tactic, as all are employed relatively often. 

Resource-Oriented: Most of the actions are accessing resources. This learning strategy is the second largest and is ranked thrid in terms of number of sessions.

Grade-Oriented: Most of the time is spent on grade menus (largest cluster). 

Flexible: Adaptation to different courses, buyt not equal “balanced” engagement in all learning tactics.
extracted.Implications:
Different strategies suggest different levels of engagement with Surfave vs Deep approaches to learning. 

Temporal level of granularity of analysis also impacts the analysis of transitions between strategies.

All strategies are found to have a significantly different impact on grade as showcased by a Kruskall-Wallis test. 

Results also show that being consistent in terms of learning strategy ois more effective than changing strategy midway.
extracted.Comments:
Authors also look at how learners change their behavior (strategy) during a course - often considering both at a half or at a quarter.

End-result: Adopting a consistent deep-level approach to learning is the best option to succeed. However, transitioning between strategies is better than consistently adopting a surface-level approach to learning.
extracted.Limitations:
Survivorship bias (only learners who were present over the 2 years were considered).

LMS are a partial and incomplete picture of learner behavior.

No specifics on the internal conditions of each course.

annotations.text: The paper presents Moodle Plugins that can be used for analytics purposes and then shows how they can be used at a Faculty in Zagred to interpret student behavior and frame it in the context of student success.

references.title: Multimodal prediction of student performance: A fusion of signed graph neural networks and large language models

extracted.Students: 761 Biology students, 528 Law students, 383 Cardiff students, 382 Sydney19 learners and 198 Sydney23 learners
extracted.Features: [Structural Embeddings from Positive and Negative answers to questions, MCQ Embeddings - GLoVe, Student Embeddings]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
Previous graph models struggle with 2 things: 

1. Establish a relationship between learners and questions.

2. Dealing with noise associated with the randomness of learner content generation.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
The model creates augmented graphs for students and questions. These are subsequently passed through 2 encoders (a positive encoder and a negative encoder). This graph encoder is “compatible” with any GNN model.

The second innovation comes from the adopted contrastive objective  function - which aims to maximize similarity of nodes across different views. 

The final innovation stems from the use of GPT to generate semantyc embeddings.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Augmentation of the graphs is done using stochastic methods to create the positive and negative sub-graphs.

Authors use Graph Attention Networks as the model used for the creation of embeddings. Sequences are converted to knowledge components using the GPT 3.5 Turbo, which are converted to embeddings using GloVe.

The initialization of learner embeddings is random - since there is no student profile.
extracted.Models: Random, GCN, GAT, SGCN, SBGNN, SBCL, LLM-SBCL
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a novel graph model that includes a positive and a negative encoder for the sequences of the answers before feeding the embeddings to an MLP. 

Authors also use an LLM to extract the knowledge concepts and use them as a feature for the models. 

Ablation studies show that the model LLM-SBCL has every layer be relevant.
extracted.Limitations:
- What are the average and standard deviation of F1-Score for in an implementation of the holdout method?

- Small student population.

- Limited capacity in cold-start scenarios

- Does not consider interactions among students or questions

- Does not explore any other LLMs

annotations.text: Learner sourcing platforms often rely on multiple choice questions to assess students. While a mechanism for feedback, this system introduces noise that hinders the ability of predictive models to make a correct prediction about student dropout or overall performance. This noise is often generated by a model’s inability to semantically distinguish between a correct and an incorrect answer.

To address this limitation, the authors propose a combination of LLMs and Graph Neural Networks that receive,as inputs, data from multiple types of sources. The model uses a bipartite graph to represent student answers. Then, they apply constrastive learning to the signed graphs and combine them with knowledge point embeddings created by the LLM.
Evaluation of the model on 5 different datasets, the authors find that it outperforms other methods, with close to 4% improvements on F1 score relative to the best baseline models.

references.title: A Semi-Supervised Learning Approach for Predicting Student's Performance: First-Year Students Case Study

extracted.Student Performance Definition: End of First Year Success (divided by Clustering Labels)
extracted.Features: [Gender, Traveltime, Age, Cost, City, Activities, School, Part-time Job, Fedu, Sports, Fjob, Paidclass, Medu, Study Method, Mjob, Study time, Income, Motivation, Famsize, Free time, Siblings, Health, Location, Major, Distance, CGPA, Transport]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Data is collected from questionnaires, with 27 variables. 

The data is given to a k-means algorithm with each cluster label representing one possible value of target.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.96 - NB, Sensitivity: 0.9286 - NB, Specificity: 1 - NB}
extracted.Comments:
From description, it seems that clusters are created using entire dataset.

Target variable is obtained as result of clustering.
extracted.Limitations:

Holdout method

Only uses NB

Likely has implementation errors

annotations.text: Academic achievements is a hall-mark of a high quality academic institution and the performance of students in the firsat year is usally a good indicator of student success later down the line.

The authors use Semi-Supervised learning to classify students in an Indonesian University based on their performance - Medium/High. Authors use sample of 140 students and 27 features. The approach starts with the use of clustering (k-Means) followed by classification (Naive Bayes).  Model performance published indicate very high performances.

references.title: Tracing Knowledge Instead of Patterns: Stable Knowledge Tracing with Diagnostic Transformer

extracted.Features: [Sequences of student responses to questions]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Prior KT models tend to overfit to patterns of responses instead of tracing stable underlying knowledge states; they suffer from information bias where inferred states change abruptly after single responses.
extracted.Novelty of Model: Proposes Diagnostic Transformer (DTransformer) with Temporal and Cumulative Attention (TCA) and a contrastive learning training paradigm to achieve stable knowledge state tracing instead of mere pattern prediction.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Uses Rasch embeddings for question–concept pairs; augments sequences with flip-response, item-drop, and swap-adjacent to enforce contrastive stability.
extracted.Models: DTransformer (full and w/o CL), DKT, DKT+, DKVMN, SAKT, AKT, CL4KT
extracted.Comments: Authors argue that stable knowledge tracing is more faithful to the learning process. Visualization shows reduced sensitivity to slips/guesses.
extracted.Limitations: Validation limited to 4 datasets. Computational cost is higher. Stability evaluated indirectly via prediction decay rather than ground-truth knowledge.

annotations.text: Knowledge Tracing is intended to provide a representation of a student’s knowledge state: what knowledge does the learn possess. However, recent applications o KT modelling focus on understanding if next question will be answered correctly, which may result in the model’s only ktracing learning activities undertaken and not knowledge states.

To address the limitation, authors propose Diagnostic Transformer, a new arquitecture that goes from question-level to knowledge level. In other works, the model diagnoses the learner’s knowledge from question mastery states. Th new training paradigm also focuses on stability of knowledge state diagnosis.
Empyrical results demonstrate that Dtransformer outperforms other models both in terms of Knowledge Tracing Tasks but in obtaining stabler knowledge state representations as well.

references.title: The Establishment of College Student Employment Guidance System Integrating Artificial Intelligence and Civic Education

annotations.text: Authors argues for the need of a job recommender system for fresh college graduates. The current system is flawed in the sense that in only considers the one-way student desires, often not taking into account ever-changing differences in student behavior.

Thus, authors propose a recommender that that aggregates learner preferences and needs while also taking into consideration employers. Interestingly, the authors stress the importance of ideological and political education to their recommender framework.

references.title: Predictive Learning Analytics and University Teachers: Usage and perceptions three years post implementation

annotations.text: Predictive learning analytics dashboards have been in use for a couple of years, altough not many systems have been deployed on a large scale. This work is a technology acceptance model work. Authors survey university professors on the adoption of PLA.

Reesults show that adoption is correlated with less experience in teaching, low anxiety and more positive attitudes. It is also negatively correlated with low effort expectancy and alck of facilitating conditions.

references.title: Portability of Predictive Academic Performance Models: An Empirical Sensitivity Analysis

extracted.Student Performance Definition: 6 Potential Forms of Academic Performance are Tested
extracted.Courses: 4 Different Classes of the same Business Management across 2 years
extracted.Features: [EvAU score, 1st course grade, math. 1 grade, math. 2 grade, early assessment, late assessment]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors test more than 60 alternative models - all possible combinations of the six variables (up to 3 simultaneous variables)

This is done by separating the students into different categories.

Variables ewre standardized.

Authors compare performance of group specific models vs general models and find that portability is different because the groups themselves are different,
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {AUC: 0.951 - Model 2}
extracted.Comments: What was done: The authors built logistic regression models to predict whether university students would pass or fail, using simple inputs like prior grades and early assessment scores. Models were developed on a base group of 39 business students, then tested on five other groups varying by subject, teacher, and language of instruction (170 students total across two years).Main findings: The models performed well on the group they were built for (AUC above 0.9), but their ability to correctly identify at-risk students (specificity) dropped consistently as the target group shared fewer characteristics with the original group. Notably, the student group itself mattered more for portability than the subject, teacher, or language. The authors conclude that universal predictive models are very difficult to achieve, even under tightly controlled conditions — echoing findings from the learning platform literature.
extracted.Limitations:
Small sample size

Only 4 groups could be used

All logit models had, at most, 3 variables

Single institution and Degree

Base model is trained on 39 students

annotations.text: Few studies in LA use previous evaluations as features. This work intends to see effect of portability when preceding performance is key factor.
Authors use data from 170 students collected across 2 academic years. They create a set of models using data from 39 and select the best 4. Then, they test the model on the remaining groups.
Authors find that, the more different the students are from the base group, the specificity of the four models tends to decrease.

annotations.text: VLEs have changed the paradigm of analytics for educational purposes as now more data than ever is available. This works deploys Deep Learning on a set of unique features to predict students at risk.

Model achieves more than 90% accuracy, greatly outperforming Logistic Regression or SVM models. Including Legacy Data AND Assessment-related data greatly impacts the performance of a model. Also, students who go back to check previous lectures also seem to perform better.

references.title: Fine-Grained Interaction Modeling with Multi-Relational Transformer for Knowledge Tracing

extracted.Students:
8300 ASSISTments 2009 sequences, 
67100 ASSISTments 2012 sequences, 2.3M Ednet-KT1, 447.8k Eedi sequences
extracted.Features: [Sequence of Previous Questions,  Answers and Related Concepts, Sequence of Correctedness]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: The current KT models often struggle with non-adjacent interactions between question-answer pairs: DKT implicitly models adjacent interactions (hidden state updates of RNN), and few attention models learn both adjacent and non-adjacent interactions.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
MRT-KT introduces a novel form of relationship encoding - unique for each two question-response pairs.

It also has a multi-relational self-attention mechanism stacked with multi-relational tarrget-dependent attention. 

In addition, the architecture also includes relation-specific temporal kernels.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Inputs (question, concepts, correctedness) are encoded sequentially to be given as input to the model.

Relationships between 2 question-answer pairs are represented by 2-bit (multi-relational target dependent) and 4-bit (multi-relational self-attention).
extracted.Models: DKT, SKT-Forget, SAKT, EKT-A, AKT, HawkesKT, LPKT, SAINT+, MRT-KT
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a model  that includes 3 relationship: 

- adjacent past interactions 
- target-dependent interactions between question and past questions, 
- non-adjacent interactions with other questions with distance grater than 1

The proposed model includes 2 attention layers (one for target interactions and the second for non-adjacent past interactions).

Authors do a set of experiments to:

1. Assess model performance against other alterrnatives,
2. Do an ablation study to check the relevance of the different layers introduced in the model.
extracted.Limitations:
- Holdout Method

- No probabilistic modelling, which hinders ability to work with open-ended questions.

annotations.text: Knowledge tracing is an important in student modelling as it is an application that infers knowledge states and allows the prediction of sucess in future questions based on past questions. A limitation of the current knowledge tracing methods is the way question-response pairings are reporesented.

The authors start with an analysis that identifies cross effects between question-respponse pairs within a sequence of anwers. Then, the authors create a multi-relational transformer for knowledge tracing that better encodes question-reponse pairings. Results show that the new Transformer model outperforms state-of-the-art is four widely-used datasets for knowledge tracing.

references.title: A Fuzzy Tree Matching-Based Personalized E-Learning Recommender System

annotations.text: Digitaslization of learning has brought many opportunities to improve learning practices. However, the proliferation also massively increased the amount of materials and content which makes it difficult for learners to navigate without guidance.
Recommender systems can address this issue, but need to deal with:

The authors employ a fuzzy approach to create a learner model and a learning activity model. The use of the fuzzy-tree architecture is used to infer relationshios between activities and requirements. Moreover, the authors create a hybrid recommender system using this approach that recommends content. Performance of the algorithm is compared against knowledge-based filtering and collaborative filtering approaches using the MovieLens dataset.

Q-network weights are randomly initialized for both agents
extracted.Updates to Recommendations:
- High-level agent selects a learning goal.

- Low-level agent recommends a course sequence to achieve the goal.

- Rewards are dynamically adjusted on learner’s engagement, completion and satiscfction after each interaction.

- Policies are updated using a Dynamic Recurrent Mechanism

Course vectors were created using Word2Vec

Learner features are normalized and concatenated with engagement metrics

Authors use Holdout Method, making the separation by Learner ID.
extracted.Comments:
Authors use Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning to develop a dual-based recommender system:

Higher-level Agent: Decides the students’ learning goal, 

Lower-level Agent: Recommends Specific Courses

This system outperforms multiple baselines

Authors create 2 alternative HELARs:

HELAR_W → brings the Attention Coefficient into the objective function

HELAR_A → Brings Attention Weight Matrix into Objective Function
extracted.Limitations:
- Model training requires enough data for 2 Q-matrices

- Reward design depends on platform-specific metrics

- No specific handling of cold-start scenario

annotations.text: The authors adddress the course selection in an online scenario problem by using Reinforcement Learning to create a dynamic representation of learners. However RL approaches often come short due to the exploration-exploitation tradeoff. Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning with Dynamic Recurrent mechanism (HELAR) includes a novel policy gradient method → a recurrent scheme that exploits current knowledge while also exploring learning preferences.
Results from Backtests on 2 datasets with positive results.
What the paper does:
Instead of recommending a single next course, this system builds a two-level decision process:
It learns from historical behavior and continuously updates its strategy as learners engage (or disengage) with the platform.
Why it matters:

Traditional recommenders (like CF or GRU4Rec) only look at short-term clicks. Here, reinforcement learning (RL) focuses on long-term learning success—whether a student completes and benefits from a sequence of courses. The “dynamic reward adjustment” gives higher feedback when engagement increases and penalizes the agent when students drop out or rate courses poorly.
Main findings:
Bottom line:

The system learns to recommend whole learning pathways instead of isolated courses, balancing exploration (new topics) and exploitation (familiar domains). It’s powerful but computationally demanding, requiring careful reward tuning and large-scale behavioral data.

references.title: Relationship between student profile, tool use, participation, and academic performance with the use of Augmented Reality technology for visualized architecture models

annotations.text: Authors use augmented reality to assist visualize 3D models in an architecture course. Collaborative behaviors with this system seem to improve student outcomes. Authors perform an experiment where they test the students, do an Augmented Reality Experiment and then are tested again.
Results show that use of mobile devices correlates with Motivation and Success. Authors also discuss challenges with implementation and content generation.

references.title: Data-Driven Support Infrastructure for Iterative Team-Based Learning

annotations.text: TBL is commonly used to help individuals develop collaborative learning activities, but iterative TBL has been somewhat neglected in the literature. The authors introduce a framework for iterative TBL that sub-divides iterative TBL into 4 stages of group activity workflows and 3 data flows.
The framework is put to the test in two applications and the authors discuss impacts and challenges to the development of the framework.

annotations.text: Author attempts to identify which LMS features are relevant for course achievement. For that, some SRL-derived features are included.

Experiment uses 530 students of an online course and attributes such as regular study were found to significantly predict course achievement. Authors argue for the use of more variables informed by SRL-behaviors in predictive models as they provide a more accurate picture of the student’s overall learning process.
In addition, authors also show that measures collected by the course halfway point were already predictive of performance, suggesting more potential for an early warning system.

Higher education is adopting online learning as a legitimate way of delivering education and demand is continuously increasing. However, not all students are able to take advantage of online learning, as it requires substantially developed self-regulated learning skills - as showcased by the fact that regular study correlates heavily with success while procrastination correlates negatively with it.

Most studies focus oin identifying SRL traits via self-reporting, which is not the most reliable indicator. LMS data can be used for this purpose. Learning analytics has already been employed to try and assist students. The most common predictors extracted from LMS logs are frequency measures such as the number of logins or the time spent reading specific pages → with more engagement being associated with success online.

However, there is also the notion that this frequentist approach is minimally relevant to the prediction of success (often from education science theorists such as Hadwin) and, simultaneously, provide little insights into what can be done to intervene with students. Thus, there is an argument for the extraction of different types of variables congruent with learning theory.

SRL in this paper is defined as the ability to set goals and manage their own learning and performance. Online learning requires these skills as success in it requires the commitment to complete work or tasks. Successful students are dilligent and have a good ability to manage their time and effort. Not having these skills often leads to procrastination and, in online settings, this procrastination often leads to dropout.

Among the SRL strategies, time management has been shown to especially relevant for online settings, followed by self-efficacy and positive attitude. This urges the importance of these constructs and intend to give them more attention.

A common drawback of SRL models is the limited number of instruments to capture student self-regulation (often surveys). LMS can assist in this. Logs are the most common source of data from LMS environments as they are relatively easy to process, despite still requiring some work. An example of a learning analytics application in universities is Purdue’s Course Signals - a system that intends to track student performance in terms of their risk of failing.

Despite their promise, there are also issues with learning analytics approaches. One of such issues is the grounding of the warning systems in learning theory. Another is the unusefulness of theses systems in identifying what areas of the student’s process require improvement (actionable recommendations).

The literature shows that students who are more participative tend to perform better. Engagement with the LMS is one such measure. While the positive relationship is almost ubiquitous (pointing to effort as a better predictor of success than talent), which activities lead to better performance vary from author to author.

Other types of variables can also be found in the literature despite being less explored. Asarta and Schmidt focused on effects of timingg, volume, intensity and consistency of accesses. Under this perspective quality of interactions is much more meaningful than quantity of interactions. Findings show that keeping pace is a very significant determinant of student success. The notion of regular accesses is also observed in other studies.

The data was collected from an e-learning course taught at a South Korean university, comprising 575 students, 530 of which were considered. The course Introduction to Color had 13 units and 2 offline tests. All interactions, except the offline exams, were online.

The course had a duration of 15 weeks, with the midterm occurring at week 8. Thus, 2 different timepoints were considered: course halfway point and end of course. Moreover, the authors consider two distinct targets: Course Exam Score and Final Course Score. There were also many variables related with LMS features and, finally, the Year of Study was used as a control variable.
The results from the hierarchical multiple regression analysis show that the model with 4 predictors is the best performing one both in terms of predicting Final Course Score and Course exam Score.

Authors also add an early prediction model that considers the constructs collected at the course’s halfway point AND the grade on the Midterm Exam. Surprisingly, the early prediction is better than the end of course prediction. A likely cause is the fact the early prediction model considers the midterm exam score.

references.title: Recommendation for Effective Standardized Exam Preparation

annotations.text: Most AIED techgnologies attempt to improve learning in what the authors describe to be an indirect manner. The authors present a recommender system that aims to maximize student performance directly. Essencially, this system has a pool of exercises and, for a given student, computes the expected score the student would have if the student learns the next exercise. Then, it recommends the exercise that maximizes the score.
A/B testing on 1713 students shows that the RCES outperforms other ontology-derived models.

references.title: How can valid and reliable automatic formative assessment predict the acquisition of learning outcomes?

The work uses Random Forest and is meant to compare the effectiveness of different feature sets. Usage of previous exam  increases predicting capacity on both courses.
extracted.Limitations:
- Only one model.

- Small number of courses in a specific area (Mathematics)

extracted.Students: 614 Mathematics I Students, and 199 Discrete Mathematics with Graph Theory Students
extracted.Features: [Quizzes, Homeworks, Exams, Video, E-Textbbook Consumption, Class Attendance]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors use Random Search for hyper-parameter tuning.
extracted.Comments:
The authors create an ML algorithm to predict the exam performance of the learners. 

The work uses Random Forest and is meant to compare the effectiveness of different feature sets. Usage of previous exam grade greatly increases predicting capacity.

Analysis of the quaternary confusion matrices highlights that the model is better at predicting the second and third quartiles rather than the first and 4th. Essencially, the model is good at making predictions at the margin but struggles a little more at identifying more extreme cases.
extracted.Limitations:
- Only one model.

- Small number of courses in a specific area (Mathematics)

- Does not consider activity outside the LMS as a predictor.

annotations.text: The validity of assessments heavily relies on alignment between the assessment and the intended Learning Outcomes. The aim of the paper is to identify what is the predictive power of formative assessment for summative assessment (and consequently acquisition of LOs).

Authors look at formative assessment results and log data and use them as inputs for a RF model. REsults on data from 2 math courses shows that formative assessments, combined with previous summative assessments are good predictors of summative assessment and, moreover, better predictors than any form of student engagement.
Findings also suggest that predictions are less reliable for highest performing and lowest performing students.

references.title: Recommender systems facilitate aesthetic experience accumulation in informal learning environments – a study from China

annotations.text: This is an empyrical study conducted using 67 students from China. A recommender system made suggestions on art knowledge for students to take. The authors evaluated whether the recommender increased the learners awareness about art appreciation. Satisfaction results show that students were happy with the model and that the fact that the activities were done out of school was a big motivating factor.

references.title: Adapting New Learners and New Resources to Micro Open Learning via Online Computation
references.journal: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems

extracted.Initialization Method:
The initial labelling of learners starts with the mapping of the association between learners and the MicroOERs: ratings, clickthrough rates.

OER are createdvia an ontology from the courses.  

When in cold-start it can be attributed to: new learner, new item and new community:
when triggered, the user starts with online computation.
extracted.Updates to Recommendations:
Next Optimal MicroOER obtained from learning path sequence.

In the cold-start scenario, the authors create a set of learning paths using Genetic Algorithms and, after selection of the ideal candidate learning path, suggest the first one.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Knowledge Representation: Construction of the learner profile using Ontologies and Text Mining.

MicroOER representation: mapping of ontology to chunks.


The first step is finding associations between elements. Focus in 3 major relationship types: ConsistsOf, RequiredSequence and RecommendedSequence.

Users are clustered using U-Clustering
extracted.Evaluation: {Precision: 0.48 - GA, Recall: 0.35 - GA}
extracted.Comments:
Authors intend to develop a recommender of learning paths that is “robust” to the cold-start problem - asystem they call MLaaS

The main idea is to create a learning path that is optimal to the learner and continuously suggest the next Open Educational Resource the learner should take. The proposed system is compared specifically against other approaches when it comes to the cold-start problem. 

Results show that model is robust in the cold-start scenario. Moreover, the results also show that transferability from other domains is very good for the educational domain.
extracted.Limitations:
- Validation is performed on simulated user profiles.

- No evaluation of effectiveness of the recommmendations.

annotations.text: Remote learning has been a recourse option ever since the arisal of Covid-19. This has generated a big influx of new educational materials and, simultaneously, fast migration to online has brought the cold start to the spotlight.

The authors propose what they call online computation. Authors use a lightweight learner-micro-OER profile and use 2 algorithms to address the cold-start problem for users and contents. The recommender system generates learning paths.

annotations.text: Work focuses on combining the trace data obtained from LMS logs and regularly used to predict performance with self-reported indicators often used to identify SRL. To do that, the authors perform a case-study on a sample of 145 students. Students perform a self-report assessment for SRL indicators and their logs are collected and analyzed.

Results show that a model that combines both sources of data leads to a greater explained variance of the final score than when the sources are used in isolation. Authors make no specific mention of early prediction in the abstract.
