Categoricals are One-Hot Encoded and the data was subsequently normalized.

The academic year was split into 8 partitions and the authors collect the data for each period (Ocs and Wri have 4)

Authors use 3 kNN base regressors using self-training.
extracted.Models: Linear Regression, MSSRA, IBk, M5Rules, M5 Model Tree, Random Forest, SMOreg
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model:
Friedman Rank:
{MAE:  7.625 - MSSRA, RAE: 7.00 - MSSRA, RMSE: 8.00 - MSSRA,  PCC: 8.125 - MSSRA}
extracted.Comments:
Authors present a novel frameworkk for Semi-Supervised Regression called MSSRA.

Authors keep performance for each period - SMO outperforms all others on the first 2 partitions, but MSSRA outperforms all others past that point. 

More adata leads to better predictions as usual.

Authors also test different ratios of labeled data in their dataset, showing that it is possible to obtain good results with as little as 10 to 15% of labeled data.
extracted.Limitations: Small sample size.

annotations.text: Most LA work focuses on the use of supervised classifiers to predict students at risk. However, SSL approaches have shown to be superior to supervised methods. Moreover, Regression is also an underused method.

Authors develop a Semi-Supervised Learning Regression algorithm to predict the outcomes of undergraduate students. They carry out multiple tests to showcase the superiority of their method and conclude that they can obtain a MAE of 1.23 before the middle of the academic year. Authors also discuss implications for practice.

references.title: Application of Educational Data Mining Approach for Student Academic Performance Prediction Using Progressive Temporal Data

extracted.Students: 1708 students from a Faculty of Science
extracted.Features: [Weekly Clickstream for 10 weeks, Demographic Data]
extracted.Moment of Prediction: Every Week for the First 10 Weeks of a Semester
extracted.Preprocessing Details: LMS data were synchronized with other sources over the first 10 weeks. Weekly clickstream of LMS content was aggregated so each record contained Week1...Week10 access values plus the class label. Students were de-identified. Authors also compare LMS vs LMS+DMG, showing that adding demographics improves prediction accuracy.
extracted.Models: Random Forest, Logistic Regression, J48, GBoost, SVM, kNN, MLP
extracted.Assessment Strategy: 10-Fold Cross-Validation for 6 models, 5-Repeated Holdout for MLP
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: LMS+DMG outperforms LMS alone in average accuracy
extracted.Comments: This is the Level 1 model in the paper: it predicts performance for a specific unit based on LMS clickstream. The authors argue that demographics help both predictive performance and interpretability for educators.
extracted.Limitations: LMS use was inconsistent across the university; only a small subset of units used ATutor for a whole semester, so LMS data were incomplete. Also, the model is unit-specific, limiting transferability across courses.

extracted.Students: 1708 students from a Faculty of Science
extracted.Courses: Not unit-specific; general academic performance across students in the faculty
extracted.Data sources: Internet usage log files from the university firewall; demographic data in enhanced versions; previous CGPA in the best-performing dataset
extracted.Features: [Browsing-category features and Internet-usage-application features, aggregated by time and frequency of use; top 26 browsing-category features and top 26 application features selected by Pearson correlation; enhanced datasets also include gender, academic major, year of study, and previous CGPA]
extracted.Moment of Prediction: Every Week for the First 10 Weeks of a Semester
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Raw firewall logs were transformed into Internet-usage datasets. Features were aggregated according to how long and how often students accessed the Internet. Because the data were sparse, the authors applied correlation-based feature ranking and selected top features. They tested 9 Category–Application weight combinations (90–10 to 10–90), plus comparisons among LOG, LOG+DMG, and LOG+DMG+CGPA datasets.
extracted.Models: Random Forest, Logistic Regression, J48, GBoost, SVM, kNN, MLP
extracted.Assessment Strategy: 10-Fold Cross-Validation for 6 models, 5-Repeated Holdout for MLP
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.943 – Decision Tree (80% APP / 20% CAT), Precision: 0.943 – Decision Tree (80% APP / 20% CAT), F-Measure: 0.943 – Decision Tree (80% APP / 20% CAT), ROC Area: 0.968 – Random Forest (10% APP / 90% CAT)} on the Week 9 CAT-APP-DMG-CGPA-FREQUENCY dataset
extracted.Comments: This is the Level 2 model in the paper. The authors show that Internet usage logs can complement LMS data and that adding demographics improves performance from about week 4 onward; the best results come from LOG+DMG+CGPA. They also argue that mixed model performance depends on the weighting between browsing categories and application features. Prediction becomes notably stronger from weeks 3–4 onward.
extracted.Limitations: Data come from only one university, so generalizability is limited. Early-semester behavior is noisy because even successful students may show relaxed Internet-use patterns in the first weeks. Also, Internet-use data are not purely study-related, though the authors frame this as part of students’ broader behavioral footprint

annotations.text: LMS are a limited option to evaluate student performance and the authors propose a new model that not only uses LMS data but uses Internet usage patterns as well.

The authors had access to Internet usage data and used it in conjuntion with demographics and LMS to build predictive models that identify students at-risk. The internet usage data required some for of categorization.

references.title: Educational Resource Recommender Systems Using Python and Moodle

annotations.text: Recommender system uses Youtube API to recommend materials based on students’ level of knowledge. Recommender is deployed into the context of course during a semester.

references.title: Co-Training Method Based on Semi-Decoupling Features for MOOC Learner Behavior Prediction

extracted.Target: Certificate Obtained (1) vs Certificate Not Obtained (0)
extracted.Data sources:
Student Characteristics, 
LMS logs,
Performance on the Platform
extracted.Features: [course_id, userid_DI, registered, viewed, explored, final_cc_cname_, LoE_DI, YoB, genderr, grade, start_time_DI, last_event_DI, nchapters, ndays_act, nforum_posts, nnplay_video, nevents, roles, incomplete_flag]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors use co-training to perform semi-supervised learning - training 2 different classifiers on data (represented by 2 redudant, but independent views) and obtainning predictions on unlabeled data. The highest confidence predictions are subsequently joined to a new view and removed from the unlabeled set.

 The creation of the views (and the independence of the features selected from each view) is done using semi-decoupled co-training - highly correlated variables are placed on the same view. Low correlated features are placed into 2 views (important features are also separated): 

The selection was donne using DT Feature Importances and Correlation Coefficients.
extracted.Models:
Logistic Regression, 
Random Forest, 
LightGBM, 
GBoost,
Self-Training, 
Co-Training, 
Tri-Training
Semi-Decoupling Features for Self-Supervised Learning
extracted.Comments:
Authors use a public dataset to validate their Semi-Supervised Learning algorithm - HarvardX is one, but there is also data from other contexts. 

Multiple values of k for CV were tested: 2, 3 and 5. The proportions for training and testing are tested (e.g. for k=3, 2 folds train/1  val and 1 fold train/2 folds val are tested). 

Results seem extremely impressive.
extracted.Limitations:
Data dependent: Co-Training requires a substantial number of features that allow the creation of the dual views. 

Cumulative addition of noise with the increase initerations. 

Risk of underfitting if features are still semi-decoupled. 

Risk of computational overload if number of features is too large.

annotations.text: A common way of performing semi-supervised learning is co-training. However, co-training requires redundant and conditionally independent dual views, something that does not seem to exist with enough abundance for this application.

The authors propose a co-training method that semi-decouples features from a single known view. With this, using the edX dataset, the authors were able to create models that predict learner behavior that were better than other alternatives.

references.title: Learning Analytics and Computerized Formative Assessments: An Application of Dijkstra’s Shortest Path Algorithm for Personalized Test Scheduling

annotations.text: In K-12 tests are often done in pre-determined dates without taking into consideration the pace at which different students may actually learn. The authors propose a recommender system that finds the optimal time when a student should be tested. Authors test the effectiveness of their system in a large dataset of Second and Fourth-graders.
Results show that growth could be promoted while eliminating tests that could be regarded as redundant.

references.title: Educational Data Mining Using Semi-Supervised Ordinal Classification

extracted.Comments:
The work implem,,ents a myriad of interesting techniques that are often unmentioned in other works: 

Ordinal Classication - order in class labels and is something that is often not considered even in Multiclass contexts. 

SSL - The ability to train a model with with unlabeled data…The most common paradigms are co-training and self-training.

The implementation of SSOL was tested on 3 distinct datasets.

annotations.text: Some common problems with educational data are the lack of labelled data and the use of nominal class labels (not ordinal).

Authors propose SSOC method that combines semi supervised learning and ordinal classification. This approach led to substantial improvements in  accuracy. Task still unspecified. However, the approach considers that classes have an intrinsic order, thus allowing this method to be adequate for contexts of predicting performance.
Tests on 3 distinct educational datasets validate this hypothesis.

references.title: Who will work together? Factors influencing autonomic group formation in an open learning environment

annotations.text: Independent selection of learning goals and teammates is especially relevant in online contexts. The authors extract behaviors from the LMS, text that is generated and p+ersonal attributes to perform a social network analysis to identify collaborator relationship networks.
Results show that people who are more similar and who are friends beforehand are more likely to form collaborative relationships.

references.title: The scalable implementation of predictive learning analytics at a distance  learning university: Insights from a longitudinal case study

annotations.text: The paper looks at the OU Analyze platform and how it is being received at an organizational level and by professors.

references.title: Showing high-achieving college applicants past admissions outcomes increases undermatching
references.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

annotations.text: The work is conduct using data available in the platform Naviance, which stores the information about colleges their users wish to go to. The results show that students using the platform are more likely to apply to colleges for which they are generally over-qualified and less likely to apply to colleges whose average qualifications are superiro to the student’s own qualification.

references.title: Mitigating Biases in Student Performance Prediction via Attention-Based Personalized Federated Learning

extracted.Students: 1265 FOC learners, 2304 QDS learners, MOSFETs learners
extracted.Courses: Fiber Optic Communications, Quantum Detectors and Sensors and Essentials of MOSFETs graduate courses in Purdue University
extracted.Features: [watch_noquiz, watch_correct, watch_incorrect, watch_noanswer, forum_post, forum_reply, forum_view, gender, country, year of birth]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Different activity types are represented with One-hot encoding. The nature of the data is being sequential -Activities are recorded for each timestep.

In addition, the students are separated bby different student subgroups.

Training is done with recourse to Self-Supervised learning: local-global that customizes predictions for each subgroup.

Weight updates are done with recourse to attention mechanisms.
extracted.Models: GRU, LSTM, AttnGRU-L, AttnGRU-G, FedAvg, FedAttn, FedIRT, Ourts-AtttnAgg,
extracted.Comments:
Authors train models for different subbgroups -Gender, Continent and Year of Birth. 

The experiments includee testing the training of models speficic for subgroups vs more “global models”. Results show that the Personalized Models (Local with Self-Supervised Learning) consistently outperform the Global altrernatives.

Improvements of separability are also noticeable via t-SNE visualizations
extracted.Limitations:
- Student subgroups weer defined by human criteria

No general results (irrespective of subgroup) are shown, which hinders interpretability of results.

annotations.text: Problems with generalization of student models due to under representation of some student groups. Authors make use of federaed learning to create group specific models derived from a global student model.

Authors also use an attention mechanism to obtain better representations of student behavior. Results on 3 datasets show that approach is better at predicting student outcomes for all student subgroups.

references.title: Time-on-Task Estimation by data-driven Outlier Detection based on Learning Activities

extracted.Courses: One PG and one UG course
extracted.Features: [Log in, Log out, Dashboard viewed, Dashboard reset, Category viewed, Courses Searched, Course viewed, Forum, Calendar Susbcription, Calendar Event updates, Calendar Event Deletes, Calendar Events Created, Badges Viewed, Notifications Viewed, Blog Entries Viewed, User Reports Viewed, Grade Overview, User PW updated, User updated, User Profile viewed, Tag Added/Reemoved, Tag Created/Deleted, Message Sent, Group Message Sent, Message Deleted, Message Viewed, Message Contact Added, User List Viewed, User Profile Viewed, Blog Entries Viewed, User Report Viewed]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors removed multiple irrelevant logs and group the remaining ones according to a taxonomy of Authentication, Overall Site, Profile and Social Interactions.

These are later grouped into components.

From here, the authors look at the behavior of students across different components - aggregate them into learning activities and look at the learning sequence to identify abnormal times on task - outliers.


The authors look at course and activity to identify how to deal wioth these outliers.

Authors compare 6 distinct forms of outlier treatment.
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a distinct way of identifyng outliers when it comes to time spent on tasks. Authors compare 6 forms of outlier definition and treatment:

MeanSTD: Replace outliers with mean

MedianMAD: uses Median to fill

IQRMed: Uses Median on IQR

IQRWinsor: Winsorizes

Z-Score: Removes all outside regular z-score range

TrimmedMean: Uses a trimmed mean instad of the full mean.

Other than that, authors also consider different possible absolute of thresholds for activities.
extracted.Limitations:
-  No model assessment strategy

 - Small sample size

 - Only uses Multiple Linear Regression

annotations.text: Time-on-task is a popular construct in the learning analytics literature. However, pre-processing details are often unmentioned which affects reproducibility of steps done. Authors propose a method for time on task estimation that takes into account differences in activities. Real-world experiments show that method outperforms current state-of-the-art.

references.title: Enhancing the Accuracy of Predicting Students Grades in Open-Ended Questions through Adjustments to Attention Weights

extracted.Features: [TF-IDF representation of student answers]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The authors made 5 questions to the students every week and used TF-IDF to create textual representation of the answers of students.

This is followed by a Transformer Encoder model to assign different weights to each word.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model:
Accuracy:
{0.726 - LUKE, F1-Score: 0.563 - LUKE}

Learners had to answer these 5 questions every week of the semester.

Q1: Please explain today’s content in your own words.  Q2: Write down what you understood and what you were able to do from today’s content.  Q3: Write down what you did not understand or were not able to do from today’s content.  Q4: If you have any questions, please write them down. Authors make a comparison between the use the raw answers vs the approach → model performance is significantly better.

annotations.text: Increased digitalization of learning environments has allowed for the use of Log data to predict performance. However, these models requiure a larger-scale system that continuously collects and updates the model with new data. In order to have an easier system for data collection, the authors extract lecture questions from a Japanese University and use them to classify student grades.

The authors also attempt to improve the performance of the model by utilizing a TF-IDF representation of the text answers. The goal here is to identify words that are indicative to the student’s grade. The resultts show that proposed methodoly represents a slight improvement over the baseline models tested.

references.title: A behavioral sequence analyzing framework for grouping students in an e-learning system

annotations.text: Grouping students often leads to communities, but current criteria for grouping are limited. The paper proposes a framework to group students according to behavior, which allows, in the future, to correctly assign a newcomer - in this case, groups refers to learning communities.. Using a graph-based model, authors use spectral clustering to find fair groups. Authors test their model on both a real and synthetic datasets and outperforms other options.

references.title: Prediction of Students’ Grade by Combining Educational Knowledge Graph and Collaborative Filtering

extracted.Courses: 3 Cohorts of a Software Engineering Program
extracted.Features: [Course Triplets (i.e. Entity → Relation → Entity), Student Grades Matrix]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors start by creating a knowledge graph:

1. TextRank is used to rctract top 20 entities per course
2. Create Triplets of Entity Relationship Entity.

Data is subsequently separated for holdout method: 80/10/10

From here, the data follows 2 distinct modules: 

Course Information is subjected to the TransE algorithm (meant to find courses that are similar to the one requiring prediction).

Learners are filtered using collaborative filtering.

The results of these modules are fused together for prediction.
extracted.Comments:
Authors look at the task of preddicting grade starting from a knowledge graph.

The authors explain that the features used are couse information and the student’s grade matrix.

Experiment is repeated once per term. Clustering based approach starts being better for the second—term (smaller history) but is later surpassed by KN-CF at later stages in program duration.

Essencially, the paper proposes that the knowledge graph into CF is a better alterrnative to Clustering, User-CF and Course-CF.
extracted.Limitations:
- Cold Start Problem.

- Holdout method implementation.

- No comparison against more supervised conventional approaches (Deep Learning).

- Small datasets.

- Performance improvements do not seem to follow a continuum.

- Requires validation on data from other institutions.

annotations.text: Collaboative filterming methods used to predict grades often put too much emphasys in history and not as much in contents of the course. The authors explore the ability of knowledge graphs to extract information from course descriptions and use it to predict a student’s grade. Models are evaulated using RMSE and are shown to outperform standard collaborative filtering and k-Means.

references.title: Time slice imputation for personalized goal-based recommendation in higher education

extracted.Recommender System Type: Goal-based personalized sequential recommendation; LSTM/RNN grade-prediction model with backprop-based time-slice imputation; compared against exhaustive enumeration
extracted.Target of Recommendation: Courses to take next for preparation toward a target course; objective is to maximize probability of earning ≥ B in the target course while recommending a course the student can pass
extracted.Students: 164,196 anonymized undergraduates; evaluation uses students with at least 2 semesters before Spring 2016
extracted.Data sources: UC Berkeley enrollment and grade histories, Fall 2008–Spring 2017
extracted.Initialization Method: Stage 1: collective training of the course-grade prediction model (Model 2 from prior work). Stage 2: fix model parameters, infer learner hidden state, initialize candidate-course vector with s = (1,0)^n and all-ones weights, then optimize recommendation weights via backprop; enumeration used as baseline
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Recommendations are recomputed per learner and per goal after reading the learner’s history; no explicit periodic retraining schedule reported
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Semester input = multi-hot co-enrollment vector + grade encoding collapsed to ≥B / <B. Domain filters remove courses already passed and courses above the target course level
extracted.Evaluation: RNN+Enumeration: high-perf. students 57.65, gap 21.21, overall 49.60. RNN+Backprop: high-perf. students 77.17, gap 6.51, overall 75.78
extracted.Comments: Main contribution is replacing exhaustive candidate enumeration with per-learner backprop over the recommendation time slice. Backprop improves overall course recommendation accuracy, but reduces separation between high- and low-performance groups
extracted.Limitations: Paper notes: candidate recommendations are optimized independently rather than under a constrained simultaneous-input setting; multi-time-slice imputation is difficult; domain knowledge filters are needed to avoid spurious recommendations; cold start gives generic rather than personalized recommendations

extracted.Recommender System Type: Goal-based personalized sequential recommendation; LSTM/RNN correctness-prediction model with backprop-based time-slice imputation; compared against exhaustive enumeration
extracted.Target of Recommendation: Pages/videos to view next in preparation for an upcoming quiz; objective is to maximize predicted correctness on the target quiz
extracted.Students: 4,763 learners with at least one problem answer
extracted.Courses: 1 edX Physics course; 868 book/video pages plus quiz problems
extracted.Data sources: 1,869,406 learner actions: page/video views and problem attempts; 177,623 correct and 104,711 incorrect answers
extracted.Initialization Method: Stage 1: collective training of problem-correctness prediction model on action sequences. Stage 2: fix model parameters, infer learner hidden state, initialize candidate-page vector with s = (1,0)^n and all-ones weights, then optimize via backprop toward an all-correct target quiz; enumeration used as baseline
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Recommendations are generated per learner at a time slice before the target quiz; no explicit online retraining schedule reported
extracted.Preprocessing Details: One-hot action per time slice: either page/video view or problem answer. Masked cross-entropy is applied only on answered problems. Filters remove quiz pages and pages that occur after the target quiz
extracted.Evaluation: Prediction task: accuracy 77.17%, AUC 0.8749; raw correct-answer rate 62.91%. Recommendation eval — RNN+Enumeration: quiz-correct rec. group 72.12, gap 8.10, overall 11.04. RNN+Backprop: 66.51, gap 1.05, overall 12.17
extracted.Comments: Extends the same goal-based framework from course preparation to within-course MOOC resource recommendation. Backprop gives slightly higher overall overlap accuracy, but much weaker discrimination between better and worse quiz outcomes than enumeration
extracted.Limitations: Same paper-level limitations: independently optimized candidates, difficulty with multi-time-slice imputation, reliance on domain filters, and weak personalization for cold-start learners

annotations.text: Authors use deep learning to predict what should the student’s lext action be based on his past actions. The authors experiment with their approach on two datasetsm one from a higher education institution and the other from a MOOC.

references.title: A systematic literature review on educational recommender systems for teaching and learning: research trends, limitations and opportunities

annotations.text: There is a gap in the way recommender systems are used and evaluated in the educational context even though these types of systems have, over the years, been  becoming more and more popular. This review uses 16 (?) papers.

Most studies follow an would-be-called hybrid approach and their evaluation tends to mostly be focused on evaluation of accuracy and not pedagogic effectiveness which brings to the front a need for better ways of measuring the effectiveness of recommender systems in educational contexts.
Digital Technologies are part of a decision support system. Reccomender systems can assist educators make better plans and have students receive recommendations for better content.

However, implementing in educational settings has been challenging. Variability between student needs and preferences is a big hurdle. Also, these systems also suffer from: cold-start and data sparsity. Also, there is an issue with the fact that there is a need vs preference dichotomy in education:

The authors identify research whose main purpose is to address the previously identified issues but there does not seem to exist a standardized approach to it and each author does what they sort of want to. Thus, the research aims to be a SLR on ERS.
There have been some SLR on the topic:

The authors grouped the previous essays in 2 main areas: thw first being specific ERS subjects such as recommender methods (content-based vs user-based, etc…) and similar recommendable resources. Most of the works also put little emphasys on the evaluation part of the recommender.
The second group focuses on providing a broader overview of the research landscape on the area.
The work is important because it aims to provide a detailed analysis of: how recommendations are created and presented and how to evaluater recommendations. The work uses a SLR for that.
The work is a SLR based on Kitchenham and Charters. The review considers 4 different research questions that may warrant an answer:

An interesting approach is that athe athor evaluates the quality of the papers by using a weighted sum of different determinants of success. Each paper would receive a score from 0 to 10 - selection of only papers that achieved a quality score >7. From an initial set of 756 papers, only 16 were selected for this review.

Then, the information about each paper was collected and placed in tabulated records: recommendation approach, recommendation technique, input parameters, data collection strat and methodology, evaluation of the recoomender: setting, approaches, methodology and metrics.
Authors start with a paper by paper “shopping list” of studies that advance ERS field:
The authors identify 2 main methods of presenting recommendations. Most of the works focus on recommending learning materials.

As is costumary in most LA literature up to this point, most of the development and research is still too focused on validating the usefulness of the tools and not into actually putting them into service. Very few research works actually focus on taking the recommender and putting it into use.
There are 3 main approaches: offline, user study (questionnaires and A/B testing) and fully online. None of the studies reviewed here use online testing and stick to offline and/or user study.

User satisfaction seems to be the most common parameter being measured in papers deploying user study methods combined with offline methods. There is one paper that combines user satisfaction with pedagogical effectiveness of a recommender.

In user study exclusive approaches, there seems to be an effor tto measure the effectiveness of the recommender system in promoting student learning - whether it is by influencing their learning score or whether it impacts the time students take to complete a task. System attractiveness is a second important metric. Other, less popular metrics include pedagogical effectiveness, diversity in recommendations, perceived usefulness of the materials and easiness. It should be noted, however, that most works do not evaluate their recommender on single perspective and, instead, try to find multiple perspectives to evaluate the quality of their recommender system.
The Wan and Niu papers (2016, 2018 and 2020) seem to be among the most complete research works on this area.

One of the main limitations is the reproducibility of the results. Only Wu (2020) actually has a public educational dataset for this purpose. In addition, most data used in the surveys refers to a small dataset AND a dataset from a single institution. Thus, there is a very big need for open and public data that can allow for the proper benchmarking of ERS.

Moreover, in the context of education, there does not seem to be a lot of research that focuses on measuring the impact of problems that are well-known in the recommender system community (e.g. cold-start, overspecialization or sparsity). Authors point to the need of addressing these issues also in ERS because these issues have a direct impact on the quality of recommendations.

The works published also seem to lack more fine-grained information about the learners themselves. Including other elements such as cultural information or even emotional state may play an important role later down the line. Another important area for future work may come from introducing overlooked attributes about the users.

Use of recommender systems such as these in informal educational scenarios. These can be understood as cenarios outside an educational institution where, for multiple reasons, there is not an explicit expert supervising or guiding the learner throughout the whole process. More research on this type of scenarios would be welcomed.

Another area of improvement is the use of multi-dimensional evaluation frameworks. The main idea would be to include other forms of evaluation that go beyond the evaluation of the system’s accuracy or the user’s satisfaction.

Authors present a SLR that aims to compile the different trends and views on the use of Recommender Systems in education for the purposes of teaching and learning support. The authors found that most approaches use an hybrid approach that has a user centered design. There is also an apparent lack of more advanced recommender system algorithms in education - specifically what the authors call bandit-based or deep learning approaches.

The main problems identified revolve around the notion that the datasets are small and not reproducible, calling for the appearance and publication of large-scale publicly available datasets that can be used for different purposes. As far as limitations of the study itself, the authors point towards the low number of repos used for paper selection.

references.title: Efficiency of using VNS algorithm for forming heterogeneous groups for CSCL learning

annotations.text: Authors formalize group formation as a mathematical problem and use Variable Neighborhood Search to form heterogenous groups in a collaborative setting. VNS was compared against the best results obtained by other optimization methods (CPLEX).

To show effectiveness, the authors took 3 groups of college students and, inside each group, used different algorithms to form teams of 4 in a Calculus course. Students in groups created with VNS outperformed their peers in groups formed randomly. However, the VNS groups did noty outperform the groups following ontological guidelines.

references.title: Demonstrating the impact of study regularity on academic success using learning analytics

extracted.Features: [Weekly Videos and Quizz scores, weekly Last Date of Access to Each Learning Activity]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Data was anonimized and stored in a vector containing the information for the different weeks.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Regular Learners: Often follow lecturer advice, activity closely follows recommended learning pathway.

Irregular or Selective Learners: They pick and choose which activities to partake in. 

Late Learners: Only access VLE closer to exam
extracted.Implications: Students in 2022 were informed of successful and less successful learning pathways undertaken by their previous colleagues. And this translated into a noticeable behaviorable shift relative to previous years - which also translated into a noticeable improvement in the average performance of the students in this cohort.
extracted.Comments: Clustering on this work is done for learning trajectories.
extracted.Limitations:
No ablation study to determine importance of different features.

Data is accumulated weekly (not daily)

Distance metric is not optimized

annotations.text: Flipped classrooms are better suited for learners with developed SRL skills. Authors work focuses on measuring effect of study regularity on academic success. The work defines a measure of study regularity that can uncover learner profiles in B-Learning. This measure heavily correlates with academic success.
Work finishes with a discussion of how this measure of study regularity can be used to help students develop better study habits.

references.title: Estimating student dropout in distance higher education using semi-supervised techniques

extracted.Data sources:
Student Characteristics
Attendance Records
Assessment Grades
extracted.Features: [Gender, Age, MaritalStatus, Children, Work, ComputerKnowledge, ComputerJob, OCS1, TEST1, OCS2, TEST2]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Grades in assignments were discretized into NO SUBMISSION, FAIL, GOOD, EXCELLENT.

Cross-Validation Split made kept each fold with 10% labelled data and 90% unlabelled data.
extracted.Models:
Self-Training
Co-Training
Democratic Co-Training
Tri-Training
RASCO
Rel-RASCO
C4.5
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.7673 - NB Tri-Training at 3rd step, Friedman Ranking: 3.3 - Tri-Training C4.5, Recall: 0.8443 - Tri-Training C4.5 at 3rd step}
extracted.Comments:
Authors compare performance of different algorithms at 3 different stages of course completion. 

Base learners are C4.5 and NB algorithms. Comparison benchmark is a C4.5 DT. Authors measure accuracy on the labelled data:

Best model is based on a ranking of the accuracies on the different experiments - the Friedman Test. 

Tri-Training on C4.5 was the best model and compared with C4.5 on its own.
extracted.Limitations:
Classifiers used are fairly basic: C4.5 and NB.

Single course and small sample size. 

No use of behavioral attributes.

annotations.text: Semi-supervised learning may be an interesting approach to identify students who are more likely to dropout of an online course.

Labeled data is often hard to obtain, thus the authors propose a framework that allows the existence of some labeled instances and multiple unlabeled instances. Authors use a semi-supervised approach and argue to have achieved good predictive accuracy. Authors also propose a web-based tool to estimate if a student is likely to dropout.

For multiple different reasons, dropout is a concern in distance learning higher education and we need to develop strategies to help them. Many of the current approaches for detection of students at risk are innefective and/or take too long: previous grades, tutor evaluation etc…

The authors attempt a semi-supervised approach to the problem of identify learner’s at risk. They compare the performance of SSL vs Supervised classifiers suchg as C4.5. Then, the authors present a prototype web-tool that computes the probability of a student dropping out.

ML for Dropout prediction often revolves around data that is static (e.g. characteristics) or dynamic (e.g. LMS behavior) and the use of one or both sets of data to predict whether a student will give up or not.

Different ML classifiers have been used with some success in the early prediction of dropout. The reviewed works use standard supervised ML techniques to achieve decent results. Moreover, predictive performance tends to improve as time advances. In some cases, as is the case of Atwell that the model can make predictions even before the start of the course.
The research, as it stands merely focuses on Supervised methods but the authors argue that SS Learning can be helpful.

SSL is a mixture of supervised and unsupervised learning. SSL algorithms use small subsets of labelled data and large subsets of unlabelled data (much cheaper) to generate predictions for the unlabelled data. It can be categorized into 2 settings:
Popular Semi-Supervised Learning Algorithms include:
Goal is to use SSL to create accurate predictions of dropout and to compare the performance of these algorithms against standard Supervised Approaches.

The authors use data from a Computer Science Course at Hellenic Open University. The course is divided into 12 modules and each has 4 written assignments and a final examination. Students can take the exam as soon as they finish the written assignments. There are also optional sessions.

Data comprises 344 students and prediction uses data collected before the 3rd assignment (meaning that we can assume prediction to be done at course’s halfway point maybe…). The authors select 10-Fold Cv as model assessment strategy.
The 2 experiments are divided into 3 sequential parts:

The first experiment is to run the different SSL algorithms (with C4.5 or NB as base learners) and see which ones obtain the highest accuracy.  The second experiment is to compare the best SSL approaches with a Supervised C4.5 algorithm.

As far as the first experiment goes, it becomes obvious that using the semi-supervised approach mirrors observations that are also seen in standard supervised works: The predictive ability of the models increases with the advancement of the course. The analysis of performance for this work is based on the Friedman Test → a ranking of models based on their accuracy on labelled data for the different tasks.
Depending on the classifier used as base, there are some interesting observations:

The ranking results, show that Tri-Training C4.5 is the best overall model as, based on the different experiments and steps is the model that has the highest average ranking based on the different accuracy scores obtained.

The best model selected under these circumstances was sub-sequently tested in comparison with C4.5 over Accuracy and Specificity - Recall. The model of Tri-Training with C4.5 clearly outperformed the classifier on both metrics → suggesting that the semi-supervised approach outperformed the standard supervised method.
Work also includes the exposition of a web-platform that returns the probability of dropping out based on the features. This prototype may, in the future, be used to predict dropout of students.

references.title: A Hybrid E-Learning Recommendation Approach Based on Learners’ Influence Propagation
references.journal: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering

extracted.Initialization Method:
Survey for Learning Styles,
LMS for interaction data
extracted.Updates to Recommendations:
Recommendations are updated at the end of each iteration:

Learners are updated after interacting with materials, a new self-organization epoch is ran and the recommendations are the activities that best match the user in question → done unsin sequential pattern mining.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Personality similarities are computed using Jaccard Index and Cosine Similarity.

Recommendations are generated using Sequential Pattern Mining
extracted.Evaluation:
{Average Score: 84 - SI-IFL, Self-Assessed Personalized Recommendation: SI-IFL, Flow Experience Evaluation: SI-IFL, Quality: SI-IFL,
Diversity: SI-IFL, Fitness - SI-IFL}
extracted.Comments:
Authors use Intuionist fuzzy Kogic optimization to make updates to learners.

Authors use Self-Organizing Maps to identify the stable sequences of learning. 

Learning Activities are recommended and the learner is updated post-interactions.

Self-Organization is employed to the learners in order to identify which learners are more similar to the current learner and what sequences were used by the learner. Sequential pattern mining is used to generate the recommendations.

Students who used the SI-IFL recommender achieved better scores AND displayed greater satisfaction with the recommender than learners taking the remaining baseline approaches.
extracted.Limitations:
- Needs validation with additional datasets

- Limited representation of learners and learning behaviours

annotations.text: Most recommenders in education need to be content-based because there are few interactions between users. The paper looks to add learners to the recommender system by building an hybrid recommender that combines Learner Influence Model, Self organization and Sequential Pattern Mining to recommend LO to learners.

LIM obtains interpersonal information without requiring (e.g. ratings or grades). Then, SOM is used to recommend the optimal cliques to learners and, finally, SPM is used to decide which learning objects LO and learning paths should be taken by the learner. Results show promise in efficiency and adaptability to e-learning scenarios.

With the proliferation of e-learning platforms, learners are increasingly expected to have a personalized learning pathway that is suited for their needs and learning goals. REcommender systems have been used to try to meet this need - CBF, CF or Hybrid. CBF recommenders make recommendation based on the content that is explored by the user and some common features for these recommenders can be knowledge level, learning ability and learning experience. Despite their popularity these recommenders run into the issue of overspecialization and information overload.

CF approaches compute similarity between users. However, these systems tend to suffer from problems such as cold-start and sparsity. Both of these carachteristics are everpresent in educational data. HF tries to combine information of learners with information about the LOs.
The paper focuses on presenting an HF approach to e-learning recommendations. It will require some heuristics to define what can be considered the Learner Model and their interactive behaviors.
Research Problems to address with this research are as follows:
Thus, authors propose a new HF approach. This will require:
The authors summarize the works on multiple dimensions.
The criteria for stopping is Entropy. As soon as the intra-cluster entropy (impurity) falls below a certain threshold, then the clsuter is considered stable.
After obtaining the clusters, the authors use a PrefixSpan algorithm to extract the recommendations.

The algorithm takes as input the learners in a specific cluster. Then, it uses the PageRank algorithm to sort the suitability of the different learners to the active learner. After sorting, the PrefixSpan algorithm is applied on the sequences of actions of the learners in the cluster to return the “optimal” sequence for the active learner.

The authors use data from a C course taught at their institution. The course includes 4 modules and the open digital resources available are in the foprm of video, audio, PPT, Word, HTML, pages, etc…Students in 6 institutions take the course and each institution is considered a group.
Groups were not balanced because the authors argues that there were no significant differences between the different groups in previous years. All students completed C and were in the final exam.
The different groups were subjected to distinct recommendation strategies:

Students that were subjected to the SI-IFL algorithm as the recommendation strategy received the highest average score and, as per usual in other works presented by these authors, also achieved those greater scores while spending less time with the system and, simultaneously, accessing a smaller percentage of the candidate LOs.
Total evaluation of the recommender is based on the following metrics:
From the primary results, it seems that SI-IFL, the model with the most considerations of influence does seem to outperform the remaining models.

When it comes to overall satisfaction, the SI-IFL seems to be one that has students be the most content with out of all the considered options. This is also the model in which the learners seem to be the most content with. Other approaches using self-organization also outperform the remaining strategies when it comes to attention focus and curiosity.

When it comes to personalization, the authors note that the influence models are the models that generate the recommendations that are more accepted by the students. All-in-all, the IFLs usage of fuzzy-logic showcases a smaller degree of rejection of LOs than other models.
When it comes to diversity, the authors analyze the results according to 2 different perspectives:
AS in previous works, the authors show that their system is not only the most accurate, but the most diverse out of all options tested until this specific moment.

E-Learning has carachteristics of time continuity, which makes attributes such as diversity important for recommender systems to consider as these are key factors that contribute to a better learning experience and better learner satisfaction. 

The authors propose an influence-based learner model that is independent from ratings. The authors use IFL to optimize their learner model and use that model to cluster learners nto different groups using SOM. For each cluster, the interactions are recorder using PrefixSpan. Thy proposed hybrid recommender system has shown to be effective, diverse and highly adaptable.

references.title: Predictive learning analytics using deep learning model in MOOCs’ courses videos

extracted.Courses: 2 editions of 1 Mining Massive Datasets and an Automata Theory Course
extracted.Features: [Fraction completed, Fraction spent (fracSpent), Fraction played (fracPL), Number of played, Number of pauses, Fraction paused (fracPa), Fraction Forward (Fracfrwd), Fraction Backward (FracBkwd), Number of seek Backward (NumSBW), Number of fast forwards (NumFFW), Wekly Counts of: Play, Pause, Seek, Rate-Change, Load, Stop]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
For every week, the authors appended the vector of the new week to the vectors of the previous weeks. These were subsequently padded to ensure that, for all students, the vectors were all of the same length. 

For each model, authors focused on learners that learned at least 20% of the videos of the current week.
extracted.Comments:
Authors do predictive modelling of course performance on video watching behavior. 

The representation is somewhat dynamic. There is a vector of fixed length that includes the calculations up to a certain week and, for each week, the interactions are gradually being concatenated.
extracted.Limitations:
Holdout method

Does not consider additional data sources

MOOC-specific

annotations.text: Video lectures are a pivotal component of MOOC and a source of interesting data for analytical purposes.

Thus, the authors use Deep Learning from video clickstream data to understand how a student will perform on a given week of the MOOC. Authors use LSTM for this purpose and find that the approach outperforms all other baseline models used - with accuracies ranging between 82% and 93%.

references.title: Can we predict success from log data in VLEs? Classification of interactions for learning analytics and their relation with performance in VLE-supported F2F and online learning

extracted.Features: [Student–student, Student–content, Student–teacher, Student-system, Transmission of content, Creating class interactions,  Evaluating students, Evaluation of teachers and courses, Computer-based instruction, Active, Passive]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Categorization of the logs. Logs were categorized in 3 perspectives:

Agent; Frequency; Mode
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {RSquared: 0.356 - Agent Classification, Adjusted-R2: 0.346, Agent Classification}
extracted.Groups Created: Not clustering, but authors distinguish between F2F courses and Online Courses.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Performance in F2F courses is not predicted by any of the variables extracted from the LMS.

On Online Courses, all variables related to Agent, Mode and Frequency were found to be significant at alpha=0.05, with Transmission of content being the only variable not significant at 0.01.
extracted.Implications:
It is necessary to study moderating effects of interactions in Online courses and additional data sources are needed to predict performance in F2F settings.

Identifying the most effective types of interactioins with the system may help course creators to re-design their courses to correct undesired behaviors.

There is potential is proceeding with better eploration of variables that explain performance, but also create recommender systems that benefit students.
extracted.Comments:
Features extracted refer to types of interactions. 

No relationship could be found between any of the features and student final score in Face-to-Face Contexts.

Authors make a comparison between different forms of Log classification and try to find what are ones that best predict performance.

Authors argue that promoting the types of interactions that correlate with performance would be helpful.
extracted.Limitations:
Approach only reveals to be effective for online contexts, not VLE-Supported F2F.

Validation in other educational settings. 

Limited data sources.

Interactions are only evaluated by their type, with no specifics about the content of the interaction.

annotations.text: A big challenge in learning analytics is understanding whether an interaction is meaningful for learning or not. Authors focus on understanding which interactions are meaningful and which aren’t. For that, they develop a nomenclature distinguishing between Online Learning contexts and F2F supported by VLE contexts.
Authors found that, indeed, there are pedagogically meaningful interactions in Online Learning but failed to obtain similar results for F2F supported by VLE contexts.

It is difficult to identify what are the contributions of different types of interaction to student learning. In traditional learning paradigms, the teacher is often the guide by which learning occurs as the professor is the mediator between student and content.
To that effect, there are 3 types of noteworthy interactions to consider:

The authors argue that the latter 2 are more easily enhanced in e-learning scenarios. Moreover, these types of interactions on face-to-face scenarios are often informal which makes it really difficult to quantify their impact towards learning. LMS sort of facilitate this as they record interactions.

The authors view LA as a field focused on the study on VLE logs. It emerged from the adoption of VLE in educational institutions and the proliferation of DM techniques. Since the goal of learning analytics is, at the end of the day, to understand and improve learning processes it is essential to understand and contextualize the types of interactions that exist between student and the VLE. The authors identify main types of approaches in LA:

Over time, researchers have acknowledged that the interactions of learners with LMS or VLEs have some form of pedagogical meaning. However, researchers do not agree on what types of interactions have pedagogical meaning, what are the criteria for selection of relevant types of interactions. Thus, the proposed RQs are :

Ultimately, the goal is to identify what are the correct types of interaction that it becomes easier to design early warning systems. But the goal is not to design an early warning system itself. The authors start the process of categorization of system independent interactions in two perspectives:
Agent - who/what is the student interacting with → content, teacher or peers.
Frequency of use - How often is this interaction present in a typical VLE (not how often it is used) - Most used, Moderately used, Rarely used.
Participation Mode - Based on how student interacts with VLE.

The first part of the paper stems from validating the 3 previously identified frameworks for classification of interactions and see whether these map to differences in student performance. The authors use data from 6 online courses (138 students) and 2 face-to-face courses (218 students).
Authors use Moodle for all the courses. Traditionally, the materials gradually become available as the semester advances but remain available afterward.
Data Extraction

Moodle records the logs of interactions between students and content. The authors resorted to extracting the logs. Filtering out of interactions according to the designated taxonomies is, at this stage not possible and requires additional pre-processing.

Each possible interaction has been manually labelled by 2 experts. These classifications were subsequently assessed by 3 additional domain experts. The resulting categorizations are integrated in a Moodle Plugin in order to return the classified interactions. In essence, the work starts with manually labelling all of the interactions.
Data Analysis
Data analysis was conducted in 2 distinct ways:

From figure 2 - despite it being possible to see some differences in absolute number of interactions, most of the behaviors are similar between contexts - difference being Evaluating Students for the reason that F2F did not submit evaluations in the VLE.

The authors look at the categorizations under the 3 perspectives. Most of the interactions in F2F contexts have very little correlation with the final grade and, in fact, none have statistically significant results.
However, for an online context, the types of interactions do seem to be linearly correlated with the final grade, with all correlations being moderate.

When it comes to the regression, no predictive power was found in any of the types of interactions. In essence, the authors attempted 3 forms of categorization of the logs and attempted to see which led to the best predictions of the final grade via a Multiple Linear Regression.
Results show that best performance is achieved when logs are classified according to the type of agent the learner is interacting with.

The authors' goal was to establish a course-independent nomenclature for labeling VLE interactions, which could indicate the importance of specific interactions. Despite the difficulty, they consider that they have been successful.

This comes from the fact that they identify 3 types of interaction types that exhibit predictive potential of a student’s final grade. In more detail, they even find that Agent interaction type classification to be an effective way of labelling interactions of students for ONLINE contexts.

The authors also discuss some of the reasons for why their approach did not reveal any insights for F2F contexts: the tools used may not have the necessary resolution to be helpful for these contexts.
Results do not always mirror the literature → in this study student-student interactions were found to be less important than in other works.
Study has a high focus on interaction types and their ability to predict student performance. It is very exploratory in nature.

references.title: A Cluster-Based Approach to Understanding Students’ Resource-Usage Patterns in an Active, Blended, and Collaborative Learning Environment

extracted.Courses: 1 Dynamics course in a US University
extracted.Features: [Peers in Class, Course LectureBook, Examples and Solutions, Course Blog, Questions in Class, Office Hours, Online Resources, Outside Students, TAs]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors conduct interviews to assess the median time of interactions of students with the different resources. 

After cluster formation, the analysis is complemented with interviews to understand the reasons behind the decisions reported on the surveys.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {BIC: –16,490 - 8 clusters}
extracted.Cluster Description:
C1: Multiple resource uses and heavy use of tutorial room. Receptive to help.

C2: Primary reliance on core resources.

C3: Use of core resources and tutorial room.

C4: Seek help from external students.

C5: Did not use blog, but use non-course online resources more.

C6: Similar to C2

C7: Least utilization of lecturebook. Moderate use of other resources.

C8: Peers and lecturebook usage.

C9: Infrequent use of peers.
extracted.Implications:
Due to the variablility in usage of materials… lecturers should spend less time advising on which materials to use. 

It becomes apparent that students look for help in mulitple sources.
extracted.Comments: Authors select the number ok k that achieved the 2nd lowest BIC because, in the author’s eyes -  the 9 cluster solution was more meaningful than the 8-cluster solution interpretation-wise.
extracted.Limitations:
- Innapropriate metric for selection of ideal k.

- No use of LMS data.

- Small dataset

 - Reliance on self-reported data

annotations.text: The goal of the work how and why students engaged with different learning resources. Work creates clusters from the interactions of 581 engineering students and, for 44 of them use survey data to understand the motivation behind the usage of those resources. Authors argue that their results may be useful to coach students and design better curricula/resources.
