references.title: Targeting At-risk Students Using Engagement and Effort Predictors in an Introductory Computer Programming Course
references.journal: Data Driven Approaches in Digital Education

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Success in Next Computer-Based Lab Exam
extracted.Students:
138 Year 1 students → Train Data
128 Year 2 students → Test Data
extracted.Features: [Unspecified Log related features]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: There was a model trained for every week… Every new week, the features of the previous week’s classifiers were concatenated and the new data was appended.
extracted.Models:
Logistic Regression
SVM-Linear
SVM-Gaussian
kNN
Random Forest
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Misclassification Error: 0.2984 - Logistic Regression}
extracted.Comments:
This work is a short paper on the development and implementation of an early warning system to predict success in next test based on interactions and submissions. 

The authors train on a sample of Year 1, select the best model and then deploy it on the second year - identifying students at-risk and providing additional mentoring to the students who were flagged this way. 

Performance on the test data on the end of the course reveals 64.38%  accuracy.
extracted.Limitations:
The way this differential is computed is extremely sus. 

Vague in terms of preprocessing and modelling.

Only metric for whom results are mentioned is Misclassification Error.

Small sample size.

annotations.text: Paper presents an approach to identifyind at-risk students in a programming course. Predictions use clickstream and progression on programming labs and are made at every week. Students labeled as at-risk were provided with additional support. Results show that the progression of students who received assistance was 4 time higher in students who received the intervention relative to the students who attended the course of the first training data.

Programming is a difficult task and many researchers have been looking for ways to improve the learning experience of programming students. Behavioral data is, hopefully, a reflection of a student’s effort and learning progress. This notion is similar to the use of LMS logs to predict performance. Sin programming courses tend to generate even more data (due to more interactions)  the authors expect that leveraging this data for predictive modelling is viable in their programming course.
Thus, the authors present a retrospective study that simulates real time predictions via an early warning system from LMS logs. The questions to answer are:

Data is From Computer Programming, a 12-week long course I taught at Dublin University. The course combines 4 hours of lecture with 4 hours of practical lab materials. Students are assessed 2 to 3 times over the semester on lab-based exams.
As far as students go, there were 138 registered in year 1 and 128 in year 2.
The goal of the models designed here is to use interaction logs on programming work to predict success in a computer-based programming lab exam. To do that, the authors use data from:

The authors take data from the first year → the 138 students in 2015/2016 to train a predictive model. Task being passing or failing on the next lab exam. In Year 1, there were 2 labs (Week 7 and End-of-Semester). Data from Week 1 to 6 was used to predict success in midterm.

When it comes to representation, this not a full aggregation performed every week. Instead, it seems to have a concatenation of the features of the classifiers of the previous weeks and appending data for that specific week. Multiple different classifiers are compared against one another, with selection being determined by the smallest error rate using 10-Fold Cross-Validation. For interpretation purposes, results reflect the average of the weekly analyses done across the entire semester.
Model assessment results on Year 1 data shos that Logistic Regression achieved the lowest average misclassification error across the 12 weeks (29.84%).

This model was sub-sequently pseudo-deployed on the 2016/2017 cohort. Students flagged as at-risk were offered assitance and personalized mentoring opportunities during the lab session. The overall model achieved an F1-Score of 0.6438.
To answer the second question (does assistance improve performance), the authors focused on utilizing an approach where the differences in performance between Test 1 and Test 2 for both co-horts.

In year 1, there was no intervention. However, both groups of students improved their performance from Exam 1 to Exam 2 (with the improvements on students who failed being 23.64%). In essence, students who failed improved more “percentage-wise” than students who had passed. In this work, the difference is given by a construct called differential (positive 11.52%).

In Year 2, students who succeeded on Exam 1 actually saw their performance significantly drop, while the performance of students who were predicted to fail improved. The differential was, again positive, but into the range of 50%.

references.title: How Do Table Shape, Group Size, and Gender Affect On-Task Actions in Computer Education Open-Ended Tasks

extracted.Courses: 41 courses at a Dutch STEM university
extracted.Features: [Announcement, Discussions, Assignment, Submission, Learning Materials, Mandatory Quiz, Optional Quiz, Metacognition andd Course Browsing]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors aggregated different types of activities in the 9 categories described as the features.

Moreover, the authors defined learning sessions, considering that two clicks were in the same session if they were within 12.1 minutes and cannot have more than 111 clicks/learning events.

Data was filtered to remove outliers,  leading to 5110 students in 34 sessions.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model:
Log-lokelihood,
Bayesian Information Criterion,
Akaike Information Criterion
extracted.Cluster Description:
Assignment-Focused tactic: Most common tactic. Dominated by Assignment Pages and Mandatory Quizzes

Material-Focused tactic:  More than half of the clicks are on materials. Second most common tactic as well.

Mixed Tactic: More diverse set of actions with no clear dominant action.

Quizz-Focused Tactic: Least common tactic in which students opt to do mandatory quizzes or optional quizzes.
extracted.Implications:
The different learning tactics are the backbone of the learning strategies covered layer. More than just characterization of the clusters over the features, authors also describe clusters in terms of most likely transitions in actions.

For example, the Mixed Tactic revels itself to be the most common way of a student starting a course.
extracted.Comments:
Selection of Groups number was done via the Expectation-Maximization Algorithm.

The first level of clustering was for learning tactics, the second for learning strategies.
extracted.Limitations:
Survivorship bias (only learners who were present over the 2 years were considered).

LMS are a partial and incomplete picture of learner behavior.

No specifics on the internal conditions of each course.

extracted.Courses: 41 courses at a Dutch STEM university
extracted.Features: [Quizz-Focused Tactic, Mixed Tactic, Material-Focused Tactic, Assignment-Focused Tactic]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors obtain the clusters of learning tactics from the Logs using EM and then use the model outputs as the features for the second clustering algorithm of learning strategies.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: “Best fit”
extracted.Cluster Description:
Balanced Strategy: No dominant tactic, as all are employed relatively often. 

Resource-Oriented: Most of the actions are accessing resources. This learning strategy is the second largest and is ranked thrid in terms of number of sessions.

Grade-Oriented: Most of the time is spent on grade menus (largest cluster). 

Flexible: Adaptation to different courses, buyt not equal “balanced” engagement in all learning tactics.
extracted.Implications:
Different strategies suggest different levels of engagement with Surfave vs Deep approaches to learning. 

Temporal level of granularity of analysis also impacts the analysis of transitions between strategies.

All strategies are found to have a significantly different impact on grade as showcased by a Kruskall-Wallis test. 

Results also show that being consistent in terms of learning strategy ois more effective than changing strategy midway.
extracted.Comments:
Authors also look at how learners change their behavior (strategy) during a course - often considering both at a half or at a quarter.

End-result: Adopting a consistent deep-level approach to learning is the best option to succeed. However, transitioning between strategies is better than consistently adopting a surface-level approach to learning.
extracted.Limitations:
Survivorship bias (only learners who were present over the 2 years were considered).

LMS are a partial and incomplete picture of learner behavior.

No specifics on the internal conditions of each course.

annotations.text: Evaluates how different attributes affect collaborative learning in the context of computer education. Authors conduct a set of experiments with table size, group size and gender and measure their effects on a sample of university students.,

references.title: Framelet-based dual hypergraph neural networks for student performance prediction
references.journal: International Journal of Machine Learning and Cybernetics

extracted.Students: 3983 OULAD students, 649 Portuguese Students, 2000 Math students, 5820 Gazi University students, 7543 World University City Platform
extracted.Courses: OULAD, Portuguese, Mathematics, 2 Classes in Gazi University in Ankara
extracted.Features: [Dataset Dependent]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Work takes as input a feature matrix. The feature matrix is processed via UDFmT, separating students and ccreating 7 uniform hypergraphs (constructed suing cosine similarity).

These hyypergraphs are then sent to a dual hypergraph learning module which is then directed to an attention-based layer.
extracted.Models: SVM, Logistic Regression, SLFNN, GCN, MTGNN, FD-HGNNN
extracted.Comments:
The work by the authors invollves the creation of a novel neural network arquitecture meant to predict end of course performance.

The authors provide a mathematical description of the different elements:

- Uniform Hyper-graph creation
- Dual hypergraph learning module
- Attention Layer

Authors show that the model outperforms all other tested models for the same task.

Ablation study shows that all elements of the network are relevant towards the prediction - first for elements. Then, the authors test different forms of Hypergraph formation and find UniGAT to be the “best one”.
extracted.Limitations:
- Holdout method

- Prone to over-smooth

- No exploration of Temportal Dynamics

annotations.text: Most methods for the prediction of student performance tend to focus on attributes of the students and not as much in the relationships between the students themselves. The paper introduces a framelet-nased dual hypergraph neeural network  that decomposes student features in order to obtain model student relatinships.

By integrating a more robust model of student relationships, the feature space now includes dimensions that were not observable before, which results into the creation of better and more accurate predictive models. Validation of the new model against traditional classifiers AND Graph neural networks on 4 real-world datasets demonstrates the superiority of the designed approach.

references.title: Examining Student Effort on Help through Response Time Decomposition

extracted.Features: [(Hint Request, Hint Request), (Hint Request, Attempt)]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors look at the difference in time between the first and the second action (Action pairs).

 All the distributions are log-transformed. 

Authora also looked at differences in time when the answer was correct vs when it was incorrect. 

Effort was constructed as a measure taken from the time the user takes to understand the hint.
extracted.Cluster Description:
High Effort: 

The time between actions is longer.

Low effort: 

Time between actions is smaller
extracted.Implications: Time to response can be used to identify effort in student responses. Low-effort students are more likely to wheel-spin.
extracted.Comments: Simple analysis of time between actions and clustering.
extracted.Limitations:
No integration with other aspects of learning theory.

No exploration of other known forms of behavior that may explain differences in time.

annotations.text: Computer systems have provided tools to provide immediate feedback and provide supplemental information while researchers can use logs to their research. However, these logs provide limited insights into learning.
The paper uses Response Time Decomposition to explore help-seeking behavior. This is followed by an analysis between student effort and student performance.

references.title: Predicting Students Success in Blended Learning—Evaluating Different Interactions Inside Learning Management Systems

extracted.Features: [Count, Average, Median, Zeroed weeks, Average of the Difference, Commintment Factor, Cognitive Count, Teaching Count, Social Count, Other Count]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors do an aggregation of different actions into bigger constructs: Commitment, Cognitive, Teaching and Social.

Different datasets are constructed using different cmbination of variables. In total there are 13 configurations.

All datasets were oversampled using SMOTE.
extracted.Models: kNN, MLP, Naive Bayes, AdaBoost, Radndom Forest
extracted.Comments:
Authors create 13 datasets and run each dataset over 5 classifiers - 65 configurations in total. 

They create a model for each specific edition of the dataset and measure the Scores using AUC.
extracted.Limitations:
Small Sample Size

Single Course Context

No additional Data Sources

Only considers SMOTE for Resampling.

annotations.text: Courses such as algorithms and programming have very high rates of dropout. Authors create multiple early detection algorithms with different pre-processing techniques to identify which combinations generate the best predictions.

Authors find that, indeed, different features/algorithm combinations did not lead to statistically significant differences in predictive performance, ultimately finding that activity counts and some derived attributes are sufficient for the task. However, the authors did find that predictions in the second semester were much better → 0.7 AUC to 0.9 AUC. SMOTE did not improve performance of the models.

references.title: Students' LMS interaction patterns and their relationship with achievement: A case study in higher education

extracted.Courses: 1 module as part of a eTRAL course
extracted.Features: [Time Tasks, Time Theory, Time Forums, Word Forums, Relevant Actions, Days “hand-in”]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The 6 variables were created from the extraction of 12 raw LMS variables. 

Data is standardized (0 mean, 1 SD).

ANOVA tests were performed to identify the importance of the different features.

t-Tests were performed to analyse differents in final course achievement between different clusters.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: Log likelihood: -6.82178
extracted.Cluster Description:
Pairwise similarities between Clusters 1 and 4 and Clusters 2 and 3 when it comes to task orientation.

Cluster 1 (5.90): NonNon Task or Theory Oriented Group (non-procrastinators)

Cluster 2 (7.20): Task Oriented Group (socially focused)

Cluster 3 (6.49): Task Oriented Group (individually focused)

Cluster 4 (4.85): Non Task Oriented Group (procrastinators)

Clusters 2 and 3 exhibit the highest performance wchich suggests that spending time on different tasks is heavily related with good performance.
extracted.Implications:
Understanding the characteristics of different student interactions can be used to identify students at risk.

Task-oriented student behavior may inform creation of recommender systems.
extracted.Comments:
Variables exhibited positive correlations with the exception of Time Theory and Time Tasks.

Likewise, the time variavbles were negatively correlated with Days-hand in.

In general, the work can be described in the following steps:

1. Extract data and create features
2. Create a clustering model
3. Analyze whether the features used are contributed to cluster separation or not
4. Analyze whether different clusters have different performances at the end of the course.
extracted.Limitations:
Does not explain why students with different engagement patterns get similar performance.

Lack of additional data sources.

Small sample size with homogenous sample of 3rd year psychology students.

annotations.text: The study looks to evaluate the processes by which students asynchronously interact with their Learning Management System. This approach involves the extraction of the LMS logs of 140 undergraduate students, followed by the creation of clusters with the k-means algorithm.

Results allow the authors to separate students into 4 distinct groups with distinct behaviors. The analysis of the average performance of each group shows that different learning strategies on the LMS can affect how students perform.

Learning Management Systems are more popular than ever but students often fail to adapt to it. Modern education requires more effort and better SRL skills in order to be successful. More than students, educators often do not adapt to these systems either: identifying student effort and behaviour is harder in these systems as it requires the collection and processing of data at a very fundamental level (the interactions). However, uncovering patterns in the LMS is useful to create Adaptive Educational Systems.

The work under scrutiny uses an EDM approach on LMS logs to uncover student learning strategies. This requires the extraction of relevant indicators from logs (effort, time working, etc…) to answer multiple research questions versing over a student’s ability to adapt and succeed in these environments. Previous applciations include the tracxking of student activities or the prediction of dropout or academic success.
The novelty of this study is that is collects data extracted from a real course and real activities to identify learning strategies on the LMS.

The literature features different types of works leveraging LMS data: from identification of Learning stules to prediction of motivation. From the literature it is possible to see that students adopting different learning styles also tend exhibit different types of behaviors in the LMS. In previous works, the utilization of clusters was merely descriptive.

In this study, the authors want to predict achievement. Thus, they also turn to review works that predict it. Previous work has shown that there is a relationship between dedicated to learning activities and achievement (even if it is not a perfect measure and different authors obtain different results). One interesting observation comes from Plant et al. mentioning that more active students plan their activities → greater success with less time studying.

Frequecy-related variables (either counting the number of times an activity was accessed or measuring the time spent) are focal variables in learning analytics approaches. Another variable that are considered useful is a measurement of procrastination. In fact, it has been shown that procrastination in e-learning is even more detrimental than in face-to-face. There is novelty in the fact that procrastination is measured from logs.
Thus, the research questions are:

The data comprises 140 student attending a psychology degree during 2012-2013. The experiment was conduct as part of a course called eTRAL. The program was executed entirely outside teaching hours. Students who did the program were given an extra point in their final course subject if they submitted at least 80% of the assignments. The program under evaluation was composed of both Theoretical and Practical portions. Moreover, there was a forum. On average the materials are expected to take 2 to 2.5 hours every week.
The data was collected after the experiment. We refer to 12 raw features extracted from LMS logs here. These were used to calculate the total 6 variables used in the study:

Moreover, the authors also extracted the final grade on the subject → meaning that the logs under analysis only refer to a small part of the total course. It is unclear how early in the course these materials are created.
The analysis was done in different stages:
The authors organize their results in order to align them to the Research Questions.
The authors identify 4 as the optimal number of clusters for both EM and k-means. Then, the authors represent each cluster by the coordinates of the centroid. A description of clusters follows:

The results show that a high amount of variance (71.5%) is explained by the variables, which is remarkably high. When analyzed individually, it is possible to see that all variables exhibit meaningful differences across the different clusters, which is a hallmark of good separation.
Teh results of the t-tests between the clusters on the final course performance show that there are indeed statistically significant differences between the majority of the clusters:

Cluster 1 vs Clusters 2 and 4
Cluster 2 vs Clusters 3 and 4
Cluster 3 vs Cluster 4
Meaning that the only clusters without statistically significant differences are Cluster 1 vs Cluster 3.

references.title: Modeling the learner group formation problem in computer-supported collaborative learning using mathematical programming

annotations.text: The authors introduce a novel way of addressing the group formation challenge using binary integer formulation. 32 students were divided into two sections - one with the suggested method and the second formed teams using a modified version of k-means. Proposed model optimally solved the problem in 8 secs.

references.title: user2code2vec: Embeddings for Profiling Students Based on Distributional Representations of Source Code

extracted.Features: [Code2Vec embeddings from Code Submissions]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Representations based on logs have been popular… However, doing richer representations is still seen as a challenging problem.
extracted.Novelty of Model: The creation of user embeddings based on embeddings of their code submissions.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Code2vec embeddings are padded to 50 sentences and contain 100 dimensions (Words, Python Categories, Python Token Words and AST Nodes).

Code submissions of learners are used to build a User Submission Matrix. These are flattened and a user embedding is created with 100 dimensions.

Visualization is done using Principal Components of the 2 variables.
extracted.Comments:
Authors create an embedding structure called code2vec that is meant to represent code submissions.

After identifying the best form of code embeddings, they use these as inputs for the user representation: user2code2vec

annotations.text: Authors propose a new method to profile computer science students through embeddings. The idea is not only to predict whether a code submission is correct but also to create a student profile that allows for better and more adequate recommendations to the learner’s knowledge level. Authors identify the extraction of relevant student features as a limitation since, in LA, there is no such thing as an infinite amount of students to build embeddings from.

references.title: Leveraging Multi-Criteria Integer Programming Optimization for Effective Team Formation

annotations.text: Paper introduces a new approach using multi-criteria integer programming for team formation. The model considers multiple factors including skill level, background and personality traits. Algorithm focuses on maximizing whithin-group differences while minizing between-group differences.
The approach starts with a K-means clustering followed by a constrained optimization. Results show that potential on the approach.

references.title: A sequence data model for analyzing temporal patterns of student data

extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, Student characteristics, surveys, Course Assessments
extracted.Features: [citizenship type, current age, employment type, gender, marital status, major, lab section/group, Weekly course data: class activities, lab activities, quizzes, lecture tests, assignments; Reflection features derived from LIWC sentiment analysis]
extracted.Moment of Prediction: Weekly during the semester (17 weekly nodes + background + outcome)
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors build a within-semester sequence model where each week is a node. Student data were organized into 19 nodes: 1 background node, 17 weekly nodes, 1 outcome node. In total, the model included 4 background features, 110 gradable test features, and 18 reflection features selected from LIWC sentiment measures. A progressive classification approach was used: at each week, an SVM classified students and recorded the distance to the decision boundary to create a temporal signature. Then a 3-degree polynomial curve was fit to the signature and the coefficients were used as temporal features. Temporal models were compared with non-temporal feature-vector models under different feature-group settings
extracted.Models: SVM for progressive classification; polynomial curve fitting for signature feature extraction
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.9564 - SVM_tests+salient}
extracted.Comments: Main contribution is proposing a sequence data model that explicitly preserves temporal dependencies instead of flattening student data into a static feature vector. In the within-semester case, temporal signatures built from weekly progression improved prediction of course success. Reflection-derived features were particularly useful, and the model is also presented as more interpretable through contextualization and “storytelling.”
extracted.Limitations: Small sample and imbalanced dataset (only 12/91 failed). Strong methodological contribution, but not a direct comparison across many standard ML models. The predictive gains depend on the proposed temporal representation and signature-construction pipeline, which may be harder to operationalize than standard tabular models.

extracted.Target: At-risk of not graduating on time vs successful on-time graduation
extracted.Courses: Computer Science major / College of Computing at UNC Charlotte
extracted.Data sources: University student records, course records, LMS logs
extracted.Features: [age, gender, citizenship type, ethnicity, marital status, previous institution, previous school GPA, previous school rank and size, Semester data: courses taken, course grades, academic information such as major/advisor; Statistical features: % courses with A–D, % F courses, average course level, % IT courses with A–D, % IT courses with F, average IT course level; salient temporal features included course level and/or course grade]
extracted.Moment of Prediction: Every semester across the degree program
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors build a between-semester sequence model with an initial background node, semester nodes, and an outcome node. They limited analysis to undergraduate students who spent 8 or more semesters at UNC Charlotte and selected CS as their major at some point. To generate temporal signatures, they used progressive clustering with HDBSCAN on semester nodes and recorded the percentage of at-risk students in the cluster for each semester. Then they fit a 3-degree polynomial curve to the signature and used the coefficients as temporal features. Temporal models were compared with non-temporal ones across 3 feature-group settings.
extracted.Models: HDBSCAN for progressive clustering; SVM for final classification on extracted temporal features
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.9720 - SVM}
extracted.Comments: The paper argues that course level progression is a stronger temporal predictor than course grade for degree-level risk prediction. This is one of the more distinctive papers in your set because it explicitly models temporal structure and compares it against non-temporal baselines using the same data. The sequence representation also supports narrative interpretation of student progression.
extracted.Limitations: Not really an “early warning” paper in the short-term sense, since the between-semester model focuses on longer academic trajectories. It is also tied to a fairly elaborate custom sequence/signature pipeline rather than off-the-shelf predictive models. The sample is restricted to one institution and one major context.

annotations.text: Authors propose the introduction of temporal data in models meant to predict student performance. Authors show that within- and between-semester features could be utilized to predict success.

Authors show effectiveness of approach by using a sample of 10 years of data collected from UNC Charlotte for between-semester interactions. Within-semester activities were measured using an introductory course in Computer Science.
Results show that temporal model outperforms models who do not use these temporal features.

references.title: Using Transformer Language Models to Validate Peer-Assigned Essay Scores in Massive Open Online Courses (MOOCs)

annotations.text: In MOOCs, learners are often requested to perform peer-reviews. However, the peer-review process is often innacurate. The study uses a Transformer-based Language Model (a fine-tuned Distillbert) to do essay-scoring. The authors find that using this approach generates more reliable results than standard peer-review by learners.

references.title: A Reinforcement Learning Based Recommendation System to Improve Performance of Students in Outcome Based Education Model

extracted.Courses: Multiple Courses in a Software Engineering Program
extracted.Data sources: Course Learning Outcomes, CLO marks, PLO Description, Course Information
extracted.Initialization Method: The start state is based on Cosine similarity → which measures the degree of similarity between the learner’s CLOs and each Bi-Cluster.
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Recommendations are designed in a way that maximizes what the author’s call return.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Dataset preparation involves the mapping of courses to CLOs (which are themselves mapped to PLOs and OBEs)- Students are assigned a score on CLO based on their marks - leading to the creation of a student CLO matrix.

Students are bi-clustered (in a way that a student can be assigned to different CLO sets - using Bibit - which requires converting CLO scores to 1/0 form. 

After forming the bi-clustersm the authors attempted to merge them absed on Jaccard similarity.
extracted.Evaluation: {Precision: 0.4241 - 500 episodes, Recall: 0.5389 - 500 episodes. F1-Score: 0.47420 - 500 episodes, CLO Coverage: 0.8045 - 450 episodes, Student Coverage: 0.9827 - 350 episodes onward}
extracted.Comments:
Authors use Reinforcement Learning to create “optimal learning paths”. 

Authors start with the creation of bi-clusters, and  these bi-clusters will be used as reference for trainining along a Square Grid. 

From here, Q-Learning is used to learn the optimal policy for the student in question. 

Authors do a set of tests comparing the optimal policy against other randomly generated policies. Evaluation is done via comparison of prediction of a deficient CLO for a student vs actual deficient CLOs. Performance is tested on multiple episodes and the model is shown to be better at later iterations.

Authros also perform different optimizations of parameters.
extracted.Limitations:
- Training requires labels for deficient CLOs. 

- Only uses Q-Learn to solve the Reinforcement Learning problem. 

- Only Bibit is explored as a clustering approach.

- Does not use student information beyibd the CLO scores for each student. 

Can only use used retrospectively: can only identify CLOs and make suggestions for CLOs taken in previous semesters.

annotations.text: Outcome Based Education is an educational model with 3 main objectives:

The objective of the work is to recommend online resources so that students can develop their course Learning Outcomes. The authors create their own dataset and use it to create a student to CLO matrix using a bi-clustering approach.
The Student student bi-cluster are then used as input for Reinforcement Learning environment that generates learning path sequences.

references.title: Automated AI-driven System for Early Detection of At-risk Students

annotations.text: Authors propse an automated system for detection of at—risk students called RADAR. The authors do a pilot test of the system on a set of students from a school district.

The model uses data from multiple sources (multi-modal data sources), preprocesses it, performs feature selection and also returns predictions of performance/dropout. Authors argue that the approach on pilot tet achieved high accuracy. The system intends to provide continuous and automated monitoring of student progress.

references.title: Investigating Student's Problem-solving Approaches in MOOCs using Natural Language Processing

extracted.Features: Survey Answers
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Question 2 of the survey is an open question where students are requested to outline their approach towards solvinfg a specific problem. 

This was of course treated using a set of NLP techniques: POS tagging and Key Phrase extraction.  using KeyBERT.

For Topic Modelling Approaches, the authors explore 3 main approaches: 

A transformer-based approach that uses DBSCAN as clustering algorithm, while other 2 more traditional approaches (LDA and GSDMM) use a BoW representation
extracted.Models: LDDA-BoW, GSDMM-BoW, DBSCAN, OPTICS, HAC, HAC
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: ROUGE
extracted.Cluster Description:
Strategy 1 (n=899): Write code in paper, 

Strategy 2 (n=307): Use information from video to answer

Strategy 3 (n=106): Use IDE to correct wrong question

Strategy 4 (n=51): Trial and Error

Strategy 5 (n=49): Python tutor

Strategy 6 (n=46): Auxiliary print statements

Strategy 7 (n=35): Read instructions

Strategy 8 (n=26): Use IDE shell

Strategy 9 (n=18): Graceful solution from stackoverflow

Strategy 10 (n=17): Pseudocode

Strategy 11 (n=16): Base knowledge plus trial and error

Strategy 12 (n=15): Check lecture

Strategy 13 (n=14): Try on IDE

Strategy 14 (n=13): Use previous knowledge

Strategy 15 (n=12): Ask a friend

Strategy 16 (n=9): Calm and methodic

Strategy 17 (n=6): Check online resources

Strategy 18 (n=5): Check and IDE and compare against expected solution
extracted.Implications:
Authors created a task-structure framework and based on their results, the selection of strategy depends on the task at hand. 

E.g. Coding & Debugging require different skills than Understanding the Problem. 

More practical tasks usually required a more “active learning” skillset. 

Also, results should be toaken with grain of salt due to the MOOC exclusive approach.
extracted.Comments:
Authors use a NLP pipeline to uncover different strategies to problem students from students attending a MOOC about Python programming. 

Different Topic Modelling algorithms are comapred.
extracted.Limitations:
MOOC-based approach only

Considers only one specific set of problems (programming) in the context of one course.

Does not examine profficiency vs choice of problem-solving strategies.

annotations.text: Understanding how students problem-solve is an esencial tool towards developing better tools to fully develop their learning process. The approach starts with survey collection and the answers are processed using NLP techniques. The discovery process revealed 18 distinct learning approaches. Simultaneously, the results also show that learners use tools that vary significantly from task to task.

references.title: An Architecture Model of Recommender System for Pedagogical Design Patterns

annotations.text: Work draws from authors’ previous work and suggests a conversational Recommender System. The recommender is divided into 2 sections:
Context and patterns are combined to generate recommendations. Paper merely describes the conceptual model of the recommender, does not use it or test it in any capacity.

references.title: Which Planning Tactics Predict Online Course Completion?

extracted.Courses: 247 MOOCs from Harvard, MIT and Stanford
extracted.Features: [Manually Coded Learning Plans]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors do a manual coding process to identify planning tactics: essencially to answer 3 questions when, where and how. 

This was done using a 3 stage plan created from a sample of courses and plans,

Learning tactics were found to be non-collinear using VIF
extracted.Cluster Description: The manual coding allowed to creation of multiple features associated with different SRL constructs.
extracted.Implications:
Time is found to be the only factor positively associated with Course Completion. 

Students who plan to prioritize and make a routine end up exhausting themselves early and dropping out. 

Breaking down end-goal into sub-goals is often better and more effective. Course Designers can and should consider this in their course design.

Many individuals do not follow planning instructions.
extracted.Comments:
Authors essencially do a Regression analysis based on 2 distinct feature sets. On the one hand, authors use When/Where plans to evaluate completion or Progress in a MOOC. 

On a second analysis, the authors look at How plans to do the same analysis.
extracted.Limitations:
MOOCs considered are mostly from STEM courses.

Dubious long-term reliability of results since most planning is done for the short-term.

Only uses OLS but should consider other options that allow the modelling of co-variate relationships.

Does not consider the specificity of a plan.

annotations.text: Planning is core self-regulated learning skill. There is a lack of studies that focuses on the contents of planning and the feature is usually assessed on the effects of planning. Authors perform a qualitative analysis of 650 learner plans from 15 MOOCs.

Authors start with identifcation of planning tactics and mapped which planning tactics lead to course completion. Learners whose plans specify specific times of day are more likely to complete the MOOC event though only 25% of students engaged in this tactic.

references.title: A dual learning-based recommendation approach

annotations.text: The current forms of addressing cold-start and data sparsity issues in recommender systems are not vvery effective when there is a shortage of labebled data. The authors addess this by introducing an approach that treats recommendations as two-substeps:

Authors test the effectiveness of their approach in Movielens and Bookcrossing with simulated sparsity and cold-start scenarios. Results show model outperforms other hybrid recommenders on these datasets.

references.title: Predicting Learning Outcomes with MOOC Clickstreams

extracted.Students: Main course: 590 students in semester 1, 346 in semester 2, 427 in semester 3
extracted.Courses: 1 MOOC foundation course (6 weeks, 55 videos); additional validation on a second MOOC course (9 weeks, 95 videos)
extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, SQL course data, course scores
extracted.Features: [umber of course units entered, number of videos viewed, number of viewing sessions, 7 behavioral sequence types (Rewatch, Skipping, Fast Watching, Slow Watching, Clear Concept, Checkback Reference, Playrate Transition), number of available tests, number of answered tests, number of attempts, unit test score]
extracted.Moment of Prediction: Weekly prediction during the course; authors also generated weekly tutoring lists for at-risk students
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Raw OpenEdu logs were processed into video-event sequences. Twelve low-level click events were defined (e.g., play, pause, seek forward/backward, rate change, stop). N-gram analysis was performed on clickstream sequences and 4-grams were selected empirically. These sequences were then grouped into 7 higher-level behavioral actions using a “don’t care” matching scheme. The resulting course-unit features were merged with test performance data, yielding 85 features initially, from which 14 were selected for model building. Separate analyses were also conducted by video type: theoretical, experimental, and analytic.
extracted.Comments: The paper’s main contribution is transforming raw video clickstream into interpretable behavioral sequence features using 4-grams and grouping them into 7 cognitive participation behaviors. ANN performed best among the three methods. A further analysis showed that using only theoretical + experimental video data could yield prediction accuracy close to using all video types, which may help teachers evaluate which videos matter most. The system also produced weekly tutoring lists for students needing support.
extracted.Limitations: Based on only one main MOOC course plus one additional course for generalization. Evaluation relies on holdout splits rather than cross-validation. The prediction framework combines clickstream with unit-test behavior, so the effect of clickstream alone is less isolated. Some methodological choices, such as selecting 4-grams and the behavior-grouping rules, are partly empirical and domain-specific.

annotations.text: The work uses video clickstream records on a MOOC to identify 7 types of cognitive participation models. This feature extraction is followed by the creation of a predictive model that predicts student performance, with ANN taking the take as the one achieving the highest accuracy.

Authors follow-up by attempting to improve the original model by pre-classifying the different videos according to the content - theoretical, experimental and/or analytical. The creation of different models shows that by model performance is relatively unaffected if the models only consider theoretical and experimental videos.

references.title: Using learning analytics to explore help-seeking learner profiles in MOOCs

extracted.Features: [25 features indicative of help-seeking behaviors]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Sample only includes students who achieved at least 40% of the course grade.

Feature matrix was normalized and, subsequently followed by a t-SNE before clustering.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Low Engagement: Low grades, low activities, No forum threads/posts

Assessment-Focused - low grades: high attempts of assessments, low video interactions, low pageviews

Passive Engagement: Meduim grades, moderate pageviews, low activity on forum

Acitve Engagement: High Engagement, moderate aassessment attempts, high forum participation

Assessment-focused - high grades: High grades, high assessments, low pageviews and video
extracted.Implications: MOOC designers need to make interactions with forums and/or learning contents more engaging for learners… otherwise these will only be passively used.
extracted.Comments: Authors use Affinity Propagation to identify different patterns of help-seeking-behavior from MOOC data.
extracted.Limitations: • Does not consider student information,

annotations.text: The ability to reach tutors or peers is often hindered in MOOCs in comparison to traditional settings. The authors look to identify different types of help-seeking behavior in MOOCs from Melbourne University. To do that, they use different unsupervised techniques (affinity propagation clustering and t-SNE). Authors identify 5 patterns of help-seeking behavior and evaluate the relationship of these profiles with performance.

references.title: Personalized Learning Path Recommender Based on User Profile Using Social Tags

extracted.Initialization Method: Users are initialized via the profiling via social tags- users are asked to rank the social tags according to their preference.
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: After taking an activity
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The fiull preprocessing pipeline goes as follows:

1. Select the representative items for each learner.

2. Do not consider items that user has not viewed and build item-tag matrix.

3. For each item-tag relationship, highlight if it is part of the user preferences. 

4. Rank tags

5. For each item, form a vector consisting of tag rankings

6. Create a profile of user u for each item based on the tag rankings. 

7. USer simmilarity will be computed using Pearson Correlation.

8. Select most similar users and aggregate the learning path (sequence of steps from user U up to now).

Predictions on most most likely next action arre made using Bayes Theorem.
extracted.Evaluation: {Learning Time: 115.2, Average Grade: 81.4, Knowledge Unit Mastery: 0.913}
extracted.Comments:
Authors do 2 experiments:

Experiment 1 that only executes Step 8 - Aggregation and recommendation.

Experiment 2 which follows the full logic of the recommender framework.

Learners who were part of experiment 2 spent less time studying and achieved better grades and knowledge mastery.
extracted.Limitations:
Only considers preferences from the most interested learners. 

Small dataset.

annotations.text: The goal of educational recommender systems is to improve performance. The authors propose a recommender system that uses social tags to build user profiles. Then, it uses Bayes formula to predict next learning materials out of the ones used by similar users (so, collaborative filtering).

references.title: Decoding student success in higher education: A comparative study on learning strategies of undergraduate and graduate students

extracted.Features: [Total Clicks, Clicks (%), Online Sessions, Clicks/Session, Clicks/day, Forum Clicks, Discussions Viewed, Forum Posts, Folder Clicks, Resources Viewed, URLs Viewed, Course Clicks, Assessments Started, Assignments Viewed, Assignments Submitted, Submissions (%), Total Time Online, Average duration of session, Largest Period of Inactivity, Days with 0 clicks, Days with no clicks (%), PercCourse_NLogin (N= 1, …, 10)]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Data was separated into different programs. Preprocessing pipeline went as follows:

1. Gaussianity was tested using the Jarque-Bera test, 

2. Features that did not follow this were subjected to the Yeo-Johnson transformation and subsequently standardized.

Feature selection was done per perspective: First via relevance and secondly by creating an instance of k-Means just with the data from one perspective and measuring the explained variancxe by each of the variables.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {SSD: selection of k, t-tests were used to measure differences between clusters}
extracted.Cluster Description:
Mildly Frequent, mild activity in short sessions:

Likely Procrastinators: Students who do not access Moodle frequently but, when they do, they tend to do it late in the course AND have long sessions. Performance is the second lowest.

Mildly Frequent and Task-Focused: Second most frequent access to Moodle, usually in short bursts and directed towards folders and assessments

Active, Prolonged and Frequent: Learners who have the most frequent accesses to the Platform and the students who achieve the highest grade.

Inactive: Close to no interactions with the LMS and have the lowest grade on average. High standard deviation, however, shows that this group also includes successful students who likely use other means to learn.
extracted.Implications:
Potential for generalizable ML descriptive models for characterization of graduate and undergradaute behaviors since hierarchy of relationships generalizes.

LMS only provide a partial picture of learner performance (see Inactive behavior and grades).

Potential of identifying personalized interventions to different profiles. In addition, course designers could take into account the most successful behaviors and design pedagogical strategies that promote them.

In the case of graduate learners, Inactive and Likely Procrastinators achieve the same average grade. 

Comparison between graduates and undergraduates show relatively the same patterns of profile. Graduate students are, however, more efficient with their time and resource usage.

annotations.text: The work uses the k-means clustering algorithm to identify learning strategies on two sets of higher education students. The first set aims to make predictions BSc level students while the second focuses on making predictions for MSc-level students.

Results show that it is possible to identify 5 different learning strategies in both sets that are similar in both sets. Moreover, the authors identify that the relative effectinevess of these strategies is consistent in different instruction levels.

Results on OULAD show that there is some merit in the approach even though the authors are not explicit on whether they have an early warning system or not.

Data was very sparse as only 12% of entries: user-submission-assignment were known.

Authors analyized the differences in distribution between CFA and non-CFA:

1. Playing behavior: Playing the video for more time contributes to a higher likelihood of getting CFA.
2. Pausing behavior: Pausing multiple times, either very short pauses or very long pauses.
3. Playback rate behavior: Having average playback rate different from default speed.
4. Jumping behavior: Rewinding at least once.

Authors setup a baselineand compare its performance against the models incorporating video-watching behavior.

Authors attempt to do some fiine-tuning of the models.

annotations.text: Paper focuses on predicting whether a student will get a question right or wrong on First Attempt. For that, authors use video-watching behavior from student-video pairs.

Authors identify that there are sets of behaviors that do in fact lead to a higher probability of getting a question right. Thus, the authors identify which sets of intervals are the most reliable estimators of success and use the behavior in those intervals as features in a predictive model for Knowledge Tracing.

Results show that the model constructed in this manner significantly outperforms other models exclusively using log data and that the improvement is even more pronounced when considering a scenario of early prediction (unclear at this stage if this is still knowledge tracing at this point, but it seems to me).

In the world of MOOCs, where the ration instructor/student is smaller than 1%, scale is big factor. More explicitly, automation of processes to enhance learning are paramount for success at scale. The study intends to answer the following research questions:
To answer these questions, the authors use Coursera data from a Princeton university course. The data includes 1.3 M records of interaction with video player and over 40k Quizz submissions.

In the field of Learning Analytics (LA), student performance can refer to various aspects. Knowledge Tracing is one such LA task. This specific work builds upon Item Response Theory, which computes the probability of an examinee's answer to a particular item. One of the major modern applications of this approach is in models that use study behaviors to predict whether a specific answer will be correct on the first attempt (CFA).

A common set of models used for this task are Collaborative Filtering approaches. These have gained significant popularity since the Netflix Prize competition, which showcased their effectiveness in recommendation systems. These models excel at uncovering hidden relationships between different users as they interact with specific pieces of content, making them particularly useful for predicting user preferences and behaviors.

Other approaches include Probabilistic Graphical Models, Hidden Markov Models, Bayesian Networks, Decision Trees, and Factorization Machines. These methods are often employed when dealing with coarse-grained information about users and/or courses across multiple sessions. Coarse-grained data in this context might refer to high-level, aggregated information such as:

The approaches of predicting success in next question using behavioral data have their own sets of challenges when it comes to MOOC specifically. First, a lot more users simply dropout which means that the fraction of assessments completed by each user is relatively small. Also, the number of users is much larger than in traditional settings.

Contribution of paper: Video watching behavior is correlated with performance, which means that it can be used for CFA. Also, the contribution of the behavioral data is larger at earlier stages of course.
Data

The data used refers to a data from a MOOC that has already had multiple offerings. This course is structured for Online Learning. It has 20 lectures that are spread across 12 weeks. Each lecture is subdivided into multiple videos, totalling 93 videos.

Videos were supplemented with in-video quizzes to test understanding. Questions were Multiple Choice with 4 options. Students had to answer as they watched the video → meaning that they only answer questions for videos they have watched. This problem is particularly important because there are close to 5000 users and close to 100 questions but there were only 40000 user-quizz pairings. Only 12% of the entries of a user → quizz matrix were known. The criteria for active users is having answered more than 20 questions.
From the submission historyit is possible to see that a lot more students made submissions earlier on. Moreover, the authors analyzed two things:
Bottom line, being more active leads to more submissions and better scores.
Clickstream data

Data from the clickstream was extracted from Coursera. Every action with the videos was recorded: pause, play, rate change or seek. Only pairs that featured the existence of logs and submissions were considered.
For each user-video pair with logs and submissions, the authors extracted features related to video watching behavior: time spent on video, number of rewinds, fast-forwards, etc…
Exploratory anlaysis on both classes shows some important insights on higher change of CFA:

The authors start with algorithms that only use performance data to predict CFA. The metrics for evaluation of the models were standard classsification and regression metrics: Accuracy, RMSE & AUC. Algorithm predictions will be a continuous score [0, 1]. These scores are rounded for 0 or 1 in CFA.
The baseline algorithms themselves were the following:

In addition now, these baseline algorithms ended up being compared against a set of algorithms that used the data of previous performance AND data from clickstream on videos. The attributes were separated into 3 categories:

Computation of CFA estimates are a function of the behaviors occuring in specific intervals. This allows the of LAplace rule of succession to compute the probability of a new observation belonging to class C or not. This is also complemented with a Bayesian adjustment to make more confident predictions.

In preparation for the final models that incorporate the clicksteam, the authors implement an SVM-based classifier. These classifiers, called VID-A and VID-B differ in the way they incorporate success estimates into the classification predictions.  Evaluation of the different models is based on 5-fold CV.
Evaluation of the different models is done by looking at the percentage improvement relative to the Naive benchmark that, for reference had 66% Acc and 0.5 RMSE & AUC.

Evaluation starts by looking at the scores obtained exclusively for the Active Users, followed by all users on the first 2 weeks and, finally, All users throughout the full course. Regarsless of context, the results show that making predictions with the video data leads to better CFA predictions than not using them.
Moreover, it is also possible to see that accurate predictions are easier to obtain on more active students.

The use of video-watching behavior may significantly assist the prediction of CFA earleier on in the course. The reason for this stems from the way models are built_ Biases and MF rely on the creation of complex models of the users → something that requires more data to be effective. 

VID-A outperforms VID-N for all tasks involving all users. The difference between both approaches stems from the KDE. The smaller value of  \eta leads to more bins in the continuous attributes. The authors identify 3 points of future investigation:
Generalizatbility of the findings may be hard to replicate due to some constraints on the building of the experiment: for example there is a 1 to 1 correspondence between video and quizzes.
Authors show that video-watching logs are decent predictors of CFA, especially earlier on.

annotations.text: Success in MOOCs requires good SRL skills. However, not all students have these skills developed. Thus it is important not only to be able to identify which SRL strategies should be promoted BUT to also identify which SRL are underdeveloped in the student population.

Authors of the paper use a sample of close to 5000 students and collect logs, survey answers and course achievement variables from them. The analysis of this data shows that Goal Setting and Strategic Planning are the most effective SRL skills to have to be successful while struggling students who seek help often underperform their peers.
More interestingly, the authors also found that multiple student characteristics such as motivation or demographics were predictive of SRL skills.

annotations.text: Personalized recommendations should be aligned with learner goals while also being perceived as adequate by educators and learners. The paper focuses on presenting practical guidelines into how to develop a recommender system in Online Learning. To illustrate, there is evidence from an experiment conducted with 125 educators and 595 learners.

MOOCs are online courses for an amount of students that greatly supersedes the traditional classroom. Providing timely and useful feedback and guidance in this context requires a hefty amount of resources that, more often than not, do not exist. Recommender systems can reduce the tutor workload.

As a matter of fact, there is too much information and resources available, which can hinder a student’s learning. Recommenders can provide some structure on this regard. However, in education, the task is more complex than simply recommending resources that the student will like. They need to promote student learning as well.

Most of the research of recommenders focuses on measuring the performance of different algorithms in tasks sucjh as information retrieval, with little emphasis on the design of the recommendations. Previous work on this exists but is scarce: there is is history of recommender systems whose recommendations are proposed by educational experts (not an algorithm). Authors argue that information retrieval cannot be the only form a recommender system works and it should take into account the educator.

Thus, the work presents a set of practical guidelines into how to create an educational recommender system. The authors showcase their guidelines on 2 distinct educational scenarios involving 125 educators and 595 students.

Educational recommenders have constraints that are not found in traditional scenarios and their design should take these restrictions into account. One such examples is preferences - an educational recommender should not take only learner preferences into account as they should, at least, be also made in a way that fosters and promotes learning.

Upon review of the recommender system literature, the authors found that most recommenders focused on only making recommendations of specific types of content, not specifically recommending a future action. This is interesting because LMS and MOOCs are often thought as capable of promoting active learning, which runs counter-intuitive to the recommendations.

When it comes to design, most of the recommender systems do not consider the users needs in the design process. Moreover, few works measure learning gains from the recommendations. In addition, the authors are proponents of including the educator in the design process, finding their experience invaluable towards the design of better educational recommendations → and more acceptance of the technology.

Due to their view on the importance of human-centered design for adaptive learning systems, the authors focus their discussion on ISO 9241-210 ‘Ergonomics of human-system interaction e Part 210: Human-centred design for interactive systems’ which provides an international standard for human-centered design.
Thus, the paper intends to provide guidelines into how to create recommendations that take into account the context, needs and preferences.

The authors identify their previously proposed TORMES methodology, which aims to identify recommendation opportunities in learning environments. TORMES combines Data Mining and User Centred Design. It designs recommendations in 3 steps:

The design process involves multiple phases present in ISO ISO 9241-210 which, at this stage are not particularly relevant to discuss. The authors proceed to discuss how this methodology was used for 2 distinct educational contexts:
The authors do an A/B test of the recommendations derived from a recommender developed using this methodology.

There were two rounds for implementation. The first was proof of concept and the second was the Elicitation of Recommendations. The proof of concept involved meetings with educators, students and ICT managers that use dotLRN. Learning style adaptation was recommended to follow the Felder and Silverman Learning Style Questionnaire.

In the Elicitation of Recommendations stage, the authors focused on deriving an educational context by surveying and interviewing educators. Moreover, the Data Mining analysis shows that stuudents who do not participate tend to not be successful, while participating in activities such as filling the profile, do self-assessments or fill the learning style questionnaire have been found to successful.

Similar to Context 1, the first was the Elicitation of Recommendations and the second was the Delivery of Recommendations. Elicitation followed a process similar to context 1. Educators, with the assistance of a Decision Tree, identified which conditions could lead to student success andmade suggestions (validated) on recommendations to make.

In the Delivery of Recommendations, the system provided recommendations as soon as the specific conditions for them were met. Then, students and educators were asked to provide feedback on the usefulness of the recommendations.

In the context of human centered design of educational recommender systems, it is essencial to have guidelines for the discovery and delivery of recommendations that focus on learning needs and foster active learning. Using TORMES methodology, the authors were able to empirically showcase 2 examples of systems whose recommendations go beyond merely recommending content to read but also recommend specific actions to be undertaken in the platform.
The recommendations done in the experiments were obtained via 2 main ways:

annotations.text: Variability in teaching methods and learning styles has effects in the potential positive effects of technology adoption in student performance. The paper presents an experiment setup based on SEM. Motivation is the main variable to determine success in the context of online learning.
ICT infrastructure aims to improve student performance and achievement. The paper attempts to answer 2 main questions:

ICT has shifted education considerably. It creates new opportunities for better communication between teachers and students, it also forces new interactions with educational resources. The potential is recognized for scale factors: improve the performance and achievement of more students with the same or even fewer resources. However, it is not self-evident that making the investment is enough. Institutions require specific adaptations to the way courses are delivered in order to make full use of the digital evolution.

Some research shows that e-learning students actually perform worse than their face-to-face counterparts. These works often fail to distinguish between teaching methods or learning styles, instead opting to treat online learning as an homogenous entity. Out of those works, there are some interesting observations that explain this difference:

Other works show that adjusting teaching methods to the digital tools actually has positive effects in student performance. Taking into account the appearance of active teaching methods it is possible to adapt teaching methodologies to become more student-focused and simultaneously make full use of ICT.

Thus, it becomes essencial to identify key variables that explain the effects of technology adoption in student performance. E-learning enviroments are a good playground to implement active teaching methods. Yet, there is a problem of resistance by current professors who look at the method as inneficient at best and inefective at worst. Also, the fact that a tool is available does not necessarily mean that it will be used by the students.
The question for the current paradigm is not should we use technology. Instead it should be how to use technology at an institutional, teacher and student level.
The work uses a SEM theoretical framewok that considers different sets of explanatory variables to explain online student success:

The data was collected via questionnaire. The data was plugged into a created SEM and an analysis of the explanatory variables was performed. Motivation has the highest standardized coefficient (0.298), meaning that it was found to be the most important variable of the model.

Some variables expected to be relevant such as Schooling and Feedback Intensity were not found to ave an effect on student performance.  Conversely, motivation was found to be the most important feature.

Motivation is the most important driving force for student ability to pass exams and, according to this model, is actually the only variable with a positive and significant effect on student performance. Motivation is positively influenced by self-perception of efficiency. 

Other variables have indirect positive effects:

It is possible that these variables affect perception of efficiency which, in turn, has positive effects on motivation and consequently Student Performance. Older students tend to be more confident but, simultaneously, tend to be more experienced when it comes to working and having better pre-knowledge.

However, somewhat surprisingly, the authors find no relationship between pre-knowledge and performance. Also, variables measuring student effort also do not seem to impact student performance.
