Authors procceed to compare the performance of multipe ML algorithms on the task of predicting student performance - score on final examinations. Logistic Regression achieved the best scores at predicting the exact final grade - 69% for students who passed and 89% for students who failed.

references.title: DeepLMS: a deep learning predictive model for supporting online learning in the Covid‑19 era

extracted.Students:
1037 students in DB1
180 students in DB2
52 students in DB3
extracted.Courses:
DB1 - 5 Courses in FMH

DB2 - 1 Course in a UAE university

DB3 - 1 Course in a Greek University
extracted.Features: [C1 to C14]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The interesting thing about this work is that it uses 3 different databases at different resolutions:

Institutional, 
Course and Discipline. 

The raw logs are given to a categorization module that extracts 14 features and these are subsequently fed into a Quality of Interaction Module that converts the logs into a quality of interaction score. The output of the quality of interactions is subsequently fed to an LSTM, which then predicts QoI for next interaction.
extracted.Comments:
DeepLMS is a deep learning system designed to predict how well a user is engaging with an online learning platform (LMS), specifically Moodle. The motivation comes from the Covid-19 pandemic, which forced a sudden shift to online learning and created a need for tools that could monitor and support that process in real time.
The system works in two stages. First, a pre-existing fuzzy logic model called FuzzyQoI takes raw LMS interaction logs — things like login frequency, resource access, forum activity — and converts them into a single daily quality of interaction (QoI) score per user, normalized between 0 and 1. Second, an LSTM neural network is trained on each user's historical QoI scores to predict their next day's score.
The practical output is a feedback signal. By comparing the current QoI with the predicted next QoI, the system generates a difference score that can trigger personalized alerts — warning a student or educator when engagement is expected to drop, before it actually does.
The model was tested on data from three universities across Portugal, UAE and Greece, covering both pre- and during-Covid periods, and involving over 1,300 users and 647,000 LMS interactions in total. Across all three datasets the model achieved very low prediction error and high correlation with actual QoI values, and performed considerably better than the previous benchmark model.
The authors are careful to position QoI prediction as a complement to traditional grading, not a replacement, and acknowledge that whether QoI actually correlates with academic outcomes remains an open question for future work.
extracted.Limitations:
No prediction of grade/performance

No correlation analysis was done between QoI and actual academic performance (grades, exams) 

Data covers only one or half an academic year per database, limiting confidence in long-term consistency.

The feedback mechanism is proposed conceptually but never actually tested with real users to see if it changes behavior.

DB2 has only 76 days total, meaning the test window is around 8 days — very thin for drawing strong conclusions.

The model is trained and tested on the same users across time, so true generalization to new, unseen individuals is never demonstrated.

The three databases differ substantially in size, duration and scale, making direct cross-database comparisons difficult to interpret cleanly.

annotations.text: Covid caused a massive transition to remote teaching platforms. The authors propose a ML algorithm called DeepLMS whose intent is to forecast the quality of interaction of the students with the LMS. Results show very small error between predictions and ground truth QoI.

While this model does not predict success, it can potentially be used to predict quality of interactions of students with an LMS. This, can provide additional features related to student motivation that are usually not present in just the prediction of student success.

references.title: An Exploratory Study on Student Engagement with Adaptive Notifications in Programming Courses

annotations.text: Students in a course were provided with an opt-in option to receive personalized notifications about how they will do in a course and some personalized recommended resources. In addition, struggling students received code solutions from high performing students.
The engagement of students was tracked and has been shown to contribute to student development → as showcased by the difference in score between one exam and the next.

references.title: A Review of Self-regulated Learning: Six Models and Four Directions for Research

annotations.text: The paper discusses 6 of the most well known models of SRL. They present differences in history, description of the model, empirical support and available instruments that stem from differences in the model. The authors perform a meta analysis over the different models and draw reccomendations for practice. They outline that SRL is a coherent framework for research in educational psychology and call for researchers and educators to draw from the current knowledge in SRL to enhance student’s learning and SRL skills.

SRL has made substantial contributions to education psychology since its inception. One of the key conquests was the discrimination between SRL and metacognition. With time, the number of SRL models has been increasing and, in 2011, a handbook outlining the established methods of SRL was published. The authors propose to review the, at the time, existing knowledge on SRL.

The criteria for inclusion in the work were only established SRL models that have been previously published and cited in peer reviewed papers. Models that did not fulfill these conditions were dismissed. The authors kept the Zimmerman, Boekaerts and Winne that were assumed to still be active - for example Borkowski’s model have not been considered.

Other models that have been used after 2001 were also considered for the literature, thus bringing the models used to be those by: Zimmerman, Pintrich, Boekarts, Winne, Hokfides and, finally, the model by Hadwin, Jarvela and Miller.

Zimmerman developed 3 distinct SRL models and started his approach using socio-cognitive theory: people acquire knowlesge from socialization and observation of others. The initial models was known as a Triadic Analysis of SRL that analyzed 3 forms of SRL - environment, behavior and personal level.

The second model by Zimmerman focused in the Cyclical Phases of SRL explains the interrelation of metacognitive and motivational processes. This model was later tweaked in 2009 with more metacognitive and volitional strategies.
The third model represents the four stages in which students acquire self-regulatory competency.
Zimmerman uses 3 stages:

The cyclical model has been extensively tested in studies, both by Zimmerman and other authors. A study testing athletic skill profficiency between experts, non-experts and novices in multiple sports showcases that SRL skills play a role in the behaviour that is exhibited. In one example using basketball, experts performed more SRL related actions in comparison to others. In another example of Volleyball, SRL skills accounted for more than 90% of the explained variance in serving skills. Also in academic skills, higher achieving students exhibited an more SRL subprocesses than their colleagues. Keeping logs and diaries over progress has also been shown to positively affect SRL skills and performance.

Zimmerman’s model measures subprocesses that are partly based from the Self-Regulated Learning Interview Schedule. Moreover, the author has also developed his own methods to assess SRL in experimental settings.  Another method was later developed by Magno using the Academic Self-Regulation Scale which has validated using correlation analysis with the results obtained from other models - more specifically the MSLQ and the Learning and Study Strategies Inventory.

The work by this author is more focused on the role of setting goals and the motivation of students. The author has developed 2 distinct models of SRL. The first model was a structural model divided into six components:

The second model was evolved into what is called the Dual-Processing Self-Regulation Model. This model draws from multiplical psychology frameworks including motivation, emotion, metacognition, self-concept and learning. This model distinguishes different purposes of self-regulation during the learning process:

According to the Boekarts model, students will be prone to adopt strategies that align with their emotional state (motivation). Students who see a task as threathning will shy away from it in order to maximize their well-being. On the other hand, if the tasks align with the student’s goals and needs, they will actively look to pursue development and acquire knowledge on their path to mastery.

The authors identified how cognitive appraisals determine emotion and learning intentions and, for example, found that different genders receive, emotionally, the appraisals, resulting into different responses. These results were validated in other studies, showing that, for example, boys and girls attend differently to some types of problems such as math: boys tend to be more positive and more willing to put the efforts whilst girls were less prone to engage. In addition, the authors showed that the interpretation of what is meant to be done in a learning activity are better explainers of learning intentions than other domain related measures such as ability or interest in the domain.

Success in mathematics homeworks under this model was also shown to be positively influence by value appraisals. In addition, the author managed to create neural networks (using 56 predictors) that achieved an accuracy ranging between 94 to 100% accuracy.
Online Measurement Questionnaire - measuring sensitivity to learn in concrete situations
Interactive Learning Group System
Confidence and Doubt Scale
Use of Neural Networks to measure SRL

Winne and Hadwins model has been very popular in the implementation of computer supported learning settings as it mostly relied on the measurement of metacognitive strategies and their impacts on motivation.
The creators of this model believe that internal feedback is of paramount importance to SRL. The model presented by the authors occurs in 4 distinct phases:

While there is strong empyrical evidence for their work, there are some challenges lying in front of this model: the first is being more explicit on how phase 4 works - what processes are undertaken and what do the authors refer to as “long-term changes in their behavior”. Second - the model does not incorporate motivation. Thirdly, a discussion of how SRL evolves over the lifespan and finally, what is the impact of learning disabilities in SRL. More recent work has focused on applications of Data Mining and learning analytics to measure SRL. Up to this point, Winne’s model has been the only one explicitly focused on measurements of SRL using Trace data.

Pintrich is one of the main fathers of SRL research as his theoretical framework, analytical tools (MSLQ) and empyrical studies have, in large proportion, significantly contributed to the field. The Pintrich model follows 4 distinct phases:

The Pintrich conceptual model includes metacognitive research such as perceptions of learning and of knowledge acquisition. Moreover, his work also shows that both regulation and affection can be regulated and argues that individuals can make attempts to control their own overt behavior. The final component is regulation of the learning context.

No direct evidence concerning empyrical validation of the model, but Pintrich has made multiple contributions to the SRL field. MSLQ is the most widely used instrument when it comes to the measurement of SRL or self-efficacy.

Efklides has one of the strongest metacognition focus in comparison to other models (with the exception of Winne and Hadwin’s models). However, Efklides also puts a strong emphasis on motivation and affect. Despite it being fairly young (2011), the model has contributed to SRL research in great ways. The advantage of this model is that it builds upon existing SRL frameworks while also adding a connection with other metacognitive models.
The model has 2 levels:

This model seems to be focused in describing how SRL comes into play in the context of collaborative work. The model draws from Winne and Hadwin’s model and tries to complement it with the introduction of other SRL constructs in a group context.

references.title: Enhanced personalized recommendation system for machine learning public datasets: generalized modeling, simulation, significant results and analysis

extracted.Initialization Method:
Users are initialized via a questionnaire. The questionnaire returns the learner’s interests and capacity. 

This amtrix is normalized using L1-loss.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Users are initialized via a questionnaire to assess their interests.

The learners’ learning style is inferred using clustering and, subsequently, the recommendations are applied on top of the clustering results. 

Cosine similarity is used to recommend items based on the ratings of other users.
extracted.Evaluation: {MAE: 0.10, Accuracy: 0.84, Computational Time: 90 seconds, Ranking Score: 0.06, Recall: 0.55, Precision: 0.54}
extracted.Comments:
Authors create a recommender system that is meant to use tests to assess the users’ knowledge interests and knowledge and, from there, identify groups of similar students. 

Students are clustered together to identify learning styles and cosine similarity is used to compute the ideal recommendation. 

Authors test their results using multiple different datasets, among them OULAD.
extracted.Limitations:
- Simulated Experiment

- Attempt different clustering techniques

High computational time

annotations.text: Elearning allows learners to access educational content whenever and wherever they desire. This new power also comes with the great responsability of enabling personalized learning, something that traditional Recommender systems often fail at due to making incorrect recommendations and, oftentimes, taking to long to properly reach the recommendation.

The authors look to address these issues by proposing and Enhanced vector recommender that automatically adjusts the learner’s interests, requirements and knowledge level. The improved preprocessing is subject to Content Based Filtering recommenders in order to deliver the actual recommendations. These recommendations are sub-sequently ranked using a collaborative filtering approach.
The method was tested on multiple datasets (including OULAD). The simulated outcomes outperform other existing strategies.

references.title: A multi-objective optimization approach for the group formation problem

annotations.text: The apper explores the group formation problem using 3 constraints: inter-homogeneity, intra-heterogeneity and empathy. Optimization is done using a Genetic Algorithm. Comparison wtih 3 baselines on computastional terms and in a real setting against random and self-organized methods. In both, the proposed method showed potential.

references.title: Collaborative activities recommendation based on students’ collaborative learning styles using ANN and WSM

extracted.Students: 80 undergraduate Computer Science students (University of West Attica, Greece)
extracted.Data sources:
Questionnaire (Gardner & Korth Collaborative Learning Styles), 
Expert-Defined Weights from 14 instructors
extracted.Initialization Method: Learners complete a collaborative learning style questionnaire (Gardner & Korth, 1998), which classifies them into four styles: Accommodator, Converger, Diverger, Assimilator. Initial weights per style and per activity are defined by experts and fed to the ANN.
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Static once per learner profile (no continuous updates). System generates new recommendations per learner instance but does not adapt dynamically.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Weights defined by experts are normalized and fed to the ANN as input nodes. WSM (Weighted Sum Model) acts as the activation function, producing an output vector representing the percentage likelihood of participation in each collaborative activity type.
extracted.Evaluation: {Satisfaction on Assignment: 8.55/10 vs 6.625/10, Grades: 7.825/10 vs 6.25/10}
extracted.Comments:
Combines Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) with the Weighted Sum Model (WSM) for multi-criteria decision-making, based on learners’ collaborative styles.Model outputs participation percentages per activity type → used for recommendations and grouping.

Empirical evaluation shows significant improvement in satisfaction and learning outcomes.Extends adaptive CSCL personalization by incorporating group-level learning style intelligence.
extracted.Limitations:
Recommendations are static, not adaptive to ongoing learner behavior.

Learning style detection depends entirely on self-reported questionnaire data.

Expert weighting introduces subjectivity.

Evaluation limited to a single course and small population.

No long-term validation or group interaction analysis beyond the Java course.

annotations.text: The personalization of learning activities in computer-supported collaborative learning is something that has garnered the interest of educational researchers. The recommender system uses Artificial Neural Networks and Weighted Sum Model to recommend which collaborative activities a learner should take going forward.

To test their model, the authors created an intelligent tutoring system for Java. Results on this ITS show that the solution is not only feasable but also tends to lead to positive impacts on learning.

references.title: Enhanced Harris Hawks optimization as a feature selection for the prediction of student performance

annotations.text: Organizations will benefit from correct preditions of student performance. Multiple models exist nowadays and, in this scenario, the authors propose a new feature selection algorithm:

After optimization, the authors employ multiple ML algorithms to predict student performance and see impact of dataset. Tecnhiques used involve NB, LRNN, ANN and kNN. results show that combining HHO and LRNN outperformed all other classifiers in benchmarked dataset with 92% accuracy.

references.title: An Enhanced Genetic Algorithm for Heterogeneous Group Formation Based on Multi-Characteristics in Social-Networking-Based Learning

annotations.text: In social network learning, forming appropriate groups is both essential to promote an optimal learning process and time-consuming. The paper offers an approach to group formation that hinges on 3 key attributes: academic, cognitive and social components.

These components are used on a genetic algorithm approach that uses 2 crossover operators. Results show this approach considers more parameters and is more efficient at achieving an optimal solution than standard GA.

references.title: Intelligent Group Formation in Computer Supported Collaborative Learning Scripts

annotations.text: Groups based on Collaborative Learning Flow Patterns often lead to good outcomes. While forming groups based on pedagogical guidelines, time and complexity often prevent the formation of optimal groups.
Authors explore the use of constrained optimization to form groups and results show that approach may help form student groups.

Authors reach out to learners flagged as at-risk to provide some addtional feedback.

The recommendations are a part of intervention.

At the end of each week, the system sends an e-mail notification to students with their progress and, if learner failed at submission.

Authors measured “improvement” between students who opted in vs students who opted out:  All students performed badly on second exam but the decrease for students who opted-in was, at least, smaller.

Positive feedback for interventions.

annotations.text: Data about students can be extracted from multiple sources in an HE context. Combining data from multiple sources is essencial to create an accurate picture of students.

Paper presents a system that automatically students at risk and provides personalized adaptive feedback in programming courses. Authors combine data from multiple sources to create a system that predicts whether they are at-risk or not. These predictions are generated weekly.

More specifically, students under the system praised it and heavily recommended its usage by subsequent students. Also, students who accepted the personalized recommendations also performed better than their peers.

Filling of missing values takes advantage of the sequential nature of the data. If a point is missing at moment V, it is filled using an “average” of V at t-1 and V at t+1. 

Additionally, the authors address the class imbalance by using ISMOTE - which tests generated samples and decides on whether the generated sample should be accepted or not. 

The sequences are given as input to 2 distinct modules simultaneously: a teacher model which uses Attention Layers into a Bi-Directional LSTM Network (which is pre-trained). There is also a Student Model that uses a CNN-based ResNet-101.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.996 - EDF_KDF, Precision: 0.9921 - EDF_KDF, Recall: 0.9921 - EDF_KDF, AUC: 0.99 - EDF_KDF}

Authors propose a new DL model for the prediction of student performance.

annotations.text: Universities are constantly look into ways to improve the academic performance of their students and the introduiction of VLEs represents a significant step toward that direction. Authors use VLE data to create an early warning system that makes recommendations for intervention.

Authors perform pre-processing on the data and use ISMOTE to deal with data imbalance. Authors feed the preprocessed data as input to a EDL_KDL mode, a deep learning explainable model to predict performance and that can be explained using SHAP values. This model achieves 99.6% accuracy and 99.21% precision on OULAD.

The authors use an Hidden Markov Model to mine sequential behaviors and create an EWS. Authors use OULAD as validation and achieve 90%´accuracy and AUC. In addition the authors also find that the optimal moment for early prediction is either the first or second quarter of a course.

annotations.text: The paper focuses on understanding how students consume and reuse course materials. To do that, authors present a basic classifier that identifies struggling students. Then, they introduce indicators on when students employ a concept for the first time. The improvements resulted in a 85% accuracy score.

Computer science degrees tend to have high churn and high rates of failure in introductory courses. However, little attention is placed on this fact. This difficulty reduces the number of available skilled professionals for ICT companies. So, undergraduate courses are a good place to start addressing this to, hopefully, get more ICT graduates.

Fortunately, LMS and the birth of Learning Analytics has contributed to a better understanding of the phenomena behind learning. LMS and other VLE’s can produce data in enough quantity and quality necessary for this. With this, it has been possible to, for example, identify students in risk of dropping out.

During the literature review, the authors point to studies that have found relationships between interactions with the LMS. The most surprising observation notes that extremely high student activity levels are predictors of negative student performance. Another relevant study is the one of Course Signals taht identified students in need and sent them an e-mail. This system, and others, were effective at improving performance but often are criticized by being one-size fits all solutions and, by definition, probably too general. However, there are opportunities in more context specific domains like programming:

The authors identify other potential prospects: the Blackbox project asked users of an IDE about the collection of their data. Another study points to the use of virtual machines on an engineering course. The VM would store the information and send it to a central server for analytical purposes.

Another set of authors found that tinkering had a role to play in the learning process. Others focused on understanding the robustness of tutoring software. More specifically in the prediction of performance, other authors used DTs to distinguish between low and high-performing students on a Java course → achieving between 70 to 80% accuracy.
The authors note that LMS often DO NOT give real-time analytics. This raises for the exposition of a platform that can be used to provide support to students in a real-time laboratory session.

The authors developed a platform for module delivery. The platform was trialed in 2nd year CS students. The platform SOCS handles content and learning activities. Since the platform is custom made, it allows for a more fine-grained analysis of the activities performed in the LMS.

When it comes to learning materials, SOCS features 20 lessons, 8 lab exercises and a simulator. Students were encouraged to explored the simulator. Students undertaking the platform had an introductory course on it and are informed about the data collection. In total, 111 students used the platform, recording more than 9 million interactions and made more than 58000 programs.
Observation of the logs shows 3 distinct spikes in activity, each spike referring to periods before either lab exams or final exam.

Another important use case for this LMS is to test whether a certain concept described as important by the educator is adopted in the coding submission. As an experiment, the authors selected 6 concepts and an auto-grader designed to capture those concepts in the programs written by the students. Some of the concepts are easier to detect than others:

This allowed to detect when did a student first submit code that actually captured a certain concept, allowing the measurement of indicators such as the time to adopt a specific concept. An interesting observation is that adoption sort of follows a sttepped nature that overlaps with labs or worksheets. Interestingly as well, when covering more complex topics, the authors noted that:

Moreover, not using a concept before a specific moment in time usually makes it likely that the student wil not employ the concept, which would make sheets, labs or tests forcing that employment a suitable intervention. This is also a good form to identify potentially struggling students.

The authors also developed a Pass/Fail on the final exam classifier to apply to their students. To do that, the authors explore multiple different options for classifiers, compare their performance and select the best one. The authors compare the different models using Cross-Validation from sklearn. 

As inputs, the models use data collected from the LMS throughout the entire semester (a prediction is attempted at the end of every week). The variables take advantage of the fact that this is a custom platform that is optimized for programming. Thus, in addition to standard LMS variables, the variables also include successful compilations of code or complexity of the failed compilations. The authors use use a ratio of on-campus/off campus interactions.
The authors test different forms of preprocessing: scalers, feature selection (altough unspecificied). Models are also compared using the ROC AUC.
The AUC score evolves with time as expected, gradually increasing over time. Moreover, authors identify the CART as the best model on the later sections of the course.

More than just making these predictions, the authors attempt to compare these models with other models that incorporate concept adoption features. Their incorporation increases the performance of the models after week 10.

Statistics-relevant messages: These Facebook posts were also coded using Bloom Taxonomy

Students who produce fewer messages that are relevant to the course are at an increased risk of failing.

Authors hypothesize that using social media information may be a relevant predictor of success in a Statistics Course.

To do that, the authors measure the messages posted in a Private Group and use Supervised ML to classify messages according to their relevance towards the course. From here, the authors create 2 Linear Regressions:

Model 1: Counting number of messages in Forum

Model 2: Counting number of messages in forum but splitting them according to their relevance towards the course.

Results show that using ML to automate the process of classifying message relevance and subsequently using the classified messages may be a worthwhile effort.

This paper presents an actual system that, at least, was implemented at Purdue. 

The system leverged data from multiple sources and returned a flag about success of a student. 

The system also allows for multiple ways to reach out and help the students. 

Risk indicators are course-specific.

As far as analysis, the authors perform an analysis of co-horts across different dimensions:

1. Retention rate,

The earlier the student encounters CS, the better. Also, CS seems to be an useful system even for those students who are going for Standardized TEsts-

annotations.text: Paper presents Course Signals. The system is implemented on Blackboard Vista. The system analyzes logs, past academic history and previous grades. Paper shares structure of the system and preliminary results and reception.
Students receive feedback over a personalized e-mail followed with traffic-light color coding.
There are 3 necessary conditions for student persistence:
Thus, the authors look at Course Signals and present the way it promotes academic integration:

The system has been under employment at Purdue University since 2007. The system uses predictive models to identify, in real time, potential students at risk. For that, there is a Predictive Student Success algorithm that is run on-demand by the instructors.
The SSA is a proprietary algorithm that weights 4 components that encompass:
Course Signals allows for identification of students at risk by combining all dimensions into a single score that represents the likelihood of failing on the course.

Thse system was initially intended to help identify students at risk and was built from the ground up. The system currently (in 2012) had been used by 145 instructors, impacting 24000 students. In the courses where CS has been used, the authors have noted a general increase in the numbers of As and Bs received by the students, accompanied by a relateive decrease in the number of Cs. 

Moreover, the relative proportion of really low grades (Ds, Fs, and even Dropout) has substantially decreased in courses using the system.

To showcase the effects, the authors display results obtained using cohorts of 2007, 2008 and 2009. For these cohorts, the authors specified conditions of students of belonging to a co-hort and then computed the retention rate for each co-hort (inside the co-hort the students were split by the number of courses using Course signals undertaken).
Students undertaking CS courses are much more likely to continue studying and not dropout relative to their peers. Morever, this relationship seemed to hold.

The second analysis is a comparison of students who had CS courses in a period vs the students who did not have any CS course. Results show that the system is benefical for students. The survey data collected to measure student satisfaction also suggests that.

Positive experiences with CS by 89% of respondents and 58% would like to have CS in all courses. Students feel validated as individuals by the e-mail, describing the experience as motivating- with 2 out of 1500 respondents being the only instances of the feedback being demoralizing.

Faculty claim that their life towards the design of more personalized interventions is definitely asier thanks to CS. The multi-modal nature of the data is also appreciated. Also, from the professor’’s perspective, the fact that the system automatically warns students about their progress contributes to having the students start other tasks (e.g. projects) earlier than before. 

In addition, the students demonstrate more interest in the prokects as they make more questions about it and other assignments.
The main concern of professors is the flow of e-mails that they receive from students who were flagged.

references.title: Multiple Features Fusion Attention Mechanism Enhanced Deep Knowledge Tracing for Student Performance Prediction

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Correct on next exercise on first attempt
extracted.Features: 24 exercise and behavior features
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Previous models primarily relied on limited exercise features, ignored most student behavior features, and used manual feature selection with uniform feature weighting. Existing models also struggled with processing long learning sequences and capturing the varying importance of different features.
extracted.Novelty of Model: The MFA-DKT framework addresses these limitations by automatically processing both behavior and exercise features using Principal Component Analysis. It employs an LSTM network with an attention mechanism to dynamically weight feature importance.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: PCA for feature dimensionality reduction, Removal of NaNs, duplicates, and text features
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {AUC: 0.98 - MFA-DKT, Accuracy: 0.97 - MFA-DKT, RMSE - 0.26 - MFA-DKT, MAE-0.20 - MFA-DKT}
extracted.Comments:
MFA-DKT outperforms all baselines, better accuracy with larger training set.

Second experiment compares model against different combinations of features and outperforms all others.
extracted.Limitations: Ignores relationship between knowledge concepts, uses PCA for feature embeddings

annotations.text: The authors of thjs work distingusih between 2 types of features. Those that relate to exercises: i.e. is a specific task being correctly executed? and those who relate with behavior. The paper proposes a framework for the prediction of student performance using:
To do that, they use a Deep Learning architecture to automatically extract features that is followed by an attention-based model.
According to authors, the model seems to outperform the current state of the art.

Greater ability to produce content has contributed towards an increase of supply in educational contents. Tracking performance in these contexts is especially important, as it opens the door of personalized support to students in need.

There is a distinction between exercise features - related with knowledge concepts and how they lead to a student’s response - and behaviour features - how a student behaves and/or what is his/her background. Combining multiple types of features is, generally speaking, a better approach than working with “isolated features”. However, combining data from multiple sources is relatively hard. Over the years, the prediction of performance has seen advancements using Knowledge Tracing, Deep Learning. Deep Knowledge Tracing, more specifically, combines both DL and KT as it intends to use the Sequential Nature of RNNs into a knowledge training task (KT - does a student know a concept or not).

So, essencially, the research gap is that the models that predict student performance either focus on Knowledge Tracing - exercise features - OR behavior related features, with little work being done to combine BOTH types of Features. So, the authors suggest a model that fully utilizes both exercise and behaviour features - MFA-DKT.

The model starts by incorporating raw data into a KT model to identify a representation of the features. Then, this embedding is combined with the sequences of knowledge states to predict student performance (more details below, possibly). Benchmark results show promising signs.
Authors describe 3 different kinds of student performance prediction:
Definition of RNN can be skipped (for obvious reasons). WE can now move to sub-section C - Attention Models

Attention models draw from intuition: when presented with a lot of information, we tend not to focus on thon a certain segment. Think of it like a vector of weights that assign different importances to the elements of a sequence.
Implementation of the Attention Mechanism
The attention layer refers to the weighted sum of the different hidden student states present in the sequence.
The Multiple Feature Fusion Attention Deep Knowledge Tracing Mechanism (MFADKT)that is made out of 4 distinct modules:

The authors start by using PCA to reduce the dimensionality of the behavior features. This allows them to reduce the dimensionality of the dataset “without” going through those pesky parts of the work such as feature selection.
The embedding of the features is passed as an argument to LSTM for training. The idea is to have the LSTM predict the performance of student i on exercise j at t+1.
The prediction will be a binary label: 1 for correct answer and 0 for incorrect answer. Thus, the loss function to minimize is binary cross entropy.

Thus far, we have had a description of the overall architecture of the Multiple Feature Fusion Attention Deep Knowledge Tracing Framework. At this stage, we will move on with the description of the different experiments conducted to validate this approach:
Comparison with other popular frameworks used in the task of predicting whether the next question will be correct or not!
BKT: Uses Hidden Markov Models to predict the probability of the student’s “mastery level”.
DKT: Uses RNNs
DKT+: It takes DKT but adds a regularization term to smoothen some of the issues reportedly found in the traditional DKT approach.

DKVMN: Also DKT with a Twist. The twist here refers to the use of a static key matrix and a dynamic value matrix (essencially, the key matrix acts as an identifier “ish” while the value matrix is updated regularly.

Repeated Holdout method of sequences. When comparing the results of the MFA DKT vs the Baseline approaches such as BKT, the authors found that the traditional methods were not as effective for the proposed task. In fact, MFA DKT outperformed all other approaches across all metrics whereas BKT was consistently the worse model. Also, the performance always seems to increase the larger the training set is: as it consistently shows that 90% training size for the Repeated Holdout achieves the best scores.
Ideal number of hidden units seems to be 16.

At a second level of experiment, the authors look to analyze what is the impact of including the behaviour related features into the models used. For comparison purposes, the authors look into benchmarking MFA DKT agains DKT (which uses no features) and IRF DKT (which uses a few student features).  Again, MFA DKT which uses attention mechanisms to extract “information” about all student features seems to outperform all other models used.
Thus, integrating multiple features into a knowledge tracing model does indeed seem to contribute to better model accuracy.

Authors argue that a greater ability to identify knowledge states in students opens the door for better learning pathways that are directed towards the students “actual needs”. This could allow, for example, instructors to spend more time covering parts where students struggle more than the parts where the students seem to struggle less. As a limitation, the authors discuss that the work “ignores” the relationship between knowledge concepts when predicting student performance: meaning that prior knowledge on topic does not seem to still be fully realized on this model.

Final Overview: The paper introduces a novel way of performing knowledge tracing. The novelty aspect resides into the full incorporation of not only exercise related features, but behaviour related features as well. They for some reason use PCA to create embeddings of the features. This model seems to outperform other approaches to knowledge tracing on the ASSISTments dataset.

Authors propose SimStu, built on a Decision Transformer. SimStu is a data simulator that generaties learning behavioral data that is essencial to train ITS models. Results show that SimStu is effective at mimicking real students when it comes to action frequency distribution, thus allowing for the generation of data that could improve the efficiency of ITS training.

Authors finish by arguing that statistical techniques should be preferred for this domain due to their interpretability: EDM and LA should aim for explanation and not prediction.

Results show that recommender is able to generate more than 60% of the students’ actual decisions.

references.title: An improved genetic approach for composing optimal collaborative learning groups

annotations.text: Group formation is the task of assigning students to the best learner group in order to succeed at a collaborative learning task. The authors propose a new general method for group formation. The method mathematically formulates all requirements of the learner group formation problem and uses an improved genetic algorithm to solve it. The authors test their model and optimization using multiple contexts and grouping requirements. Simulated results show that the model is effective.
Moreover, an empyrical study on real data also shows that the groups formed using this method got better individual, group grades and satisfaction.

Authors show that model performance increases with longer datasets.

Accuracy is computed for every day of prediction. Authors make 2 different sets of comparisons:

annotations.text: There is a problem with current classifiers of performance as they often are done in a course or a small subset of courses at the end of the course. Moreover, binary classification seems to be the preferred task. Thses limitations hinder the ability to provide meaningful feedback and student support.

To address this gap, authors propose a day-wise model to predict performance called ANN-LSTM. Model was compared against a baseline RNN and baseline GRU model. Moreover, results were compared against other state-of-the-art methods. During model testing, it was found that, by the 3rd month of a course, ANN-LSTM obliterated the baselines, achieving 70% accuracy in comparison with the 53% and 56% of RNN and GRU.
In addition, the model also outperformed the state-of-the art models between 6% and 14%, showing LSTM as an effective way to model temporal relationships in the data.

references.title: Helping university students to choose elective courses by using a hybrid multi-criteria recommendation system with genetic optimization

extracted.Students: 95 Computer Science students from University of Cordoba
extracted.Data sources: Academic History and Ratings, Course and professor Metadata
extracted.Initialization Method:
Each student is technically represented by 2 vectors:

1. Historical performance in each course

2. The learners course ratings based on a questionnaire

When it comes to the content-end, there are 4 vectors created for each course: 

1. Professors (who teaches it vs who doesn’t)

2. Competences (what competences are covered vs not not covered)

3. Knowledge area - Scaler 1 to 8.

4. Frequency vectors of course contents.
extracted.Updates to Recommendations:
– Learner model updated after each session: weights on knowledge, interest, and behavior adjusted using exponential decay

– Resource similarity re-evaluated dynamically based on updated learner vector
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The authors propose a recommende system that combines CBF (course information) and CF (Learner Preferences).

The genetic algorithm regulates how these 2 are combined:

- Assigns weights do each component (CF or CBF) 

- Assigns weights to each individual vector for each component

- Similarity metrics

The genes are trained in a way that prevents the mating between “too similar” - what they call incest prevention.

Uniform Crossover

Elitism selection strategy

– Uses genetic algorithm to automatically tune feature weights across diverse criteria.

– Improves prediction accuracy and personalization versus single-method RS.

– Adaptable to various academic domains.
extracted.Limitations:
- Computationally expensive (GA convergence).– Tested on a limited dataset (≈ 500 learners).

– Requires extensive feature engineering and domain knowledge.

– Does not capture temporal evolution of student preferences.

– Cold-start still partially unresolved.

annotations.text: Recommender Systems can be used to help students select courses who are better suited for them. Paper suggests a hybrid RS that combines both Content-Based Filtering and Collaborative Filtering.  Optimization of the parameters of the Recommender System is done via a Genetic Algorithm.

An experiemntal sewtup on the university of Cordoba, a dataset with 2500 entries with 95 students and 63 courses. Results show excellent performance of recommendations in comparison to previous models. Moreover, the authors also delve into the interpretation of the most relevant criteria for course recommendation.

annotations.text: Predictive models for early identification of struggling students often overlook the reason for why a model may perform or not. To identify possible factors that influence model performance, the authors used 7 datasets from 3 institutions.

Results show that the performance of a predictive model is related with the number of significant features, the number of categories of the significant features and, more particularly, the Spearman Correlation Coefficients for the significant features.

For users, Knowledge Extraction, it takes the sequence of the courses taken and computes the average representation aacross the different dimensions.
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: - Retraining after a learner takes a course.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Dimensions of Word Embedings are set to 100.

Separate layers are creation:
- Creation of a Curse Knowledge Graph to understand course relationships

- Knowledge Background from users: Uses sequences of courses taken

Final prediction is obtained via the product bbetween user embedding and the course embeddings
extracted.Evaluation: {AUC: 0.9143 - HRL, HR@5: 0.8269 - Proposed Model, HR@10: 0.8785 - Proposed Model, NDCG@5: 0.6808 - Proposed Model, NDCG@10: 0.6973 - Proposed Model, MRR: 0.6433 - Proposed Model}
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a new N architecture that inclues different modules:

Knowledge extraction Module:  includes the representations of all courses available in the platform

User Knowledge Background: Creates a representation of the user’s knowledge based on the courses taken up to this point → uses as input the sequences of courses taken.

User Interest: Results from the product of the user’s history with the representation of each candidate course → leads to a prediction of probability of enrollment.

Model outperforms other baseliines considered for a similar task.
extracted.Limitations: - Does not consider user history beyond the sequence of courses taken.

annotations.text: MOOCs have plenty of flexibility when it comes to the learning paths they wish to pursue. This great flexibility gives plenty of options to students and current course recommenders tend to rely on previous courses to suggest good next steps. However, these recommenders do not account for student knowledge.

Authors propose a knowledge-aware recommendation that uses historical enrollments and learning feedback. The model uses a Knowledge graph to represent a learning sequence. The Learning sequence was combined with learning feedback to extract the students’ knowledge. Finally, an Attention module is used to predict the probability of enrolling in a course. Comparison with other baselines show that method can recommend MOOCs.

50 interactions seem to be enough to get a good understanding.

Recommender works by creating embeddings of the users and embeddings of the items.

Graph preprocessing connects similar items and adds exponential decay edge weights. Negative sampling is used to create Y.

User features are preprocessed for the baseline Heater model.

The work uses Tomplay dataset - an educational dataset that is sometimes used by College Professors.

The authors propose a GNN-based approach based on LightGCN., which is compared against a myriad of other common RS approaches. 

Best performance at 1 GNN layer; connecting similar items didn’t help; sensitive to γ

Item averaging may lose information (even if computationally more expedient).

Graph connections of similar items does not seem to improve recommender accuracy,

Dataset only contains positive interactions (negatives are assumed). 

Cold start module cannot learn → forward pass only

For cold-start evaluation: 20% new users with ≥325 interactions are chosen

annotations.text: Authors test effectiveness of recommender systems in educational platforms, bringing forward the example of music platforms - TomPlay - which suffer from the problem of having sparser interactions than other recommender system scenarios.

To deal with the sparsity problem, the authors propose a Deep LEarning based recommender that uses Graph Neural Networks and show that it outperforms standard NNs. Moreover, the authors  try to assert the amount of interactions required for the recommender to properly model the user interactions, concluding 50 to be the optimal number.

references.title: Process mining techniques for analysing patterns and strategies in students’ self-regulated learning

extracted.Data sources:
Think-Aloud records
Mouse and Keyboard clicks
extracted.Features: [ORIENT, SETGOAL, PLAN, SEARCH, EVALUATE, EVAL, MONITOR, READ, REPEAT, ELABORATE, ORGANIZATION, MOT, REST]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Recordings were labelled according to Bannert’s taxonomy. Each utterance was classified according to an SRL activity.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Fitness: 40% for Fuzzy Miner}
extracted.Cluster Description:
Model is capable of explaining 40% of the sequences that were actualy ovbserved in the logs. 

The model does not seem to be able to discriminate between groups, with successful students achieving a marginally better fitness than the less successful ones.
extracted.Implications:
Process Mining should not be used directly for educational purposes.

Theoretical underpinnings to proces mining are required BUT the theoretical model should also be able to match the level of granularity offered by the data.
extracted.Comments:
Authors use 38 to perform an empyrical study of SRL. 

They collect data from 38 students taking individualized sessions. During those sessions, students engage in a Think-Aloud learning process after which they are tested.

The utterances were classified according to different SRL activities and the authors made a comparison between the best performing students and worse-performing students.

In general, students who were more successful engaged more often in processes associated with SRL. 

This is followed by an application of Fuzzy Miner on the timestamped sequences of events done by the 2 groups (excluding off-topic utterances):

More successful students had a more diverse type of SRL events, with READ being connected to MONITOR and ELABORATE, with some REPEAT in the mix.

Less successful students had READ into MONITOR or READ into REPEAT.

The model is subsequently tested:

1. Create a theory model and see if there is a match using Goodness of Fit - authors find 41% fitness

2.Have no model but see if sequences exist in event trace - authors found that the queried sequences were more prevalent in successful students group.
extracted.Limitations:
Small sample size

Lack of standardized routines for PM in education

Focus on process logic, not considering elements of processing environment

Lack of theoretical underpinnings 

USe of deterministic models that cannot be subjected to statistical analysis. 

Process Mining is often not usable for statistical testing.

annotations.text: Most research of SRL focuses on the frequency of activities that hint at the existence of SRL behavior. Authors hypothesize that not only do effective SR learners engage more frequently in these activities but they also execute them in different orders.
Authors focus on implementing process mining to identify SR processes on a sample of 38 students.

There is a trend in SRL that says that focus of tracing these attributes should be events and not necessarily skills or aptitutes. This change in paradigm is observable, at least partly, in the way that behavioral research based on e.g. activity logs is surpassing questionnaire-based research.
The authors intend to follow on this perspective to introduce Process Mining as a potencial avenue for SRL research on a type of data that is usually seen in this field - think aloud data.

Learning requires regulatory activities such as orientation, planning and controlling. Effective learners exhibit all of these traits. SRL is often represented by theoretical models that intrinsically have all of these activities being executed in some sequence.

Authors draw from SRL research that looks at it as a sequence of different activities. Most models make reference to the existance of a sequence but often do not explicitly mention an appropriate order. Most of the research shows that the sequence of the activities may change depending on the student, the task. However, from SRL theory, the authors assume that more metacognitive behavior usually translates into better learning outcomes.

There are some commonalities between SRL works that support that there is indeed a relationship between activities and better learning outcomes but generalization is dangerous for multiple reasons. Different data sources, learning settings, tasks put a dent into the comparison of different studies. Process Mining can, thus, not only be interesting to compare differences in frequency of learning activities but to develop theory on SRL or CSCL.

One interesting observation is that the authors argue that different students have different patterns of organization and, for that reason, analysis should be done to student subgroups. The expectation of the authors using this Process Mining approach is that the process patterns of students match the patterns described in the SRL literature.

These methods are generally used for sequential and temporal analysis. When it comes to education, the methods employed tend to treat time as an event ontology rather than a continuous variable. However, the granularity level of process models tends to be overlooked.

In SRL research, sequences of events describe a process of self-regulation. Process mining, despite not being born in psychology, is viable because behavioral SRL measures can be conceptualized as measurable by event logs.
Models generally used in PM include Petri Nets or Finite State Machines. Petri Nets describe a directed graph with Places (events), Transitions.

In the figure, Places (events) are described by the circles and transitions by the squares. Starting at the black dot, the transition A is fired. A transition can only be fired (triggered) if all preceding nodes are tokenized. In this case, transitions B and C need to occur before transition D can be initiated. The parallelization means that both B or C need to occur after A and before D, but the order between B and C is irrelevant.

Petri Net representations can be used to determine if a sequence of activities matches a theoretical model or not. Another interesting application is to simulate behavior. Process mining should not be confounded with sequence mining, sequential pattern analysis or lag-sequential analysis. The events and processes in PM exist formally. → Meaning that there is an implicit assumption that the sequence of events is governed by a process and thus describes a process model. In the context of SRL this process can be understood as a learning strategy.

In other words, a learning strategy involves a sequential set of steps that need to be followed. The actions undertaken by a student following a strategy can be understood as a sequence of events undertaken to fulfill the strategy. The strategy is, thus, a process model.

Since educational process models are characterized by discrete events, they are deterministic (not stochastic). This means that, for theses models, there is no statistical testing → Transitions between states occur or do not occur → there is no probability.
The approach of Process Mining will test theoretical assumptions of SRL using Process Mining. The study will follow 2 stages:
Participants

38 undergraduate students in psychology participated in individual learning sessions of 1h30 mins. The sessions encouraged the use of think aloud methods and to employ it during 3 to 5 search tasks. Total time dedicated to learning was 30 minutes. All actions on screen, mouse and keyboard and think aloud recordings were used.
The students were tested afterward using paper-pencil-tests.
Learning Materials

Students had a controlled hypermedia space where students were allowed to search for information. The learning outcomes were measured as part of Bloom’s Taxonomy. The verbal protocols were categorized under a theoretical framework developed by the authors. Each part of think aloud mentions was associated with a category of SRL: metacognition, congnition, motivation, goal orientation, etc…
All transcripts were assessed by 2 trained raters (with first rater having preference in case of tie) - rating for each student took roughly 6h.

A statistical analysis of the utterances made by the students during their learning time was performed. On average, there were 143 events, with 65 being metacognitive, 57 cognitive, 1.4 motivational and 20 off-topic utterances.

As previously mentioned, the authors decided to not make a fully aggregate analysis on all 38 participants and, instead, opted to create sub-groups between successful and unsuccessful students based on post-test scores.

In truth, the most successful students did in fact show, on the test that they had much better scores on knowledge of basic terms, comprehension of basic concepts and ability to transfer knowledge to new problems.

In general, when it comes to the activities themselves, the authors identified that the most successful students engaged more often in metacognitive processes than the least successful ones (even though, to some extent, all students engaged on the processes here and there. More surprisingly, the authors found that motivation was the field with the largest relative difference between the 2 groups.
Process mining of events

While the statistical results already showed some differences in usage of SRL processes (with the more successful students engaging more often). The authors use the Fuzzy Miner algorithm to create a transition diagram for each of the groups.
Fuzzy Miner allows to cluster events into larger units based on the needs of the analyst.  The models for the most successful and the least successful students are presented below:
The algorithm has nodes (events) and edges (transitions). The scores are computed according the relative importance and order of all events in the sequences. Fuzzy Miner uses 2 metrics:

Aanalysis was conducted by using the following parameter settings: edge filtering was set with the edge cutoff=0.2 and the utility ratio=0.75; the significance cutoff of node filter was set to 0.25. Some processes were not considered for the model due to not reaching the 0.25 significance cutoff point (meaning that process model was simplified for interpretability purposes).

The more successful students had 8 event categories and the less successful ones had 5 events with 2 clusters. Successful students had a clear relationship between READING with MONITOR and ELABORATE. Unsucessful students had READ into REPEAT or MONITOR as the most common sequences.
Thus, the authors conclude that successful students engage in high frequency of preparing activities that are subsequently monitored. The model is something akin to Zimmerman’s SRL model.

For less successful students, however, there is less preparation that is often connected to reading activities which are followed by repeats. Less successful students do not engage in deep learning approaches and often engage in surface level activities (repetition).
While these differences exist, at this stage, there is no statistical analysis done. To do that the authors follow-up with model testing in 2 paradigms:
The theoretical model assumes the ideal sequence of SRL learning activities: Analyze task, Search, Read, Process or Repeat, Monitor progress, evaluate and either stop or repeat the cycle.

In the partial way of monitoring, the authors looked at the created groups and identified how often did specific sequences of events occur. Unsurprisingly, the types of sequences expected of SRL behavior were more present in successful students.

Successful student sequences of events decently map into existing theoretical frameworks of ideal SRL models. Unsuccessful students often miss the engagement with deep learning approaches. 

The authors also identify some important limitations that warrant future work:

references.title: An optimized group formation scheme to promote collaborative problem-based learning

annotations.text: The fitness functions used in Genetic Algorithms for group formation may have, as unintended consequence, the creation of imbalanced groups. The authors propose a new Genetic Algorithm formation that looks for homogeneous social interactions despite heteregenous knowledge-levels. The authors compare the effectiveness of their approach against self-selection and random group formation.
Tests on 6th level students show that the approach outperforms random in terms of scoring and is significantly superior in terms of group interactions

references.title: Modeling Student Performance using Feature Crosses Information for Knowledge Tracing

extracted.Students: 3852 learners in ASSISTments2009, 27485 learners in ASSISTments2012, 5000 EdNet learners
extracted.Courses: Each dataset refers to its own context
extracted.Features: [Question Embeddings, Concept Embeddings, Difficulty Embeddings]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
When it comes to Graph models for KT, GKT does not select the most suitable graphs to be used for a KT model whilst others do not establish relationships between questions and knowledge concepts. 

Moreoverr, other models (CoKT) use far too simplistic measures of similarity,
extracted.Novelty of Model: The authors introduce Feature Crosses - essencially the creation of non-linear higher-order featurescreated by combining 2 or more features of the original feature space.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The authors create an Multi-Relational Heterogenous Graph establishing relationships between learners, questions and concepts. 

Difficulty of questions is defined from the ratio between incorrect responses and total responses to a question.

The original embeddings are converted into a different set of embeddings from the product between the original forms of embeddings at the feature cross module - mapping the feature space into a Hilberrt space. 

From the Feature Cross Module, the authors feed it into the Knowledge State Evolution Module where the knowledge state of the student is created and updated before going into the prediction.

Embedding size was set to 100.
extracted.Models: BKT, KTM, DKVMN, DKT, DKT+, GIKT, GAKT, CoKT, FCIKT
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a new model that includes Feature Crosses to change the feature space before using that space as input for the knowledge state estimation.

The model outperforms all baselines. Moreoverr, the authors introduce an Ablation Study that evaluates the performance of the KT model while testing the removal of different elements: results show that all elements are important for prediction, with the feature crosses being more important than multihead attention.

Additionally, the authors also did a sensitivity analysis, a case study, and a visual analysis of the latent concepts.
extracted.Limitations: - Holdout Method

annotations.text: Recent years have seen the proliferation of Deep Learning to address Knowledge Tracing problems. However, these tend to oversimplify exercise records into knowledge sequences which leaves behind the importance of individual questions. Moreover, the models focus on question-right vs question wrong and don’t tend to worry whether the learner actually knows the concepts involved in the question.

To address the issue, authors propose a new method FCIKT that attempts to make a more complete representation of these phenomena. The main idea is to use a fusion module to cross features and organize them with graph convolutional networks. The model also incorporates attention mechanisms and GRU to dinamically capture and update student knowledge state before the final prediction.
Experiments demonstrate that the approach “works” and is interpretable.

annotations.text: E-learning has made it possible to democratize education across multiple learners with different knowledge, goals and abilities. However, adopting one-size fits all solutions to learners in this context is also challenging, which is an argument for Recommender Systems. The goal of the work is to propose a transductive support vector regression hybrid recommender system.

The paper introduces a set of optimization techniques at multiple stages of the RS: one-source denoising, modified anarchy society optimization, sequential pattern strategy. All in all, the data leads habits and interest, later used to make recomnnedations to learners. Authors eprform extensive validation on public datasets (including OULAD). Overall, system achieves 82 to 98% accuracy and 5% to 19.2% MAE.

references.title: Identification, Exploration, and Remediation: Can Teachers Predict Common Wrong Answers?

annotations.text: Students engage in faulty learning processes all the time and it is very rare that they are “special”. So, it is possible for experienced professors to identify common wrong answers and common faulty learning processes that lead to the CWA.
Thus, the authors propose a study with 3 objectives:
Authors more of an econometrics focused approach to measure the effects.

annotations.text: The authors look to explore how SRL can lead to performance by having 140 online students and 466 b-learning students fill the MSLQ. The results show that B-Learning students engage more in help-seeking and peer-learning, while online students engage more in SRL behavior on the remaining constructs.
Moreover, the authors found that, whether online or in B-Learning, there were no significant differences when it comes to the predictive power of different SRL constructs.
