references.title: State of the art and practice in AI in education

annotations.text: There are problems with the current understanding of AI in education. This may be due to lack of technical knowledge or just a strict look at what education is. Authors develop a typology of Ai in education and learning (AIED) to describe the 2022 state of the art of how AI is used in education.

The proeminence of artificial intelligence (AI) is ever increasing but there is plenty of misinformation surrounding it. There have been many advancements when it comes to the attention that AI has received over the years, not only from the perspective of policy and regulations, but specific applications as is the case with Ai in education - both in the perspective of using it for teaching or learning, but AI literacy in general. In the context of education, it has been argued that AI can not only automate teacher tasks, but augment human cognition as well.

AI in education has been mostly pushed by more technical focused researchers but, more recently, has become more and more mainstream, and there is plenty of reason to be skeptical about its repercussions for practice. One such example is Intelligent tutoring systems (ITS - which are systems that are mainly focused on automated feedback and personalized learning experiences). AI systems in education operate under the assumption that learning comes from the development of problem solving capacity leveraging the efficiency of the human mind. Problem solving itself can be boiled down to finding a chain of heuristics that minimize the distance between expected result and known solutions - understand the problem, plan, execute the plan, look back and reflect on progress.

Historically, AIED focused on student comes from automated feedback systems on multiple choice questions - since there is an obvious right answer, setting the heuristics is relatively simple - allowing for immediate feedback to the student and, more importantly, opening the door to automate the correction of grading events. The evolution of ITS encompassed the development of other tools: Skinner (1950’s created a teaching machine that required students to build their own answers, thus acting more like a private tutor. Pask developed a system to train keyboard operators that gave them tasks based on previous performance. The first real explicit application of computer-aided instruction comes from a PhD thesis by Carbonell in 1970 who generated responses to student statements by looking at the semantic relationships between the response and a network of concepts that it was trained on.

Nowadays, AIED in the context of teaching is now spread throughout multiple different disciplines: collaborative learning or LA and EDM. Moreover, there is even potential for the use of AI for administrative or teacher support purposes, opening the door for new pedagogical approaches.
So, what are the motivations of the usage AI?

One motivation may be in promoting learning gains in specific knowledge domains independently of who is the human teacher. For example, it is possible to assess the student before he came into contact with a topic, teach it and assess the progress afterwards - which is especially useful when the knowledge that needs to be acquired is pre-defined. Common ITS focus on test preparation. Acquisition of pre-defined knowledge is, in fact, the most focused function of AIED, with socialization and individuation receiving less attention.

Bloom’s (1968) Mastery Learning is a key pedagogical model for student-focused AIED. Students start with different prior knowledge and capabilities, so they would need different amounts of instruction to achieve the same level of mastery. AIED systems like ITS are meant to provide this personalized component to bring the student to expected level of mastery. Using the mastery learning model, Bloom found that students receiving personalized feedback had almost 2 standard deviations of increased learning gains relative to whole-class teaching. As human instruction tends to result in below 1 standard deviation differences.

The effectiveness of ITS is a hotely debated topic in AIED research. Most work shows small to moderate learning gains in relatively small settings. Also, effectiveness  of ITS has also been tied to the prior knowledge of the student. Empyrical evidence from the US Department of Education shows that one the best known ITS (Cognitive Tutor) has translated, at the group level, in arguably mixed or even minor learning gains. However, there is a lot of recognized potential for AIED:
The authors work intends to look at the history of AIED in order to facilitate the undertstanding of what potential avenues appear next.

From an academic perspective, AI has no universally accepted definition and definitions tend to be context dependent. The political understanding of AI focuses on products accessible to the masses. The UNICEF’s definition of AI refers to them as machine based systems that can make decisions, predictions or reccomendations that influence real or virual environments and show an ability to adapt their behaviour from context. A key aspect in this definition stems from the fact the objectives of AI are human defined. This definition, altough brief, encompasses the current knowledge of AI and AI based systems.
For educators, it is important to know 2 distinct ways of producing AI:

Data-driven AI looks at very large amounts of data and uses them for optimization of a specific criterion. Training becomes optimizing a set of parameters or functions for something. In data-driven AI the optization is done using Calculus - the goal being to find the set of parameters that would minimize some loss function. Data-driven AI systems tend to vary on their optimization function and the amount of parameters that we need to optimize.

The authors follow-up with a brief overview of neural netwoeks and how they work in different applications. It is important, however to note that a human is always behind setting the objective: In supervised learning, what an “accurate prediction” is comes down to the creator of the AI system. It follows up on what has led to such overwhelming advancements in the AI field:
This trial-and-error approach has been labelled as inneficient and energy intensive - a reason why the authors argue that energy consumption is a major roadblock facing the field of data-driven AI.

In essence, data-driven AI has seen a lot of sucess due to external factors that have allowed it be successful. However, these AI systems seem to be very inefficient and, at the end of the day, do not seem to actually represent actual knowledge or reasoning.

However, there are other relevant fields of AI such as symbolic or knowledge-based AI. These systems are created in order to attempt to mirror what could be called “expert-decision making”. These systems tend to be less generalizable for multiple domains, especially in comparison with data-driven AI.

Knowledge-based systems update use an inference engine to create a model - these models are more heuristics-based, meaning that different algorithms use different criteria to make a decision. However, these models tend to mirror what is known about the knowledge-domain in question. Ultimately, these systems can be understood as a set of sequences of if-then rules.
The authors classify AI systems into 3 distinct AI categories in education:
Tools that have either been developed or repurposed to assist students throughout their learning experience.
Interfaces developed to assist professors understand the progress of their learning: the knowledge they have acquired and their misconceptions.
Tools that are focused on allocating resources and funding at an institution wide level or in mere administrative tasks.

Intelligent tutoring systems offer step-by-step tutorials in structured subjects as is the case mathematics that are “personalized” for each student. In short, as students engage with the system by clicking and participating in the contents, the system creates a personalized pathway that aims to address the difficulties and misconceptions revealed by the student.

Apps that help students automate learning tasks and, in some cases, their own homeworks. These include, but are not limited to apps that are AI-assisted language translation tools or AI-assisted mathematics apps.

Augmented reality experiences that are meant to give students the contact with quasi-realistic scenarios. One example is using AR to train neurosurgical residents in neurosurgical procedures. I have also attended on lecture displaying its use to simulate interviews.

Apps that have either been developed to assist learners with disabilities or have been repurposed for that effect: example, automatic generation of sign language for students with hearing difficulties.

Large language models that generate texts that have human-like syntax. Chat-GPT and other methods have been made available and represent a great concern into how to assess students in the future. The results on whether it helps students or it harms them is still unclear and there will, for sure, be plenty of research as this is a very hot topic in education.

Another application of large language models. Many currently available chatbots handle standard administrative or logistical questions that can be automated. One of the most commo known applications is Georgia Tech’s AI virtual assistant who acted as a human teacher in answering questions about the course and its contents.

Apps that use NLP to analyse long-form student answers and provide personalized feedback. Due to the natural difficulty associated with processing text, there still are few commercially available tools that allow for this. There are, currently, automated AFA autograders that analyze student answers in coding exercises. Surprisingly, feedback from the machine has been received more positively than the feedback provided by a human professor.

AI systems that connect people engaged in Education. There is a system called Open Tutor that students can use: by providing a prompt on materials where the students have faced difficulties, the app can connect them to a tutor who can help them.

Systems that promote learning not by providing answers to the student’s questions but rather by asking new questions in order to allow them to get to the correct answer themselves. Thus, these systems simulate the type of interaction that would occur between a student and a human tutor - acting as facilitators instead of mere sages.

While ITS and DBTS offer a step-by-step approach, ELE encourage students to explore and manipulate different elements of the learning environment. Exploratory learning is controversial in the literature however, with some authors arguing that the lack of direction may lead to poor learning outcomes due to cognitive overload.

These systems have been argued to be potentially very helpful as apps that assist students throughout their learning journey. However, no research has been dedicated to this sort of applications even with the rise in popularity of concepts like the metaverse or digital twins.
Systems that are meant to measure the similarity between a deliverable and other resources that are available. Turnitin being the most popular one.
Tools that are meant to find and scrape resources online in response to teacher queries.

Some applications have been developed in order to identify whether students are paying attention or not. For example, there is evidence of video recordings being used to monitor where students are looking at. Other applications include the use of electroencephalography headsets to record brain activity, thus monitoring attention. Another application uses the school’s app to monitor where students are and spend time while on campus.

Autograders are a very popular area of research and there are multiple options currently available. Usually their application has been found to achieve great results in written tasks in very specific fields such as computer science or mathematics. While the efficacy of autograders has seen great developments over the years, it is still controversial and there is and its use in high-stakes testing poses a significant high-risk use case.

AI-powered tools that are meant to take over teaching tasks. This can be either via the use of LLMs or chatbots that take student questions, thus giving time to educators for other tasks even if, arguably, it limits the role of the professor. Professors can also use these tools to develop their own knowledge.

An AI tool that helps students manage their time and their attention while in the classroom. For example, within a context of TBL, the system can identify which groups would benefit the most from receiving a visit of the professor. Still very experimental with little research,

AI system applications that are used to determine whether a student should be admitted at the institution or to a specific program. There was an attempt to use a system called GRADE at the university of Austin-Texas. However, the program was discontinued because it replicated the same issues it was meant to address (probably because the training data incorporated those biases).

Systems that use AI to ensure that academic integrity is mantained on remote learning environments. Students are monitored using cameras and microphones whilr completing a task or examination. The authors consider e-proctoring as one case where AI has been used to promote poor pedagogic practices.

Teaching consists of lectures, exercises, examinations and tutoring an all of these tasks are time consuming and can be automated using AI. Personalized learning is the most likely another relevant path for AI.
To be successful, personalized learning runs into multile ethical issues that ae often overlooked by AIED advocates. Thus, the authors focus on the ptential roadblocks to AIED:

There are close to 80 sets of ethical AI principles, usually centered around a human rights perspective. There are some works that focus on ethics in AI use in education, something that heavily contrasts with ethics in Learning Analytics. A council of Europe report notes some key relevant concerns:

The authors argue that AI research should not only focus on Data or data analysis techniques but also have a pedagogical perspective, thus arguing for human development as a starting point for an adequate ethical framework.

While it has been discontinued for some time, the emergence of recommender systems has brought forward the implementation of personalized learning in education. Few AIED applications actually seem to make an effort to create actual custom pathways for students and, instead, seem to point to some homegeneization of the students.

Current evidence by some meta analysis claims that AI systems seem to overperform human teachers in large classes while, simultaneously, tend to underperform human tutors in one on one scenarios. However, most of these studies have been performed by the developers of the AI system on limited numbers of students.

Also, most works focus on practical results instead of answering fundamental questions such as “is AI needed in education”. Also, AIED research also needs to focus on measuring its impact to cognition and brain development.

It has been argued that AI can address issues with accessibility for those who do not have access to qualified teachers, especially in developing countries. However, many of these areas lack the most basic infrasctructure and resources that would even allow for that to take effect.

AI research is mostly conducted in the Global north using datasets built from people in the these developed countries, which exacerbates current biases and does not account for meaningful cultural differences outside of these areas. This may involve, but is not limited to: languages used, pedagogical practices. Ultimately, tools that are more advanced by more developed nations will end up crowding out local solutions.

references.title: Advanced Deep Learning Model for Predicting the Academic Performances of Students in Educational Institutions

extracted.Features: [Exam, IN_Sem1, IN_Sem2, IN_Sem3, IN_Sem4, IN_Sem5, IN_Sem6, IN_Sem6, InPc]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors use a model based on GRU. The data is modeled as a having multiple features.

As a start, the authors start with the creation of a fully connected layer which is then given as argument to the GRU, whose outputs are subsequently given to a MaxPooling layer followed by a Dense Layer which does the predictions.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.997 - GRU_Based, Precision: 0.986 - GRU_Based, Recall: 0.963 - GRU_Based, F1-Score: 0.974- GRU_Based}
extracted.Comments:
This paper is not very good. IT presents multiple imprecisions when it comes to techniques (AdaBoost is presented as standard Bagging) and descriptions (k-Fold of 1). 

Authors create a GRU-based model and do benchmarking.
extracted.Limitations:
- Technical errors on description of some of the models (e.g. AdaBoost)

- K-Fold of 1?

- No Ablation study

- Limited exploration of models found in the literature.

annotations.text: Paper focuses on Deep Learning to develop recommender systems of new content. The paper introduces a new model to detect students at risk using GRU. Model was assessed on dataset featuring 16000 records of student assessments from various institutions.

The system was compared against other deep learning based recomender systems. Its unclear to me what is being recommmended though. High accuracy of the model signals the importance of these models in addressing underachievement and potential dropout.

references.title: A Word Embeddings Based Clustering Approach for Collaborative Learning Group Formation

annotations.text: The benefits of collaborative learning have been previously shown both theoretically and empirically. The work wishes to propose a novel way of forming groups via pre-trained word embeddings. The implementation can account for homogenization or heterogenization constraints. Evaluation is done using a dataset of a course in programming.

references.title: Development of an early warning system for higher education institutions by predicting first year student academic performance

extracted.Courses: All first-year courses at a Turkish university
extracted.Features: [Academic Year, Gender, TR citizenship, Start academic year, Registration academic year, Birthdate, Father educational status, Mother educational status, Scholarship rate, Course registration status, Secondary education score, Prep school status, Prep school leave type, Prer school grade, Prep school grade, Prep school absence, Prerp school starting level, Prep school level repeat, Lateral transfer, SIS login activities, Disciplinary punishment, Disciplinary status report requests, Transcript document, Fall semester CGPA, Last spring semester GPA, Fall semester gained credits, Fall semester attempted credits, Transferred credits, Period of matriculation, Course registration date, Fall semester selected courses, Spring semester selected courses, Conflicting classes info, Summer school info, Placement preference order, Number of students in programme, Number of students in faculty/school, KYK scholarship, Placement score, Placement score category, High school type, High school category, Region of the high school, High school name, City of the high school, High school diploma grade, Course instructor info, Program campus info, Planned campus info, Fall semester weekly class hours, Spring semester weekly class hours, Fall semester weekly online class hours, Spring semester weekly online class hours, Number of frozen semesters, University registration type, Registration semester, Programme name, Department name, Faculty/school name, Medium of instruction, Faculty/school type]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors perform some feature engineering based on the original set of features. 

Authors do parameter optimization but do not specify how they reach the best set of hyperparameters. 

Authors test models by including (or not) the fall semester GPA as part of the models.
extracted.Models: Random Forest, Decision Tree, Naive Bayes
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.902 - RF with FallGPA, Precision: 0.927 - RF with FallGPA}
extracted.Comments:
Authors perform a simple classification pipeline at the halfway point of the academic year to predict CGPA in a classification setting. 

Results show that including performance of the first semester greatly increases the model’s predictive capabilities.
extracted.Limitations:
Minimal Preprocessing executed, 

Small number of algorithms tested

Holdout method

Unclear Hyper-parameter optimization

Single institution data

annotations.text: Authors propose an early warning system that identifies academic success to first-year higher education students. Dataset includes close to 12000 students and 69 features that predict academic success at the end of the first year.  Authors use NB, RF and DT, with RF reaching 90% accuracy.

Findings show that including grade of the first semester severely increases performance of the model. Program name and university placement score also contribute to the system. Model is meant to be used as early as the second week of the spring semester.

references.title: Predicting at-risk students in the early stage of a blended learning course via machine learning using limited data

extracted.Courses: 1 Spring Semester Course and a 1 Fall Semester Course
extracted.Features: [early_learning, study_in_time, catch_up, cramming, study_again, #videos_watched, #quizzes_taken, #audio_recordings, #listener_to_native_speaker, #listener_to_recorded_audio, #learning_sessions, duration_of_engagement]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors draw from SRL models to create 5 of the variables used. 

Authors use SMOTE. Data is always Aggregated from moment 1 to moment 7.
extracted.Comments:
Authors do 2 different works:

1 - A Benchmark study which, across different periods does a holdout separation using the entire sample. 

2 - A prospective study which usus the 2018 version of the course as trainig and the 2019 as test data.

The comparison between RF-Benchmark and RF-Prospective shows that performance is better across the board. Authors use SHAP to understand the importance of the features and find study in time as being particularly relevant.
extracted.Limitations:
- Small sample size

- Does not consider other contexts - too course specific

- Only uses RF

- Does not consider additional data sources

annotations.text: Work tries to dientify students at risk in a blended learning course with limited data. The authors compare 2 approaches:
The authors extract and categorize multiple motivational variables.
Authors found prospective study to be in line with the benchmark study. SHAP emphaiszes importance of time management variables for early identification of students at risk.

references.title: Exercise recommendation based on knowledge concept prediction

extracted.Students: 4217 ASSISTments09 learners, 574 Algebra 2005 learners, 335 OLIES students
extracted.Initialization Method:
Learner knowledge is initialized via weighted-average of first-attempt scores on each concept.

Exercise profiles are created from difficulty and concept coverage
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Learner Mastery is updated after each attempt.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors create a concept-exercise matrix that highlights the concepts covered by each exercise.

Student scores on each exercise are normalized
extracted.Comments:
Authors use Knowledge Tracing - and more specifically DKT to understand a learners’ mastery level of different concepts.

Then, the authors compute similarity between exercises the suggest exercises that are in an approppriate difficulty for the knowledge concept.
extracted.Limitations:
- Cognitive-level estimation estimation depends on manual tagging of exercises.

- Does not use text of exercise to estimate difficulty (or correctedness).

annotations.text: Exercise recommendations can make or break learning. To be effective, an exercise needs to be challenging enough to foster interest while, simultaneously, being accessible. Collaborative Filtering is not effective at this since it recommends exercises based on past behavior of similar students.

Authors propose a model that uses DKT to predict a learner’s mastery level and uses this prediction as a form to filter exercises eligible for recommendation. Results show that model does better than baseline approaches both in terms of difficulty of exercises but also diversity and novelty.

references.title: Improving Learning Environments: Avoiding Stupidity Perspective

annotations.text: The authors make an interest argument when it comes to recommender systems: its better for the model to not make stupid recommendations. Authors thus, propose what is stupidity and what data-driven techniques may exist to avoid it.

references.title: Utilizing Student Time Series Behaviour in Learning Management Systems for Early Prediction of Course Performance

extracted.Target: Good vs Poor performance (Poor = C+ or below; Good = above B-)
extracted.Students: Education course: 141 students in semester 1 and 527 students in semester 2; Follow-up biology course: 290 students in semester 1 and 311 students in semester 2
extracted.Courses: 1 mandatory undergraduate education course; follow-up in 1 mandatory introductory biology course
extracted.Features:
Main deep-learning input: daily click frequencies over the first 28, 42, 56, or 70 days. 

For conventional ML baselines, 21 aggregated LMS features were extracted.
extracted.Moment of Prediction: Early prediction at multiple time points: first 28, 42, 56, and 70 days of the course
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Data from semester 2 of the education course were split into training/validation (72%, n = 407) and test (28%, n = 120); semester 1 was used as an external/generalizability test. Class imbalance was handled with SMOTE on training data because very few students actually failed. Missing values were imputed with kNN imputation. Aggregated ML features were standardized (mean 0, SD 1). Feature selection for conventional ML used RFE with Random Forest, resulting in 7 selected features.
extracted.Models: LSTM,MLP, Logistic Regression, Naïve Bayes, Gradient Boosting Machine, SVM, Decision Tree, kNN, Random Forest
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model:
Main study (education course): {Validation AUC: 75.2% – LSTM with first 56 days; Test AUC Semester 1: 68.2% – LSTM with first 42 days; Test AUC Semester 2: 68.2% – LSTM with first 42 days}. 

Best conventional ML baseline reached 66.2% AUC (Naïve Bayes with selected features, validation; also 66.2% on Semester 2 test for selected features). 

Follow-up biology course: LSTM achieved up to 83.0% validation AUC and about 78.7% test AUC on semester 1.
extracted.Comments: The paper’s main contribution is methodological: showing that LSTM on simple daily click-frequency time series can outperform conventional ML models using engineered aggregated LMS features, while also showing somewhat stronger generalizability across semesters and course types. The most influential baseline features were mostly frequency-related rather than time-related, supporting the choice of click frequencies as the core representation. A follow-up study in a biology course further supports generalizability.
extracted.Limitations:
Sample sizes are still relatively small for deep learning. 

The definition of “at-risk” is somewhat arbitrary because actual failures were rare, so students with C+ or below were treated as poor performers. 

Class imbalance required SMOTE, which may introduce bias. 

Generalizability across semesters was only moderate, and the approach was tested in only two institutional course contexts.

annotations.text: Most attempts that look to use time-series representations to predict student performance seem to underwhelm. Thus, the authors propose a method to use daily-click counts to train a LSTM model to predict end-of-course performance. Comparison shows that the approach outperforms traditional approaches.

references.title: An ontology engineering approach to the realization of theory-driven group formation
references.journal: International Journal of Computer-Supported Collaborative Learning

annotations.text: Team formation is one of the most important steps in successful collaborative learning activities. However, it is difficult for a multitude of reasons: talent, knowledge, interactions, etc… The main goal of the paper is to create a theory-driven framework to group formation.
Authors validate their approach on a sample of 20 students and four instructors.

references.title: Towards Explainable Group Formation by Knowledge Map based Genetic Algorithm

annotations.text: The black-box nature of ML is still a barrier to acceptance of its use in educational practices. The authors propose a method of group formation that uses course knowledge structures, reading behvaiour and assessment analysis for group formation. Optimization for both homogeneity and heterogeneity is attempted.  Case study on high-school data show that the decisions of the algorithm are explainable, which helps garner trust for these models.

references.title: A novel justice-based linear model for optimal learner group formation in computer-supported collaborative learning environments

annotations.text: Previous attempts at finding optimal groups have drawbacks:

Authors propose a binary integer approach that can be used in the construction of a linear model. On a “second experiment”, the authors create a new construct that quyantifies justice. The experiments were conducted with 35 undergraduate students.

Authors evaluate their approach on 4 indicators: time, mean deviation from optimal, mean student satisfaction and mean acquired knowledge. Method was not as fast as Rules-based solutions, but did achieve an optimum quite fast.

references.title: Transfer Learning from Deep Neural Networks for Predicting Student Performance

extracted.Students: Five course datasets; course sizes ranged from 117 to 282 students depending on course offering
extracted.Courses: 5 compulsory undergraduate courses from 2 programs: Physical Chemistry I (Spring 2018), Physics III (Spring 2018 and Spring 2019), Analytical Chemistry Lab (Spring 2018 and Spring 2019)
extracted.Features: [forum views, page views, resource views, folder views, URL views, assignment views, submitted assignment grades, total course views, total course activity, gender]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: For each unique pair of courses, datasets were rebuilt to share a common set of features. Features of paired courses were matched one by one by type; when a matching feature did not exist, an empty zero-valued feature was created. A four-layer dense neural network was used. Baseline models were trained from scratch for 150 epochs with 10-fold cross-validation. Transfer learning initialized the target-course model with source-course weights and then fine-tuned it for 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 100, or 150 epochs.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: transfer learning outperformed baseline in 18/20 course pairs after 100 epochs and in 16/20 pairs after 10 epochs; improvement significant for epochs 10–150 (best p = 0.0002 at 100 epochs)}.
extracted.Comments: Main contribution is showing that a deep model trained on one course can be repurposed for a related course, often improving predictive accuracy and reducing training effort. The paper is especially useful for settings where data for a target course are limited but related past-course data exist. The study also shows that transferability is not guaranteed in every case and depends on course relatedness.
extracted.Limitations:
Very small number of courses. Courses came from only two departments and five offerings, limiting generalizability.

 The study focuses on related compulsory courses with manually engineered feature alignment, so portability to more heterogeneous courses is uncertain. It is not an explicitly early-warning design, since prediction is based on course-level semester data rather than intermediate checkpoints. 

There was also at least one non-improving transfer case, showing transfer learning is not universally beneficial.

annotations.text: Transfer Learning refers to the process of taking a model trained in one context and applying it to another. Authors attempt to perform transfer learning on educational data and thus obtain data from 5 compulsory courses of two undergraduate programs.
Results show that it is possible to use this approach. However, it requires datasets of students who have attended other related courses.

references.title: A Fair Clustering Approach to Self-Regulated Learning Behaviors in a Virtual Learning Environment

annotations.text: In K-12 there are common applications of VLE’s for classroom interaction. However, the greater success, even at this age, of using a VLE is often determined byu the learner’s SRL skills. And, while clustering techniques have been used to uncover different SRL constructs, little attention is given to algorithmic bias or fairness.
Authors use what is called Fair Capacitated Clustering on data from 14251 learners in K-12. Results show that 3 clusters appear for high-performing students:
Moreover, 3 other clusters appear for low-performing students:

references.title: An e-learning recommendation approach based on the self-organization of learning resource

extracted.Courses: 1 Visual Basic Course (4 classes) and 1 C Programming Course
extracted.Data sources: Survey for learning styles, LMS for interactions, expert suggestions for LO initialization
extracted.Initialization Method: Learning Objects initialized with expert suggestions and feedback from previous learners. Initial recommendations based on learning style and preferences
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Self-organization of LOs based on learner interactions, with active and passive preference updates
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Each LO is represented by a set of Learning attributes (basic attributes and extended metadata)

Learners are represented by a tuple of 3 attributes:
- Learning goals
- Learning Styles
Behaviours 

Each attribute is represented by multiple sub-attributes.
extracted.Recommendation types: Top-N recommendations based on learner-LO similarity and LO self-organization
extracted.Evaluation:
{Effectiveness and Efficiency

- Effectiveness: Learning Gains increases

- Efficiency: Time spent learning and utilization

2. Personalization:
- Fitness function

-  Hit Rate

- Marking of LOs

3. Diversity
- Diversity in TopN
- Novelty

4. Learner satisfaction with approach

The algorithm outperforms the baselines on all these metrics.
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a recommender system that uses self-organization of the LOs:

Learners are initializd via a survey and LOs are initialized based on pedagogical recommendations.

As learners interact with the system, the LOs are being adjusted to match the user’s learning style and goals. The process repeats itself until the Learner goals are achieved (flagged by learner). If a recommendation is not useful, the learner can Mark it as well.

Proposed approach is compared against 3 other recommenders and outperforms them on all metrics: students learn more, spend less time on the recommender and get a more personalized and diverse set of recommendations. Moreover, users also report higher satisfaction levels.  

Quality of recommendations is done using 3 main grand criteria, which are subdivided:

1. Effectiveness and Efficiency

- Effectiveness: Learning Gains increases

- Efficiency: Time spent learning and utilization

2. Personalization:
- Fitness function

-  Hit Rate

- Marking of LOs

3. Diversity
- Diversity in TopN
- Novelty

4. Learner satisfaction with approach

The algorithm outperforms the baselines on all these metrics
extracted.Limitations:
- Self-organization only on LOs

- Requres validation from other Datasets

- SOM are Computationally expensive (even though better than Genetic Algorithms or Markov Chains)

annotations.text: Most content-based recommender systems are not effective at modelling student, which hinders their ability to make an approappriate dynamic mapping between student to Learning Object. In this paper, the authors now model Learning Objects (LO) as “intelligent” entities with the capacity to self-organize. The idea is that this recommender is an improvement over previous recommenders as it now better adapts to the user’s updated knowledge after reviewing the remaining content.

E-learning recommender systems attempt to provide a learning pathway towards the acquisition of a new skill. However, these systems often lead to overspecialization and information overload which hinders learner’s abilities to actually develop (the so-called Tutorial Hell). Also, the systems fail to account for changes in learners. Authors propose improvements to the current landscape of CB-Recommender systems.

The authors distinguish between content-Based Filtering, Collaborative Filtering and Hybrid Filtering. CF has many problems related with sparsity of reviews: students have no incentive to rate the materials and, often, limited time to study. CB recommenders are seen as preferred by the authors as they do not suffer from the cold-start problem AND seem adequate to recommend materials that are relevant to the user’s preferences and objectives.

In standard adaptive CB recommender systems, the approach involves the creation of a Learner Model and a Learning Object Model. Then it looks to recommend LO that better match the learner based on a set of constraints or rules. Still, CB recommenders have problems:
The authors propose improvements to adaptability and diversity by incorporating self-organization into the mix. In this case, the self-organization of LO.
In this schema:

When learners begin, their recommendations are based on the information that exists - learning style and preferences. Interactions with LO’s stimmulate self-organization of the LOs, thus adapting the LOs to now better match the student’s needs and learning goals - new recommendations are made once the self-organization process stabilizes.

Modelling users involves data that is deterministic - demographics and other carachteristics, but there is also some place for situations where there is uncertainty. Historically, these models consider attributes such as age, gender, educational level and others such as learning styles.
Some techniques that have previously been used are Bayesian Belief Networks, Fuzzy-Logic Techniques to model the complexity of human bahaviors. Collaborative Filtering is another popular approach.

On the end of LO modelling, the authors refer to ideas such as concept maps or ontology as initial LO models. Most of the previous research was based on similarity with, to the author’s knowledge, no previous attempt to treat LO as intelligent self-organizing objects.

Most approaches using Content-based Filtering have a Learner Model and a LO model. Recommendations are based on similarity learner and LO. After learner consults the recommended materials, then the learner is updated and, from that, new recommendations may arise. The problem is that this system first:

The consequence becomes that the learner profile will, with time, become an incorrect representation of the learner which will, with time, make recommendations that are ill-adapted to the needs of the learner. In addition, it needs to be taken into account that learner curiosity also dwindles with the lack of diversity in recommendation.

One option to increase diversity is to make space  probability-based recommendation mechanisms. Influenced-based learning has also been attempted to update learners but, again, to the author’s knowledge there is no previous attempt to use influence-based learning on the LO themselves.

In essence, the authors incorporate self-organization of the LO into a CB learner-oriented recommender system of LO. The idea is to address the known limitations of CB recommenders such as lack of diversity and unresponsivess to changes in the learner.

Each learner is represented as a tupple that features Learner Goals, Learning Styles, Learner Behavior. Each of these dimensions is a multivariate in itself - for example, learning style uses that survey (http://www.sojump.com/jq/7090233.aspx) to determine Competency, Media Preference, Content Preference, Purpose and etc…

Each LO is represented by 2 types of variables: Basic Variables and Extended Variables. Basic attributes are initialized using expert suggestions and feedback from previous learners and includes elements such as importance and difficulty. Updates will be based on time and frequency of visits during the learning process. EL is extended metadata.

Different LOs may share carachteristics: knowledge point, media type and content type. If they share these elements they are considered as Same LOs (this is a big difference from previous formulation where highly correlated LOs were suggested together). They weight updates considers Same LOs - the amount of time and frequency of accesses to Same LOs.

The recommender environment is a SOM module and referred to as E. This module refers to explicit preferences of the learners, the quality of the clusters and their current state. The authors also define what they call a predictive environment PE.

The PE takes the states of E and looks to obtain predictions on what LOs should be the most adequate for the next iteration - in plain english the PE takes the current preferences and looks to rank the LOs according to what would be the next preference (something to review more in depth later if I work on this).
The learner is surrounded by possible LOs he/she may consult next - Priority is set clockwise (unspecified reasons for why this is the case).

Learners start by being recommended LOs that are more compatible - so the LO is the Processing Unit and the first part is finding the BMU for the learner. Then, the active LO (BMU) is updated and influences the neighborhood - when a stale LO appears then a recommendation is made.

Input comes in and initializes recommendations based on closest LO. The interactions of the learner with LO will result in updates to the LO and the environment. These updates will trigger Self-Organization - rearrangement of the LOs in the enviroment space. Upon convergence, recommendations are made. Stop when learner goals are achieved, otherwise repeat the process and make new recommendations.
To simplify the process, authors organize the dynamic preferences of the learner into 2 distinct types:

Active LOs will be attracted towards the learner, Inactive will be sent away from the learner - criteria for weight update. Thus, self-organization considers: similarity between LOs and the ranking of candidate LOs in terms of desirability for the learner.
As we start, the LOs that are recommended are the ones more similar to the learner. The pseudo code for the algorithms is presented below
The overall algorithm is described below as algorithm 3:

The authors will use different optimization strategies. The new algorithm will be compared against Traditional Method (recommendations based on expert opinion without consideration for learner interactions), GA and Markov Chains.

The authors use data from 2 courses - a visual basic course and another based on C-Programming. VB course is chapters long, subdivided into 3 modules. The authors use 2 of these modules for testing, with the modules having 175 and 482 LO respectively.

The class of VB is divided into 4 distinct classes. Each class has its own recommendation strategy - Self, GA, MC and Tra. The classes were assembled in order to make them as homogenous as possible based on prior performance and demographic carachteristics.
The C class has 623 students and the students are divided into 3 classes - Self, GA and MC.

Learners are recommended contents based on survey results first and self-organization afterwards. There is also an option to request for additional adjustments in case the students feel that the recommended materials are unsuited for them.
The authors evaluate the results across multiple dimensions - similar to what had been done in their previous work.
As for Diversity, the authors focus their evaluation on two main aspects:

This, again, suggests that the Self approach ability to adapt the LOs to changes in the student is higher than the remaining options. Other options such as GA and MC seem to reduce diversity in order to obtain better accuracy - pointing to the previously identified problem of overspecialization.

Finally, the authors also make what they call a subjective evaluation based on a satisfaction survey done at the end of the semester. Students on the Self Class are happier than their peers whjen it comes to the content they received AND the time required.

Moreover, Self users were generally more happy when evaluating their experience with the system, as they felt more in control and, consequently, were more curious, interested and eager to learn. Finally, the recommender Self was the one that achieved the highest level of satisfaction of the users.

Finally, the authors measured the entropy of the recommendations to see whether the recommendations outputted by the system are “stable”. Self achieves stable solutions faster than their counterparts while also not suffering from scalability issues shown by other algorithms.
A look at the U-MAtrix also shows that the most suitable items are more likely to be recommended to the user.
Paper hints at using the self-organization approach to learners instead of LO. Paper also hints at the possibility of combining self-organization of learners AND LOs in the future.

references.title: Using learning analytics with temporal modeling to uncover the interplay of before-class video viewing engagement, motivation, and performance in an active learning context

extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, Student Characteristics, Questionnaire, Performance
extracted.Features: [Male,  Age, Pre-test, Frq. of SB, Freq. of Pause, Freq. of SF, Ratio of SB, Ratio of Pause, Ratio of SF, Autonomous Motivation, Controlled Motivation]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Totasl dataset is comprised of 631 lesson-based records. Missing values were not considered.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: Chi-Squared Statistics,RSquared, RMSEA
extracted.Cluster Description:
Division across demographic groups shows that:

Males have higher levels of autonomous motivation.

Older learners not only have more autonoumous motivation but also have more controlled motivation.

Frequently rewinding videos reveals less controlled motivation. However, larger steps when rewinding videos are indivative of higher controlled motivation.

Only autonomous motivation was found to be a significant predictor of performance.

Student behavior tends to be consistent across different lessons.
extracted.Implications:
Autonomous motivation plays an important role in predicting learning performance in weekly quizzes.

Individual differences between students are, in general, better indirect predictors of performance.

Assisting students who show poorer prior knowledge may be an effective way of increasing the motivation of theses learners. 

Moreoever, especially when it comes to video-based lessons, preparing learners for more difficult concepts (e.g. providing a poause before introcing them) cvould be benefical to keep them engaged.
extracted.Comments:
Authors do a sequential test of different variables and video-watching behaviors across a set of different lessons.

Dynamic SEM allow the use of longitudinal data while, simultaneously, tracking how behaviour change throughout the lesson.

Analysis is done before watching a video, after watching a class and what are the impacts for next lesson.

Analysis is done at different levels:

- Demographics and before watching

- Direct effects of watching video on next lesson

Indirect effects between video-watching engagements

Consisentecy across succesive lessons
extracted.Limitations:
STEM-specific cohort.

Could have benefitted from considering data from additional sources (or different feature sets).

Does not use final grade as the metric for course achievement - focus is on weekly quizzes instead.

annotations.text: Authors use a Dynamic SEM model to create an integrated temporal model that is meant to estimate motivation of students from video-watching behavior. Results show that there are patterns in video-watching behavior that can determine student performance.

references.title: Using Machine Learning to Train a Wearable Device for Measuring Students’ Cognitive Load during Problem-Solving Activities Based on Electrodermal Activity, Body Temperature, and Heart Rate: Development of a Cognitive Load Tracker for Both Personal and Classroom Use

annotations.text: Fitness tracking is now being applied to Cognitive load. Authors propose weareable educational fitness trackers. Authors use physiological data to predict cognitive load.
Authors find that body temperature, skin conductance and heart rate could be used to predict whether a student is in a problem-solving activity, leisure or daydreaming.
A second study shows that it is possible to predict self-reported mental focus. Paper finishes with discussion on how a wearable device could be used in the real world.

references.title: A hybrid approach for composing groups in collaborative learning contexts

annotations.text: Paper proposes a strategy to form groups that:

The proposed algorithm uses a local brute-force approach that is still scaleable as it is NP in group sizes, but P in student numbers. Multiple experiemnts with different numbers of students show decent performance and running times.

references.title: A deep cross-modal neural cognitive diagnosis framework for modeling student performance

extracted.Students: 2601 CMDT learners, 513 CBDT learners and 10000 Junyi learners
extracted.Data sources: Questions and Answers, Pre-requisite Concept Mapping
extracted.Features: [Question Text, Question Image, Prerequisite Relation Graph]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
Multiple Item Response Theory models foten do not consider cross-modal information about similar exercises (difficulty and contents). 

Models do not consider cognitive complexity of concepts.
extracted.Novelty of Model: The model includes a set of modules capabble of handling contextual information from different formats (image and/or text). It looks to create multiple embeddings from these differrent sources AND then computes similarity via an affinity matrix.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The model includes 3 distinct modules:

The first module is about the extraction of features from the multi-modal data (CA-GCF) - where we create embeddings from BERT (on text) and ResNet (on images). These are fused together. 

Subsequently, the authors also have a pre-requisite mappuiuung of concepts and create an embedding of knowledge concepts.

Exercises and Learners are One-Hot Encoded.
extracted.Models: IRT, MIRT, PMF, NeuralCD, RCD, CMNCD
extracted.Comments:
Author proposes a new model specific for multi-modal exercises. The authors make a comparison between different model and see that CMNCD outperforms all others.

The ablation study shows that all elements of the arquitecture (text, Image, CA-GCF, Concept Complexity, Deep MIRT and Exercise contents) are relevant as the best performance is only achieved when all modules are includes.
extracted.Limitations:
- Specific for cross-modal exercises (text and images).

- Does not seem to consider many of the most well-established KT models.

- Assumes Pre-Requisite Knowledge Graph is “fixed”.

- Holdout Method

- Dynamic learning of exercises’ trait based on concept complexity. It is currently done offline.

annotations.text: Knowledge tracing is a core component of any intelligent educational system. The way knowledge tracing is modelled involves mapping the learner’s knowledge state against the knwoledge requirements of an exercise.

However, the current ways to model exercise traits struggle with cross-modal exercises. To mitifgate this gap, the authors propose a Deep Cross-Modal Neural Cognitive Diagnosis Framework that first, builds on current models but also exlores cross-modal contents of exercises for better modelling of student performance. The model also seems to be effective at understanding pre-requisite relationships between concepts.
Results on 7 real-world datasets show that this model outperforms current state of the art.

references.title: Predicting Student Performance in Online Learning: A Multidimensional Time-Series Data Analysis Approach

extracted.Target: Distinction vs Pass vs Fail vs Withdraw
extracted.Courses: 4 courses in STEM and 3 couses in Social Sciences

Authors “normalized” course duration as 270 days. 

The presented model receives a dataset containing multiple multi-variate time series (each timeseries consists of a student in a course module edition). This dataset is passed through 3-layer LSTM, whose output is passed to a Dropout layer (into an attention layer).

The embedding resulting from the Attention module is the input to the “final” MLP.

The authors’ work is done in multiple stages: 

1. One experiment evaluating class specific metrics and no early prediction comparing multiple “candidate algorithms” - MTAPSP wings on all acounts.
extracted.Limitations:
- Reports mentioned in Abstract are misleading - they refer to Wi-FD on 100% course duration.

- Holdout method

- Only tested in OULAD

Authors “normalized” course duration as 270 days. 

The presented model receives a dataset containing multiple multi-variate time series (each timeseries consists of a student in a course module edition). This dataset is passed through 3-layer LSTM, whose output is passed to a Dropout layer (into an attention layer).

The embedding resulting from the Attention module is the input to the “final” MLP. v
extracted.Comments:
2. Authors do different forms of classification at different stages of course completion:

WF-PD: Pass-Fail

W-PD: Dropout vs Pass

3. Ablation studies to see effects of the different layers in the overall predictive process.

annotations.text: Online learning has become increasingly popular even though it lacks formal communication channels for feedback. Authorsd propose a multi-dimensional time-series model that is able to consider multiple features of students: behavior, characteristics and even prior performance.

Results show that the model achieved close to 100% accuracy (?) on OULAD in the task of early detection of students at risk. Moreover, the model achieves 74% and 74% F1 score in the multiclass problem of OULAD. As usual in these papers, model beats all other models it is being benchmarked against.

references.title: Adaptive learning path recommender approach using auxiliary learning objects

extracted.Students:
144 Mooshak learners, 61 Enki learners for offline evaluation

32 learners for Online evaluation
extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, Course Metadata, Available Time
extracted.Initialization Method: Users select a lesson as their starting point. Since there is an hierarchy between different lessons - authors assume that if the starting point is X, the learner is already familiar with the pre-requisites of X.
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Paths are adapted lesson by lesson according to the learner’s performance: if the user score is below expectation, auxiliary Learning Objects (LOs) are added; otherwise, the next lesson is recommended.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: LO-level interactions (time and score) are clustered using K-Means to compute average or median time and score estimates (Clust.Mean and Clust.Median). Features include time spent, completion rate, and visit frequency.
extracted.Recommendation types: Lesson-based learning paths with auxiliary recommendations for weakly mastered lessons.
extracted.Comments:
Authors use 2 approaches at building a Recommender System: 

1. Depth-First Search on Course Generation from all LO sequences - it allows the learners to follow a path, but often fail to get to the end within the available time.

2. A lesson-based method based on a 2-layered course graph. 

Auxiliary LOs are used when learners fail to meet estimated performance.  Experimental group outperformed control in lesson completion and time efficiency but not significantly in final exam scores.

Matrix Factorization and IRT models were also compared for benchmarking.
extracted.Limitations:
- Small datasets for offline experiments.  
- Limited number of participants (n=32) in live evaluation.  
- Cold-start issue when insufficient data to estimate time/score. 
 - Scalability and evaluation framework not validated on large datasets.  - Some over/underestimation of score predictions.

annotations.text: Paper proposes 2 approaches of recommender systems aimed at recommending resources that maximize score while taking into account the user’s time constraints. The approach is based on a two-layered course graph (lesson and LO).

The method uses a Depth First Search (DFS) to find sequences that start with a less and assings each learning pathway with the LO it fulfills. REcommendation is a learning path that suggests resources whose LOs will contribute to maximing score (again, constrained by time).  Authors validate their approach first via estimation and secondly in a real setting. In estimation, algorithm outperforms other recommenders. In real setting, it shows that learners advance more in the course than in other e-learning systems.

references.title: An artificial intelligence tool for heterogeneous team formation in the classroom

annotations.text: Teamwork is becoming an increasingly relevant skill to develop in the modern world. In an educational setting, not only do teams often lack support, but often fall short of forming optimal groups. The authors propose a new algorithm to form groups that uses uses a mix of AI and ontology:
The algorithm beats other alternatives in:
Authors test their algorithm on a real sample of students and point out that it has potential.

references.title: PCRS: Personalized Career-Path Recommender System for Engineering Students

annotations.text: Poor selection of undergraduate program leads to demotivation and greater dropout. To help Highschool graduates, the authors propose a career-path recommender system (PCRS), a recommender based on Fuzzy Intelligence from academic performance, personality type and extra-curricular skills.
The recommender was trained using data from students attending university and that are happy with their choice. Results show agreement between the discipline and recommendations.

references.title: A supervised learning framework: using assessment to identify students at risk of dropping out of a MOOC
references.journal: Journal of Computing in Higher Education

extracted.Courses: 8 Editions of a Learn Moodle Course
extracted.Features: [Positive Ratings in Database, Positive Ratings in Forum Posts, Attempted at least one Quizz, Percentage of Quizzes Attempted, Failing a Quizz Attempt]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Students whose enrollment dates are outside course duration were excluded. Same with students with no accesses.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.8881 - MLP, Precision: 0.9151 - MLP, Recall: 0.9531, F1-Score: 0.9337, AUC: 0.7312}
extracted.Comments:
Paper presents a full supervised learning framework that can be integrated in Moodle and is generalizable for multiple different tasks.

Main idea seems to be that the system is meant to be put into production witht he ability of having multiple possible targets.
extracted.Limitations:
Few alternative algorithms

Only uses interaction indicators

annotations.text: Authors develop and present an Analytics Framework for Moodle. Moreover, the authors develop a case study of a MOOC offered at the school that includes 47000 students.
Analytics framework uses a neural network to make predictions after the first, second and third quarters of the course, achiving 88.71% accuracy, 94% F1 Score and 73% AUC.

references.title: Homogeneous Group Formation in Collaborative Learning Scenarios: An Approach Based on Personality Traits and Genetic Algorithms

annotations.text: Automated group formation remains a key challenge for educators wishing to implement collaborative learning pedagogical activities. The authors propose a genetic algorithm that strives for personality trait homogeneity. The authors perform an experiment with 132 students using the Big 5 framework.
Results show that approach generates better learning outcomes than a strategy of grouping based on students’ preferences.

references.title: Insights into undergraduate pathways using course load analytics

extracted.Target of Recommendation: Course load predictions to inform course selection
extracted.Students: 128 students for survey (596 ratings); 58,325 student-semester pairs for longitudinal analysis (9,106 students from Fall 2017 cohort)
extracted.Courses: 332 courses with ratings; ~10,000 courses in full catalog; 4,305 courses analyzed for discrepancy
extracted.Data sources: Survey data (ground truth: time load, mental effort, psychological stress ratings on 5-point Likert scales); Canvas LMS data (Spring 2017-2021); Enrollment records; course2vec embeddings
extracted.Initialization Method: Not applicable - supervised learning on surveyed course ratings. Models trained on 85% of 332 rated courses (5-fold CV); 15% held out for testing. Random search hyperparameter tuning (N=25 draws)
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Static model - trained once on Spring 2021 survey data, then scaled to historical enrollments. For courses missing LMS data in a semester, imputed via average predictions across semesters with available data (12.56% of offerings, avg 2.80 course iterations)
extracted.Preprocessing Details: LMS features (31 total): assignments (13; deadlines, count), submission comments (4; response rate, size), forum posts (14; reply speed, frequency). Enrollment features: credit hours, course GPA/SD, grade distributions, STEM status, prerequisites (total, satisfied in current/past semesters). course2vec: 300-dim embeddings + average prerequisite vectors. Missing LMS data: control variable imputation (keep 0s, add binary indicator) outperformed k-means. Averaged multiple ratings per course (1.80 avg) before training.
extracted.Recommendation types: Course-level workload predictions on continuous 5-point scale for: time load, mental effort, psychological stress, combined (average of 3). Scaled to predict semester load (PCL_sem) by summing course predictions per student per semester
extracted.Evaluation: Cross-validation: MAE, % improvement over mean baseline. Test set: MAE with 95% CI via bootstrap (N=1,000). Longitudinal: compared PCL_sem vs credit hours across semesters, STEM vs non-STEM, transfer vs non-transfer. Stop-out & delayed graduation: logistic regression (AUC, McFadden's R², likelihood-ratio tests). Discrepancy analysis: partial correlations controlling for credit hours
extracted.Comments: Ensemble model best for combined load: 21.9% improvement, MAE=0.626. Mental effort most predictive (18.2% improvement). Time load hardest (10% improvement). First semester highest by PCL_sem but lowest by credit hours. STEM courses show higher positive discrepancy (predicted > credit hours; M=0.54 vs -0.35 non-STEM). Students with low credit/high PCL_sem more likely to stop out (R²=0.39% vs 0.21% credit-only). Prerequisites most associated with discrepancy (r=0.54 for satisfied prereqs). High-discrepancy STEM courses (>2 SD) linked to stop-out. Average prerequisite course2vec explained more discrepancy variance (R²=0.75) than course's own vector (R²=0.63)
extracted.Limitations: No real-world deployment/intervention testing - can't confirm if CLA-informed decisions improve outcomes. Survey data from single semester (Spring 2021) at one institution (UC Berkeley). Simple semester load = sum of course predictions (no temporal granularity for assignment deadline overlaps). Models explain little variance in stop-out (AUC=0.57) and delayed graduation (AUC=0.55) directly. Credit hour adjustment suggestion (0.40 units) based on inference, not tested. Selection bias: AP credit holders may appear to have fewer satisfied prerequisites. Privacy constraints excluded assignment content analysis. Scale reliability moderate; needs more items for time load/stress subscales. Generalizability unknown across institutions/demographics

annotations.text: Course Load Analytics is a more accurate representation of course-work than credit hours. The study uses data from course catalogs of a public university and create a machine-learning model meant to predict course-load ratings. Analysis of results shows that students experiencing greater course-load are more likely to dropout - especially in STEM and other fields that have a very high pre-requisite burden of knowledge.
Work is meant to help administrators create better recommendations of courses to their students.

references.title: Improving retention: predicting at-risk students by analysing clicking behaviour in a virtual learning environment

Removal of VLE categories that were found to not represent learning but could have impact on prediction: Home-Page and Others. For the models, these categories were not excluded.

Students who did not interact with LMS were filtered out.

Models created were course-specific:

Multiple experiments:

Moment of prediction is not fully specified, but data used indicates to be end-of-course. 

Authors seem to discuss having done early prediction and that precision decreases as time advances (which is counter to what I know from this domain).

extracted.Students:
4397 - Module A
1292 - Module B
2012 - Module C

extracted.Preprocessing Details: Setting of maximum number of antecedent to 5 and setting confidence threshold to 70%.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: NA
extracted.Cluster Description:
GUHA was able to identify, with high degree of confidence, students who failed TMA 4, TMA 6 and TMA 7. 

Authors do not go too in-depth on the description of rules obtained.
extracted.Implications:
Difficulty in drawing conclusions about learner engagement exclusively from clicks on the VLE. 

Need to take into account how a module is structured and how the VLE is intended to be used within the context of the course.
extracted.Comments: Model trained to predict module failure.
extracted.Limitations:
Limited number of algorithms to use. 

Features used seem to too simple.

annotations.text: Work focuses on predicting retention at OU. Features used include VLE logs and “other unspecified data sources” on 3 distinct modules of the OU. It is possible to predict failure when comparing changes in current behavior vs previous behavior or the expected behavior of students with similar behaviors.
The authors also use General Unary Hypothesis Automation to generate hypothesis about students who fail.

Aim of the work is to make identification of students at risk in modules of the Open University. To do that, the authors use data from the VLE and combine it with data from other sources. The authors intend to use a rules-based approach to create rules that may hopefully generalize to other contexts.

Some of the exising body of work shows that online dropout is significiantly higher than the one experienced in face-to-face contexts. Demographics, Technological and Social factors and the major contributors to this difference.

The creation of tracking systems could be an helpful alternative that could allow institutions to identify and provide support to students form an early stage of their courses. The most effective way is to combine data from multiple sources in a way that also integrates behavioral data. Even moreso, predictive models (the course specific kind) could be very helpful tools.

The authors use data from the Open University, an Online Higher Education Institution. The goal, again, is to combine institutional data with behavioral data. For that, the authors use data from 3 modules: behavioral, institucional information about the students and any marked assessments that may exist in the modules.
The modules are anonymized but, according to the authors, mirror the typical workings of courses at the OU. Given the differences between courses, the authors opted to create course-specific models.

Analysis of student behavior was done in 2-stages: aalysis of all clicks and analysis of click patterns broken down into separate categories. The authors found that activity increases when there is a test coming.
The author claims that there is no such thing as an average student → some students barely click and pass while others click a lot and fail and these observations were true for all models.
The authors create course-specific models for 2 distinct classification problems:

Decision Trees were the best models and combining different sources seems to result in best performance. Authors also attempt to do some form of transfer learning and end up finding that the models perform better when they are used on the course they were trained on (obviously).

In order to provide some explainability to the predictive model results, the authors also resorted to Association Rule Mining to identify specific patterns of bahavior that lead to unsuccessful outcomes.  for that, the authors to an algorithm called GUHA.

references.title: RecRec: Algorithmic Recourse for Recommender Systems

annotations.text: Authors propose a recourse framework aimed at providers of content. Essencially, the recommender has a set of rules that, if fulfilled, lead to a change in ranking or recommendation. Validation of this dataset on 3 public datasets shows RecRec is effective at leading to valid, sparse and actionable recommendations.

references.title: Towards Portability of Models for Predicting Students’ Final Performance in University Courses Starting from Moodle Logs

extracted.Courses: 24 university courses from 4 degrees (Computer, Education, Engineering, Physics) at the University of Córdoba
extracted.Features: [assign submit, assign submit for grading, assign view, assign view all, assignment upload, assignment view, assignment view all, choice choose, choice choose again, choice report, choice view, choice view all, course enroll, course user report, course view, course view section, folder view, folder view all, forum add discussion, forum add post, forum mark read, forum search, forum subscribe, forum subscribe all, forum unsubscribe, forum view discussion, forum view forum, forum view forums, page view, page view all, questionnaire submit, questionnaire view, questionnaire view all, quiz attempt, quiz close attempt, quiz continue attempt, quiz review, quiz view, quiz view all, quiz view summary, resource view, resource view all, url view, url view all, wiki edit, wiki info, wiki links, wiki update, wiki view, wiki view all]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Moodle logs and grade files were integrated with a specific preprocessing tool. Student identities were anonymized. Only student events were retained, and 50 Moodle event attributes were selected. Two datasets were created per course: numerical and discretized. Moodle event attributes were discretized with equal-width binning into HIGH/LOW. Final grade was manually discretized into FAIL (<5) and PASS (≥5). Courses were grouped in two ways for portability experiments: by degree and by Moodle usage level (Low, Medium, High).
extracted.Comments: Main contribution is studying when course-to-course portability of performance prediction models is feasible. In general, numerical datasets yielded slightly higher AUC, but discretized datasets yielded much better portability (lower AUC loss). Portability was more feasible between courses of the same degree than between courses only grouped by similar Moodle usage. The most portable models used Moodle events related mainly to forums, assignments, choices, resources, and pages.
extracted.Limitations:
Most transferred models had only modest predictive performance, often just above chance. 

Direct portability was feasible only under some circumstances, mainly with discretized datasets and especially in some same-degree groups. 

Models may include activities absent from target courses, which can reduce transfer quality. 

The study used only one algorithm (J48) and one institution. 

The approach tests direct portability, not tuned transfer learning or domain adaptation.

annotations.text: The literature features a lack of course-agnostic models and mostly focuses on models that aim to achieve the best predictive performance in a single course. Thus, the aim of the paper is to study portability of models across multiple courses.

The work groups courses according to similarity in student behaviors or by degree and how different feature engineering strategies affect model performance. Authors find that, while portability is possible, it is only achievable under certain constraints.

references.title: Regularized ensemble learning for prediction and risk factors assessment of students at risk in the post-COVID era

Authors look at different forms of pruning for a Random Forest:

extracted.Comments: This work is meant to explore how students who had part of their education during COVID and authors look to see how they perform after returning.

Small sample size. 

Does not consider behavioral data.

annotations.text: The transition back to face to fface learning may have hindered the ability of some students to learn. After all, COVID demanded a substantial change to one’s study habits and it may have been hard for some students to go back to the previous normal. Therefore, it is important to understand what potential risk factors exist for low performance.

Standard ML models often find it hard to identify insights that may be useful for the design of early interventions. To address this, the authors create a Random Forest trained under 3 distinct pruning paradigms:
The suggested methods outperform all other methods considered in the study in the task of identifying students at risk.

references.title: A Process to Detect Exploitation and Exploration Student’s Behaviors in Higher Vocational Education

annotations.text: The model presents a classification work that is meant to be used as a percursor for an educational recommender system.

extracted.Features: [LrnApp01,  LrnApp02, LrnApp03,   LrnApp04, LrnApp05, LrnApp06, LrnApp07,  LrnApp08, LrnApp09, LrnApp10, LrnApp11, LrnApp12, LrnApp13, LrnApp14, LrnApp15]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: The authors collect data from multiple instruments and  evaluate the different clusters using different perspectives.

extracted.Cluster Description:
Passive strategists

No tool users

Testing directed

 Intensive tool use and lectures

All high

Tool users testing directed

Students who incorporate testing as a significant component of their learning strategies demonstrate above-average performance.

Prior mathematics does not exhibit effects on student learning.

Learners who do not use tool or act more passively are more prone to fail as, on average, they achieve a worse grade.

Coversely, learners with more activity tend to eprform better.

Flexibility is a key factor for elarner success as the most successfull students are the ones who engage on more learning strategies.

Authors take survey answers and use them to create clusters.

Tbhe clusters are subsequently profiled using different instruments:

Motivational Scales, Learning Strategy Profiles, Affective Scales, Programs and Gender, Course Performance.

Learners in this PBL course were provided with a plethora of different resources.
