DAta from a single course (even though 3 separate editions).

annotations.text: The authors follow the idea that race and survey data should be combined in order to bvalidate the identification of SRL adjacent behaviors. Work uses logs and answers from Study of Learning Questionnaire (SLQ) to identy learning dispositions. Then, it explores hjow these learning dispositions can act as antecedents of of specific learning strategies.

references.title: Enabling real-time adaptivity in MOOCs with a personalized next-step recommendation framework

extracted.Initialization Method: The model is trained on navigational logs from a previous course offering. URLs are tokenized as indices combining chapter, section, and vertical hashes to represent each learning page. Learners’ paths are modeled as sequences of visited URLs and associated dwell times.
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Real-time adaptation per learner session. Recommendations are updated dynamically via client-side logging and immediate model queries after each navigational event.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Logs filtered to navigation events only (excluding video pauses, quizzes, etc.). Duplicated contiguous page visits removed; dwell times bucketed (0–10s, 11–60s, 61–1799s, ≥1800s). Data split into 70/30 train–test; early stopping applied on validation loss.
extracted.Evaluation: {Accuracy: 0.64 - Bucket/Non-Concat.}
extracted.Comments:
Framework integrates real-time JavaScript logging + Node/Flask servers for low-latency recommendations.

Uses an LSTM behavioral model predicting next likely page and expected dwell time, enabling “forward-stepping” recommendations to skip trivial resources.

First proof-of-concept of real-time adaptive navigation in MOOCs without changing the platform codebase.
extracted.Limitations:
- Model assumes stable course structure; reordering of lessons reduces accuracy. 
- No pedagogical optimization (focuses on behavioral efficiency, not learning gain). 
- Cold-start issue for new course versions. 
- Evaluation based on prediction accuracy, not direct learning outcomes. 
- Requires heavy preprocessing and server–client integration setup.

annotations.text: Authors present a new model of student behavior and an adaptive Recommender System that uses clickstream data. Their pilot intervention on edX provided adaptive learning pathways shown as a navigational suggestion. The model predicts next page where they would be likely to spend more time - based on behavior (without any ontology or assumptions).
The prediction of time is done via different Recurrent Neural Network approaches. The paper also discusses multiple implications for real-time deployment.

references.title: Applying Learning Analytics for the Early Prediction of Students’ Academic Performance in Blended Learning

extracted.Features: [Days of activity per week, Activities per week, Days of watching videos per week, Videos per week, Complete watches per week, Fwd or Bwd seeks per week, Videos Paused per week, Videos Stopped per week, Plays per week, FWD per week, BWD per week, Pauses per week, Stops per week, Online practices per week, Number of Calculus units practiced per week, Days in online practice per week, Days of Calculus practice per week, Weekly practice score, Weekly Homework Score, Weekly Quizz Score, After School Tutoring per week]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors use the variables to create 5 distinct datasets:

W1-6 - aggregated across first 6 weeks, 

W1-12 - Aggregated across first 12 weeks

W1-18 - Aggregated across first 18 weeks

Duration datasets: 
W7-12 - Aggregated between week 7 to 12

W13-18 - Aggregated between weeks 13 and 18
extracted.Comments:
Authors use data from a Calculus course to do prediction of performance at multiple points in time.

They use Principal component Regression to calculate the final score.

Authors later perform an analysis of the distinct forms of of the course to assess the contribution of each individual element - separating the Online from the Blended from the Traditional face-to-face elements.
extracted.Limitations:
Small number of algorithms tested.

Single course

Small number of students

annotations.text: Blended learning mixes F2F classes with Online Learning and LA is meant to help identify students early and provide timely interventions based on student learner profiles. Authors extract a set of multiple variables, ranging from video-watching behaviors to homework and quiz scores.

Results show that predictions can be made when only on-third of the course has passed. Moreover, the authors identify critical factors that affect student performance - 4 online and 3 more traditional ones. Combining data from both contexts improves model performance.

references.title: Evolutionary Analysis of International Student Mobility Based on Complex Networks and Semi-Supervised Learning

annotations.text: The paper uses data of student mobility between 2007 and 2016. The authors have constructed a weighted directed network that analyses how mobility changed over the years.
Main findings are as follows:
Overall, the authors seem happy at how their model reflects the reality of student migrations.

references.title: A Survey of Knowledge Tracing: Models, Variants, and Applications

annotations.text: Knowledge tracing is one of the fundamental tasks using student behavioral data in Learning Analytcs. In essence it aims to infer the knowledge state of a student and how it can be leveraged in problem-solving. The paper makes a survey on the evolving landscape of knowledge tracing.

Moreover, the paper introduces 2 open-source algorithms. The first downloads and preprocessing of KT-related datasets. Also EduKTM implements the most common KT models. Potential future directions are also discussed in the paper.

references.title: Predicting Academic Performance via Semi-supervised Learning with Constructed Campus Social Network

extracted.Target: 4 quartiles of GPA performance (I, II, III, IV)
extracted.Data sources: Digital Card interactions with Campus Infrastructure
extracted.Features: [Social influence Matrix on each Location]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The weight of each location is assumed to be similar from the start. 

GPA was converted into a rank of all grades inside the same program. Moreover, the ranks were normalized to account for differences in number of students in the course.
extracted.Models:
LP-Teaching Building
LP-ALL
Label Propagation on Multiple Networks
extracted.Comments:
Semi-Supervised Learning work. The training dat a includes a random percentage of labeled and unlabeled data (20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, 100%). Experiment is repeated 20 times.

Performance increases with more labelled data, but the increases in performance are not very big compared with, for example, the 40% sample - meaning that the semi-supervised approach works rather well-
extracted.Limitations:
Limited use of evaluation metrics.

Ethical issues with student monitoring.

Unsatisfactory definition of student performance.

annotations.text: The authors identify a gap in the study of performance prediction that relates to social influence. To address this, the authors construct a model of student social relationships that is based on their behavior while on campus (what campus bahavior means is somewhat unclear). By using an experiment on close to 5000 students and 14M behavior records, the authors verify that this approach does indeed contribute to better predictive models. Some details on the models themselves are unspecified.

Early prediction of academic performance can assist educators in inverning near students and providing them with the necessary guidance for them to succeed. Few studies consider social influence as a predictor of academic performance.

The growth in computer-based systems in campus life makes it possible to track activities in and outside campus, which makes it possible to create social relationships from this behavioral data. Since there is no way to actually measure whether students are friends or companions, tracking whether there are  co-occurrences of location may point towards the existence of a relationship.

To do that, the authors compute the number of co-occurrences in physical spaces, followed by building what is a null model → allowing for a comparison between the expected number of co-occurrences in the wild vs being friends. It could be possible to identify social ties in this manner if the training data was labelled (meaning we knew if 2 students were friends based on the relationship).

However, that is not the case, which makes trewating this as a supervised learning problem unfeasable. Thus, the authors use a semi-supervised approach to predict academic performance based on social influence. The work starts with looking if students with high co-occurrences tend to have similar performance → the model used is called Label Propagation algorithm on Multiple Networks. Under this model, different lcoations are meant to have different weights.

Model is tested on 5000 students with 14 Million records. The performance of the students is not the GPA, but instead a 4 level multiclass problem based of which quartile the performance is. 20% of the sample is considered the test data. In addition, training is done with a random percentage of training observations labeled (so it can be, 20, 40, 60, 80 or 100% of the training data being labelled).
Co-occurrences in a geographical spaces are a common form of identifying social ties. However, this type of application of predicting social ties on-campus is still under-explored.

However, when it comes to the prediction of performance, multiple models exist. Usually these models use either longitudinal data or tensor-based data collected from an e-learning environment to predict whether a student will be succesful or not (difference being the way the data is preprocessed from the extraction of the activity logs). While popular as a task, the literature does not seem to have works predicting student performance using, as predictors, the social connections between students.

The authors start with the building of a campus social network model that is based on the millions of interactions between students and campus (e.g. using the smartcards). Under this context, co-occurrence is identified as being recorded on the same location within a short interval of time. The authors build a model that considers 7 possible distinct locations where students can be:

Overwhelming amounts of co-occurrences are very likely a sign of 2 students being close friends (as highlighted in other work). In this work, however, there is no actual label to determine whether they are friends or not. Determination of frienship is constructed by using a null matrix:

The social ties are determined by comparing the co-occurrences in different locations. To combine them, the authors use a weighted sum of the co-occurrences in each location (weight being the strength of the social ties - determined via an algorithm). This is important to acknowledge because relationships are often not fully symmetrical.

It is hypothesized that students being friends has an impact on the performance→ friends should have similar performance. The GPA of all students was ranked within the students in the same major, followed by a normalization by the number of students attending that specific major. Then, the authors test whether the performance of friends is similar using paired-samples t-test:
The results show that there is a correlation between strength of social ties and similarity in academic performance.
The model uses the constructed campus social network to predict academic performance.

The social network W is an N*N affinity matrix and the performance matrix T is a N * 4 featuring the level of the students. Unlabeled observations were full 0. The output of the model is prediction matrix F which has the same shape as T.
The loss function for Label Propagation are divided into 2 different terms:
The Label Propagation algorithm is meant to provide contribution of each one of the 7 locations.

The data includes 5388 students daily routines on 7 different locations. The model is evaluated using precision. The training data includes a random percentage of data that need to be labeled (I, II, III or IV). The experiment is repeated 20 times.

The first results highlight that the social ties created in different locations do indeed have a a different weight when determining performance. As it can be seen from the figure, regardless of the the percentage of unlabeled samples, the LP-Teaching Building are the social ties that at predicting the success position of each student.
Another interesting observation comes from the fact that the semi-supervised approach makes it possible to obtain relatively good performances while working with a subset of labeled data.
To check how the proposed algorithm works, the authors make a comparison of 3 distinct models:

Again, results increase with more labeled samples, and the LPMN giving weights for different locations outperform the other options. Surprisingly, LP-Teaching Building outperforms the model considering all locations with equal weight.

references.title: Performance Prediction of Students in Higher Education Using Multi-Model Ensemble Approach

annotations.text: It is equally important and challenging to create early warning systems that are accurate and understandable. The paper look to use ML to create models for multiple different relevant tasks in LA:

The study focuses on comparing performance and efficiency of different ensemble classifiers for the aforementioned tasks. The auhtors rely on data extracted from a Pakistani university. Boosted Trees outperforms bagging when dataset is larger, while stacking is better for smaller datasets.

references.title: Attention-Based Artificial Neural Network for Student Performance Prediction Based on Learning Activities

extracted.Target: At-risk (C, D, F) vs Not at-risk (A, B)
extracted.Courses: 3 editions of 1 Program Theorry I and 1 Digital Signal PRocesing Course, respectively
extracted.Features: [Attendance, RReport Submission, Course Accesses, E-book reading time, Highlights in E-Books, Notes  in E-Book, Total Actions in E-Book, Word Count in E-Portfolio]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors extract data from  Moodle and the BookRoll systems. 

Quizzes were not considered for the experiment. Information is represented as a vector for every week.

The logs are converted into Active Learner Points on the different topics for each week. ALP is a score between 0 and 5 that is distribution-based.

This is subsequently given as input a to the weighted attention. The weighting occurs both at a feature and temporal dimensions.
extracted.Comments:
The authors do a new approach to student performance prediction using a temporal representation with Attention Weighting on both a time and feature dimension level. 

The authors compare their approach against other sequence-based approaches (LSTM, GRU). The results show that it is possible to obtain good results by the Courses’ Halfway Point. 

Surprisingly, this approach seems to exhibit some diminishing returns (model eprformance does not significantly increase past a certain point in time).  

Ablation studies show that the approach seems to generate better results than using the time or the feature dimension separately. 

The time dimension alone also does, on occasion, outperform both modules.
extracted.Limitations:
- Small and unbalanced dataset

Difficult to generalize to other educational outside Informatics

- Availability of Data from Moodle and BookRoll

- Does not consider other additional sources of data.

annotations.text: Most predictive models of student performance consider either student attributes or learning activities. However, few consider external factors such as course-duration. The authors propose an attention-based method to incorporate data for these external factors. The attention module is intended to give weights to different factors, thuis allowing the distinguishing between time dimensions and learning activity dimensions.

The model, Attn-ANN combines weightings on time and feature dimensions to identify significance of lectures and learning activities. The model seems to outperform convencional algorithms at the task of early prediction of student performance. Moreover, an analysis of the netwroks decisions shows potential for synergy between model and educational experts.

references.title: Prototyping an Adaptive Learning Platform’s Learning Analytic Dashboards on Behavioral data to Support Teachers’ Pedagogical Actions

annotations.text: Learning Dashboards can be obtained from Adaptive Learning Platforms to provide an overview of student progress to the educator. Most ALP are evaluated on their ability to use data from multiple sources, their predictive performance but the effectiveness of the Learning Analytics Dashboards is something that is not as explored.

Authors make a valdiation of their proposal of Learning Analytics Dashboards and notice that they improved awareness of the professor of motivational issues related with the student. The second prototype LAD was considred hard to understand.

references.title: Pilot Recommender System Enabling Students to Indirectly Help Each Other and Foster Belonging Through Reflections

extracted.Data sources:
– Student reflection texts (2 prompts per week)

– Historical dataset of prior students’ “challenges” and “solutions”
extracted.Initialization Method: Sentence embeddings initialized with pre-trained stsb-roberta-large transformer (no fine-tuning)
extracted.Updates to Recommendations:
Static: no retraining during deployment

Recommendations recomputed for new students using existing reflection corpus
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
– Removed incomplete or short reflections

– Token cleaning and normalization– Sentence-to-vector encoding (RoBERTa embeddings)

– Cosine similarity top-k (k=5) retrieval
extracted.Comments:
– Novel reflection-driven educational recommender leveraging NLP (semantic similarity) rather than grades or logs.

– Encourages peer empathy and sense of belonging by connecting similar challenges.

– No labeled data needed (unsupervised, lightweight).

– Demonstrates positive affective outcomes and high perceived usefulness.
extracted.Limitations:
– Small pilot sample (N = 16 post-survey).

– Dependence on reflection quality; vague or humorous answers degrade results.

– Lack of adaptive feedback (static corpus).

– Negative correlation between similarity & usefulness suggests semantic proximity ≠ pedagogical value.

– Limited generalization (single course, single institution).

annotations.text: The work proposes a recommender system that suggests peers and solutions to challenges based on reflections from students on the previous semesters. The recommendations are the suggestions of previous students. When testing it, the authors report that 70% of the recommended solutions were helpful.

references.title: An Exact Algorithm for Group Formation to Promote Collaborative Learning

annotations.text: Putting students into the correct groups is one of the most challenging tasks. Authors explore an approach to create heterogenous groups that have inter-group homogeneity. The approach assumes that there is a hierarchy to the characteristics the professor wants to use for grouping. Authors use this hierarchy to transform the hierarchy into a lexicographic optimization problem.

A pilot test was done on freshmen students with 3 characteristics: knowledge level, demographics and motivation. Results show computational feasibility of the approach. Authors argue for large-scale studies.

references.title: Centralized student performance prediction in large courses based on lowcost variables in an institutional context

extracted.Courses: 811 unique undergraduate face-to-face courses with LMS support; analysis restricted to large courses (>50 enrollments)
extracted.Features: [AGE, GEND, F_SCHO, HS_CGPA, EX_LEN, EX_MAT, SCHOOL, C_DURA, CRED_S, E_SEM, CGPA, WEB_READ, CONT_READ, SYLL_READ, MSG_READ, MSG_NEW, MSG_REPLY, MSG_FORW, QUIZ_SUB, AS_SUB, AS_REV, SEC_READ, FOR_READ, FOR_RESP, CHAT_NEW, POLL_VOTE, POLL_READ, NEWREAD, WIKI_READ, WIKI_REV, WIKI_NEW, PREF_UPD, PREF_ADD, PROF_PRIV, PROF_PREF]
extracted.Moment of Prediction: Mid-semester and end-of-semester; authors compare prediction using only fixed data, full-term LMS data, and midterm LMS data
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Data from DARA and SAKAI were integrated into a unified database. Records were cleaned for inconsistencies, recoded into standard formats, and encrypted. LMS actions by the same student of the same type within 60 seconds were collapsed into one action. Courses were filtered into three sets: Large Courses, Large Top 20%, and Large Top 10% according to enrollment size and LMS use. CGPA and LMS usage variables were z-score transformed to improve comparability across courses. Final grades were normalized to 0–1.
extracted.Models: Linear Regression, Robust Linear Regression, Random Forest
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {RMSE: 0.0848 – Random Forest, Large Top 10%, M-LMS; RSquared: 0.6736 – Random Forest, Large Top 10%, M-LMS; R-Fscore: 0.5349 – Random Forest, Large Top 10%, M-LMS}
extracted.Comments: Main contribution is a centralized, low-cost institutional model for early identification of at-risk students in large classes. Random Forest clearly outperformed Linear Regression and Robust Linear Regression, especially when LMS data were added. Importantly, midterm LMS data performed as well as or slightly better than full-term LMS data, supporting early intervention use. Variable relevance analysis showed CGPA as the strongest predictor, followed by enrolled school; LMS variables had lower but still useful predictive value, improving RMSE by up to 12.28% according to the abstract.
extracted.Limitations: Focused only on large face-to-face courses supported by LMS, so results may not generalize to small classes or fully online contexts. LMS variables were mostly low-level aggregated counts and reflected relatively passive LMS use. Prediction still depends strongly on prior academic performance (especially CGPA), which may limit interpretability from a learning-process perspective. The study was conducted in one university with a particular institutional context.

annotations.text: There is a plethora of studies focusing on the extraction of insights about student interaction with Learning Management Systems and its relationship to student success → problem is small samples and expensive variables. Thus, the authors use a sophisticated algorithm (unspecified) with what they call low cost variables to make early prediction of success in large courses.
Results show that this model outperforms other alternatives. Moreover, adding LMS data also improved the performance of the predictive model by close to 12%. This is a regression problem.

references.title: Multimodal Predictive Student Modeling with Multi-Task Transfer Learning

extracted.Data sources: Pre-Test,  Post-Test, Eye-Tracker and Facial Expression, IMI survey
extracted.Features: 460 multimodal features per student
extracted.Moment of Prediction:
Every 2 minutes during Game
Before Post-Test
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Student’s knowledge was encoded from the answers to the pre-test.

Then, data was collected and the data was subsequently treated using PCA to reduce dimensionality.

3 forms of PCA usage were tested (No PCA, 1 PCA per modality and PCA on the entire space). 

Parameter-tuning uses nested-CV with 3-folds
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Standardized Conversion Point: 0.428 - RF-Gameplay&ReflexionTest, F1-Score: 0.749 - RF-Gameplay&ReflexionTest}
extracted.Comments:
Only the first experiment was conducted on College Students. The second experiment was conducted on 8th-grader…

Authors train different models for post-test scores and interest (in here we consider only the ones for test-scores). 

Different sources of data are compared separately ahnd then comined into a single RF model.
extracted.Limitations:
Data is not sequential

Only considers Random Forest

Small Sample Size

annotations.text: Game-based learning has demonstrated pedagogical effectiveness and recent advancements in sensors and nlp made data acquisiton a whole new ball-game. Due to the requirements of intertaction with a game, GBL is a prime candidate for effectiveness of LA-based early warning systems.

Thus authors explore a multi-task and multi-modal learning analytics framework for Game-Based LEarning Environments. Framework is evaluated on 2 distinct student populations (n = 61 and n = 118). The models are tasked with predicting post-test performance and interest.

Moreover, the authors make a comparison between training paradigms. On the first, each dataset is trained independently and performance is assessed on the data the models were trained on. On the second paradigm, the authors pre-train a model in one dataset to then fine-tune with the other. Resutlts show that multi-task models were able to improve performance for one the tasks, but not for both. Moreover, transfer learning also seems to contribute to improvements in model performance.

references.title: Accessing online learning material: Quantitative behavior patterns and their effects on motivation and learning performance

extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, MSLQ, Scores on Final Exam and Homework Assignments
extracted.Features: [Total Seconds on Learning Materials, Time on Lecture Slides, Lecture Slides Accessed, Time Spent of Each Access to Lecture Slides, Time Spent of Video Lectures, Video Lectures Accessedm Avg Time on Video Lectures, Time on Shared Assignments, Shared Assignments Accessed, Avg. Time on Shared Assignments, Time on Posted Messages, Number of Posted Messages, Avg. time on each access of posted messages, Messages Posted]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors collect data from 4 different sources.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Cluster 1 (n=21): 

More time on videos, shared assignments, posted messages - Consistent use Students

Cluster 2 (n=19): 

Smallest amount of time intertacting with the different kinds of learning resources. These students achieved, on average, a worse overal final examinatioon score relative to their peers. 

Cluster 3 (n=19): 

Slide intensive students. These pass consistenly more time consulting slides thant heir peers while still checking other learning materials. 

Both cluster 1 and 3 consistently outperform the students in Cluster 2.
extracted.Implications: Stuydents who were more engaged and willing to interact with course materials tended to outperform their peers - and these differences in interaction are often associated with motivational factors.
extracted.Comments: Standard clustering work on data from LMS and mapping into different SRL constructs of MSLQ and the learner’s final performance.
extracted.Limitations:
Small sample size.

A third of the students was in the less use students. 

While Clusters 1 and 3 had different strategies, these often translated into a similar exam performance - meaning that there are factors not being accounted for in the available data. 

Not transferrable to courses that contain e.g. a textbook,. 

LMS data is noisy.

annotations.text: Work uses data from 59 students on the LMS to cluster students according to their interactions with the environment. Authors identify some interesting common patterns related to usage: e.g. students spend more time on lecture related materials. Clustering of the students revealed 3 main groups: “consistent use”, “Slide intensive use” and “less use”.
Authors then make an association between these 3 groups and their motivation and academic performance.

references.title: Toward effective group formation in computer-supported collaborative learning

annotations.text: Group formation, although essential for collaborative learning is a very difficult task. The authors use a form of graph-based modelling to identify similarity between different members. Then, the authors propose a binary integer model to solve group formation on a sample of 32 students (groups of 4).
Model found optimal combination in 20 seconds. Results were also compared against an altered version of the k-means algorithm.

references.title: Student achievement prediction using deep neural network from multi-source campus data

extracted.Data sources: Web Browsing, Consumption Data, Library, Gateway Logins, Student Demographics
extracted.Features: [Sequences of Logs, OHE vectors of static data]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
For target, the authors discretize GPA into a multiclass problem where the top X% (X=5, 10, 15, 20) are Excellent, the bottom X% are poor and all students in the middle become considered as good. 

For every feature, authors select different levels of aggregation - 4 hours for web page browsing. In others, aggregation was daily.

Records with same data, time and place were merged.

Static data is converted into a OHE vector .

Depending on the source, data is either interpreted by an LSTM module or a 2DCNN.

Class imbalance addressed with weighted loss function instead of the common cross-entropy.

annotations.text: Authors start by mentioning that other LA works thus far do indeed use logs to predict success but neglect the practical aspects of an early warning system: predicting early in order to support student success.

Thus, the authors extract log data and use it to create an early warning system. Time-dependent variables have shown to be especially useful in this endeavor. Moreover, a CART supplemented by an AdaBoost showed to be the best model out of the ones attempted.

LMS are, nowadays, a useful tool to identify learners who may be at risk. Prior work has shown that using activity logs AND learner data that is stored on the LMS may be enough to create effective systems for this.

A bigger challenge, however, is creating a model that integrates the wealth of online data that is now generated and, more importantly, actually having an LMS with built-in capacity to leverage the data and make predictions. This study attempts to make prediction using learning portfolio datasets while a fully online course is still in progress. In other words, early prediction that considers time-dependent variables and other activities undertaken by the students.
The Research Questions are as follows:

To answer the questions, authors use data from 330 learning portfolios of students attending a fully online course. The data was collected as the course was taking place. This data was then used to compare different candidate ML algorithms for identification of learner’s at risk.

For this paper, learning portfolios are understood as collections of events and activities undertaken by the student. Now that it is possible to make online creation of portfolios by looking at the history of learning activities, these portfolios are more and more popular tools in the prediction of student performance. However, there are still few studies looking at the effectiveness of early warning systems created in this way.
By the time of writing there were already some studies exploring the potential of creating EWS. These are divided into 2 categories:

Very few studies actually use time-dependent representations. This means that most of the models use whole-semester information that hinders the ability of a model to be used as an early warning system → little to no time to help student.

Course has 2 editions and takes the whole semester. Students are required to attend online classes and watch specific videos during specific intervals. The LMS stores the interactions as logs, meaning that, to be usable, the data must undergo some transformations that resulted into 14 distinct features.

Users are described by 4 types of feature: Logins, Online material usage, assignments status and discussion forum status. The authors claim to have 330 portfolios but they only recruited 300 students. Moreover, problem is extremely imbalanced (284: pass vs 16 fail). In order to include temporal components into the models, the authors create 3 distinct datasets → one for each moment of prediction (W4, W8 or W13).

These datasets are then used to train and compare different classifiers on WeKa → 3 baseline models in C4.5, CART and Logistic Regression and one ensemble model in AdaBoost. Dataset imbalance was addressed by mixing undersampling and oversampling. This process was repeated 30 times on different seeds. So, in essence, the authors do 30 repetitions of 10-Fold Cross-Validation. Average performances are stored and compared using:
Differences between models are also assessed using a cross-validated t-test.
As far as results go, they can briefly be summarized as follows:
In general, results show that early prediction is possible at the 4th week and that predictive performance does not seem to improve that much with the passing of the weeks.
The authors also develop a prototype for a course-level early warning system. The model is composed of 3 components:

Final sections of the paper discusses the how do I warn the student part of the problem with EWS - something that may be useful later on, but is not for now. Short-version, the school establishes an early warning agent that warns both professor and student.

There is also a flowchart for learning improvement. You can see it here for the lulz.

references.title: Semi-Supervised Detection of Student Engagement
references.journal: Twenty First Pacific Asia Conference on Information Systems

annotations.text: Using computer vision to detect student engagement is a daunting task due to multiple domain specificities. Moreover, the number of available datasets is also lacking. To address the issue, the authors propose a semi-supervised learning framework on data that only has a small portion of annotated instances. Results show that the proposed methodology S4VM outperforms the purely supervised method and achieves acceptable results on the literature.
Specific task being addressed is unspecified on abstract.
User engagement is regarded as an important proxy for quality of teaching and learning. User Engagement measures come in 3 types:
Automated measures have been gaining attention due to rise of cameras. The paper uses facial expression videos to detect user engagement in a computer-supported educational environment.

Data requires an extensive amount of annotation, thus the question becomes whether it is possible (via semi-supervised learning) to detect User Engagement with few amounts of annotated data. To explore this question, the authors use S4VM, a semi-supervised approach to support vector machines claimed to work at the same level of the vanilla algorithm.

Engagement is especially useful metric to follow because engaged students pay more attention and are more likely to explore (even if they make mistakes). The lietrature features a small amount of works measuring engagement from facial features.
Moreover, works that have used this approach often rely on the combination of video and self-reports to make the annotation of the video. In addition, models to predict engagement in the literature make an overall analysis at the level of the session, but provide little insights over the state of the engagement of the student on a moment by moment basis.

Another work by Bosch was able to be used, but achieved AUC scores not greater than 0.68. Additional models intended to measure engagement also added other variables to the facial recognition such as heart rate. Current state of the art is Whitehill et al. or Monkaresi et al.

The data includes students attending 9th or 10th grade. Data involved the collection of fourteen 30 minutes sessions (20 fps). The dataset is not fully annotated, suggesting an SSL approach. The approach is summarized as follows:

The data contains video footage in reading and writing tasks. A selection of 6 videos and 1000 frames considered reliable was done to annotate. Sample included 563 engaged frames and 437 non-engaged frames. Engagement is identified via immersion and  concentration on the system (but not frustration). Annontation was eprformed by multiple annotators and had 0.78 of agreement between anotators.
Authors use Facial Action Units, specifically through the OpenFace Toolkit and decrease dimensionality using PCA (unspecified number of components kept, only that 95% of the variability is kept.
Model used is S4VM, a safe version for semi-supervised learning that does not underperform supervised models such as SVM.

annotations.text: The paper focuses on the identification of help-seeking behavior on a sample of 203 CS students. Authors analyze which of 8 potential factors are the best towards determining the behavior, with problem difficulty being the most important. Depending on the type of help-seeking (online or professor-assisted) other predictors were also found to be relevant.

Higher education is becoming more and more sought after by young adults who look at it as an opportunity to better employment and economic stability. However, this increase also increases pressure to develop new ways of teaching that are necessary to provide support to students in need.

Help-seeking is a kill that is very important in these new teaching methodologies. Specifically, online help-seeking is easier for students who may be unconfortable in face to face contexts even though, sometimes, they may struggle in finding the information they need online.
Thus, the paper aims to provide guidelines on online help-seeking. To do that, the authors first determine what are the factors that predict online help-seeking.
Help-seeking is a sequence of actions: realizing you need help, identifying problems and making questions. Literature identifies 3 types of online help-seeking:

Online settings offer a wealth of information that is more unstructured than regular tutor-led help-seeking. While still in its infancy, the authors found that students with smaller prior knowledge of the discipline under study are more likely to not know when to seek help or even to formulate what to ask. Similarly, experienced learners are also more likely to prefer online help-seeking. Novices tend to require structure and someone to tell them what to look for.
Another predictor of help-seeking (especially in face-to-face contexts) is academic performance. Better students tend to be more confident and are more likely to engage in help-seeking.
There are other potential factors such as problem difficulty, age, gender that have the potential to predict help-seeking. However, most of these require validation for online contexts.
What are the most important predictors of student online help-seeking behavior?

The sample uses 203 students attending CS courses, out of whom 162 were novice learners and 41 were advanced learners. The courses used an LMS that allows for communication and help-seeking. Data collection for identification of online help-seeking behavior was done via survey with the exception of dimensions Academic Performance (grade averages) and Learning Proficiency (course being undertaken). These variables were used as input to a Multiple Linear Regression model in order to ascertain what were the most significant predictors of different online help-seeking behaviors (online search and asking peers). Results were evaluated using 1000 repetitions of 10-Fold CV.

For Online help-seeking, the model that resulted in the lowest error was the 4-factors regression comprising the constructs: Academic Performance, LEarning Proficiency, Epistemological Belief and Problem Difficulty.

On the end of seeking Teachers, the 5-factor model (Gender, Academic Performance, Learning Proficiency, Epistemological Beliefs and Problem Difficulty) was the best one, even though it only achieves na R-Squared of 0.08. Finally, on the searching for Peer assistance model, the results show that the 2-factor model (Interest and Problem Difficulty) were the best ones.

Problem difficulty was the most important predictor on all tasks, with 3 others being important in both Online Search and interactions with Professors. The discussion of the negative and positive effects of these constructs are justified under the perspective of learning theory.

It is important to assist students develop online search skills: Students who are novices or academically challenged feel less confortable with having to learn on their own and often tend to avoid reaching out for help due to self-confidence issues. Another important factor is to encourage students to be more active in their learning → maybe even needing to be convinced that independent learning is a viable alternative for them.

extracted.Novelty of Model: Authors argue that it is possible to achieve better performance while doing away with the Attention Layers and using simples dynamic models using embeddings and recurrent or time-delay feed-forward neural networks.

The 2 models that the authors propose:

Bi-GRU Model creates 2 forms embeddings (Skills from Question Sequence) and Responses from Answer Sequence. These are concatenated and passed through a Bi-Directional GRU. 

TDNN is almost similar but, instead of having the GRU layer, it adds 2 Dense layers more.
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a simpler model that beats some of the most common KT methods around.

This is done by introducing embeddings for both skills and responses.

Results show that Bi-GRU outperforms Deep-IRT and DKVMN in most of the benchmark datasets.
